discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Devon and Siroj become closer while making a new snack. Have you ever made food with someone, like a friend or family member? How did cooking together make you feel? In your opinion, can food help us better understand one another?

Devon and Siroj create a new food by combining two of their favorite snacks: Indian Papadum and mini pizzas. What is your favorite food? Partner up with a classmate to see how you can put your two favorite foods together to make a new, yummy snack. After you have thought of your snack, think about what we can learn from each other — how can making something new together help us make friends?

In the story, Devon feels sad that his sister has left for college and is no longer around the house. Have you ever had a sibling or member of your family move away? Did you feel similarly to Devon? Why or why not?

Siroj is a high school student from India studying in America. How do you think Siroj might feel living in another family's house, far from his own home, in a place with different foods, different ways of doing things, etc.? How do you think this story would be different if it was told from Siroj's point of view? As a creative extension, write the story from Siroj's point of view, using the same dialogue, but showing how Siroj feels about living in Devon's house.

articles.title: The Sign
articles.author: Laura Wrang
articles.description: A boy and his mother help a slave escape to the North.
articles.intro: Laura Wrang has written for Highlights . In this short story, Jess and his mother help Orry, a boy escaping slavery.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Jess and Orry’s relationship.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Jess tried to steady his trembling legs. It wasn’t easy to do with the strangers in the house. They were searching every room and surrounding Ma, who stood there bravely.
“A slave?” she was saying. “We have no slaves on our farm.”
“We’re looking for a runaway named Orion,” said a bearded man. “If you’re hiding my slave… ”

Jess didn’t wait to hear more. Ma had helped many slaves on their way to freedom; she was an expert at handling men like these. Jess slipped outdoors. In the distance, the river roared in the night. A light flickered on its bank. Jess ran toward it and gasped, “Orry, they’re searching for you!”
In the glow of the fire, Jess saw Orry wince. Then, with his good arm, Orry grabbed a pail of water and put out the flames.
“Their search won’t be easy.”
“The men won’t find you,” Jess said firmly. “Ma told me what to do. I’ll row you across the river. Then, you go to the farm Ma told you about. They’ll help you from there.”
Orry shook his head. “Taking the boat wouldn’t be safe. The men will probably look here soon. They’d spot us in the water.”

Orry crawled into the pit that he had dug, where he had slept the past few nights. He came out with a bundle and tied it to his belt with a bandanna. He stuck a hunting knife in his sheath, then took a step toward the river.
Jess went cold as he looked across the raging river’s expanse. “Swimming’s too dangerous.”
Orry chuckled softly. “I’m not afraid of the river. I’ll be fine.”
Jess gulped. How could Orry swim with a wounded arm? Six days ago, Orry had staggered into their yard, hurt from his journey. Ma had treated his arm, but it still hadn’t healed completely.
“Even if you make it across, will you be able to find the farm in the dark?” asked Jess.
“Look at the sky,” Orry said gently. “All the stars are out. I reckon they’ll guide me safely.”

Jess tilted his head to the stars he and Orry had loved studying during their brief friendship. Jess had told Orry their names and drawn the constellations for him. But it was Orry who had brought the stars to life. He had made up stories about the dancing Seven Sisters, the rumbling Big Bear, the laughing Little Bear…
“The stars will guide me,” Orry repeated. “When I make it to the opposite bank, I’ll leave a sign for you. Then you’ll know I’m safe.”
“What kind of sign?”
“Why, I’ll — ”
Orry stopped at the sound of a shout. “They’re on their way,” he whispered.

Jess felt a warm hand on his shoulder. Seconds later, Orry was gone. Orry had been his friend for six days of his life; he would miss him all the rest of it. “You’ve got to be safe, Orry,” he thought. “If you can just swim the river, you can make it to the North, too.”
The roaring river filled Jess’s dreams that night. He slept fitfully. But in the morning, the river had quieted. Ma followed Jess to the boat. Together they rowed across the water.
“The men might come back,” said Ma.
“It doesn’t matter,” said Jess. “Orry must be far away by now. When we see his sign on the bank, at least we’ll know that he made it across the river.”
But as they docked, Ma gasped. Tangled in some riverbank weeds was a bandanna.
Jess gritted his teeth. “Orry’s all right. The sign has to be here.”
Jess stumbled through the brush, searching everywhere. He paused at a tree to study some marks on its trunk. Had Orry made them with his knife?
But Ma shook her head. “It’s just woodpeckers, Jess.”

Ma was blinking back tears as they rowed home. Jess stared silently at the shoreline ahead. He tried not to look at the mighty river; he tried not to think of Orry lost in its depths. When they got to the farm, Jess went to work in the yard. He chopped wood until his hands were blistered and burning. Then he dropped to the ground for a rest.

The sky above Jess was blue. But in his mind, it was night again, with the stars shining. Orry’s words came back: “The stars will guide me… I’ll leave a sign… ” A terrible sadness filled Jess. But as he stared upward, a pattern came to mind. Like pieces of a puzzle, the pattern clicked into place.
“Ma!” shouted Jess. Trembling with joy, he jumped up and ran into the house.
“Orry’s name, Ma. Remember what it’s short for?”
Ma said softly, “Orion.”

Jess grabbed his slate and drew a pattern on it. “Those marks on the tree weren’t from woodpeckers. They’re the shape of a constellation.” Jess held out the slate. “The stars of Orion, Ma. Orry made them. He’s safe!”
Ma gave a happy cry and hugged Jess. From the open door came the sound of the rushing river. To Jess, it sounded just like Orry’s soft chuckle.

options.label: Helping others in need is important, even if it's a risk to your own safety.
It's important to pay attention to signs from nature.
Good things happen to those who help others.
It's important to remain positive, even during difficult times.

In the context of the story, what obstacles does Orry have to overcome to achieve freedom? What traits do you think are important for someone to have in order to overcome difficult challenges? Describe a time when you overcame something difficult.
How do you think Jess and his mother are putting themselves at risk by helping Orry? Describe a time when you risked something in order to help someone else.

articles.title: Tsunami
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker describes the wave of a tsunami.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson is an author of books for both children and adults. In this poem, a speaker describes a tsunami wave.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what words the speaker uses to describe the tsunami.
articles.permissions: From Black Stars in a White Night Sky by JonArno Lawson. Copyright © 2008 by JonArno Lawson. Published by WordSong, an imprint of Boyds Mills Press. Used by permission.

blocks.text: The wave travels silently,
without companions,
gathering them into itself.
Passing through
everything
like a ghost,
it rushes with something
to tell the shore.
But by the time it arrives
it can only roar.

options.label: Tsunamis are powerful and sometimes quiet.
Tsunamis are strong and happen again and again.
Tsunamis are an example of the beauty of nature.
Tsunamis are the single most powerful force of nature.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence best describes the theme of the poem?

articles.title: What a Pro Knows: Playing to Win
articles.author: Christine Louise Hohlbaum
articles.description: Christine Louise Hohlbaum discusses Tamika Catchings, a women's national basketball star.
articles.intro: Tamika Catchings is a retired professional basketball player. She spent her entire 15-year career with the Indiana Fever, a women's American basketball team. In this informational text, Christine Louise Hohlbaum discusses Catching's life and accomplishments.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what challenges that Catchings faced growing up.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Olympic gold medalist Tamika Catchings is a star both on and off the court. The daughter of NBA player Harvey Catchings is a Women’s National Basketball Association (WNBA) All-Star. She is also the creator of a foundation that helps children. It may surprise some people to know Catchings was born partially deaf. She says that’s exactly what motivated her to become the star she is today.
Meeting the Challenge

Because she was hard of hearing, Catchings could not speak well, and she had to wear hearing aids. Her classmates teased her constantly. She would run home from school and hide in her room for hours. “I cried and prayed a lot,” she admits.
Catchings spent a lot of time at school playing basketball. She was good at it and she worked hard. She worked hard on her class subjects, too. She began to dream of a career in sports.

“Being good in sports [and at school] was my challenge to those who made fun of me,” she says. “People… couldn’t say that I wasn’t smart, and they couldn’t challenge me on the court, field, or wherever. That was my challenge to them.”

Catchings knew from visiting her father’s practices how much hard work it took to be a pro basketball player. But she knew what she wanted and was willing to work for it. The fact that there was no professional women’s basketball team at the time didn’t stop her from reaching for her goal. She just thought she would have to join a men’s team. Then in 1996, the WNBA was formed.

Today, Catchings plays for the Indiana Fever. She does not wear her hearing aids on the court. She does wear them when she speaks publicly and whenever else she thinks she needs them. She travels the world to play basketball and to speak out on behalf of hard of-hearing people.

Catchings’s dedication to her family is constant. “There were plenty of times that I wanted to give up, but my family was there to support me to strive for bigger and better things,” she says. “Giving up was not an option.”

options.label: Catchings felt pressured by her father to be the best female player in basketball.
Catchings experienced unfair treatment in basketball because of her gender.
Catchings didn't think that she could play basketball with her hearing loss.
Catchings used her struggles in life to drive her to do her best in sports.

questions.prompt: PART A: What is the central idea of the text?

"Catchings knew from visiting her father's practices how much hard work it took to be a pro basketball player." (Paragraph 5)
"She travels the world to play basketball and to speak out on behalf of hard of-hearing people." (Paragraph 6)

articles.title: Little Yin and the Moon
articles.author: Jeremy Yoder
articles.description: A little girl helps her family by restoring balance in nature.
articles.intro: Jeremy Yoder is an American author. In this story, a little girl named Yin must take action when hard times hit her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Yin’s thoughts and actions as she tries to solve a problem.
articles.permissions: "Little Yin and the Moon " by Jeremy Yoder, Spider, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: One hot day, Yin heard her mother weeping in a corner of their hut. In her mother’s arms lay Yin’s baby brother, Zhou, who was also crying. Yin drew close, placing a hand on her mother’s arm. “Mother, why do you cry?”
Her mother spoke between sobs. “Little Yin, I cry because the fields are almost empty, and the cow has stopped giving milk. Our family is hungry, and I am frightened.”
Yin had never seen her mother look so sad or so scared.

“Mother, I will find a way to help,” Yin said with determination. She put on her wide straw hat and went out to the stable where their red cow stared over the wooden fence. Yin gently stroked the cow’s forehead. “Cow, why do you not give milk to feed my family?”
The cow blinked her large brown eyes. “Little Yin, the brook has very little water. Without more to drink, I cannot give any milk.”
Yin crossed the dry rice field to the skinny brook. She knelt down and gently placed her hand in the warm water. “Brook, why do you not give more water so that the cow may drink her fill?”
The brook, who usually rushed past, now only trickled. “Little Yin, I cannot give much water because the mountain lake does not offer me more.”
Yin looked up the mountain. Traveling to the lake would take all day. After taking a tiny bit of food and a blanket for warmth, she kissed her family goodbye and headed out.

As darkness fell, Yin reached the small lake halfway up the mountain. She took off a shoe and dipped her toes into the water, creating ripples on the glassy surface. “Lake, why do you not give more water to the brook so that he may laugh again?”
The lake brushed a few feeble waves against the shore. “Little Yin, I have grown shallow. If I give more water I will disappear, and then there will be no brook at all.”
“How can I give you more water?” Yin asked.
The lake sighed. “There is nothing you can do. I only grow when it rains. The sun has burned brightly for a long time and will not let rain fall.”
“There must be something I can do,” said Yin. “But for now, I must wait for the sun.” Yin spread out her blanket and ate the little food she had brought. Still hungry, she lay down and slept.
The next morning, the sun peeked over the horizon. Yin wondered how she could talk to the sun because he was so far away. Gathering her blanket, she looked up the mountain and started to climb.

At long last, she reached the top. As the sun rose to the highest point in the sky, Yin squinted against the heat and shouted, “Sun, why do you burn so brightly? Why do you not let rain fall and fill the lake?”
Sun gazed down with a stern look. “I burn brightly because I am angry that I cannot help Moon, who has cried for many months.”

Not knowing what to do next, Yin waited at the top of the mountain. She was very hungry by now and wanted to go home, but the thought of her family gave her strength. Yin wrapped herself in her blanket. Finally night came, and with it, the moon and stars.
Moon stared down with such sadness that Yin felt she might cry, too. “Moon, why do you weep and make Sun angry?”
Moon sniffled several times. “I cry because I am sad, for I am always cold. Sun is angry because he cannot give me his warmth, for he may only shine during the day, and I at night.”
Yin was just a poor peasant girl, but she would offer all she had. Draping her blanket over her hands, she held it up to Moon. “Please, take my blanket and stay warm.”

A soft wind blew the blanket high into the air. Moon accepted the blanket and drew it tight, until only a sliver of Moon could be seen. “Thank you, Little Yin, for your generous gift. I shall now sleep well.”
Though she was very cold, Yin slept soundly. The next morning, the sky swirled with dark clouds. Yin hurried down the mountain. When she reached the lake, lightning flashed, and rain began to fall.
As Yin ran faster, the sky opened and great drops fell into the lake. By the time Yin reached her home, the brook was bubbling with laughter, and the cow was drinking her fill.
“Little Yin!” her mother cried happily as Zhou bounced in her arms. “Because of you, our family may eat once again.”

While Yin’s mother milked the cow, Yin gathered what little rice remained in the field, knowing more would grow back soon. After they ate a meal together, Yin wept with joy as Zhou smiled and fell asleep with a full belly.

Over the years, as Little Yin grew into an old woman, she would gaze up at Moon and see her blanket. Sometimes only a little bit covered Moon’s toes, and other times, the blanket covered Moon entirely. Each time Yin watched, she felt happy, knowing Moon would forever stay warm.

articles.title: Grandpa's Magic Hat
articles.author: Marilyn Helmer
articles.description: Two brothers come up with a plan to test their grandpa's magic hat.
articles.intro: Marilyn Halmer is a Canadian author who has written many books and stories for children and young adults. In olden times, farmers had young children run through crop fields to scare crows and other birds away from the harvest. Today, farmers use sticks and straw to create a human-looking body to scare away birds and other animals from their crops.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Matt and Luke feel about grandpa’s magic hat.
articles.permissions: "Grandpa's Magic Hat" by Marilyn Helmer, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: My grandpa had a hat, a plain old wide-brimmed straw with a red band around the crown. Strange thing was, he never wore the hat. He just kept it on a stand on his dresser, like it was something special.
One day my brother Luke and I were in Grandpa’s room, helping him sort his fishing tackle. The hat caught Luke’s eye. “Grandpa, you never wear that old straw hat,” he said. “Why do you keep it?”
Grandpa picked up the hat. “This here’s a magic hat, Luke,” he said, his voice whispery low, like in a scary movie.
Luke’s eyes almost popped out of his head. “A magic hat?”
Grandpa nodded. “It can bring things to life.”
I’m three years older than Luke and a whole lot wiser. “Come on, Grandpa. There’s no such thing as a hat that can bring things to life.”
“You know that for sure, do you, Matt?” Grandpa looked me straight in the eye so it was hard to tell if he was joking or not.
Before I could answer, Grandpa’s friend Clem hollered through the screen door, “Hurry up, Jack, the fishing crew’s waiting.”
Grandpa put the hat back on its stand. “See you tomorrow.” With a wink, he was gone.
Luke had that spacey look he gets when he’s thinking hard.
“You know that hat has no more magic in it than a burst balloon,” I said.
I could see Luke was fighting with himself. He suspected I was right, but that wasn’t what he wanted to believe.
“We’ll prove it, Matt,” he said. “One way or the other. And I know just the thing to do it. Old Man Miller’s scarecrow.”

Old Man Miller lives up the road around the bend. He has a big garden, and smack in the middle of it is a scarecrow. It’s no ordinary scarecrow. Every year it gets a new set of old clothes. I’ve heard people say it’s so lifelike, it has fooled a few folks coming home late at night.
I glanced at the hat stand. “It’s nothing but a plain old hat. It’s not going to bring a scarecrow to life. The magic part is just another of Grandpa’s tall tales.”
The set of Luke’s jaw told me words alone weren’t going to work. “OK,” I said. “You want proof, we’ll get it.”

Luke nodded. “This evening, on the way to the barn dance. Mom and Dad are going early because Dad’s playing the fiddle. We’ll sneak the hat out of the house and put it on the scarecrow’s head. Then we’ll see what happens.”

I thought that over. The barn wasn’t far past Old Man Miller’s place. We could put the hat on the scarecrow’s head, watch nothing happen, sneak it back into the house, and go on to the dance. No one except Luke would be any the wiser. “OK. We’ll do it. Then you’ll see for sure that there’s no magic in Grandpa’s straw hat.”
Luke’s plan worked like a charm. Almost.
After Mom and Dad left, we got Grandpa’s hat and headed for Old Man Miller’s. The house was in darkness. Old Man Miller goes to bed with the chickens.
We sneaked into the garden. The scarecrow stood wedged on a pole between two large rocks. He was dressed in jeans and a green-and-white plaid shirt. Around his neck was a jaunty yellow bandana.

I climbed onto one of the rocks. As I put the hat on the scarecrow’s head, I looked into its pale, red-cheeked face. I thought my heart was going to jump right out of my chest. I could have sworn the thing winked at me.
“What’s the matter?” Luke called up.
I jumped down, too spooked to answer. That’s when Old Man Miller’s dog started barking. Lights flicked on inside the house.
“Let’s get out of here,” Luke hissed. I didn’t need a second invitation. We took off up that road, leaving dust clouds in our wake.
We didn’t stop running until we reached the barn. I was trying to catch my breath when Luke let out a groan. “Matt, we left Grandpa’s hat behind!”
I looked back. The scarecrow was shrouded in evening shadows. Grandpa’s hat was clamped on its head, just the way I’d left it.
My heart was still jumping like a crazed bullfrog. “Are you satisfied now?” I growled at Luke. “I told you there’s no magic in that hat. That scarecrow’s as dead as a doornail.”
“But what are we going to do about Grandpa’s hat?” Luke persisted.
“We’ll get it on the way home,” I said.
Fiddle music flowed from the barn, safe and sweet. I headed for the door with Luke right on my heels.

Inside, Dad was playing the fiddle while Mom called the steps. Folks clapped in time, dancing and exchanging how-do-you-dos. My spooky feeling eased away. The barn felt like the safest, friendliest place on earth.

At the back, a table was laid out with dishes of homemade food. The food and the fiddling were what Luke and I came for. We danced a bit first, just to show we could. As soon as we thought it polite, we filled our plates and found a place to sit.

I was halfway through a slice of the best pork pie I’d ever tasted when Luke whacked my elbow. He pointed to the door. A stranger had come in. He was wearing jeans and a green-and-white plaid shirt. Around his neck, below his pale, red-cheeked face, was a yellow bandana. On his head sat a wide-brimmed straw hat with a red band around the crown.
“Matt...,” Luke choked out.
I hardly heard him. A cold, prickly feeling swept through me as I stared at the stranger.

He was dancing with Miss Betty Macon. Miss Betty is the best dancer in the county. The stranger was every bit as good as she was. Back and forth, round and round they went. Folks stood back to watch them.
The stranger was working up a sweat. I could see it, glistening on his forehead. Miss Betty must have noticed it, too. She took a dainty lace handkerchief from her pocket and handed it to him.

Luke and I didn’t have a chance to see what happened next because Mom marched over. “You two are white as paste,” she declared. “I reckon you’re coming down with something. I’m taking you home right now.” Neither of us protested.
Mom took the main road so we didn’t go past Old Man Miller’s place. It was just as well. The way I felt, I didn’t want to know.
When we got home, she sent us to bed, so there was nothing we could do about Grandpa’s hat. Luke fell asleep but I lay awake, searching my mind for a rational explanation of what we had seen.

Finally, it hit me. A word I had missed on a spelling test. Coincidence . That’s what it was. A coincidence . The more I thought about it, the more it made sense. The clothes the scarecrow was dressed in weren’t unusual. They could be found in any dry goods store. Same with the straw hat. It just happened to look like Grandpa’s. Eventually the cold, prickly feeling eased and I fell into a dreamless sleep.
At the first light of dawn, I shook Luke awake. “We have to go get Grandpa’s hat back.”
Luke gave me a groggy stare. “We can’t get Grandpa’s hat back. The scarecrow man’s got it.”
I let out a hissy sigh. “Luke, Old Man Miller’s scarecrow will be right there in the garden where we left it. There’s no magic in that straw hat. The scarecrow didn’t come to life.”
“But his clothes...,” Luke began.
“The stranger’s clothes just happened to look like the ones the scare-crow was wearing. You can buy clothes like that anywhere,” I snapped.
“His hat was exactly like Grandpa’s magic hat,” Luke snapped back.
“Hats like that are a dime a dozen," I countered.
“We’ll never get Grandpa’s hat back,” Luke went on as though he hadn’t heard me. “It brought the scarecrow to life, Matt. He’ll never go back to Old Man Miller’s garden again.”
“Luke, the scarecrow did not come to life. He’ll be there in the garden like always. You’ll see.”

Outside, the grass sparkled with dew as the sun rose in a blaze of glory. As we came around the bend, I sent a whoop of joy into the quiet morning air. The scarecrow stood where he always had. On his head was Grandpa’s straw hat.
I raced ahead, scrambled up onto the rocks and grabbed the hat. I looked into the scarecrow’s face. Not a wink, not even a twitch.

But as I went to jump down, something caught my eye. To this day, I haven’t told anyone about it, not even Luke. Neither rational explanation nor coincidence can explain it. Sticking out of the scarecrow’s pocket was a dainty lace handkerchief.
So... is there such a thing as magic or not? I’ll let you decide.

articles.title: The Child with Your Name
articles.author: Debbie Urbanski
articles.description: In this text, told in the second person, a child with the reader's name goes on an adventure.
articles.intro: Maggie Urbanski is a writer who has published many science fiction and fantasy short stories for adults and children. In this story, a child with your name uses their imagination to go on an adventure.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the child with your name’s actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Child with Your Name " by Debbie Urbanski, Spider, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Once there was a child with your name. Though it wasn’t you. The child had hair about your length and your color eyes. But this is probably a coincidence! Because this child was about to go on an adventure. You don’t like to go on adventures, do you?
Oh, you do? Perhaps it was you.
The child with your name put on your puffy coat, grabbed the ancient map from your bookshelf, and headed outside.
Wait. You don’t have an ancient map on your bookshelf? Maybe you should look more closely next time (on the bottom shelf in the far back).
A red line appeared on the map. The line began at your house, twisted through the enchanted woods, and ended at a star. Below the star were the words DANGER, HELP, and WATCH OUT!!!
The child with your name followed the map. The enchanted woods were dark and covered in sticky shadows. The child bravely entered the woods anyway.
Was this a good idea? I’m not sure. Not all adventures are good ideas.
There was no path between the trees. The child with your name tripped over rocks and roots. Something in the woods slithered. Something hooted. Something scratched.

Finally, the child stumbled into a clearing where there was a cottage, a cauldron, and a star. Well, only part of a star because stars, as you probably know, are very large. This particular star part looked miserable. It was chained to a tree.
When the star saw the child, it began to shine.
“Who lives here?” whispered the child with your name.
“A star-eater,” the star whispered back. “Hurry. Can you throw me high enough so I can get back home?”

Stars are heavy. Even part of a star is difficult to lift. But the child with your name was so strong. The child unchained the star, took three deep breaths, and threw the star upward (as hard as you can throw). Golden stardust swirled in the air and coated the child’s coat sleeves.
“Thank you!” the star cried out before it found its place in the sky.
Oh, you didn’t know a child with your name could rescue a star? Well, now you know.
A roar erupted from the cottage. Tentacles, scales, and a spiky tail swarmed out the cottage door. “Where is my star?” yelled a crackling voice.
The child with your name turned and ran.
A star-eater is fast, but you are faster. The child with your name hurried through the forest and back home, and spent the rest of the afternoon scraping off stardust.
The child kept the golden dust in a jar in your closet. The jar shone all day and all night.
You aren’t sure you have a jar of stardust in your closet? Check the top shelf on the right.
Hold on to such light. It might come in useful for future adventures to dark places, should this child with your name — or you — go adventuring again soon.

In this story, the child finds magical objects in their room to take on an adventure. What would you take with you on an adventure? Why? How would it help you?

The child in the story acts bravely to free the trapped star. How do you think the child felt when they saw the star? Why did they decide to help? When was a time you had to be brave? What did you do?

articles.title: Finding Prince Deming
articles.author: Clare Mishica
articles.description: In this short story, an adviser helps a king discover who is the true prince.
articles.intro: Kings and other rulers have advisers, or people they trust who give them advice, or helpful tips, to help them make good choices. In this story, the king's adviser, Hartwig, must help the king solve a problem.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Hartwig’s actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “Hartwig! Hartwig!” Old King Bo called. He had a puzzling problem and needed someone with quick wits to help him untangle it.
“What is it, Your Majesty?” asked his adviser, Hartwig, hurrying into the throne room. His robes swirled around him, and his bright gray eyes sparkled like polished silver.

“Two young men have come to the castle, and both claim to be Prince Deming from the North Forest,” King Bo explained. “As you know, the true Prince Deming will eventually be crowned king, and he is supposed to start studying with me tomorrow. How will I tell which is the impostor?”
Hartwig sat on the velvet footstool near the king’s feet. “First, tell me about them.”
“This morning, both men arrived dressed in dusty cloaks,” King Bo began. “They’d been traveling from the same direction and their faces had tired lines across them.”
“Did you speak with them separately?” Hartwig asked. He was the kind of person who reviewed all pieces of a puzzle, studying the edges and colors.

“Yes,” King Bo said. “I asked each of them about the North Forest, and they both said the pine trees there are so tall they look like prickly green mountains. They gave the same answers to every question I asked. Each one claimed he’d met the other prince while traveling and that the other had followed him to the castle hoping to steal his place.” He groaned in frustration.“They look alike, too! How will I know which one to trust with my secrets?”
Hartwig thought for a while, then finally said, “I have an idea.” He explained his idea to the king, and the king nodded and smiled in approval.

That afternoon, Hartwig asked the two princes to follow him. He led them across the drawbridge and down a grassy hill to a patch of black soil. Then Hartwig took some little round seeds from a silky, small bag. In a mysterious voice, he whispered, “ Ebba-ebba-ebba-bee .”
The princes stared at Hartwig with eyes the size of dinner plates. They had heard he had amazing powers and wondered if the seeds would turn into a toad or a bear or a dragon.

Hartwig faced them, the hint of a smile tugging at his lips. He handed each prince a seed. “You must each plant one seed. In the morning, there will be a purple flower growing, and it will show me the true Prince Deming.”
So he does use magic to find his answers , Prince One thought as he nervously dropped a seed into the earth.
Prince Two planted his small round dot. His spells must be powerful .

“When the sun rises in the morning, we’ll return to this very spot with King Bo,” Hartwig explained as the two princes stared at the black soil. They were expecting to see a green shoot sprout out of the earth.
“You’ll have to wait until morning for the answer,” Hartwig told them. He rubbed his chin, hiding a grin behind his hand.

The next day, as soon as the sun’s rays poked into the castle windows, the two princes scrambled from their beds. They found Hartwig and King Bo waiting for them, and they marched down the grassy hill again.
When they neared the patch of soil, only one stem with a purple flower bobbed in the gentle morning breeze. It stood where Prince One had planted his seed.
“Look!” Prince One boasted. “Your magic spell proves that I am the true prince. I am next in line to be king.”

Hartwig chuckled. “But there was no magic spell. Your seed has grown because you crept outside in the moonlight and planted the purple flower. I watched you from the tower. In fact, that blossom proves you are an impostor.”
“What?” sputtered Prince One.
“Only an impostor would worry about the flower not growing,” explained Hartwig. “The true prince did not need to hide the truth by planting a flower.”
“You tricked me!” roared Prince One, his face turning red as a blazing fire.
Hartwig shook his head, “Your own lie has tricked you.”
King Bo pointed toward the road, and Prince One stomped off like an angry bull.
Then King Bo turned toward Prince Two. “We’ve found the true Prince Deming.”
“I will try to be a good student, Your Majesty,” Prince Deming said as he bowed before the king. Then he turned to Hartwig. “Will you help me, Hartwig?”
“Of course,” said Hartwig. “I may know a few more tricks.”

articles.title: Mirabella the Magnificent
articles.author: Cheryl Mendenhall
articles.description: A young princess uses quick-thinking to teach a dragon a lesson.
articles.intro: In this story, a young princess named Mirabella must protect her castle from a dragon.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Mirabella’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Mirabella the Magnificent" by Cheryl Mendenhall, Cricket Magazine , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Princess Mirabella was sprawled on the throne-room floor with her favorite jigsaw puzzle when the dragon arrived.
“Princess,” the prime minister said, stepping carefully between the scattered puzzle pieces, “there’s a dragon at the gate.”
“Can’t the guards handle him?” Mirabella asked.
“The guards are hiding under the castle welcome mat,” the prime minister said.
Mirabella sighed. She brushed off her jeans and wandered out to the courtyard. The dragon towered above her, a mountain of claws and spikes and shiny, green scales.
“May I help you?” Mirabella asked.
“Go away, little girl,” the dragon snapped. “I have a message for the important, big people.”
“The important, big people are all busy,” Princess Mirabella said. “You’ll have to give the message to me.”
“Oh, I’ll give it to you,” sneered the dragon. He threw back his head and shot a jet of flames into the air. “Cough up your gold,” he roared. “Or this castle is pot roast.”
Princess Mirabella cupped her hands under her chin. “Gold?” she said.
“Yellow stuff,” the dragon snapped. “Stuff that shines like the sun.”
“Oh, we have that,” Mirabella said. “I can show you.”
So Mirabella the Magnificent laced up her hiking boots and led the dragon into the woods.
“Funny place to keep gold,” the dragon mumbled, his wings snagging on low-hanging branches.
“There.” Princess Mirabella pointed to a hole in a tree.
The dragon narrowed his eyes and stretched his neck.
Beads of sunlight trickled down the bark. With a wicked grin, the dragon crammed his snout into the opening.
“YEOW!” The dragon crashed away through the underbrush with a thick carpet of bees swarming around his head.
“It’s polite to ask first when you want honey,” Mirabella called after him.
The following week, Mirabella the Magnificent went rollerblading around the castle garden.
“I’m afraid the dragon’s back,” the prime minister announced.
The princess finished her figure eight and rolled out to the courtyard to see what she could do.
“You again!” The dragon wrinkled his snout. It was still puffy from the bee stings and dotted with pink goop.
“Forget the gold. Fork over your diamonds.”
“Diamonds?” Princess Mirabella asked.
“Little bright things,” the dragon snapped. “Things that sparkle like the moon and stars.”
“Oh, we have those,” she said. “Follow me.”
Mirabella the Magnificent pulled on her waders, grabbed a net, and led the way to the castle duck pond. The dragon slipped and belly-flopped down the muddy bank.
“Look.” Mirabella pointed to the bright flashes darting between the ripples.
“Funny place to keep diamonds,” the dragon mumbled. But he plunged his head under the water.
“MURPH — MUMFLE — MULP — KERCHOO!” The dragon came up sneezing and sputtering. A stream of wiggling small fish poured from his nostrils.
“They’re easier to catch this way,” the princess said. She dipped her net into the pond. “See?”

The dragon’s face went purple. His belly rumbled. He opened his jaws, but all that came out was a damp, gray cloud. Mirabella just looked at him. The embarrassed dragon slunk away with his tail tucked between his legs.
He was back by evening.
Princess Mirabella was setting up her telescope on the tower balcony.
“Hello!” She waved. “We should get a good view of Draco and Cassiopeia tonight.”
The dragon thumped his tail against the castle wall. “No more games! I’m here for the princess.”
“The princess!” Mirabella cried. She sat on a ledge and swung her feet, which were sporting pink bunny slippers. “What would you do with a princess?”
“Lock her in my cave and enjoy her screams,” the dragon snarled.
“A cave!” Mirabella clapped her hands. “Does it have bats? What about stalactites and stalagmites?”
“Stalag — what?” the dragon asked.
“Pointy rocks: some growing up, the others growing down,” she explained.
“Well, actually, yes. We have those,” the dragon admitted.
“Then what are we waiting for?” Princess Mirabella said. “I’ll get my headlamp, and we can go exploring. I bet I can find all kinds of things to show you in a cave.”
The dragon’s jaw dropped to his toenails. “You? You’re Princess Mirabella?”
“Mirabella the Magnificent,” she said, beaming.
The dragon’s green scales turned pale and waxy as he slowly backed away from the princess. “Um . . . sorry,” he said. “Another time, maybe. I have to...uh...meet this knight named George...”
Then the dragon quickly threw open his wings and flew away as fast as they could carry him.
Princess Mirabella watched through her telescope until he disappeared from sight. “Not a bad guy, for a dragon,” she commented. “But he has a lot to learn about princesses.”
And, folding up her telescope, Mirabella the Magnificent showed herself to the castle kitchen for a peanut butter sandwich.

Even though Mirabella is a princess, she still likes to do jigsaw puzzles and rollerblade around the castle. If you were a prince or princess, what kind of things do you think you would do in your castle? How would living in a castle be similar or different to how you live now?
Imagine that the dragon came and asked you for something that is important to you or your family, like a TV or jewelry. Would you give the dragon what it wanted? Or, would you trick the dragon? Why?

articles.title: The Water Bucket
articles.subtitle: A Chinese Folk Tale
articles.author: Phyllis Gershator
articles.description: In the Chinese legend of Shui-mu, a poor girl receives a magic water bucket.
articles.intro: Phyllis Gershator is an award winning children's author. This story is a retelling of a Chinese legend about the water spirit, Shui-mu. In this story, Shui-mu has a hard time pleasing her future mother-in-law.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Mother’s actions.
articles.permissions: "The Water Bucket" by Phyllis Gershator, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: When Shui-mu was still a baby, her parents arranged for her to marry a boy in another village. Then, while still a child, even before she had grown old enough to marry him, she was sent away to live with his family.

Like a good daughter, she helped the boy’s mother wash, sew, cook, sweep, garden, scrub, polish, mop, and shop. But the harder Shui-mu worked, the more work she was given, all day long and late into the night. Though Shui-mu tried hard to please, no matter how exhausted she was, Mother always found fault.
“Lazybones, didn’t you see this speck of dirt on the floor?” Mother would scream. “Sweep it again!”
At mealtimes, she’d shout, “Taste these pickled vegetables! They’re far too salty!” And to prove it, she threw them to the pigs.
She was never satisfied, however spotless the floor or saltless the pickles.

Once Shui-mu grew a little older and strong enough to carry water, Mother dismissed the water carriers. “We cannot tolerate idleness, Shui-mu,” she said. “Since the water carriers no longer work for us, you must carry the water from the well yourself.”
“How can I feed the pigs, mend the clothes, cook the food, clean the house, and carry all the water, too?” Shui-mu asked. “The day isn’t long enough.”

“How can you do it? What a question! Do you have two arms and two legs? You ungrateful girl, do you think you can talk back to me? Do you think you can disobey your future husband’s mother? My son will hear of this!”

Roused from his afternoon nap, Shui-mu’s future husband did hear of it, and said sleepily, “Shui-mu, my mother cannot do all the chores herself. You have to help. The least you could do is carry a bucket of water once in a while.”

And so, as soon as the sun came up, Shui-mu could be seen returning from the well with two buckets of water, one balanced on each end of the pole she carried over her shoulders. During the day she returned again and again to the well for water. She needed water to wash clothes, to cook the wheat and millet, to make tea, to fill the pig bucket...water, water, water, water!

Back and forth she went, until one day she sat down on the edge of the well and cried. “I cannot carry another drop of water. What shall I do? Run away? But where can I go?” She looked down, deep into the well…
A soft voice said, “Come, come, nothing is as bad as all that.”
An old woman with long white hair appeared at her side. She took Shui-mu by the hand. “Tell me what is troubling you,” she said. She spoke in such a kindly way that Shui-mu told her everything.
The white-haired woman gave Shui-mu a bamboo rod. “From now on, when you wake up in the morning, strike your bucket once with this rod and say:
Come, sweet water, drop by drop,
Fill my bucket to the top.
“But remember,” warned the old woman, “never strike the bucket more than once.”
With those words, a gust of wind carried the woman away. Her white hair left a wispy trail of clouds behind her as she rose over Xuanweng Mountain, high up into the heavens.
The next time she needed water, Shui-mu did as she’d been told. She struck the bucket only once, saying:
Come, sweet water, drop by drop,
Fill my bucket to the top.

No longer did Shui-mu trudge back and forth to the well all day. Her future mother-in-law noticed and wondered where the water in the water bucket was coming from. One morning, instead of taking her midmorning nap, she spied on Shui-mu. She saw Shui-mu strike the bucket with a bamboo rod and heard her say:
Come, sweet water, drop by drop,
Fill my bucket to the top.
Mother burned with impatience to try the magic rod for herself. As soon as Shui-mu went outside to feed the pigs, she had her chance. She snatched the rod and bucket and repeated the words:
Come, sweet water, drop by drop,
Fill my bucket to the top.
Slowly the water began to fill the bucket. “Hurry up!” she shouted. “I don’t have all day!” She struck the bucket again and again.
The bucket continued to fill, faster and faster. The water didn’t stop flowing when it reached the top. It spilled over the rim and onto the floor.

Mother beat the bucket, crying, “Stop! Stop!” But the more she beat, the faster flowed the water. It rushed and whirled out the back door. The river of water swirled so broad and fast, it uprooted a tree standing in its path! The old woman slipped and was instantly carried away in the deluge.
“Help!” she cried, as she followed the tree downstream.
The shouting woke her son from his midmorning nap. Rising from the couch, he called loudly, “Shui-mu, why don’t you come and help my mother?”
The torrential river washed him away, too, along with his couch.
When Shui-mu saw the flood of water, she ran back into the house through the front door. It was just as she thought. Someone had struck the bucket — more than once!
She bravely grabbed the overflowing bucket and flung it out the back door, into the hole left by the uprooted tree. Loose mud and stones filled the hole and buried the bucket.

Water continued to bubble up from the ground. Shui-mu built a stone and earth wall, guiding the water from the spring into a gently flowing river. And she continued to work hard tidying up after the flood, feeding the pigs, weeding the garden, and preparing perfectly salted pickles.

Mother and her sleepy son never returned. Shui-mu didn’t miss them. She didn’t miss the magic water bucket either, because the water never stopped. It bubbled away, sparkling clear and sweet. Shui-mu shared the water with her neighbors, saying it was for all of them, for all time.

Because of that good deed, a grateful village built a temple in her honor, and the temple is still there, at the foot of Xuanweng Mountain. So is the spring, and the water is as sparkling and sweet as ever.

options.label: Wait calmly or rush into trouble.

Help others in their time of need.

articles.title: Sir Thomas and the Dragon
articles.author: Monica M. Roe
articles.description: A scared knight faces his fear of scaly creatures.
articles.intro: Knights were soldiers who worked for kings and queens to protect them, or keep them safe. They were known for being very brave and often put themselves in danger. In this story, one knight has a secret fear.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Sir Thomas solves his problem.
articles.permissions: "Sir Thomas and the Dragon" by Monica M. Roe, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “Sir Thomas, come out this minute! This is no way for King Alfred’s Protector of the Realm to behave!” Elberta, the castle cook, rapped on the door of the chicken coop with her wooden spoon.
The door opened a crack. “Is it gone?”
Elberta sighed. “Yes, Sir Thomas, the toad is gone. I chased it out of the yard.”

The door creaked open. The knight emerged, stuffed a squawking rooster back into the coop, and slammed the door. There were feathers stuck in his armor, and his helmet was crooked. Looking at him, Elberta shook her head.
“This is going to be a problem.”

It certainly was. Sir Thomas had arrived last month in King Alfred’s kingdom in need of a job and had been hired as Protector of the Realm. He’d already chased away two bands of robbers, a wild boar, and a cranky giant, earning himself a fine reputation. People called him Sir Thomas the Triumphant. But Sir Thomas had a secret that only Elberta knew. An embarrassing, terrible secret. A secret that had cost him his last job.

Sir Thomas was afraid of all creatures slimy or scaly. Frogs, lizards, and snakes sent him into a panic. But dragons were the worst. One look at a dragon, and Sir Thomas’s armor-clad knees knocked together like trash can lids, and he stuttered in terror. In short, Sir Thomas the Triumphant quickly became Tommy the Terrified.

In the last kingdom where he’d worked, he’d dutifully walked onto the drawbridge (knees clanking embarrassingly) to challenge an invading dragon who’d mistaken the castle moat for a private swimming pool. While trying to draw his sword, he’d accidentally knocked his helmet over his eyes, lost his balance, and tumbled into the moat. The dragon laughed so hard he nearly drowned, and the splash attracted the attention of everyone in the castle. Sir Thomas had been fished out and immediately fired. What good was a knight who couldn’t protect the kingdom from a dragon?
Sir Thomas sighed and picked some feathers from his helmet visor. “I know it’s a problem, Elberta,” he said gloomily, “but what can I do? I’m afraid of them, and that’s that.”
“But you’re going to have to fight one sometime.” Elberta looked sympathetic but worried. “That’s part of a knight’s job.”
Sometime came quite soon.
On a thunderstormy Thursday, Sir Thomas received a royal summons.
“Yes, Your Majesty?” Sir Thomas said, standing before the royal throne.

“A dragon has moved in to an empty cave at the northern border,” growled the king as he lounged with his legs over the throne’s armrest. “As if I didn’t have enough trouble as it is! The Fall Frolic and Pheasant Feast is two days away, and it hasn’t stopped raining for a week! Not one stick of dry wood in the realm! Will someone please tell me how we can have the year’s biggest celebration if we can’t even start the pheasant-roasting fire?” For emphasis, the king tossed his crown into the air, where it got stuck on the chandelier.
Sir Thomas’s heart plummeted. “And you wish me to...”
“You’re my royal knight, man!” the king roared. “I want that dragon vanquished by sundown tomorrow!”
“Y-yes, Your Majesty.”
“Well,” said Sir Thomas sadly, ducking his head against the driving rain as he strapped on his armor, “that’s the end of this job.”
Elberta tried to cheer him up. “Maybe it won’t be as bad as you think.”
It’ll probably be worse, he thought.
Sir Thomas mounted his horse and splashed off to face his greatest fear and, he was sure, the end of his job.
When he arrived at the cave, there was no sign of the dragon. Sir Thomas took a shaky breath. “C-come out, D-dragon! I ch-ch-challenge you to b-battle!” he stuttered.
Nobody appeared in the doorway, but a small voice whispered, “Please go away!”
Slightly puzzled, Sir Thomas tried again. “I-I c-come to r-rid the k-kingdom of the awful d-d-dragon!” His armor began to tremble and clank.
“Why can’t you just l-leave me alone?” The voice sounded more scared than scary. Sir Thomas didn’t know what to think.
“Well,” he began, feeling less panicky, “I-I’m the king’s knight and I’m supposed to chase away all dangerous enemies.”
“Oh sure,” said the voice sadly, “just because I’m a dragon, people think I’m going to eat everyone or burn things up.”
Completely confused, Sir Thomas asked, “You mean you’re not going to do those things? I thought that was what dragons do.”
“How many dragons have you asked?” said the small voice, echoing out of the cave.
“That’s a good point,” noted Sir Thomas. “Look, could you come out here?”
“Not if you’re going to chase me around with a sword!”
Sir Thomas put his sword away. “I promise — no sword.”
“OK.” Slowly a small, green dragon appeared. He was shaking a little. At the sight of him, Sir Thomas felt a bit woozy, but not as frightened as usual.
“So let me get this straight. You don’t want to destroy the kingdom and eat people?”
The dragon sniffled. “Of course not. I like people, but they’re always chasing me away.”
Sir Thomas could understand about wanting a place to belong. He scratched his head. “Well, maybe you could stay here.”
“But how?”
The knight’s brow wrinkled thoughtfully, then he smiled, “I’ve got it! Come with me!”

Sir Thomas and the dragon squished back to the castle, where the knight told the king his plan for saving the Pheasant Feast and adding a new member to the castle staff. Though King Alfred was cautious at first, the little dragon’s roaring success at getting the soggy firewood blazing at the Feast impressed him greatly. While chomping a golden, juicy pheasant leg, he announced that the kingdom had gotten a brand-new Diplomat of Dragon Relations.

Thereafter, whenever another dragon came into the kingdom, the castle dragon was always sent to establish friendly contact. Sir Thomas never again had to challenge a dragon. His job was safe for good. The only problem was that a toad in the courtyard would still send Thomas the Triumphant fleeing to the chicken coop. But Sir Thomas, Elberta, and the chickens all agreed to keep that secret to themselves.

options.label: You should never be scared of anything.
You can still be brave when you are scared.

You should work hard to keep the king happy. E. Find friends who will keep you safe from danger. F. People are often scared of what they do not understand.

articles.title: The Hanukkah Candle
articles.author: Samantha Beal
articles.description: On the second night of Hanukkah, a young girl loses the shamash candle during an air raid.
articles.intro: The Blitz lasted in England from 1940-1941. This was a time when Germany dropped bombs on England. To stay safe, people went into bomb shelters, areas which were built into the ground and made of materials like brick and steel. In this story, a young girl, Atara, and her mother are celebrating the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah by lighting the menorah, which is a candlestick with nine branches. One candle is lit on each of the eight days of Hanukkah using the ninth candle, called the shamash.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why the shamash candle is special.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Mama set the menorah on the kitchen table and handed me the beeswax shamash.
“Light the candles after we pray, Atara,” she said.

This was the first year I lit the menorah. It was the first Hanukkah without Papa too. He was fighting Nazis for England. Our Hanukkah was small, just Mama and me exchanging small gifts and reciting the blessings.
“Blessed are you, Lord our God, Sovereign of the universe...,” Mama began.
Usually, Papa’s loud voice resonated throughout the kitchen. Mama’s voice was gentle, soft, and sweet. The difference made me miss Papa even more.

I watched the shamash’s flame flicker. Candles were precious in wartime, as was light. We weren’t supposed to use lights after dark. On the radio, Prime Minister Churchill said we must cover our windows if we did. I looked at the kitchen window, draped with a thick blanket. Mama said it was more important to keep safe than to let our menorah shine through the neighborhood.
Mama continued, “...who commanded us to kindle the Hanukkah lights.”
Mama nodded, and I reached out to light the first candle.
Before I could light it, air-raid sirens made me jump. I blew out the shamash's flame.
“Hurry! We need to get to the shelter!” Mama cried, grabbing the knapsack by the door.

My heart pounded as loudly as the sirens as we ran into our backyard. I saw our neighbors, the Wrights, running from their house toward the bomb shelter we shared. There were three of them: Mrs. Wright, Johnny, and Sara, who was my age. Mr. Wright was fighting for England with my father.
Johnny reached the shelter first and yanked open the door. We crowded inside and Johnny slammed the door shut.
Mama pulled the ceiling bulb’s thin cord, and light flooded the little room. I sat on one of the two beds. Mrs. Wright set down the basket she brought, and Johnny dropped his knapsack on the floor.
“A fine Christmas this is,” Johnny grumbled as he plunked down by a stack of canned goods.
I’d forgotten the Wrights were celebrating Christmas today. Christmas fell during Hanukkah this year.
“Shush, Johnny!” his mother scolded. “Be grateful we’re safe.”
As Mrs. Wright settled down to talk with Mama, Johnny sighed and took out his sketchbook. Sara smiled and sat next to me.
Suddenly, I realized something was wrong. “Sara!” I whispered frantically.
“What is it?” she asked.
“I’ve lost the shamash candle!” I said.
I was devastated. We did not have another shamash, for wax was rationed.
“I must have dropped it outside!” I cried.
“We can find it,” said Sara.
I shook my head. “It will be unclean. We can’t use a dirty flame. How would we find it in the dark anyway?”
“Can’t you light the menorah with a match?” she asked.
“It must be with a candle! Oh, Sara, what will we do?”
The menorah must be lit! It was only the second night of Hanukkah! I closed my eyes. Papa would be ashamed, I thought. We can’t light the menorah!
Its light represented God’s strength, even in the most difficult times.
“I miss my papa,” I whispered.
Sara nodded. “I miss mine too.”
Hours later, bombs stopped. Sirens sounded the all-clear. We packed up and left the shelter. I could see dark smoke rising over London. This was a sad night.
Mama and I said good-bye to the Wrights, and we went our separate ways.
Mama closed our kitchen door. “Well,” she said with a deep sigh, “let’s light the menorah. We’ll say the prayers again.”
“We can’t, Mama,” I said quietly. “I’ve lost the shamash.”
I couldn’t bear Mama’s disappointed face.
A knock came from the door, and Mama and I went together to answer it.
It was Sara. In her hand glowed a stubby beeswax candle. She held it out to me. “It’s from our Christmas tree!”
“Oh, Sara!” Mama said behind me. “It’s perfect!” She saw Mrs. Wright standing outside and smiled at her. “Happy Christmas, Helen!”
Mrs. Wright grinned. “Happy Hanukkah, Abigail!”
And so our menorah would shine after all.

options.label: It is important to celebrate holidays the same way each year.
Hard times can be easier with help from friends.
Being away from home is hard for everyone.
Be careful not to lose important things.

Sara does something very kind for her neighbor, Atara. Do you know your neighbors? Do you and your family ever help your neighbors? If so, how do you help them?
In the story, Atara and Sara both miss their fathers, who are off fighting in the war. Do you have any family members that you do not get to see often? How does that make you feel?

articles.title: We Will Walk
articles.author: Sarah Kovatch
articles.description: A girl and her family participate in the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
articles.intro: Sadie and her family live in Montgomery, Alabama in the 1950s. During this time, Black people were made to sit at the back of the bus or stand if there were white people who needed seats. In this story, a young girl named Sadie rides the bus with her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Sadie’s feelings about riding the bus
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: My name is Sadie, and my little brother’s name is David. We live in Montgomery, Alabama. David and I spend a lot of time together, even though I’m seven years old and he is five. I have a lot to teach David since I’m the big sister. So far, I have taught David how to tie his shoes and how to say ten different tongue twisters. I also taught him how to roast marshmallows.
Our parents work very hard. Mom cleans house for another family, and Dad is a cook at a restaurant. At seven o’clock every morning, we take a city bus to town together.
I think it is fun to watch all the different people. I imagine where they are going.

There is a woman with a funny hat. It has colored patches of material on it. There is a boy carrying a fiddle. There is a very old man with worn-out shoes. It’s a good thing he doesn’t have to walk today!

So many interesting people take the bus. But there is one big problem. The bus is divided. There are different rules for different people. If you are Black like my family, you have to sit in the back of the bus. White people get to sit where they want. If there is not enough room in the back, then Black people have to stand up.

Tuesday was one of those days. The bus was very crowded because Montgomery’s winters are chilly, and people do not want to walk. When a white family boarded the bus, the bus driver pointed to my family and said, “Stand up to make more room!”
My parents looked very angry and sad at the same time, but we all did as we were told.
That night we all sat down for dinner. I asked, “Why do Black people have to stand up to give white people seats on the bus? Are white people better than us?”
Right away my mom put her fork down. She said, “No, Sadie. Please don’t think that. We are all members of this great world. Everyone is equal, no matter what color his or her skin happens to be.”
“Then why do Black people have different rules?” I asked. “My teacher says rules are supposed to help people live together nicely.”

“Your teacher is correct,” my dad answered. “Rules usually do help people live together fairly. But some rules these days just aren’t helping anyone. Some rules are not fair. But if we don’t obey, we could be put in jail. School and work are better places to be than jail.”
The next day, the same thing happened. A white woman and a white man boarded the bus, and the bus driver pointed to my family and said, “Stand up and make some room!” So we moved.
Then we went to school, and our parents went to work. We all felt a little sad again.

When we had dinner that night, something changed. Mom was too excited to eat. She was holding a piece of paper. “Look at this flyer!” she said. “Someone at the grocery store handed it to me today on my way home from work,” she said to Dad.

Dad read the flyer out loud. It said, “December 1, 1955: Today a Black woman named Rosa Parks was arrested for refusing to give up her seat on the bus. If you are a Black person, please stay off the buses in protest of the arrest.”

“That is a really good idea,” my mom said. “Staying off the bus is something we can do to show that we think it was wrong to arrest Rosa Parks. Staying off the bus can also help to change an unfair rule.”
“I agree,” my dad added. “I think we should do it!”
“But how will we get to school?” David asked.
“We’ll walk!” I told him. “Don’t worry, I’ll walk with you.”
“But what if it’s cold?” David asked. He didn’t like the idea of giving up the bus.
“Then I’ll bundle you up with a warm coat, mittens, and boots,” my mom answered. “We will not ride the bus until the city changes that unfair rule!”
My heart was racing. I felt excited. We were going to protest! I couldn’t wait to give up the bus. It was an important thing to do to get people to know how we felt.
The next morning we had to get up early to walk to school and work. It was a long walk, but we felt strong.
It’s a good thing we were strong, because the rules didn’t change right away in Montgomery, Alabama. We walked from December 5, 1955, to December 20, 1956. That’s one year and fifteen days!

We walked in coats, mittens, and boots in the winter. We wore raincoats and walked with umbrellas in the rainy spring. We stopped to smell the flowers during the summer. And our feet crunched the autumn leaves in the fall.
A lot of Black people decided not to ride the bus that year. And guess what? The buses were almost empty!

Finally, the bus rules changed. Black people didn’t have to sit in the back of the bus anymore. Black people didn’t have to give up their seats for white people. Every seat on the bus was for all people. We were all equal. Everyone had to be treated fairly.

Although walking was fun, I’m happy to sit on the bus again. I can watch the different people get on and off. I think it is more fun now to go on the bus. It feels a lot better to know that everyone is equal. My heart is happy!
By the way, one thing hasn’t changed. David and I still enjoy sitting by the window.

options.label: You should always help your friends and family if they need it.
It is important to go to school every day, even if you have to walk.
If you are polite and follow directions then people will respect you.
It is important to stand up for the people and things you believe in.

How is a protest with lots of people different from a protest by only one person? In your opinion, are the actions of the many or by just a few more important at creating change?

articles.title: No Shoes Aloud
articles.author: Stacey Lane Smith
articles.description: Ryan, a member of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing Community, welcomes hearing friends to dance class.
articles.intro: In this story, Ryan, a member of the Deaf and Hard of Hearing community, attends a dance class with his friends.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Ryan feels about Demi.
articles.permissions: "No Shoes Aloud" by Stacey Lane Smith, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The music blasts as the dancers kick their legs high. I sit on the side, feeling the rhythm flow through me. I tap my fingers to the beat. I just can’t sit still. I can’t wait for my favorite friend in the whole wide world to show up. This will be Harmony’s first dance class ever, but I can help her. I’ve been taking dance for three whole months now, so I know all about it.
The studio door swings open, and I grin at Harmony. My smile disappears when I see who’s standing behind her . . . Demi . . . Harmony’s copycat little sister.

Demi tackles me with a sticky hug. Harmony plops down and peels off her sneakers. She pulls new ballet shoes out of her bag and slips them on. I point to my bare feet. At my dance school, we don’t wear shoes. “We like to feel the floor,” I sign to Harmony.
“What’d Ryan say?” Demi asks. I can’t hear her, but I know that’s what she’s saying because that’s what she always says when I use sign language.
Harmony and Demi’s dad is my sign language interpreter at our school. He taught Harmony lots of American Sign Language, which is called ASL for short. Demi doesn’t know much ASL yet.
Harmony tells her what I signed. Demi asks, “Why?”
“No shoes make it easier to feel the vibrations of the music,” I sign.
Harmony gives Demi my answer. Demi lifts her eyebrows and speaks. Harmony signs, “Demi wants to know what a vibration is.”
Hmm…this is tricky to explain to such a little kid. “When there is a noise, sound waves shoot out super fast and bump into stuff, making it wiggle back and forth.”
Harmony is talking as fast as I’m signing. Demi looks around like she expects to suddenly see everything wiggling.
“It’s just a teeny tiny wiggle. Most times you can’t see it. But you can feel it, like in the wood floor when the bass is really pumping,” I add.
Demi stares at the floor with her face scrunched up. Some people think you hear music only with your ears, and I feel bad for them. They are missing out. I feel music with my whole body.
The song ends. The big kids bow to Miss Isabel. They leave, all signing about how much fun class was. Like me, most kids at my dance school are in the Deaf and Hard of Hearing Community.
It’s finally our turn! We rush to Miss Isabel and sit in a circle. Miss Isabel signs, “Hello, class.” She talks out loud too, so kids who don’t know ASL won’t feel left out. “Are you ready to dance?”
I always use my hands, face, and body to communicate. Dancing is telling a story and showing feelings using your body, so dancing comes easily to me.
Miss Isabel passes out drumsticks. She taps her drumsticks on the floor once. Then she taps them together twice. She taps the floor once again. She is creating a pattern.

Now we try. When I hit my drumsticks together, I feel tiny tingles. When everybody pounds the floor at the same time, the vibration is super strong. I can feel somebody is off beat. I look around. Demi wildly drums in no pattern at all! Miss Isabel signs and speaks, “Great job.” She collects the drumsticks.

She turns on the music. I feel a happy rumbling. Led by Miss Isabel, we stomp the rhythm with our feet and count with our fingers. We are getting to know the feel of the song. Each instrument creates a different kind of vibration. This song is fast with lots of bass. My favorite kind!
Miss Isabel demonstrates our new hip-hop routine. I watch her closely while counting in my head. Then we learn our dance step by step. We practice, practice, practice until we know it by heart.

At the end of class, we do what Miss Isabel calls “freestyle.” That means we get to rock out however we want. I do a huge leap. Harmony is super impressed. Harmony does a cartwheel. I am super impressed.

Demi twirls around and around like a tornado. She crashes in a giggle fit. With her cheek pressed against the floor, Demi’s eyes pop open with wonder. She grins at me and makes wiggly sound waves with her arm. She can feel the vibrations!

I feel bad for wishing she hadn’t come. Demi wants to dance. Anybody who wants to dance should get the chance. I grab her hand and pull her up. I grab Harmony’s hand too. Together we spin, dancing our hearts out. We may not experience music the same way, but that’s what is so neat about music. We love it because of the way it makes us feel.

options.label: It is okay to ask for help if you need it.
Listening to a teacher will help you get better.
Learning to dance can be a fun but scary thing to do.
People who are different can share special moments.

In "No Shoes Aloud," Miss Isabel uses sign language and speaks out loud to make sure no kids feel left out. Have you ever felt left out because you were different in some way? What happened? What could adults or other kids have done to help you not feel left out?

In the story, Ryan communicates using American Sign Language (ASL). Do you use or do you know anyone who uses ASL to communicate? If you know ASL, what are a few letters or words that you can use to communicate? Why do you think it might be important to learn ASL?

articles.title: The Secret of My Success
articles.author: Liz Huyck
articles.description: A funny cockroach explains what makes their species so adaptable and successful over time.
articles.intro: Cockroaches are flat, brown, fast-running insects with long, thin antennae. In this text, a cockroach tells why their species, or type of alike living things, survives, or continues to live on Earth.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the cockroach says is “The Secret of My Success.”
articles.permissions: "The Secret of My Success" by Liz Huyck, Ask , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Hello! Wait! Don’t run away. Yes, I know I am a cockroach. SO, what’s wrong with that? You are speaking to the most successful species on the planet, you know! We’ve been around for 350 million years! Survived five mass extinctions! Dinosaurs, gone. Trilobites, gone. Dodos, gone.
But WE’RE STILL HERE.
So what’s the secret of our success? Be small! Run fast! Eat anything! Lay lots of eggs! Let me show you how it’s done.
It all started a long, long time ago. The venerable family of Roach has been crawling around since at least the Devonian.
By the Carboniferous era, half the insects on earth were roaches! Some people call the Carboniferous the Age of Coal, but around here it’s known as the Age of Roaches.
Sure, we’ve had plenty of enemies. If you’re small and full of protein, everything wants to eat you. Fish! Amphibians! Reptiles! Mammals! And don’t even talk to me about birds.

So what did we do? We got quick, is what. We learned to hide, is what. Today’s lean, mean, turbo roach can squeeze so flat, we can fit through a slot no bigger than a dime. And we’re virtually uncrushable! Really! I dare you, try it.
Over the last 350 million years, we’ve had plenty of time to perfect our amazing design.

And we never pass up a meal! What do we eat? What have you got? Rotting leaves, garbage, glue, paper, leather, insulation, corks, poop...and if you like to eat dead things and poop, I tell you, the world is your refrigerator! Just don’t offer me a cucumber. Blech.

We’ve had to share the earth with all kinds of weird-looking creatures. None as weird as you, though. I mean, where’s your exoskeleton? Where are your scales, your stingers, your beaks? Honestly, I don’t know how you manage. But however odd the animals look, they all leave little packages of food around, just for us.
Ice ages came and went. Dinosaurs went extinct. Then the mammoths. But roaches, we just kept going.
Once humans came along, roaches went everywhere humans went. In ships, on planes, in camel packs across the desert. Roach road trip!
We even tagged along to space! Like Uncle Anton. He hid on board the Apollo XII capsule — he was so mad when they just came right back to earth.
You can’t have too many roaches, I say. Now there are 4,000 species of us: Asian, American, German, smokybrown, Madagascar hissing cockroaches...I hope you get to meet them all someday.

options.label: Cockroaches are funny insects that can always make you laugh.
Cockroaches are special insects that have been able to live on earth a long time.
Cockroaches are very similar to people in the ways that they live and take care of each other.
Cockroaches are very different from most other insects and we should do more to take care of them.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: are both hunted by other animals.
are both found in the same places.
have bodies made of the same things.
have both been around since the Carboniferous era.

questions.prompt: Humans and cockroaches —

options.label: by using a funny point of view to show that there is more to cockroaches than people may think
by telling how cockroaches began to live on earth after the dinosaurs went extinct
by showing how humans have helped spread cockroaches across the world
by sharing a joke about cockroaches that most people think is funny

options.label: The singer had a perfect voice.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses "perfect" in the same way as paragraph 8?
