discussionQuestions.prompt: The author chose to share the information in this text from the point of view of a misunderstood cockroach. What is an animal, insect, or plant that you feel is misunderstood? What would you want others to know about this living thing? How could using humor help you share your ideas? As a creative extension, choose a living thing and write a short story from that point of view using the same humor and funny tone as the author in "The Secret of My Success."

Have you ever seen a cockroach? What did you do or how did you feel when you saw the cockroach? After reading this text, do you think you will react differently next time you see a cockroach? Why or why not?

The cockroach is just a small insect, but it has survived and thrived for longer than humans. Based on this text, who's in control: man or nature? Do you feel that humans or cockroaches are more fit for life on earth? Explain why.

articles.title: Meet Hannah Wynne: Teen Storyteller
articles.author: Kathiann M. Kowalski
articles.description: Kathiann M. Kowalski discusses a teen storyteller.
articles.intro: Hannah Wynne is a professional storyteller, meaning that she performs stories for audiences. In this text, Kathiann M. Kowalski explores how Hannah became a storyteller and what telling stories means to her.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what kind of stories Hannah tells.
articles.permissions: "Meet Hannah Wynne: Teen Storyteller" by Kathiann M. Kowalski, Appleseeds © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/info/licensing2 for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Like most kids, Hannah Wynne has always loved hearing stories. But Hannah doesn’t just listen. At age 18, Hannah is already a professional storyteller.

As a little girl, Hannah told stories to family members in Valley City, Ohio. Later, she shared stories with friends during school recess. “I loved giving oral book reports,” adds Hannah. Often Hannah dressed as a book character to tell her books’ stories to the class.

When Hannah was 15, a professional storyteller named Janelle Reardon performed at a cousin’s birthday party. Hannah knew then that she wanted to become a storyteller, too. Soon afterward, Janelle began coaching Hannah. Then Hannah began performing.

“All my stories right now are personal stories,” says Hannah. “Most of them are funny.” Most of Hannah’s stories are about eight minutes long. And most come from things that happened when Hannah was around 6 years old. But the stories aren’t just memories, she says. “Our lives aren’t like movies or books. We’re not moving toward one goal.”

Instead, starting with real events, Hannah makes up stories with a beginning, middle, and end. Even her funny stories often have a lesson. In “The Revenge of Dr. Seuss,” young Hannah wanted to hear Fox in Socks over and over. But her mom was tired of that book. She had already read it many times for Hannah’s older brother and sisters.

Hannah told that story at the National Youth Storytelling Showcase in Pigeon Forge, Tennessee, in 2007. With it, she won the title of High School Division Torchbearer. Storytelling festivals give Hannah a chance to meet other professionals.
Hannah also met other young storytellers from across the nation. “The oldest was 18, and the youngest was 7 that year,” says Hannah. “Everyone was fantastic. And I learned so much from everyone.”

Today, Hannah tells stories at schools, libraries, recreation centers, and storytelling festivals. After college, she hopes to be a professional writer and continue storytelling. Hannah especially loves when people laugh along with her.

Her stories often remind people about events in their own lives. At its heart, storytelling is about sharing a story or an experience and connecting with the listeners. “The best way to tell people what storytelling is,” Hannah says, “is to tell them a story.”
Hannah’s tips for being a good storyteller:
Find a story you love. Make one up. Adapt a folktale. Start with something short: one or two minutes long.
Listen! You’re going to learn a lot from listening to other storytellers.
Slow down. People can’t understand your words if you rush.
Don’t just talk through the story, share it. A good storyteller invites you to come in and share the story.
Even if you’re nervous, try to act confident. Remember, people want to hear your story.
Most important, have fun!

options.label: Stories are more interesting when they are based in fact rather than fiction.
Hannah still has a lot to learn before she is considered a professional storyteller.
Storytelling is a difficult profession that not many can succeed in.
Hannah captures people's attention by telling stories about her own experiences

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the main idea of the text?

"'The oldest was 18, and the youngest was 7 that year,' says Hannah. 'Everyone was fantastic. And I learned so much from everyone.'" (Paragraph 7)
"After college, she hopes to be a professional writer and continue storytelling." (Paragraph 8)

articles.title: How to Hire a Giant
articles.author: Pamela Love
articles.description: A young servant outsmarts a greedy giant.
articles.intro: Pamela Love is a children's magazine and book author. Royal families, made up of kings, queens, and other members of their family, often hired, or gave jobs to, people to work for them. These people may be servants, whose job is to clean another person's house, cook their food, or help them in other ways in their home, or even as jesters, who were hired to tell jokes to royals. In this story, a girl named Mabel meets a giant.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Mabel solves her problem.
articles.permissions: "How to Hire a Giant" by Pamela Love. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The royal family was coming home after touring their realm for months. All the castle’s servants were bustling around preparing for their arrival. Their return would be celebrated with a big feast. In the courtyard outside, knights in shining armor marched back and forth in a double row. A jester mimicked their steps, a mocking grin on his face as he wobbled atop of a pair of tall wooden stilts.
In the kitchen, the cook was waving her arms and shouting orders. “Mabel, go find the strawberry jam. It’s in the cellar. Don’t come back without it.”

Mabel was the new maid in the kitchen. She set down the buckets of water she’d just brought in and tucked a stray dark curl under her white cap. “Faster!” the cook yelled as Mabel headed toward the stairs. “I’ve got a dozen other chores for you. Oh, it’s so hard to get good help nowadays.”

Mabel hurried downstairs, lifting her brown skirt up to her ankles to keep from tripping. She’d never been in the cellar before, and had only a candle to light her way. It took ages for her to check every shelf. She thought she might lose her job if she didn’t find the jam. At last, she spotted the right jar and trotted up the steps. She was sure the cook would scold her for taking so long.

When Mabel reached the kitchen, she stood frozen with surprise in the doorway. The kitchen had been deserted. Food was everywhere, midway through being prepared. Apples lay half-peeled beside a waiting pie crust. Carrots were chopped, but no one had put them into the pot of boiling water. The chicken, on its spit above an open flame, had not been turned and one side was being overcooked.

Mabel sprinted to the courtyard, her shoes clattering against the stone floor. Their Majesties must be here already . Cook had told her that when the royal family returned, everyone in the castle would line up to greet them. Yet the courtyard was empty, too. She took a deep breath. “Hello? Is anybody there?”

Mabel looked this way and that. The courtyard was filled with everything from laundry baskets to stilts. She even spotted a pair of knights’ shields. It was as though everyone must have dropped whatever they were holding and disappeared.

She jumped at the sound of crumbling brick behind her. Before she could flee, she felt herself being lifted higher and higher. She didn’t need to look over her shoulder to know what was happening. Even though she’d never seen one, only a giant could have picked her up and carried her to the top of the castle wall. She was at least twenty feet in the air.

Gritting her chattering teeth, Mabel turned around on the giant’s palm to face him. He was freckle-faced and had blond, tousled hair. His almost-outgrown shirt was missing a button. His trousers were worn through at the knees. He reminded Mabel of her mischievous younger cousins.

A giant, but also just a kid. A kid giant. If she was a knight, she could fight this giant, she thought. But the real knights had run away. If she was a jester, she could trick him. But he had fled too. Her teeth began to chatter with fear. What can a kitchen maid do? She wasn’t even a good one. The cook complains about me all the time .
That gave Mabel an idea.
She folded her arms and bellowed, “You’re late!”
He blinked. “Late?”

“Yes, late for your first day of work. Why do you think the queen giant isn’t here? She left right after breakfast. Think she’s going to stand around waiting for a new servant?” Mabel rolled her eyes. “Unfortunately, that’s my job.”

“But I’m not a servant. I came here for money.” He held up his other fist. A few coins from the royal treasury drizzled out from between his immense fingers. Mabel could hear the rest clinking inside his palm.

She snorted. “That’s not money. Those are sprinkles for the queen’s cupcakes.” She pointed at the two shields lying on the ground. “ That’s giant money, right there. But the queen won’t pay you a penny if I don’t say you did a good job.”

The giant’s eyes widened. Shiny and silvery, the shields did look like coins a giant would use. He dumped the “sprinkles” on the ground, and with great care lowered Mabel beside them. “How can I earn them?” She pointed to the jester’s stilts. “Start by cleaning up. See those toothpicks by your left foot? Put them in the corner over there.”
He picked them up. “Toothpicks?” They were as long as his arms.
“Of course. The queen picks her fangs with them after every meal.” Mabel hoped that would scare him off. Instead, the young giant looked impressed. “You can scrub them later.”
Next, Mabel strode over to a laundry basket. Inside it were the royal bedsheets — king-sized, of course. “Now fold these in half, then in half again. Be neat.”
Pulling one out, the giant held it up. It hung down almost to his waist. “What’s this?”
Mabel tossed her curls. “Her napkin, of course. See that ‘R’ in the corner? It stands for Really Big Giant Queen.” (In fact, it stood for Royal.)

It took him a dozen tries with each sheet before Mabel would say she was satisfied. “Bring them along.” It took all her courage not to run, but that would make him suspicious. Besides, he could catch her if she did. I can’t hide either , she thought, glancing at the huge gap he’d torn in the stone wall. All she could do was to continue to pretend she was a more important servant than he was. Her plan wasn’t working. The thought of there being a giant even bigger than him hadn’t scared him off. What if he wanted to meet this giant Queen? What would she do then?

Stalling, she marched up the tower steps. She didn’t want him to pick her up again. “Hey, what’s that?” the giant asked. The king’s mattress was lying on the castle roof. Must have been put there to air out , Mabel thought. Featherbeds can get awfully musty smelling .
Mabel’s mind raced. Then she threw up her hands. “Don’t you know a biscuit when you see one?”
“A leftover biscuit?” The giant looked up at the sun. “Good. It’s almost noon. I haven’t eaten all day.” Picking up the mattress, he tore out a huge chunk with his teeth.
His face turned red. He coughed and spat. Feathers flew everywhere, like snowflakes in a blizzard.
“That’s it!” he roared. “All that work and that’s all I get to eat? I quit!”
He stomped away, the earth beneath her shook as he left. Mabel heaved a sigh of relief.

She sat there for a while in disbelief and did not move until she heard footsteps approaching. It was the royal family, they had finally arrived with their guards. The jester, the castle’s knights, and the castle’s servants, had met the royals on the way and had also returned. They had seen the giant’s departure, and listened eagerly to Mabel’s explanation. The young prince — who was about the giant’s age — was open-mouthed. The king nodded with approval, and exchanged glances with the (human-sized) queen and the captain of the guards with whom they had traveled.
The cook apologized for leaving Mabel behind. “When the rest of us saw the knights run, we knew we couldn’t protect ourselves if they wouldn’t even try,” she said.
“It is hard to get good help these days,” Mabel replied.
“Including knights,” said the queen. “Would you like to train as one, Mabel?”
“You showed great courage and quick thinking today,” the king agreed.
Mabel smiled. “Your Majesties, I think I’d make a better jester.”

options.label: The biggest and strongest always win.
Be careful who you let into your house.
Heroes can come from surprising places.
Only knights can save people from danger.

questions.prompt: What is the lesson of the story?

options.label: to make a mess.
to throw water on.
to speak in an angry way.
to give someone an award.

questions.prompt: The word "scold" in paragraph 4 most closely means —

options.label: "Mabel hurried downstairs, lifting her brown skirt up to her ankles to keep from tripping." (Paragraph 4)
"Before she could flee, she felt herself being lifted higher and higher." (Paragraph 8)
"'That's not money. Those are sprinkles for the queen's cupcakes.'" (Paragraph 16)

Why do you think Mabel said, "It's hard to get good help these days" at the end of the story?
How is this story like other fairy tales you know? How is it different?

articles.title: The Basket Weaver
articles.author: Jacque Summers
articles.description: A shy girl, who is a member of the Chumash tribe, learns a new way to communicate.
articles.intro: The Chumash People are the native, or first, people to live in the area that is now California's southern coast. They are known for making tightly woven baskets, which are made by passing threads or strips of dried plants over and under each other. In this story, a shy Chumash girl learns a new way to share with her people.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Yo’ee’s actions throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "The Basket Weaver" by Jacque Summers, Ladybug , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Thousands of years ago, the Chumash People lived on the beautiful shores of California. And in the valley Aw’hay, there was a young girl named Yo’ee. Yo’ee was very, very shy. She never spoke to anyone, except her mother, father, or grandmother. No one knew why Yo’ee was so shy, and neither did she. But every day she would play alone.
One morning, Yo’ee’s grandmother was sitting on the ground before the fire, weaving a basket. She saw Yo’ee playing. Yo’ee smiled as Grandmother waved her over to join her.
“Would you like to learn to make baskets?” her grandmother asked.
Yo’ee nodded. “I love baskets,” she whispered.

Grandmother showed Yo'ee how to start the basket by wrapping juncus rushes with split reeds and stitching them into a beautiful, coiled base. After that she sewed more rows of reeds to the base. Her hands moved gracefully, like the wings of a bird. Yo’ee loved to watch her grandmother weave baskets.

Yo’ee and Grandmother began to weave baskets together every day. Yo’ee helped her grandmother gather and prepare the reeds. They went down to the river and pulled bundles of slim, green stalks. Some they would dry in the sun until they turned brown. The others they would soak in mud, turning them black as night before setting them out to dry. Then Yo’ee and Grandmother dipped the stiff reeds in water to soften them, and wove them into baskets.

One day, as Yo’ee came back from the river with an armful of reeds, she heard the chief telling a story to the village children. The chief was not only a wise leader — he was a great storyteller. He spoke in a strong, clear voice that rose and fell like the river’s song.

The children listened as he told a story Yo’ee knew well. Coyote, Lizard, and Eagle, the first creatures of the earth, decided to make a new creature called man. But they could not decide on his hands.

“Proud Coyote wanted man to have hands like his,” the chief said. He held up his own hands and curled them as if they were sharply clawed. “Lizard and Eagle didn’t argue, though Lizard knew Coyote was unwise.
“The next day, Coyote led the others to a white rock. If he stamped his paw on the rock, it would be final — humans would have hands just like the coyote.

“Lizard had to do something! He quickly crawled to the rock while Coyote wasn’t looking and pressed his own tiny hand into the rock. Coyote was furious! But thanks to clever Lizard, we have useful hands with five fingers, just like him!” The children looked at their hands and laughed.

Yo’ee saw how the children loved to listen to the chief. She wished she could tell stories, too, to make others happy. But she was afraid. She thought her voice was too quiet to be heard. Still, she wanted to share the stories of her people.

Then one day, as she was playing with a blue feather, she had an idea. She ran to tell her plan to Grandmother, who thought it was a wonderful idea. That minute they sat down and began working on a very special basket.
Soon it was time for the Acorn Harvest. Everyone in the village came together for the festival, eating, singing, and dancing. Yo’ee brought her new basket along.

After the feast, the chief asked if there were any who would like to tell a story. Yo’ee stood up, clutching her basket. Everyone was surprised. The chief nodded to her and sat down, ready to listen. Yo’ee took a deep breath and held up her basket for all to see.

On the first side of the basket, Yo’ee had woven a scrub jay. He was black and featherless, with his head cast down in sadness. The people murmured, impressed by her handiwork. When everyone had seen the scrub jay, she turned the basket.
On the next side, the black jay was flying up to the basket’s blue-feathered rim, as if asking the sky for some of its color.
Yo’ee then turned the basket again. Now the sky dropped its blue feathers down to the jay. She turned the basket once more.

On the last side of the basket was the beautiful blue scrub jay, finally dressed in his sky-colored feathers. Yo’ee stood very still to show her story was over, then went back to sit by her grandmother.
Everyone cheered at Yo’ee’s tale. The chief stood and said, “Our village has another great storyteller. Next festival we look forward to another story from Yo’ee.”
Yo’ee’s family hugged her proudly. Her grandmother’s eyes twinkled. “You’ve found a way to tell a story without speaking a word,” she said to Yo’ee. “Your story was in the weaving of the basket.”
And Yo’ee knew there would be plenty more stories to tell.

options.label: "She never spoke to anyone, except her mother, father, or grandmother." (Paragraph 1)
"Then Yo'ee and Grandmother dipped the stiff reeds in water to soften them, and wove them into baskets." (Paragraph 6)
"She wished she could tell stories, too, to make others happy." (Paragraph 12)
"Yo'ee stood very still to show her story was over, then went back to sit by her grandmother." (Paragraph 19)

Can you think of a story you have heard many times but still enjoy? What story could you hear many times and still like? What about that story do you like the most?
At the end of the story, Yo'ee finds a way to share stories with the village by using her special talent. What special talent do you have? How can you share it with the people around you?

In the story, Yo'ee learns how to make something special from her grandmother. Is there something like a craft or special recipe that your family makes? Would you like to learn how to make it? Why or why not?

articles.title: The Quarrel
articles.author: Maxine Kumin
articles.description: A lightning bug, firefly, and glowworm learn an important lesson about arguing.
articles.intro: Fireflies, lightning bugs, and glowworms are all names for insects that glow, which means they give off their own light. In this poem, the insects get into a quarrel, or a fight.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s point of view about the insects.
articles.permissions: "The Quarrel" by Maxine W. Kumin. Used by permission of the Maxine W. Kumin Literary Trust

blocks.text: Said a lightning bug to a firefly,
“Look at the lightning bugs fly by!”
“Silly dunce!” said the fly. “What bug ever flew?
Those are fireflies. And so are you.”
“Bug!” cried the bug. “Fly!” cried the fly.
“Wait!” said a glowworm happening by.
“I’m a worm,” squirmed the worm. “I glimmer all night.
You are worms, both of you. I know that I’m right.”
“Fly!” cried the fly. “Worm!” cried the worm.
“Bug!” cried the bug. “I’m standing firm!”
Back and forth through the dark each shouted his word
Till their quarrel awakened the early bird.
“You three noisy things, you are all related,”
She said to the worm, and promptly ate it.
With a snap of her bill she finished the fly,
And the lightning bug was the last to die.
All glowers and glimmerers, there’s a MORAL:
Shine if you must, but do not quarrel.

options.label: If you see a glowing insect, be very careful.
If you show off, you can get yourself into trouble.
If you fight too much, you might miss important things.
If you laugh with your friends, you can have a great time.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of this poem?

options.label: They treat each other kindly.
They help each other stay safe.
They try to beat each other in a contest.
They will not listen to each other's ideas.

questions.prompt: How do the insects act toward each other?

options.label: They fly to and from the same spot together.
They continue to fight with each other.
They start to listen to each other.
They pass food to each other.

questions.prompt: What does this line show about the insects? "Back and forth through the dark each shouted his word" (Line 11)

options.label: making the body taller
keeping the same idea

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "standing firm" mean as it is used in line 10?
What is the speaker's point of view about the insects?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, the insects fight and wake up the bird that eats them. Sometimes when we fight with others, there are negative consequences, or bad things might happen. Think of a time you have fought with someone. What happened after? What could you do differently next time so that there are not negative consequences?

In the poem, the insects continue to fight, and they keep saying the same things over and over. How would you help the insects work out their differences? What would you tell them to think, say, and do? What other situations have you been in where you helped people solve a disagreement or a problem? What did you do?
In the poem, the insects fight about what they should be called. Do you think it is ever okay to tell someone their name is incorrect? Why or why not? How might that make someone feel?

articles.title: Claudette Colvin
articles.author: Britannica Kids
articles.description: This text outlines the life of the Civil Rights activist who inspired Rosa Parks' infamous bus ride.
articles.intro: During the U.S. civil rights movement (1954-1968) Black leaders and their supporters fought to end racial injustice. One injustice, or something that is not fair or right, during this time was that buses were segregated. This meant that Black people were made to sit at the back of the bus, or stand if there were white people who needed seats. This biography tells about Claudette Colvin (1939-present), a civil rights activist, or someone who tries to make a change in the world.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on people’s point of view about Claudette Colvin.
articles.permissions: Excerpted with permission from Britannica Kids, © 2022 by Encyclopedia Britannica, Inc.

blocks.text: Introduction

Claudette Colvin was an African American teenager who, in 1955, was arrested for refusing to give up her bus seat to a white person. Her brave action came nine months before Rosa Parks also refused to give up her seat. It was Parks’s action that sparked the U.S. civil rights movement.
Early Life

Claudette Colvin was born on September 5, 1939, in Birmingham, Alabama. She later lived with her family in Montgomery. There, Claudette attended a high school for African American students. She learned about Sojourner Truth, Harriet Tubman, and other important African Americans in history. She and her classmates also talked about the unfairness of segregation (the separation of Black and white people).
Civil Rights Activist

On March 2, 1955, when Colvin was 15 years old, she was riding a bus in Montgomery. The driver asked her to give her seat to a white person. She refused, saying she had a right to sit there. But her action went against the segregation laws of Montgomery. Two police officers dragged Colvin off the bus. They put her in handcuffs and took her to jail. She later was made to pay a fine.

Some members of a civil rights group (the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People) thought that Colvin’s case could bring attention to the injustice of segregation. But other members thought that Colvin was too immature to represent the struggle for civil rights. In the end, Rosa Parks became the symbol of the movement.

Still, Colvin challenged bus segregation laws in court. She and three other women participated in a legal case that made it to the U.S. Supreme Court. In 1956 the Supreme Court ruled in favor of the women, making segregation on buses illegal.
Later Life

Colvin later moved to New York City. She worked in a nursing home for 35 years before retiring. Her story was largely forgotten until the early 2000s. In 2009 a book about her life — Claudette Colvin: Twice Toward Justice , by Phillip Hoose — won the National Book Award for young people’s literature.

options.label: Claudette was a brave teenager whose actions were important to the civil rights movement.
Claudette became more famous than Rosa Parks for refusing to give up her seat.
Claudette broke the law when she refused to ride the bus and was put in jail.
Claudette lived in New York City and worked for a nursing home.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of "Claudette Colvin"?

options.label: Claudette's actions made Rosa famous.

questions.prompt: How were Claudette Colvin's actions and Rosa Parks' actions related?

options.label: to invite to enter into a fight or contest
to ask someone to explain something
to stir up or make something move
to question the rightness of

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of the word "challenged" as it is used in paragraph 5?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Even though Claudette took action before Rosa did, Rosa is the one who is remembered in history. Why do some people's stories get told, while others do not? What do you think it takes to become famous or remembered in history? Do you think there are other people we should learn about that we do not talk about a lot? What should we do to tell these people's stories?

Claudette was only fifteen when she refused to give up her seat to a white person, showing that she was brave even though she was only a child. What do Claudette's actions prove about children and teenagers? What lessons could adults learn from Claudette?

In the text, members of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People thought that Claudette was too young to be seen as a leader for the Civil Right Movement. Has anyone told you you were too young to do something you wanted to do? What did you say? Did you prove them wrong? If not, how would you react if this happened to you?

In the context of the text, how do people create change? Why is it important to stand up for what you believe in? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Santas, Santas Everywhere
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: A speaker wonders how it is possible to see Santa Claus everywhere during the holiday season.
articles.intro: Jenny Whitehead is a poet and an illustrator of children's books and poems. In this poem, a speaker asks questions about Santa Claus.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s thoughts about Santa.
articles.permissions: "Santas, Santas, Everywhere" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday Stew: A Kid's Portion of Holiday and Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Santas, Santas everywhere,
but how can that be so?
They stand on every corner
from Vermont to Idaho.
Could Santa have a dozen cousins
with the last name Claus?
Do tall elves work a second shift
to help a worthy cause?
He must be pretty picky picking
out his substitutes.
They all look nice and jolly
in their big red Santa suits.
Well, maybe it’s okay to see
these Santas everywhere.
They all must have some Santa Claus
inside of them to share.

options.label: Christmas is mostly about giving and receiving gifts.
Santa Claus is the most important part of Christmas.
Santa Claus brings presents to all American children.
The joy of Christmas should be shared with everyone.

questions.prompt: Which statement shows the central message of the poem?

options.label: fun because it allows them to create new toys.
silly because it makes them laugh as they work.
boring because it means they have to work long hours.
important because it brings happiness to kids around the world.

questions.prompt: In line 8, the words "worthy cause" show that the elves' second shift is —

"He must be pretty picky picking / out his substitutes" (Lines 9-10)
They all look nice and jolly / in their big red Santa suits. (Lines 11-12)

The speaker in the poem celebrates Christmas. What holidays do you celebrate with your family or loved ones? What are some of the customs or activities that you and your family take part in during these holidays or celebrations? If students do not feel comfortable sharing, they can research and present on the holiday customs of another culture.

articles.title: Anne Frank
articles.author: Britannica Kids
articles.description: Anne Frank's diary helped the world understand life for Jewish people in hiding during World War II.
articles.intro: During the Holocaust, around 11 million people were killed by Adolf Hitler and his German Nazi Party. About 6 million of these victims, or people killed, were European Jews. This biography tells about the life of Anne Frank (1929-1945), a Jewish girl who lived during the Holocaust.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Anne Frank is remembered today.
articles.permissions: Excerpted with permission from Britannica Kids, © 2022 by Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.

blocks.text: Introduction

During World War II a young Jewish girl, Anne Frank, kept a diary for two years while hiding from the Nazis with her family. Anne and her family were victims of the Holocaust, which was Nazi Germany’s campaign to destroy the Jews. From the diary, readers have found out what Jewish people experienced and felt during the time of the Holocaust.
Early Life

Anne Marie Frank was born on June 12, 1929, in Frankfurt, Germany. In 1933 Adolf Hitler’s Nazi Party came to power, and Germany became a dangerous place for Jews. Anne’s family soon moved to Amsterdam, the Netherlands.
In May 1940, during the early months of World War II, Germany took over the Netherlands. By 1942, Jews were being rounded up and sent to camps where most of them would die.
The Secret Annex

Anne’s father prepared some rooms upstairs from his business offices. This was to be a “secret annex” where his family could hide. On July 6, 1942, the Frank family and four other people went into hiding.
For two years the eight people shared the small space. They were always afraid, but they tried to live normally. Anne wrote in her diary about her daily life and her hopes.
After the Annex

Somehow the Gestapo (German secret police) found out about the annex. On August 4, 1944, they arrested everyone. Anne and her sister went to the Bergen-Belsen camp in Germany. In March 1945 they both died of a disease called typhus. Except for her father, everyone from the secret annex died in the camps.

After the raid, friends discovered Anne’s diary. In 1947 Mr. Frank had it published. The English translation was titled The Diary of a Young Girl . The annex building in Amsterdam is now a museum called the Anne Frank House.

options.label: World War II began in 1940 with Germany's take over of the Netherlands.
Jewish people had to leave the Netherlands to get away from the Nazis.
Anne Frank was a teenage girl who was forced to hide from the Nazis.
The Secret Annex is a popular museum to visit in Germany.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, Anne wrote about her life in her diary. Do you keep a diary? If so, what are some of the things you write about? If not, what are some of your hopes and dreams? How would you feel if someone found and published your diary? Why?

Anne's diary helped the world understand what it was like living in hiding during World War II. Why are first-hand accounts important? What can you learn from other peoples' experiences? Why is it important to tell our stories?
Anne Frank was a teenager when she wrote her diary. Thinking about this text, why should we value what young people have to share?

articles.title: Thank You, Sleep!
articles.author: Carolyn Dee Flores
articles.description: In this onomatopoeia poem, a sick speaker feels better after sleeping.
articles.intro: This poem is an onomatopoeia poem by Carolyn Dee Flores. An onomatopoeia is a word that sounds like its meaning, like bang, boom, crinkle, pop, crack, or sizzle.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the letters of the alphabet are used in the poem.
articles.permissions: "Thank You, Sleep" Copyright (c) 2019 Carolyn Dee Flores. Originally published in Thanku: Poems of Gratitude edited by Miranda Paul and published by Lerner Publishing Group, Millbrook Press. Reprinted by permission of the author.

blocks.text: A-choo!
Boo-hoo!
Coughing, creeping, drippy, eww.
Flush, gunk, hic. I’m sunk.
Onomatopeia -choo !
Itchy, jitter, knocking knees,
L-m-n-o-pia ooze. (Oh, please.)
Quite sick. Queasy-quick!
Onomatopoeia - ick !
Ringing in my ears and head.
Slurping soup, I jump in bed.
Ticktock, ticktock, snooze and snore…
Thank you, Sleep! I’m sick no more.
Farewell, Sneeze. Bye, Bellyache.
I feel great. Now, I’m awake!
Ultra-Vultra, whoosh and whoom,
Out the door to play: X-Y-Zoom !

options.label: sometimes, all you need is rest to feel better.
while you rest, you will miss out on the fun.
if you play too much, you will get sick.
when you are tired, you should sleep.

questions.prompt: The central message of the poem is —

options.label: They show how the speaker solves their problem.
They show who helps the speaker feel better.
They show the problem the speaker has.
They show why the speaker is sick.

questions.prompt: Why are lines 10-12 important to the poem?

options.label: what kind of soup the speaker wants
the temperature of the soup
what the soup sounds like
the color of the soup

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "slurping soup" (line 11) help the reader to understand?

options.label: that the speaker is sad to watch their friends play
that sleep made the speaker ready to play again
that being sick can be very hard to handle
that the speaker loves to play inside

questions.prompt: What does the following line help the reader understand? "Ultra-Vultra, whoosh and whoom," (Line 16)
How is the alphabet used in a special way in the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this poem, the speaker thanks sleep for helping them feel better again. What is something that makes you feel better when you are sick? How does it make you feel better?

In this poem, the speaker thanks sleep. What is something you would like to say thank you to? As a creative extension, write an onomatopoeia poem that uses every letter of the alphabet like "Thank You, Sleep!" to say thank you.

articles.title: Each Scar
articles.author: Baptiste Paul
articles.description: A speaker reflects on a lesson from their mom about getting hurt.
articles.intro: A tricube poem has three stanzas, or groups of lines. Each stanza has three lines, with each one having three syllables. In this tricube poem by Baptiste Paul, the speaker shares a lesson they learned from their mom about scars, the mark that is left when a cut has gotten better.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s actions.
articles.permissions: "Each Scar" Copyright (c) 2019 Baptiste Paul. Originally published in Thanku: Poems of Gratitude edited by Miranda Paul and published by Lerner Publishing Group, Millbrook Press. Reprinted by permission of the author.

blocks.text: My mom says
be thankful
for each scar.
So I play.
Fail each day.
Lose my way.
Bash, crash, rough —
Mom, it’s tough
to thank scars.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The speaker's mom says they should be "thankful for each scar." Do you agree with this advice? Have you ever gotten hurt and then felt thankful for it later?

The speaker explains that their mom tells them to be thankful for scars, even though getting hurt is painful. What is something in your life that you can be thankful for that might seem bad in the moment, but that you can learn from later? As a creative extension, have students write a tricube poem about something they are thankful for, following the directions for this type of poem in the student introduction.

articles.title: The Icicle Symphony
articles.author: Julia Gousseva
articles.description: A girl in Moscow doesn't want to go outside with her uncle.
articles.intro: Julia Gousseva has written for Highlights . In this short story, a girl in Moscow, a city in Russia, doesn't want to go outside with her uncle.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Marina’s feelings throughout the story.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: I stood by the drafty double-paned windows of our Moscow apartment and looked outside. Old apartment buildings blocked most of the gray winter sky, and the ground below was brown with dirty snow. I noticed a woman crossing the street, her face covered with a thick scarf. She looked cold and miserable.
The morning was perfect for reading. I drew the curtains, turned on the light, and curled up in my bed with a book.
The phone rang.
After a minute, I heard my mom say, “Sure! Marina would love that. See you soon.”
Who was that? I wondered. And what was I supposed to love?
Mama walked into my room and opened the curtains wide again. “Time to get up, Marina. Uncle Gena is taking you to Sparrow Hills.”
Going outside on this cold winter morning? No way.
I pulled a blanket closer around me. “But it’s freezing out.”
“You’ll have fun,” Mama said.

I shook my head. “Not in this weather.” During the summer, I loved going to Sparrow Hills, eating strawberry ice cream from a paper cup and walking through the park by Moscow State University. But going there on this cold winter morning?
“Get ready,” Mama said. “Uncle Gena will be here soon.”
I slowly pulled on a blue sweater and changed into tights and thick wool pants.

Too soon, the doorbell rang. When Mama opened the door, Uncle Gena put his backpack down and hugged me. Dressed in faded jeans, a sweater knitted by my grandmother, and a black down jacket, Uncle Gena stood straight and tall. Even in the depths of the long winter, he was tan from skiing and mountain climbing. He turned to Mama and hugged her, too. “Do you have a travel mug?”
“Sure,” said Mama. “Would you like tea or coffee in it?”
“Just an empty one.” He put the travel mug in his backpack and smiled at me. “Ready, Marina?”
“I guess.” I buttoned my coat up to my nose and pulled on a warm furry hat.
We descended two flights of stairs to the narrow sidewalk, where snow was piled high. I hunched my shoulders and scowled at the cold.

We crossed the street and walked down the steep steps of the metro station to catch the train to Sparrow Hills. Marble walls and columns, a shiny floor, and a colorful mosaic on the ceiling made the station look like a czar’s palace. It was warmer in the station. My body started to relax.

I peered into the blackness of the tunnel. Two bright lights were approaching. The train blew its horn as it rushed into the station, an oil smell filling the air. When the doors opened, we climbed aboard and sat on a cushiony brown bench.
Before long, we pulled into the glass-walled station at Sparrow Hills. “Can we just ride around on the train?” I said. The daylight made me squint.
Uncle Gena gently pulled me up. “You’d miss all the fun.”
Outside, the frosty air made my cheeks tingle. The wind whistled and whined.
“I don’t know how long I can stay out here,” I said as we started to walk uphill.
“Shhh. Look.”
A patch of snowy trees sparkled with ice-covered branches. They swayed in the wind.

Uncle Gena pointed to the sidewalk dappled with rainbows. “These colors are a reflection of the iced branches, white light broken down into the rainbow’s seven colors.” He pulled me closer to the trees. “Listen.”
First, I heard only the wind. Then came a faint jingling and chiming.
“What’s that?” I asked.
“The icicle symphony,” Uncle Gena said. “Snow fell on the branches. It melted, then froze again. Now, the icy branches chime as they sway in the wind.”
I pulled my hat up to free my ears. The branches sounded like hundreds of tiny bells ringing, jingling, and chiming. I stood mesmerized, staring straight up at the branches.

Uncle Gena took the travel mug from his backpack, then walked deeper into the stand of trees and filled the mug with fresh, sparkling snow. “We’ll make tea at home. The snow gathered here makes delicious tea.”

We came to the granite guardrail above the glistening Moskva River. From the height of Sparrow Hills, Moscow looked like a fairy-tale city. Golden domes of old churches and snow-covered trees made the concrete buildings appear brighter. Then the sun broke through the clouds.
“The sun is shining!” I said.
Uncle Gena smiled. “The sun is always shining. You just need to climb high enough to see it.”
“And you have to go outside in the winter to hear the icicle symphony,” I said as we walked down the hill.

options.label: If you always hide from the cold, you'll miss the beauty of winter.
Winter is the most beautiful season in Moscow.
Spending time with family can make any activity fun.
Staying inside all the time isn't healthy for children.

How does Marina learn to appreciate the beauty of nature? How do you think her experiences in nature have changed her views on winter?

articles.title: The Danderfield Twins: Snowed In!
articles.author: Polly Horvath
articles.description: On a snow day, two friends sneak out to play.
articles.intro: The Danderfield Twins is a series of books about twins that go on adventures. In this story, friends enjoy a snowy day outside.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Marcus acts.
articles.permissions: "The Danderfield Twins: Snowed In!" by Polly Horvath, Spider. © Cricket Media, Inc.. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: “WAKE UP, DEIDRE!” Maisie jumped up and down on the foot of Deidre’s bed.
“Ow! Stop it! It’s not time to wake up for school yet,” said Deidre, turning over.
“There’s no school!” said Maisie.
“Yes, there is. It’s not Saturday, it’s Wednesday,” said Deidre.
“It’s a SNOW DAY!” yelled Maisie.
Marcus came sleepily into the bedroom. “Hey, what’s all the shouting? I was trying to sleep.”
“It’s a snow day,” said Maisie.
“Cool!” said Marcus.
“Want to go play in the snow? Dad says there’s so much that he can barely get the back door open.”
“Nah, playing in the snow is for kids,” said Marcus. “I’m, like, way beyond that.”
“How about you?” Maisie asked as Deidre looked out the window at the winter wonderland.
“Yeah, but first I better get my book report done. I thought it was going to be late, but now I’m saved!” said Deidre.
Meanwhile, Marty got a mysterious phone call. The voice on the other end of the phone said, “Hi. Wait right there. I’m coming over to pick you up.”
“Who is this?” asked Marty, who thought the voice sounded familiar.
“It’s Marcus. We’re going to do some, uh, important guy stuff this morning. Like design architecturally sound snow structures.”
“What?”
“I’ll explain when I get there. Just put on a lot of warm clothes. We’re going to be out for a while,” said Marcus.
“I hate to tell you, but my wheelchair won’t go through these drifts,” said Marty.
“You won’t need it. Haven’t you ever heard of sleds?”
Marcus helped Marty onto the sled, and they told Marty’s bubbe they would be at the park until lunch.
“Wow, this is great!” said Marty as the sled carried him like a ship over waves of snow. “But where are the girls?”
“We don’t need them. And, uh, listen — we’re not going to tell anyone what we're doing, O.K.? Not Maisie or Deidre or the guys, either, O.K.?”
“Well, sure,” said Marty scratching his head. He was confused. Marcus had never invited him to play before. “What are we going to do?”
“Important things. Guy things. Things girls wouldn’t understand. Let’s start by building an architecturally sound snow structure.”
“A what?” said Marty.
“A fortification,” said Marcus.
“You mean a fort?” asked Marty.
“Nah, kids build forts. This is really more of a scientific experiment.”

Marty didn’t care what they called it, because by the time they were done, it looked totally cool. They had built two igloos and made tunnels through the snow from one to the other. Then they’d built a surrounding wall, like a castle.
“Hey, this is great! We could protect this thing forever if there was a snowball fight,” said Marty when they were done.
“Yeah, pretty neat,” said Marcus, looking over their work. “But — ahem — of course, that’s not why we built it, was it?”
“Noooo,” said Marty, who had caught on. “We just wanted to see, out of scientific curiosity, if it would be — uh, what’s that thing you said?”
“Architecturally sound,” said Marty.
“Right. So now what do you want to do? Make snow angels?”
“Snow angels?” said Marcus. “We don’t make snow angels.” He threw himself on a patch of fresh, untrampled snow. “We make abstract art.”
“It kind of looks like a snow angel, though,” said Marty, eyeing it critically.

“Yeah, that’s because that’s what you see in it. That’s what we studied in Mrs. Anderson’s art class. It can be whatever you want it to be — kind of like when you cloud-watch and see the different shapes they make.”
“I never thought of that,” said Marty. “O.K., then I’m making a dog angel with wings.” Just then a snowball came flying from inside the fort.
“HEY! Someone’s in our fort!” yelled Marcus as more snowballs flew through the air at him and Marty. “Duck!”
“Traitor!” someone yelled. “You said you weren’t going to play in the snow!”
“It’s Deidre and Maisie,” said Marty, making a snowball to throw back. “Hey, how did you find us?”
“We called your house, and your bubbe told us. You said you weren’t going to play, Marcus, and you made a fort and snow angels without us!”
“They’re not snow angels,” said Marty. “They’re abstract art.”
“And it’s not a fort. It’s an architecturally sound snow structure,” said Marcus.
“Oh yeah? Well I know one thing your whatever you-said-it-was has in common with a fort,” yelled Maisie.
“Yeah, what?” asked Marcus.
“You’re going to have to defend it!” yelled the girls as the snowball fight began.

options.label: Playing outside after it has snowed is not safe.
Older kids have a lot less fun than younger kids.
Playing in the snow can be fun no matter how old you are.
Sometimes it is hard to decide what to play with new friends.

questions.prompt: What best states the main idea of the story?

options.label: "Marcus came sleepily into the bedroom." (Paragraph 6)
"'Yeah, pretty neat,' said Marcus, looking over their work. 'But — ahem — of course, that's not why we built it, was it?'" (Paragraph 31)
"'It can be whatever you want it to be — kind of like when you cloud-watch and see the different shapes they make.'" (Paragraph 37)

discussionQuestions.prompt: This story takes place in winter. Do you enjoy this season? Why or why not? What are some fun things you like to do with your friends or family during wintertime? What are some other snow day activities that the children in the story might like?

Marty feels nervous about going outside in the snow because he is in a wheelchair. How does Marcus make Marty feel included? Why is it important to make people feel included? How does your school or community make sure everyone is included? What other ideas do you have to help people feel included in your school and community?

In the story, Marcus does not want to admit that he still enjoys playing in the snow although he really does. Does growing up mean that we need to stop doing the things we loved when we were younger? What does it mean to be grown up?

articles.title: Too Many Vegetables
articles.author: Karen DelleCava
articles.description: A boy and his dad give zucchinis from their garden to their neighbors.
articles.intro: Karen DelleCava has written for Highlights . In this short story, a boy and his dad give zucchinis from their garden to their neighbors.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Patrick and his dad respond to their neighbors.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “What are we going to do with all of these?” Patrick asked.
“I love zucchini, but this is ridiculous,” Dad said. He wiped the sweat from his forehead.

In May, Patrick and his dad had put out six tiny zucchini plants. Now huge leaves crowded one another. Dozens of zucchini as long as Patrick's arm hid underneath. Patrick and Dad's first vegetable garden was an amazing success.
“Remember how everyone welcomed us to the neighborhood when we moved in?” Patrick said. “Let's give a zucchini to each of the neighbors.”
Dad lifted a fuzzy leaf to show even more zucchini. “Better make it two.”
They weeded the garden that afternoon and staked the tomato plants. Then Patrick picked the zucchini. He delivered them, still warm from the sun, to the neighbors.
The next day Mrs. Gianelli came to the front door. She carried a large foil-covered baking pan.
“Hi, Mrs. Gianelli,” said Patrick. “What's this?”
“Zucchini parmesan,” she said. “My grandmother's recipe. I wanted to thank you for the lovely vegetables. I had enough to make two full pans.”
Soon the rest of the neighbors arrived. They each brought their own favorite zucchini creations. There was salsa, fritters, quiche, soup, and even bread.
“The bread is made with sugar, raisins, and walnuts,” Mr. Binsky said. “It's a dessert.”
Eyes wide, Dad shook his head. “Look at all this food!”
“Let's have a picnic,” Patrick suggested.
“I'll whip up a batch of zucchini juice.”
“Excuse me?” Mrs. Gianelli said.
“Just kidding,” said Patrick. “I'll make lemonade.”

The neighbors paraded outside to the deck with their zucchini dishes. Dad passed out paper plates, forks, and napkins. As everyone talked and laughed and ate, they exchanged recipes. After the feast, Patrick made another pitcher of lemonade. Mr. Binsky served the zucchini bread.

Then Patrick joined Dad at the edge of the garden. New flowers had burst into bloom on the tomato plants since that morning. Each yellow flower meant one red, ripe tomato. Dad winked at Patrick. They smiled at everyone enjoying the zucchini picnic.
“I guess you can never have too many vegetables,” Dad said.
“Or neighbors!” said Patrick.

options.label: Gardening can teach kids to better appreciate nature.
Being kind to others can help bring people together.
It's important to thank someone when they do something kind for you.
Don't judge a food before you've had the chance to taste it.

articles.title: Dragonfly
articles.author: Bill Johnson
articles.description: A speaker watches a dragonfly.
articles.intro: Bill Johnson has written for Highlights. In this poem, a speaker describes a dragonfly.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the words that the speaker uses to describe the dragonfly.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: A dazzling-colored dragonfly
darts back and forth near me.
I pause and watch her hover there
so close that I can see
two bulging eyes,
four sparkling wings.
She then begins to fly.
Oh, how I wish
that I could join
this dragon in the sky!

options.label: Humans often don't notice the beauty of dragonflies.
Dragonflies are beautiful and special creatures.
People always want what they can't have.
Sometimes you have to look closely to notice beauty.

articles.title: From Pit to Pond
articles.author: Wendy Hobday Haugh
articles.description: A narrator describes turning the pit in her backyard into a pond.
articles.intro: Wendy Hobday Haugh has written for Highlights. In this memoir, Haugh describes how the pit in her backyard became a beautiful pond.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what changes Haugh and her family made to the pit.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: When my dad began building our home, he needed dirt to fill in around the base of the house. He hired someone with a backhoe to dig up more dirt.
“Dig down there,” Dad told the man, pointing to a low spot in our backyard. “Then haul the dirt up here by the house.”
First, a Big Pit
Soon our backyard had a huge hole! This pit was like a big bowl with a clay bottom. The clay kept rainwater from draining quickly into the earth. So the pit slowly filled with water.
Next, Frogs and Lily Pads
Before long, a few frogs moved into our pit, croaking noisily.
“Frogs like lily pads,” I told Mom.

My grandmother dug up two water lilies from her small garden pool. “Weigh the roots down with small rocks,” Gramma said. “Then gently toss them into the water. Once the plants take root in the dirt, they’ll start growing toward the sunlight.”
Then, Baby Turtles
One day in late summer I discovered dozens of baby snapping turtles crawling from tunnels in our hard-packed driveway. They were so tiny that they tripped over blades of grass.
Will they find our pit? I wondered excitedly.
I’d read that when baby turtles are born, they already know that they need to find water. Sure enough, the baby turtles scrambled clumsily down the hill to our water-filled pit.
Finally, a Pond
My best friend returned from a fishing trip and called me. “I’ve got a bucket of leftover bait fish. Can I put them in your pond?”
Pond?
Until that moment, I’d never really thought of the big, bare pit in our backyard as a pond.

By the next spring, melting winter snows raised the water level. Weeds and wild flowers sprang up along the banks. Lily pads sprouted and blossomed. Frogs, fish, and turtles played in the murky water.
I had to admit that our big pit had turned into a beautiful small pond!

options.label: Surprising places can become good homes for animals and plants.
Ponds make the best homes for frogs and turtles.
Humans should make homes for plants and animals.
It is good for humans to save rainwater for the future.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the central idea of the text?

"'Then gently toss them into the water. Once the plants take root in the dirt, they'll start growing toward the sunlight.'" (Paragraph 6)
"One day in late summer I discovered dozens of baby snapping turtles crawling from tunnels in our hard-packed driveway." (Paragraph 7)
"Until that moment, I'd never really thought of the big, bare pit in our backyard as a pond." (Paragraph 12)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: She thinks it will make the pit prettier.
She wants to make the frogs happier.
She wants to encourage turtles to come.

questions.prompt: PART A: Why does the narrator want to add lily pads to the pit?

options.label: "Soon our backyard had a huge hole! This pit was like a big bowl with a clay bottom." (Paragraph 3)

"My grandmother dug up two water lilies from her small garden pool." (Paragraph 6)
"baby turtles scrambled clumsily down the hill to our water-filled pit." (Paragraph 9)

In her memoir, the author describes creating a home for animals. What other ways do you think humans can help animals?

articles.title: Jasmine Girl
articles.author: Jey Manokaran
articles.description: A girl needs enough money to buy a special doll.
articles.intro: Jey Manokaran has written for Highlights. In this short story, a girl works hard to make enough money to buy a doll.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Shanti and Jodi’s friendship.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Shanti wanted the wooden doll in Ramu’s toy shop. “The one with the red dress,” she told her best friend, Jodi.
“How will you afford it?” Jodi asked.
Shanti smiled. “I asked my mom if I could make jasmine garlands and sell them at my aunt’s market stall. Want to do that, too?”
“Good idea!” said Jodi.

Early the next morning, the girls met at the jasmine bushes outside of Shanti’s house. Delicate white flowers surrounded them with fresh scent. They nipped blossoms with their fingers and dropped them into a basket. Then the girls sat under a mango tree and knotted the flowers into garlands. Shanti could already see herself hugging that beautiful doll.
“If we sell enough garlands, we can each get a doll,” said Shanti.
Jodi shook her head. “My family is low on money this week. I’ll use my money to pay for Kiran’s medicine.” Kiran was Jodi’s little brother.
“Is he sick?” Shanti asked.
“He is. But when he gets his medicine, he’ll be fine.” Jodi picked up her garlands. “My mother is expecting me. I’ll meet you at the market.”
Shanti spent the afternoon selling garlands at her aunt’s market stall. But where was Jodi? By early evening, Shanti had sold all her flowers.
Shanti was on her way home when she saw Jodi heading to the market with her garlands. “Why are you so late?” Shanti asked.
“I had to watch Kiran while my mother ran an errand,” Jodi said.
“Why didn’t you take him with you?” Shanti said.
Jodi shook her head. “He’s too sick to go out. He coughs and coughs.”
Shanti knew Jodi wouldn’t make enough money for the medicine now. The market would be closing soon.

Shanti looked at the money pouch clutched in her hand. It was supposed to be for the beautiful doll she’d been dreaming of for so long. Then she looked at her friend’s tired face. “Here. Keep this for the medicine.” Shanti put her money into Jodi’s hands.
That night, Shanti cried, thinking about the doll. What if someone bought it before she’d saved up enough again?
The next week, while Shanti and Jodi gathered blossoms, Kiran came, too. The medicine had cured his cough, and he ran about laughing and throwing flowers into the air.
Jodi said, “Now you’re Kiran’s best friend. Your money made him well.”

The three of them sold garlands at the market that afternoon. After Shanti helped her aunt pack up the stall, she ran to Ramu’s shop. She burst in and spilled her money across the counter. “My doll!” she cried. “The one in the red dress!”
Ramu said, “I’m sorry, Shanti. Someone bought it.”
Shanti gasped. She gathered up her money and ran out the door before Ramu could see her tears.
“What’s wrong, sweetheart?” her mother asked when she ran inside.
“My doll is gone!” Shanti sobbed.
Her mother hugged her, then handed her a bumpy package wrapped in paper. “Jodi dropped this off for you.”
On the front of the package was a note: For our best friend, Shanti. From Jodi and Kiran. With shaking hands, Shanti unwrapped it.
Her doll! Jodi had bought the doll for Shanti! The dress glowed a rich red, and the hair was braided with tiny white flowers.
Shanti hugged the doll. “I’ll call her Jasmine,” she said.

options.label: Toys are never as important as friends and family.
If someone gives you something, you must repay them.
People learn the value of money by earning it themselves.
When you are kind to others, they are often kind to you in return.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the short story?

options.label: "Shanti could already see herself hugging that beautiful doll." (Paragraph 5)
"'I'll use my money to pay for Kiran's medicine.' Kiran was Jodi's little brother." (Paragraph 7)
"What if someone bought it before she'd saved up enough again?" (Paragraph 17)
"Her doll! Jodi had bought the doll for Shanti!" (Paragraph 27)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: They show how much they appreciate Shanti by buying her the doll.
They reveal how guilty they feel and give Shanti her money back.
They spend more time with Shanti and decide to be better friends to her.
They tell Shanti over and over again how much they value her friendship.

questions.prompt: How do Jodi and Kiran respond to Shanti giving them her money?
How does Shanti feel after she gives Jodi her money? Why? Give evidence from the text to support your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Jodi and Shanti are described as best friends. How do they show each other that they care about one another? Describe a time that you did something nice for your best friend.
Why do you think Shanti decides to give her money to Jodi rather than buy a doll? When has doing the right thing been difficult for you?

articles.title: The Champion of Quiet
articles.author: Tracy Stewart
articles.description: A girl who is always picked last in gym class volunteers to be a team captain.
articles.intro: Tracy Stewart has written for Highlights . In this short story, a quiet girl volunteers to be a team captain in gym class.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify who Maggie chooses as teammates and the different reasons for choosing them.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: It was a Tuesday, and Maggie hated Tuesdays.
Tuesdays meant one thing: gym class.
Gym class meant one thing: picking teams.
Picking teams meant one thing: Maggie would be picked last.
As always.
Maggie was a new student that year in a class of champions. To help herself remember the other kids, she’d come up with a special title for each one.
There was Kevin, Champion of the Clean Desk; and Lynnie, Champion of Knowing the Answer First; and Caroline, Champion of Tardy. And so on.
She’d had to think long and hard to figure out her own special title. After five whole weeks of school, Maggie had decided.
She was the Champion of Quiet.

The Champion of Quiet sat on the gym floor, sandwiched between the Champion of Daydreaming and the Champion of Goofy Jokes. Maggie felt blue. She knew what was coming. She considered changing her title to Champion of Last Pick.
Mr. Murphy, the gym teacher, asked for volunteers to pick the basketball teams.
The usual hands shot into the air.
Maggie sighed and glanced to her right. She caught the eye of Jasmine, Champion of Cursive J’s. Maggie thought she might like to be Jasmine’s friend but never felt brave enough to talk to her.
Looking at Jasmine, Maggie realized that Jasmine seemed a little blue, too. Maggie thought, I’m not the only one picked last. Sometimes Jasmine is picked last, too.
With that, the Champion of Quiet had an idea.
Mr. Murphy asked, “Anyone else want to volunteer today?”
Maggie felt her hand slowly rise. She looked again at Jasmine and took a big breath.
Maybe it was time for the Champion of Quiet to use her voice.
“Maggie?” Mr. Murphy smiled. “You want a turn?”
Do I? Maggie wondered. “Yes,” she said.
The class murmured. Maggie leading a team?
“Yes,” she answered again, louder this time. Her voice shook just a little.

“What’s stopping you, then?” Mr. Murphy waved Maggie to the front of the gym, where the other three captains — Champion of Jumping Rope on One Foot, Champion of Never Misses a Shot, and Champion of Doesn’t Know How to Lose — stood, ready to choose their teams.
“Maggie, you get first pick,” said Mr. Murphy.
“Jasmine,” announced the Champion of Quiet.
The class snickered. Was this a joke? More than once, Jasmine had tried to score on the wrong basket.
Maggie smiled and repeated, “Jasmine.”
Eyes wide, Jasmine took her place beside Maggie.
The other three captains called out familiar names — the best players on the basketball court.
It was Maggie’s turn again. “I choose Jamie.”
Jamie, Champion of the Untied Shoelace. Jamie could not dribble, throw, or catch. He knew what it felt like to be the last pick.
But not today.
Jamie jogged to stand next to Jasmine. He tripped only once. The class sat in shock.
So it went. The names flowed from Maggie’s mouth, sounding sure and strong. The stronger she sounded, the stronger she felt.
Three very talented teams took shape.
And then there was Maggie’s team.
Maggie looked at her teammates. There stood Jasmine and Jamie, plus Gillian (Champion of Hugs) and George (Champion of Very Big Musical Instrument). They were grinning at Maggie.
Maggie grinned back. I have chosen the worst team in the history of the world, she thought.
The Champion of Quiet walked onto the court with her team close behind, ready to face players who were bigger and better.
Game on!
The basketball slipped through Jamie’s fingers, skated around Jasmine, dribbled itself away from George and Gillian, and bounced off Maggie’s head — twice. Their laughter echoed throughout the gym.
Clutching the ball and out of breath from running and giggling, Maggie heard Jasmine call out, “Mags! Pass the ball to me!”
I have a nickname, Maggie thought happily. She threw the ball to Jasmine.
Of course, Jasmine missed. They were indeed the Worst Team in the History of the World.
And they did what any Worst Team would do. They lost. They lost big.
But losing a basketball game doesn’t make you a loser; Maggie knew that much. Maggie grinned at her team. We are, officially, the Champions of No Score, she thought. Awesome.
The Champion of Quiet walked off the court with her new friends. And as the group made plans to sit together at lunch, Maggie was no longer surprised to hear her own voice joining the noisy chorus.

options.label: It can be frightening to be the new kid at school.
Taking a chance and being brave can lead to making new friends.
People who have things in common make good friends.
If you want to stop something that is unfair, it's important to speak up.

articles.title: No Turkey Required
articles.author: Kate MacEachern
articles.description: A girl's Thanksgiving plans change when a snowstorm strands her and her mom.
articles.intro: If something is required, it means it is needed. In this story by Kate MacEachern, Ashley and her mom are planning a road trip to Minnesota for Thanksgiving.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Ashley’s feelings about her pie.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Ashley ran home from school. Thanksgiving meant four days off. Driving from Ohio to Minnesota took one day, so she could spend two days with Emily. If they didn’t sleep, that was more time. Ashley hadn’t seen Emily for five months — not since Ashley had moved to Ohio. It felt like forever.
Over the phone, Emily talked a lot about a girl named Isabella. Was Isabella Emily’s new best friend?
No , Ashley decided. She couldn’t be. Only Ashley knew how to make Emily’s favorite dessert, cranberry-apple pie.
When Ashley got to Minnesota, Emily would show off her ballet steps. Ashley would draw a picture of Emily in a tutu and braids. They’d be best friends forever like they’d promised.
Ashley couldn’t wait to get back to Minnesota.
She ran into the living room. “Can I start packing the car? We said we’d leave by 4:00.”
Mom glanced up from her computer. “There’s a good chance of snow.”
“It’s warm out,” Ashley said. “The forecast must be wrong.”
“Even if it’s not snowing here, a storm could hit along the way to Minnesota.” Mom frowned. “Maybe we should postpone our trip until Christmas.”
Ashley felt her heart sinking. “Isn’t snow more likely in December? Anyway, snowplows clear the roads fast. I promised Emily I’d come.”
Mom pushed her chair back and sighed. “We’ll hope for good weather and stop if it snows. We might have to turn back.”
“I’ll keep my fingers crossed,” Ashley said. “Nothing will go wrong.”
But everything did go wrong.
A tire went flat. Ashley told Mom to take the wrong exit and they had to circle back. They were only in Indiana when they stopped to sleep at a hotel.

On Thanksgiving morning, they got up before the sun and drove. Ashley crossed her fingers, wrists, and arms like a complicated pretzel. That must have brought luck because nothing more went wrong for a while. They drove across Illinois and into Wisconsin.

Snow began to fall. Little flakes at first, then big, splotchy clumps. The freeway had no place to stop. The windshield wipers could hardly keep up. A snowplow passed and Mom followed in its tracks for a while.
When the plow pulled off the road into a rest area, Mom pulled into the rest area, too.
The driver lowered his window. “I have to head south at the next turnaround. Best you stay off the road awhile.”
“Thanks!” Mom said. She waved and parked the car, then turned to Ashley. “We’ll stay here until the roads are clear.”
“It’s Thanksgiving. I brought Emily’s favorite pie. She’s counting on me.” Ashley felt her eyes filling with tears.
Mom reached back to squeeze Ashley’s hand. “I’m sorry, sweetie. We can eat pie tomorrow.”
Ashley stared at the rest-stop building. A snow-covered truck and car were already parked. “Where will we stay?”
“The rest stop has a lobby and bathrooms. And heat. We can sleep on the floor.” Mom pulled her coat on. “Grab your car blanket and pillow. It’ll be fun, like camping.”
Ashley scrambled out of the car and followed close behind Mom. Snow hit like miniature dodge balls, cold and hard. When she tried to run, the wind pushed her backward.
Mom pulled open the door and they stumbled inside. Ashley stomped her feet, and snow slipped onto the floor.

In the middle of the lobby, a man sat between two little boys on a football blanket. A girl sat with her parents on another blanket next to them. They wore winter coats, and the girl looked like Emily.
“Hello,” said Mom.
“ Pryvit ,” said the girl.

“Welcome. Come share our Thanksgiving dinner,” said the father of the two young boys. He pulled sandwiches out of a cooler and set them on the blanket next to some bowls. “We’re sharing our food. I’ve got sandwiches.”
“We’re from Ukraine.” The girl’s mom pointed to the other food. “ Borshch. Varenyky. ”
“Beet soup and stuffed dumplings,” translated the girl’s dad. “We’ll buy water and sodas from the vending machine. All we need is dessert.”
Ashley thought about Emily’s cranberry-apple pie in the car.
“I’ll buy candy bars from those machines,” Mom said.
“ Zaproshuyemo ,” said the Ukrainian girl, smiling at Ashley.
Ashley had a feeling that meant something like “welcome.”
She thought about the first Thanksgiving and swallowed hard. Emily would understand when Ashley explained. Best friends always listened.
“We have pie,” Ashley said. “Thanksgiving pie.”

options.label: Always use polite words with adults.
It is important to be willing to change.
Taking a plane or train is better than driving.
You should bring gifts when you are a visitor.

worried that Emily has a new best friend.
happy that she no longer lives in Minnesota.
angry that her mom forgot to check the weather.

options.label: Ashley feels forgiving towards her mother.

options.label: They show the problem Ashley is facing in the story.
They teach a lesson in the story about solving problems.
They help the reader think about how the story will end.
They give the reader details about the characters in the story.

discussionQuestions.prompt: How would you feel if you were Ashley in the rest-stop? Have you ever had to make a difficult decision, like Ashley did in the story? Would you have shared Emily's pie? Why or why not?

Ashley and her mom will not get to Minnesota when they had planned. When was a time you had to cancel or put off something you were really excited about? What happened? How did you feel? What advice would you give to a younger friend or sibling who had to put off something they were excited about?
What kind of friend do you think Ashley is? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. What do you look for in a friend? Do you think you would want to be friends with Ashley? Why or why not?

articles.title: 3 Ways to Learn about Consumer Power
articles.author: Amanda Oliver
articles.description: This text discusses how we can make a positive difference when making purchases.
articles.intro: This text explains how to make better choices the next time you buy something.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, highlight ways to make a positive difference when choosing what to buy.
articles.permissions: Text adapted from "4 Impactful Ways to Teach Kids About Consumer Power" by Amanda Oliver, 2014. Used with permission.

blocks.text: What is consumer power? Think about your favorite store, toy, or food. The ability to decide what you and your family buy is called consumer power. By buying certain products instead of others, you can help make a positive difference in the world.
How can you become a more conscious consumer?
1. At the Shop
Every time you have a say in which products you buy, you are exercising your buying power.
CHOOSE PRODUCTS WITH LESS PACKAGING
Once upon a time, the world believed that extra packaging was a sign of quality and importance.
We now know that packaging is mostly waste. Next time you look at a product, look at the package it comes in.
Does a single pen really need all of that packaging? Does your new toy really need that huge box?
Thinking about these questions can help us cut down on waste.
BUY FSC PAPER PRODUCTS

The Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) is an organization that makes sure wood and paper products come from forests that are responsibly managed. It is easy to check for the FSC label on pencils and paper. By looking for this label, you are exercising your buying power. You have a choice in what you buy and where the products come from.
2. The Source
Do you know where your food comes from? How about your favorite toys?

Your favorite snacks don’t come from the aisles of the grocery store. Your favorite toys don’t come from Target or Toys R Us. Learning about where our products really come from can help us make decisions.
VISIT A LOCAL FARM
You can visit a local farm to learn more about where our food comes from and what is healthy to eat.
SUPPORT LOCAL FOOD PRODUCERS
A lot of trucks are needed every day to move vegetables and fruits from farms to grocery stores. This is a big problem! These trucks release carbon dioxide and other harmful gases into the air.
That is why you should purchase food items from local producers. Look for farmer’s markets or grocery stores that carry local products.
3. Believe in your Power
You have the power to change the neighborhood and world you live in. The choices you make as a consumer can make a difference. They can change the future of our planet for the better.
A GOLDEN RULE
Whether it’s choosing eco-friendly products at the store or writing letters to local politicians about topics that matter to you, your voice makes a difference.

options.label: You and your family can start a garden to make the world better.
You and your family cannot make a big difference in the world by yourselves.
You and your family can get sick if you don't know where your food comes from.
You and your family can make a positive difference by carefully choosing what you buy.

"The choices you make as a consumer can make a difference. They can change the future of our planet for the better." (Paragraph 13)
