articles.title: Obento: Art in a Lunch Box
articles.author: Teresa V. Mitchum
articles.description: Teresa V. Mitchum describes the lunches children bring to school in Japan.
articles.intro: At school in Japan, children bring a boxed lunch known as obento. In this informational text, Teresa V. Mitchum provides information about what lunchtime looks like for children in Japan.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how parents prepare obento for their children.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: What’s so much fun about lunchtime in Japan?
Japanese kids eat a good-looking lunch. They carry lunches to school in bento boxes. Their boxed lunch is called obento (oh-BEN-toe).
Bento boxes are small containers made out of plastic, wood, or metal. Sometimes they have sections to separate the food.
The bento boxes are usually decorated with cartoon characters, animals, or flowers. They have matching chopsticks or silverware containers, bags, cups, and lunch mats.
Pretty Food

Inside the bento box, the obento is just as colorful. Many Japanese people believe that all food should taste good and look pretty. There is a Japanese saying: Food should be made so that you could eat it with your eyes.
Japanese parents often choose foods with different colors, textures, shapes, and tastes. Then they arrange the food in the bento boxes.

Mrs. Sato, who lives in Tokyo, makes obento for her son Koki. She says, “I want Koki’s obento to be colorful. I try to include foods that are red, green, yellow, and brown. I make the obento look nice but also make sure it’s healthy.”
What do kids eat as their obento? Rice and side dishes.

Koki likes a layer of rice in the bottom of his bento box. Sometimes Mrs. Sato puts shogayaki , stir-fried pork, on top of the rice. Sometimes she sprinkles it with furikake . These are sprinkles made from nori (seaweed) or dried fish. Many children in Japan love them.

Giri, another boy, brings onigiri . These are sticky rice balls or triangles wrapped in dried, pressed seaweed. Sometimes the onigiri have a pickled plum, small bits of cooked salmon, or flaked fish in the middle.

The side dishes include pickled, boiled, or steamed vegetables; omelets made with salt and sugar; small sausages; fish; or meat. Umeboshi (pickled Japanese apricot) is a treat. The dessert is fresh fruit.
Before You Eat

Obento time is special. First, children wash their hands and gargle with a kind of mouthwash. Then they get their obento from their bags. In the winter, the sensei (teacher) may take their obento out of a warmer for them.
Kids put their obento, chopsticks, and cups on their lunch mats. They sit at classroom tables.
Before eating, they sing a fun song about obento:
We are so happy because it’s obento time.
Our hands are washed and clean.
Everybody is here now,
So let’s say, “Thank you
for the food, Mommy
and Daddy.”

options.label: Japanese children eat healthier lunches than American children.
Obento focuses on how food looks, rather than how it tastes or its nutrition.
Great thought and care is put into obento lunches for children in Japan.
Parents use bento boxes as a way to express their love to their children.

articles.title: Jumping Over Boundaries
articles.author: Linda Alvarado
articles.description: Linda Alvarado explains how an experience with inequality in elementary school impacts her today.
articles.intro: In this text, Linda Alvarado explains how she crossed a boundary, or a line that shows how far someone is allowed to go, in elementary school and how it is important to her today.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Linda Alvarado’s actions.
articles.permissions: Copyright © Highlights for Children, Inc., Columbus, Ohio. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: When I was in elementary school, we had an annual sports day. Girls did things like hopscotch, jump rope, tetherball, and dodgeball. The boys got to do high jumping.
That’s what I wanted to do — high jumping. I went to sign up.
“Girls don’t do high jumping,” the coach said. “Girls have all these other things they can do.” He excitedly described how much fun it would be.

I’d been high jumping in the backyard with my five brothers for years. I went home and told my mother what the coach had said. My mother, a small, thin Hispanic woman only four feet eleven inches tall, said, “I think we should go visit him.”
“Visit who?” I asked.
“The principal.”
“Visit the principal?” I was terrified. Would I get in trouble? Would my mother and I be crossing boundaries we shouldn’t cross?
My mom and I went to Mr. White’s office. “Linda wants to do the high jump,” my mother said.
“Mrs. Martinez, you don’t understand. This is the tradition of our Annual Sports Day,” he said.
“But Señor White, I don’t know where it says that girls cannot do this.”
The principal couldn’t find any rules that said girls couldn’t do the high jump. “I have to think about this,” he said at last.
When we got home, my mom told me something I would always remember: this wasn’t just about me. “Maybe other girls want to do this, too,” she said.
My mother waited a week, and then she walked over to the school. “Señor White, please. I ask you this not for me and not for Linda, but let’s just see how it works.”
In the end, Mr. White agreed.

As it turned out, I won the high-jumping contest. To my surprise, some of the cheers I got were from boys! My mother was right — it wasn’t about me. It was about making people think about what girls could do.

High jumping helped me to understand that people would not always accept or welcome me. I used this lesson in my career as a building contractor. Just as girls did not do the high jump, women were not supposed to construct buildings.

When I first started in business, my mother would say to me in Spanish, “Mi’ jita, empieza pequeño, pero piensa grande” (My dear little one, start small, but think big). I found small ways to begin to show what I could do. I began by installing concrete curbs, gutters, and sidewalks.

Today, my company, Alvarado Construction, builds large projects, including schools, aquariums, airports, and convention centers. I love my job. I view my career much like Robert Frost’s poem. I took the path least traveled by girls and women, and this has really made a difference.

options.label: High jump is a hard sport to learn.
Linda Alvarado is a famous high jumper.
It is important to try to change rules that are unfair.
Annual Sports Days should be the same every year.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: brave
proud
scared
surprised

questions.prompt: As it is used in paragraph 7, the word "terrified" shows that Linda felt —

options.label: "I used this lesson in my career as a building contractor." (Paragraph 16)
"Just as girls did not do the high jump, women were not supposed to construct buildings." (Paragraph 16)
"I began by installing concrete curbs, gutters, and sidewalks." (Paragraph 17)

"Today, my company, Alvarado Construction, builds large projects, including schools, aquariums, airports, and convention centers." (Paragraph 18) E. "I love my job." (Paragraph 18) F. "I view my career much like Robert Frost's poem." (Paragraph 18)

articles.title: Trix Learns to Love Winter (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: A miniature horse learns an important lesson about being honest after faking an injury.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. In this story, a miniature horse, or a very small horse, named Trix lives on a farm and pulls children around in a sled during the cold winter months.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Trix feels about the children.
articles.permissions: "Trix Learns to Love Winter (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, a gaggle of loud, tiny humans have just arrived.

Trix hates the winter time. It’s cold. The ground is hard and slippery. She has to wear a scratchy blanket with holes in it that let the wind in. Most of her friends are in hibernation, which is a long, boring sleep they take during winter. Or they’ve migrated to warmer places until the cold weather passes.

But it’s not just the animals. Humans also begin to act differently in the winter. They light candles in the windows. They string colorful lights on the trees that blink all night. Then crowds of other humans come to the farm to look at the lights. They wear puffy coats and cup their hands around steaming mugs that smell bitter and sweet. But worst of all, the farm assistants make Trix pull a cart full of small humans.

Trix walked as slowly as possible out of her stall. It was time to get ready, and the farm assistants like to dress her up. She tried to wiggle out of the way of the brushes, combs, hoof picks, hoof polish, glitter, blankets, and ribbons. But it was no use! After braiding Trix’s mane, they hooked her up to a cart that looked like a giant sled.
This year was going to be different. Trix had a plan.
Beatrice, the energetic sparrow, landed on one of Trix’s driving reins and shivered. “Gosh, Trix, it’s so cold! Where are Garbanzo and Pendragon?”

“Trying to get warm,” Trix answered in a grumpy voice. Her raccoon and lizard friends didn’t take too kindly to the icy wind blowing in through the barn doors. “Garbanzo is napping with the barn cats. Pendragon is probably basking under the hen’s heat lamp.”
“I’ll keep you company!” Bea announced with a friendly giggle.
“Thanks, Bea,” Trix said gratefully.
The sparrow hid under Trix’s braided forelock as the farm assistant led Trix out into the chilly air.

A group of miniature humans tramped out onto the field. They looked like fluffy chickens in their puffy coats. Their sticky, grabby hands rubbed her fur the wrong way. They tugged at her braids and ribbons, and talked way too loudly. Fortunately, they also gave her handfuls of sugar cubes, apple slices, peanut butter horse biscuits, and carrots. The crunchy foods were so delicious that Trix almost didn’t notice when several miniature humans climbed into the sled.
“Gosh,” Bea whispered. “They must be heavy. Do you need help?”
“I’m strong enough to pull them,” Trix whispered back, “but I wouldn’t mind the help.”
The sparrow nodded and tried to tug Trix along by her braided forelock. She flapped her tiny wings and pulled. The tiny bird’s chest heaved as she huffed and puffed.

Trix did most of the work, but it felt nice to have company as she trudged through the snow. They made it past the barn and looped around the garden to drop off the children. The whole trip took about ten minutes. Another group of miniature humans stood waiting for their turn in the sled.
“Good job, Bea,” Trix chuckled.
“All this work is making me hungry,” Bea said. “I’ll be right back, Trix!” She flew to a nearby bird feeder where she snuck a mouthful of seeds.
When she returned, Trix was limping along the trail. Her head hung low, and she looked like she was in a lot of pain.
“Trix!” Bea squealed. “Are you okay?”
Bea wasn’t the only one who had noticed. One of the farm assistants stopped the sled. “Let’s take a look,” he said.

Trix winked at Bea to show that she was just pretending. Her plan was a success! The farm assistant checked her hooves, legs, knee joints, and even her mouth, but found nothing wrong. The human then led Trix back into the barn to rest. Trix was very pleased, even if a chorus of disappointed “awww”s followed her.
The miniature horse and sparrow bedded down in the soft barn straw. They dreamed of seeds and sugar cubes.
The next day, Trix cantered happily through the pasture. She kicked up light, fluffy snow. She whinnied out huge puffs of warm air that looked like smoke.
The farm assistants spotted Trix in the pasture and saw that she wasn’t hurt. They decided she was healthy enough to pull the sled.
“Drat,” thought Trix, as she was once again hooked up to the sled. “I guess I’ll just have to try again.”
This time, Trix tried her limping trick with the other leg. But none of the farm assistants stopped the sled! Maybe they couldn’t see her limping? Trix made her steps more uneven.

Unfortunately, she was trying so hard to make it look like she was limping that she wasn’t looking where her hooves were going. She slipped on a patch of ice and fell to her knees. One of her legs twisted. It hurt even more when she stood up.
“Uh-oh.” Bea fluttered near Trix. “What happened?”
“I got hurt for real,” Trix mumbled.
The farm assistant led Trix inside and called the veterinarian — a doctor for animals. The humans called the veterinarian the “vet” for short.
Soon, the vet was in the barn. She pulled out a stethoscope to listen to Trix’s breathing and heart rates. Both were higher than usual because of the pain she felt.
The miniature humans crowded at the door of the barn. They looked worried and sad. They didn’t like going to the doctor either.
“You can do it, Trix!” said one in a puffy red coat. “At the end, you get a lollipop and a sticker.”

Trix had no idea what the small human was talking about, but it made her feel a little better to have company. She didn’t like it when the vet visited. Usually, the vet poked and prodded her. Sometimes, she put weird stuff in her mouth to grind her teeth down.
“Her leg is swollen,” the vet said. “We’ll give her medicine to start. Then, we’ll apply ice to keep the swelling and pain down.”
“Is her leg broken?” a child in a sparkly pink tutu asked. She stuck out a leg covered in a plaster cast. “I broke mine in dance class.”
“Nope! It’s just a sprain, but we need to protect Trix’s legs,” the farm assistant explained.
“Will she be okay?” another child piped up.

“Trix’s blood is gathering in the area to heal itself,” the vet said. “Horses spend their entire lives on their feet, so their legs are very important to keep healthy. Unlike humans, she can’t use crutches, a cast, or a wheelchair until she gets better. Only rest will help.”

Trix fidgeted throughout the exam. The vet and farm assistant soaked her leg in ice, dried it off, and then wrapped it up. Once they were finished, Trix sighed and leaned against her stall wall. What a tiring day! That was the last time Trix was going to pretend to be hurt.
Three small pairs of hands reached in through the bars and gently patted Trix’s forehead. They had brought her a lollipop, a heavy blanket, and a shiny unicorn sticker.
“You were very brave,” one said. The small human gently stuck the sticker to Trix’s nose.
“Feel better soon, Trix,” the other one said and gave Trix the round candy on a stick.
All together, they wrapped her in a brand new soft blanket. It was much better than the old scratchy one with holes.

And the burst of sugary sweetness did help. The blanket gave her a cozy, fuzzy feeling. It reminded her of when she was small and would cuddle up to her mother. The sticker was a mystery. She had no idea what the sticker was supposed to do, but at least it didn’t hurt. She was starting to think that miniature humans and the winter time might not be so bad after all.

options.label: Being forced to do something can make you unhappy.
Helping children will make them care for you less.
Friends should not lie for each other.
Being sick is more fun than it looks.

because it explains why she has no friends
because it explains why she lies about getting hurt
because it explains why she is mean to the other animals

options.label: It shows that she is afraid to pull the sled.

It shows that she is tired while pulling the sled.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 14, what does the word "trudged" show about Trix?
How do Trix's feelings about children change from the beginning to the end of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Trix learns an important lesson about telling the truth. Think about a time you learned a similar lesson about being honest. What happened that made you understand the importance of being truthful? You can use your own experience or the experience of a character from a book, history, or movie.

One of the responsibilities that Trix has on the farm is to pull people around in a sled during the winter months. Do you think it is okay for animals to be used for people's entertainment in this way? Why or why not?
In the story, Trix really dislikes winter. Which season do you like the least? Why? Can you think of one thing that you do enjoy about the season? What is it and why do you like it?
Trix changes her mind about how she feels about the children in the story. Think about a time you changed your mind about something. What happened to make you change your mind?

articles.title: Wolfy Ferocious (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: A miniature horse and her friends help a wolf spider find a new home.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. Ferocious is a word for something that is fierce or can cause damage. In this story, a miniature horse, or a very small horse, named Trix meets a ferocious animal that comes to the farm.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Trix’s feelings about Wolfgang.
articles.permissions: "Wolfy Ferocious (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, Trix and her friends are helping a pesky barn critter find a new home.
Trix was just about to fall asleep in her stall when she heard a rustling in the sawdust. Crunch, crunch . Something was munching on an insect in a shadowy corner.
“Hello?” Trix called, trying to be brave. Nighttime was scary. She couldn’t see anything in the dim light.
A small, fierce voice came from the corner: “I am Wolfgang the Wolf Spider.”
“Uh, okay,” Trix said, closing her eyes again. “Goodnight then.”
“I’m going to EAT you!” he cried.

The miniature horse’s eyes snapped open, and her ears twitched in the spider’s direction. Trix wasn’t usually afraid of arachnids. That was the scientific name for eight-legged creatures, like spiders. She was bigger than all of them! And spiders didn’t eat horses. Did they?
“Get out of here!” a grumpy voice called from above in the rafters. “It’s bedtime, you goon. Try to eat unreasonably large prey tomorrow.”

The grumpy voice belonged to Garbanzo the raccoon. He usually bedded down in a hammock above Trix’s head. He had brought a few smelly snacks to bed. He had taken them from the trash that the human visitors left at the park. Trix could hear crunching coming from above her head.
“Go back to making your webs,” Trix said sleepily. “You can hunt me tomorrow.”
“I don’t make webs. I’m a wolf spider,” Wolfgang scoffed.
“Go to bed!” Trix and Garbanzo said loudly.
Wolfgang was silent. The spider seemed to be thinking about their wish to come back during the day.
“Okay! I’m going, but not because you told me to,” Wolfgang said. “I’ll be back when you least expect it!”
Trix frowned. It was just one little spider . It didn’t even have a web. She snorted and settled back down to sleep.
Harrumph , she thought. I can handle one little spider… Tomorrow.

The next morning, Trix woke to eight pairs of legs crawling across her face. She stamped her hoof and shook her head. When it didn’t let go, she called out for Garbanzo, but the raccoon had already slunk out to find more snacks.
A big brown and orange spider with fuzzy legs stared at her, upside down. It was Wolfgang. All eight of his eyes blinked. “I’m going to eat you!” he announced.
“I don’t think so,” said Trix.
The miniature horse shook her head so hard she tossed the spider out of her stall. He scuttled away down the aisle, past the wash stall, and toward the barn office.
Trix sighed with relief. Hopefully, he was gone for good this time.
But Trix’s relief didn’t last long. After the human farm assistant brought her bucket of grain, the same spider popped out.
“I’m going to eat you!” the spider shouted again.
Trix kicked her grain bucket so hard that the spider fell out and onto the straw. She stamped until she was sure she’d scared him away.
Bam! Bam! Bam!
The confused farm assistant came running back to see about all the noise. “Whoa there, girl. Do you want to go outside?”

Trix rushed to the door. She danced nervously on her tippy toes the whole way out to the pasture. Outside in the warm daylight, she could breathe again. She happily greeted her friends. “Hello!” she said to the chattering ducks and chickens.
“Good morning!” she called to Garbanzo as he rummaged for garbage scraps.
Pendragon, the bearded dragon lizard, waved as he lay in the sunlight.
Trix bent down for a mouthful of crunchy hay, only to nearly chomp down on that pesky spider.
“I’m going to eat you!” Wolfgang cried, jumping toward her flaring nostrils.
Trix took off across the pasture, bucking and snorting. The farm assistant returned to check on her and tried to calm her down.
“Trix! What’s gotten into you, girl?” the farm assistant asked.
The farm assistant gave her a few fond pats and went back inside.
Trix would not be calmed. That spider was getting on her last nerve. She trotted breathlessly over to Pendragon and Garbanzo, who were sitting on the fence. They were gaping at her.
“What’s up with you ?” Garbanzo asked in between bites. He munched on a half-eaten piece of sandwich he’d found in the trash.
“There’s this spider , and he won’t leave me alone!” she said.
Pendragon and Garbanzo looked at each other. This was the most distressed they’d ever seen Trix. Usually, she was calm, curious, and totally focused on her scientific research.
“I, Wolfgang the Wolf Spider, am going to eat you!” a small voice yelled from the grass.
Garbanzo chuckled. “Is that what’s got you so upset?”
“Wolf spiders are delicious.” Pendragon smacked his lips and thrashed his scaly tail from side to side. “I’ll take care of him for you.”
“No! You can’t eat me,” Wolfgang said. “I am a mighty hunter. I am hungry enough to eat a horse.”
“Now that’s just ridiculous.” The raccoon rolled his eyes. “Go back to your web, Wolfy.”
Trix sighed. That nickname could have been cute if the spider wasn’t so annoying .
“Wolf spiders don’t make webs,” Wolfgang scoffed. “We make burrows underground and plug up our nests with pebbles when it gets too wet.”
“Shouldn’t you be doing that instead of bothering Trix?” Pendragon asked, puzzled.
This gave the spider pause. “Well,” he said. “My nest was destroyed. I haven’t found a new spot with enough tasty food nearby. I thought I’d find big prey that I could snack on for a long time.”
Garbanzo and Pendragon looked at each other again and burst out laughing.
“Let’s help you find a nice, new burrow far away from the barn,” Trix suggested. She ignored the others’ laughter. She trotted to the pasture gate and unlocked it with her teeth. “Come on.”
Wolfgang and Pendragon rode on Garbanzo’s back. The spider sat on the raccoon’s head, eyeing the lizard nervously.
They stopped at the pond where lots of birds were swooping around to catch insects.
“No,” said Wolfgang. “Too wet. Birds and lizards are my enemies.” He glanced at Pendragon. “No offense.”
“None taken,” Pendragon said.
They stopped at an open, grassy field where humans often came to play sports.
Wolfgang shook his head. “No, too dry. Too many stomping feet.”
Next, they went to a garden full of plants and tons of insects — ants, butterflies, mosquitoes, grasshoppers, gnats, june beetles, cicadas, leafhoppers, bees, and crickets galore!
“Hmm, this looks all right, I guess,” Wolfgang said grudgingly. He scuttled off toward a patch of dirt and began quickly digging.
Trix gathered sticks in her mouth to strengthen the roof. Garbanzo searched for pebbles that Wolfgang could roll across the entrance if it got too rainy.
Pendragon unhelpfully snapped up a few crickets. He wandered back to his rock to take in the warm sunlight to digest his meal.

The horse, raccoon, and spider stood back to look at their work. It didn’t look too shabby! Now they could add Burrow Architects to the long list of things they’d learned on the farm. And they added wolf spider to the growing farm species list.

options.label: Never trust wolf spiders to help you.
It is important to do things on your own.
Ideas from others can help fix your problems.
If you help others, one day they will help you.

questions.prompt: What is the main message of this story?

options.label: He needs Trix to help him hunt for food on the farm.
He needs food to eat while he looks for a new home.
He needs a horse's meat to make him the strongest spider.
He wants to scare Trix into taking him far away from the farm.

How do Trix's feelings about Wolfgang change from the beginning to the end of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Wolfgang lost his home and is worried about getting enough food. Think about a time you were worried or afraid. How did you respond in the situation? What would you do if you lost your home?

Even though Wolfgang is small, he acts ferocious in front of Trix and her friends. Think of a time you acted brave but you actually felt scared on the inside. What were you afraid of? Why did you act brave? In what other situations might people act brave but feel scared on the inside?
Even though Wolfgang tries to eat Trix in the beginning of the story, Trix still chooses to help him by the end of the story. Do you think Trix should have helped Wolfgang? Why or why not?

articles.title: The Sign of the Cat
articles.author: Sandra Havriluk
articles.description: A boy's grandmother opens their home to those in need.
articles.intro: Sandra Havriluk has written for Highlights . In this short story, a boy name Chet sees his grandmother open their home to people who are in need during the Great Depression. The Great Depression was a time period in America during the 1930s. People had a hard time finding jobs and making money.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Chet feels about their guests and why.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “Chet, pass the fried chicken to our guests,” Grandma said.

My eyes lingered on the plumper pieces. I knew that after our “guests” served themselves, only the scrawniest parts would be left. Guests like these men knocked on our door almost every day, asking for handouts. They were hopping trains, looking for jobs.
Everyone called them “askers” or “hoboes,” but not Grandma. She’d remind me, “If your dad hadn’t gotten the job at Uncle Will’s shop in Florida, he could be riding the rails, too.”
Not my dad! He dressed in neatly pressed clothes. His razor-clean face smelled like Burma-Shave. These men wore stained, wrinkled clothes and had scraggly beards.

Ray, Tony, and Sal had been the ones to knock on our door tonight. It felt strange to call grown-ups by their first names, but Tony explained, “No need for last names in Hooverville.” People blamed President Hoover for our country’s hard times, so “Hooverville” is what they called the places where hoboes set up camp.
“Mmm-mmm.” Ray passed the chicken plate to me. Only a small drumstick and a tiny wing were left.
“Your turn, Chet.” Grandma smiled.

Her eyes weren’t smiling, though. Worry weighed down on her like a wet woolen shawl. The days were getting even tougher. Dad sent less money each month. We bartered eggs and milk for flour and sugar. When I outgrew my shirts, Grandma sewed me new ones using feed sacks.

Staring at the food, I thought of one way I could try to cheer her up. I put the drumstick onto Grandma’s plate and put the tiny wing on mine. “I filled up on apples I picked today,” I said, hoping she couldn’t hear my stomach growl.
She smiled again, and this time so did her eyes.
Over a dessert of apple pie, Grandma invited the men to bunk on our porch.
Pie stuck in my throat. Now we’d have to share breakfast, too! I’d be lucky to get a spoonful of egg or half a biscuit.
Grandma patted my shoulder. “Fetch some covers for them, Chet.”
I snatched quilts from the chest and marched to the porch. Tony and Ray were playing cards with a tattered deck. Sal was whittling.
“Ever whittled, son?” he asked.
“No, sir, I ain’t.”
“You haven’t,” Ray corrected me. Seeing my surprise, he winked. “I may not look like it, but I used to be a college professor.”

Sal smiled. “Watch and learn.” He shaved bits and pieces from the chunk of wood until a cat formed. He handed it to me. “Your gatepost is marked with the sign of a cat. It means the kind lady of the house won’t turn you away.”
That’s why so many askers knocked on our door! An idea flashed in my head. “What’s a sign that would make people stay away?” I asked.
Tony slashed three diagonal lines through the air. “It means it’s not a safe place.”

After our guests left in the morning, I checked the gatepost. On the bottom slat, a tiny cat drawn in black coal stared at me. I scrubbed it off with Grandma’s detergent, then drew the symbol Tony had shown me.
That night, Grandma and I ate alone. I stuffed myself with sliced ham and buttermilk corn bread, trying not to think about askers going hungry.

When I crawled into bed, I had a hard time falling asleep. I’d eaten too much, and my thoughts kept returning to the sign on the gatepost. Part of me wanted to go outside and scrub it off. But I finally drifted to sleep.
“Chet.”
A deep voice interrupted my dreams. I cracked open an eye. Moonlight was streaming through my window.
“Son.”
I bolted up. Even with his beard and tattered clothing, I recognized him. “Dad! You’re back!”
“Yes-siree.” He mussed my hair. “Uncle Will’s shop closed. I hopped a dozen trains to get back home.”

Soon we were digging into a midnight snack of Grandma’s flapjacks. Showered and shaved, Dad looked like himself again. I wondered how many doors he had knocked on asking for food, the way Tony, Ray, and Sal had.
“What’ve you got there, Chet?” Dad pointed to Sal’s wooden cat sitting by my plate.
“A guest whittled it for me,” I said, picking it up. I explained about the sign of the cat, feeling guilt wash over me again.
Dad nodded, looking at me closely. “I kept an eye out for those signs, too.” Had he seen the symbol on our gatepost?
When the sun came up, I grabbed the detergent and did what I’d already known I had to do. Soon the sign of the cat again announced a kind lady’s welcome. And this time, mine, too.

options.label: Not everyone can help others with food and lodging.
It's important to be kind and share what you can with others.
Wealthy people should help those who are in need.
Don't judge a person based solely on their appearance.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the short story?

"We bartered eggs and milk for flour and sugar. When I outgrew my shirts, Grandma sewed me new ones using feed sacks." (Paragraph 8)
"Seeing my surprise, he winked. 'I may not look like it, but I used to be a college professor.'" (Paragraph 17)
"I grabbed the detergent and did what I'd already known I had to do. Soon the sign of the cat again announced a kind lady's welcome. And this time, mine, too." (Paragraph 33)

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Chet originally doesn't want people to come to house for food or shelter. How do his views on this change? Do you think this is an example of Chet growing up? Why or why not?
Have you ever offered help to someone in need? If so, how did it make you feel? Why is it a good thing to help others?

articles.title: Trix Versus the Giant Sky Raisin (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: A miniature horse learns about the important work that drones do to protect endangered animals.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. In this story, a miniature, or very small, horse named Trix discovers something strange on the farm where she lives.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Trix's feelings throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Trix Versus the Giant Sky Raisin (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, they’re exploring a mysterious buzzing noise from the sky.
Trix stood in a field minding her own business. She chewed on grass and closed her eyes against the bright noon sun. She shook her head and flipped her glossy, black mane.
Buzzzzzzzzzzzzzz.
A loud buzzing noise like a swarm of bees approached the paddock.
Trix snorted and dropped the mouthful of grass. She opened her eyes to see a whirring thing with wings and a beak. It looked like a bird, but it didn’t sound like a bird.
The thing zoomed overhead and hovered in front of Trix’s face. Then it blinked at her.
A shrieking battle cry came from one of the skyscrapers above. It was Sassy the red-tailed hawk!
The fierce hawk protected the farm and everyone who lived there. Her talons were like eight gleaming knives. She tore into the whirring, buzzing thing.
It gave up right away and fell to the ground.

Trix approached it with caution. She’d seen many different kinds of insects before. The sound it made reminded her of what she and the other animals called “sky raisins.” They were tiny black flies that bit her where she couldn’t reach and left itchy sores. Pendragon and the chickens loved to eat them as a snack. But this was much larger than a sky raisin. If it was a sky raisin, it was a giant one! She flicked it with her tail just to make sure it was dead.
Sassy perched on the fence nearby. “I have defended you, my lady.”
“Thank you, Sassy,” Trix said.
Trix sniffed the Giant Sky Raisin. It didn’t smell like a biting bug. “What is it? It looks like a hummingbird, but it’s definitely not one.”
“That’s a drone,” said a voice. It came from the tall, wavy milkweed. The plant bloomed a soft pink at the edge of the pasture.

“Hello there. Welcome to the farm,” Trix said politely. Sitting atop the milkweed was a bright orange butterfly. She’d seen it on the “RED” list at the barn. That meant the butterfly’s species, or group of the same kind, was protected by the law. The butterfly was definitely welcome in Central Acre Park.
“Who are you?” Sassy asked curiously.

“I’m Mariel, una mariposa monarca — a monarch butterfly,” said the insect. “I spend my winters in México and my summers in the United States. My family and I are part of an endangered species. That means there aren’t many of us left. Humans protect us as much as they can. But other humans sometimes destroy our habitats, or homes. Along with the milkweed, or algodoncillo .”
“That’s terrible!” Trix exclaimed. “What’s so special about milkweed?
“We lay our eggs on it to feed las orugas — the caterpillars. It’s called a ‘host’ plant, and it has lots of yummy milk inside for the caterpillars.”
“How do you know about this thing?” Trix asked. She nudged the Giant Sky Raisin with her front hoof. “What did you call it? A drone?”
“I call it dreadful,” said Sassy from her perch on the fence.

“ Puedo ayudar — I can help.” Mariel fluttered to another milkweed plant. “ No son pájaros ni bichos — drones aren’t birds or bugs. They’re flying machines. Humans use them to study us and find a way to help us.”

“I’ve seen them flying in the city parks,” Sassy piped up. “But they don’t follow me . Red-tailed hawks aren’t endangered. One of my friends, a plover, says they follow her all over. Her species is endangered, too, just like monarca here. When they find her, humans come and put up fences to keep people away from her home.”
“ Sí ,” Mariel agreed. “Yes, they find where we live and protect our habitats.”
Trix thought this information over. “The humans must be trying to fix their mistakes by helping endangered species. I guess we should fix the Giant Sky Raisin and get it back to them.”

With Sassy’s help, Trix took the Giant Sky Raisin into the barn and laid out the pieces. Garbanzo the raccoon took notes on the wall of the barn. Because Trix was watching, he couldn’t pretend to take notes like he usually did.

Trix studied all of the parts. There were so many! She decided she would first have to make a list before trying to put them back together. As Garbanzo took notes, a nosy barn cat named Chewy corrected each item on the list. He did so while taking a long tongue bath.
1) Small squares covered in shiny metal
“Microchips for a tiny computer,” said Chewy.
2) Stringy noodles the farm assistants eat when the sun was high in the sky
“Wires,” said Chewy.
3) Power source that makes the thing work
“Rechargeable battery,” said Chewy.
4) Swirly buzzies inside and out that allow for flight
“Propellers and a motor,” said Chewy.
5) Bird costume
“Waterproof plastic casing,” said Chewy.
6) Blinking eye
“Camera,” said Chewy.
Trix ignored the cat and mumbled to herself. “But how do the humans control it from the ground?”
“Remote control,” said Chewy. “There’s a joystick on it so they can tell it where to go. They can tell the drone how high to fly and in what direction.”
Trix shot him a sharp look. “Like when the farm assistants turn on the television from far away?”
Chewy nodded. “Sort of. Television remotes use infrared light to send to a transmitter inside the TV. The drone remote uses radio control.”
“Like the music box,” Trix muttered.
“Radio,” corrected the cat.
“How do you know about this stuff?”
Chewy preened. “One of the farm assistants studies here on her break. She’s studying to be a mechanical engineer. Sometimes, I read her notes. Most of the time, I take a nap on her computer.”
“And you remember all of that?” Garbanzo asked. The raccoon had a perfect memory but highly doubted the cat did. He must be playing a trick on them.
Chewy smiled slyly and left the barn with a flick of his tail.
Trix spent hours moving around parts to try to fix the Giant Sky Raisin. She couldn’t quite figure out how it fit together.
Garbanzo picked up a propeller and lazily spun it on his finger. “How’s it going, Trixy?”
Trix didn’t answer. Instead, she muttered softly to herself. Her research assistant shrugged and wandered into the tack room for a nap.

The miniature horse flipped the parts over so that the Giant Sky Raisin lay on its back. The words on its belly were a little scrambled. She slowly read it out loud. “If found, please call Rosie Martinez.” Below that, there was a string of numbers that made no sense to her. There was also a picture of a cheerful flower. Maybe it was a code? She studied the numbers, letters, and flower for a long time.

Finally, Trix decided to ask for help. She dragged the pieces on a saddle blanket back outside. She would see if Mariel knew how to fix it. The monarch butterfly identified the flower as a maravilla , or marigold. Before Trix could ask anything else, the loud voice of a human child startled her. Trix decided to hide behind a nearby tree and observe. She perked up her ears to listen better.
“Mama, Mama, Mama!” the voice squealed. “I found it!”
A little girl ran to the pile of useless plastic and metal. She picked up a large piece and turned it over in her hands. She grinned down at it with glee.

With nimble fingers, the girl quickly put the plastic propellers back in place. Snap, snap, snap . She threaded the wires in a complicated pattern. She pulled out something from her pocket. It sparked when she touched it to the metal chips. She pulled a small black box out of her pocket and pressed a few buttons. The Giant Sky Raisin sputtered and came to life. Buzzzzzzzzzzz .
Sassy landed on a nearby tree branch. She narrowed her eyes. “My lady, do you need me?”
“I’m alright, Sassy,” Trix chuckled. “It looks like Rosie Martinez is going to try to save Mariel and her friends. Maybe we should get ourselves a drone, too.”
“Oh dear, I hope not,” said the hawk.

articles.title: Ribburta and the Mighty Mysterious Squirrel Affair
articles.author: Joan Lennon
articles.description: Two friends work together to outsmart a thieving fortune teller.
articles.intro: In this story by Joan Lennon, a frog named Ribburta and her squirrel friend Redd meet a fortuneteller.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Ribburta says.
articles.permissions: "Ribburta and the Mighty Mysterious Squirrel Affair" by Joan Lennon, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. blocks.text: Ribburta the frog’s best friend was a squirrel named Redd. One day, as Ribburta was sitting in the long grass by the pond, soaking up the autumn sunshine, Redd came bounding over.
“It’s so exciting!” he squeaked, as he ran around in circles and jumped over her head.
“What’s so exciting?” asked Ribburta. “Redd! Calm down and TELL ME!”
“Well!” he said. “You know the hollow log, halfway between my tree and your pond? Guess who’s moved into it? A fortuneteller, that’s who! Her name is Delores Lee Happy and she can see the future!”

Not everybody knows this, but squirrels are crazy about fortunetellers. There’s just something about dangly earrings, a floaty scarf, and a crystal ball that makes all the squirrels within ten miles flap their fluffy tails and cry, “Me first!”
Ribburta didn’t really believe in fortunetellers, but she didn’t want to hurt her friend’s feelings. So when he said, “Let’s go and see her!” she said, “O.K.!”

It wasn’t far to the hollow log. A large crowd of squirrels had gathered to find out about the future. And at this time of the year, they all had the same question: “Have I hidden enough nuts to last the winter?”
Standing in front of the log was a muscle-bound guard squirrel. He glared at Redd and Ribburta. “No kids,” he grunted. “And definitely no frogs.”
Just then Mrs. Hazel came out of the log and blinked in the bright sunlight.
“Send in the next one, Reginald!” called a mystical voice.
The crowd went, “Ooooo!” and Mr. Oak rushed in for his turn.
“What was it like?” Redd asked Mrs. Hazel, his tail shaking with excitement.
Mrs. Hazel looked puzzled. “You know, I don’t quite remember. Though I’m pretty sure we talked about nuts.” Then she wandered off.
“Weird!” said Ribburta. “What do you think goes on in there?”
“I don’t know,” said Redd. “But I know how to find out!” He dragged Ribburta around to the side of the log. “Look — there’s a hole,” he whispered. “We can see in!”
They peered inside the log. There, wearing a floaty scarf and dangly earrings, was Delores Lee Happy. She waved her paws mystically.
“Look deep, deep into the Crystal Ball,” she murmured. “Deep...deep…”
And as she fluttered her paws, they saw Mr. Oak’s eyes start to glaze over.
“Deep... deep...” he said in a faraway voice.
“When you hear this sound —” Delores gave a long, low-pitched whistle — “you will do as I say....”
“I will do as you say,” said Mr. Oak.
“You will get out of bed and show me where you’ve hidden your store of nuts…”
“My store of nuts,” murmured Mr. Oak.
“When you hear this sound” — she gave a short, high-pitched whistle — “you will go back to bed and remember nothing.”
“WHAT?!” gasped Ribburta and Redd.
“Hey, you kids — scram!”
It was Reginald, the big guard squirrel.
“Yikes!” squeaked Ribburta and Redd — and quick as a blink, they scrammed, all the way back to Ribburta’s pond.
“This is awful!” cried Ribburta. She threw herself down in the long grass, panting. “We’ve got to tell somebody!”
But Redd shook his head. “Nobody would believe us.”
“Then we’ll just have to stop her!” declared Ribburta. You could almost hear her froggy brain going into overdrive.
“I know! We’ll wait until she whistles the squirrels out of their trees — and then we’ll whistle them back home!”
“That’s brilliant!” said Redd. “You’re a genius!”
And immediately the two friends tried to whistle.
PLETHTHTHTH!
All they managed to do was splutter spit!
“I guess it’s my big wide mouth,” said Ribburta.
“I guess it’s my wonky tooth,” said Redd. “My mom says it’ll be fine by the time I’m grown.”
But Ribburta wasn’t listening. She was staring down at the long, green grass. She picked a blade and put it between her thumbs.
“We may not be able to whistle,” she said with a grin, “but I do believe grass can!”
As darkness fell, Delores and Reginald came out of the hollow log.
“Time to get nuts!” she greedily announced, and they puckered up their lips.
The woods were soon filled with the sound of their long, low whistles. Sleepwalking squirrels stumbled out of their nests and, eyes shut tight, headed for their hidden stores of nuts.
“It’s working!” gloated Delores. “We’ll get every last nut!”
But then something spooky happened. From one side and then another, they heard short, high-pitched whistles. Suddenly all the hypnotized squirrels turned around and headed back to bed.
“Reginald! What are you doing?” Delores shrieked.
“It ain’t me!” protested Reginald. “It’s... it must be... Phantom Whistlers!”
The two squirrels looked at each other in horror — and then Reginald ran away as fast as his paws would take him! But Delores was made of stronger stuff.
“No Phantom Whistlers are getting my nuts!” she muttered.
She whistled her longest, lowest whistles all over the woods, but before any hidden nut stores could be revealed, a high-pitched whistle would echo right back.
Delores whistled till her pucker was sore and her throat was dry — and not one nut did she find!
The next morning, the hollow log was empty again. The grown-up squirrels awoke to find themselves strangely weary, with mud on their paws and twigs in their fur. They couldn’t imagine why!
But the Phantom Whistlers knew the answer.

“Though they’d never believe it if we told them!” said Redd the squirrel. And then his eyes glazed over. “Oooo, Ribburta — I can see something — I can see into the future! I can see that, because of our quick thinking and some grass whistles, there will be enough nuts to last the winter!”
“Now that, I can believe!” said Ribburta the frog.

articles.title: Ribburta and the Rootintootin' Highfalutin' Ballet Extravaganza
articles.author: Joan Lennon
articles.description: A frog uses her cleverness to help her family escape from an evil fox.
articles.intro: In this story, Ribburta wants to be a dancer like the other frogs in her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Ribburta’s actions.

blocks.text: Ribburta the frog had a BIG family. She had more family than you could count, even if you lined them up and told them to keep still for an hour.
Mostly, Ribburta liked being part of a big family. But there was one thing about being part of this big family that Ribburta did not like.
And that was... being the only one with little legs.
Ribburta wanted to be a dancer, more than anything in the wet wide world. All her family — even her Grandpa Hopodopolus — had graceful, long, dancer’s legs and could leap into next week.
But not Ribburta.
Great Jumping Jehosaphat, the Dance Master of the Leaping Legs School, was a family friend, and he loved Ribburta. But he didn’t let her join his school — because of her little legs.
So Ribburta sat on a log and sadly watched the dance class.
“Now TENSE ... BOING and TENSE...BING... Steady… ready... LEAP!” said Great Jumping Jehosaphat. “Good work! That’s all for today!”
Ribburta’s family took a bow and smiled, and headed for home.
Ribburta was about to follow, when she thought she heard noises coming from a nearby bush. The noises sounded like evil chuckling.
Don’t be stupid, thought Ribburta. Bushes aren’t evil!
And she hopped home.
The next morning, when Ribburta woke up, there was no one about. They were all at dance practice. Even Grandpa Hopodopolus.
It was very quiet. Then, in the silence, Ribburta heard a strange sound. It got louder. And closer…
It went BOING... “Oh, me!” ...BING... “Oh, my!”.. SPLOTT... “Oh, dear!”...
And there, suddenly, was Great Jumping Jehosaphat. He was all muddy and pale green.

“Ribburta!” he gasped. “Your entire family, even your Grandpa Hopodopolus, has been kidnapped! The evil, cunning fox, Jock, has bagged the lot! He would have got me too, except — ” and here the Dance Master hung his head —“I was so scared I fainted behind a log, and he didn’t notice me.” There were tears in his bulgy eyes.
Ribburta patted his shoulder. “But... why?” she asked.
With trembling hands, the Dance Master held out a poster.
“He’s stolen them — to put on his own show!” Jehosaphat wailed. “And there's nothing we can do about it!”
“What do you mean?” said Ribburta. “Aren’t you going after him?”
The Dance Master looked horrified.
“Go... into the Rootintootin’ Woods!?” he gasped.
Ribburta just stared at him until he threw up his hands.
“All right!” he croaked. “I’ll go!”
“No,” said Ribburta firmly. “We’ll go.”
The Rootintootin’ Woods was a terrifying place, full of spiky bushes and scary sounds and animals who thought of frogs as snacks on legs.
Ribburta and the Dance Master nervously followed Jock the Frog Stealer’s trail deep into the Woods, until…
“What’s that?” Jehosaphat whispered.
Ribburta stopped hopping and listened hard.
There was a sound, up ahead....
“Now TENSE... BOING and TENSE...BING... Steady… ready... LEAP!”
They arrived at a clearing in the trees. There was a big wooden cage and log seats, and… Jock ​​the Fox putting Ribburta’s family through their dance.
“LEAP!” cried Jock. “Higher! Higher!”
Finally he was satisfied, and he shoved Ribburta’s exhausted family into the cage.
Right, thought Ribburta. This is one show that must not go on!
And then she thought, Or maybe it should…
“Come on,” she whispered to Jehosaphat. “We’re going in.”
“What?!” squeaked the Dance Master. “Yikes!”
But Ribburta grabbed his arm and hurried over to the cage.
“RIBBURTA!! JEHOSAPHAT!!” yelled her family. “Get us out of here!”
“Shush!” whispered Ribburta frantically. Luckily, Jock was busy painting signs and didn’t hear. “I can’t get you out, but I think I know how I can save the day!”
As her whole family listened, their eyes grew bigger and bigger.
“Come one! Come all! Jock the Fox’s Rootintootin’ Highfalutin’ Ballet Extravaganza is about to begin!" Jack barked. “Please don’t eat the artistes!"
The crowd was mostly foxes, who are known to enjoy ballet, and Jock was taking in money hand over foxy fist.
“Roll up! Roll up!” he cried with delight. “Welcome to the Greatest Display of Gravity-Defying Flights of Fancy This Side of Your Wildest Dreams!”
He grinned, showing off his sharp white teeth.
“Put your paws together, please, because they’re Toadally Awesome...The Dancing Frogs!”
Ribburta held her breath. This was it!
The Dance Master hopped solemnly forward and smiled nervously. Jock looked surprised but stepped back from the stage.
The Dance Master lifted a twig. It was what he used to give the dancers their directions.
Every eye was fixed on that twig. The Dance Master held it up, paused, and then…
He turned into a crazy windmill! He waved and signaled and thrashed about, looking like a forest in a high wind.
And his dancers did exactly what he asked of them. They BOINGED wildly left and BINGED crazily right. Grandpa Hopodopolus kept leaping into the arms of the smaller frogs and knocking them over.
It was an absolute mess! The audience couldn’t believe their eyes. They started to stomp and boo.
Ribburta yelled, “YOU CALL THIS BALLET? I CALL THIS APPALLING!”
The audience yelled, too. “That’s right! This is rubbish!”
“WE WANT OUR MONEY BACK!” Ribburta screeched.
The foxes began to chant, “Money back! Money back!” until suddenly they realized... Jock and their money had disappeared!
But Ribburta saw him. “Over by the cage!” she cried. “He’s running away — with all your money!”
Shouting loudly, the audience raced off after Jack into the Rootintootin’ Woods.
Only the frogs were left. They looked at Ribburta and smiled huge froggy grins.
“Three cheers for Ribburta —the frog with BIG ideas!” cried the Dance Master.
Every frog in Ribburta’s enormous family cheered loudly three times!
And that’s a lot of cheers.

articles.title: Ribburta and the Run-Ragged Babysitting Adventure
articles.author: Joan Lennon
articles.description: Ribburta's one hundred siblings are a handful to babysit until they save her from a snake.
articles.intro: In this story, Ribburta the frog has to babysit her one hundred new brothers and sisters.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Ribburta’s feelings about her siblings.
articles.permissions: "Ribburta and the Run-Ragged Babysitting Adventure" by Joan Lennon, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “What do you mean — I’ve got one hundred new brothers and sisters?!” cried Ribburta the frog. “I already have more brothers and sisters than I can count!”
But Ribburta’s mum wasn’t listening. She was gazing down at a big ball of frogspawn, floating there in the pond, with a very goopy look in her googly eyes.
“Aren’t they just adorable ?” she cooed. “They look just like you, when you were little.”
Ribburta was absolutely sure she had never looked like that — all wibbly and wobbly and round.
“I’ll need you to help babysit when they’re a bit older,” said Ribburta’s mum.
“O.K.,” said Ribburta. “How hard can it be?”
The one hundred new brothers and sisters stopped being just round blobs very soon, and became more like one hundred little fishes with tiny, squeaky voices.
“Tadpoles are so sweet,” cooed Ribburta’s mum.
“Do you want me to babysit yet?” asked Ribburta.
“When they have legs,” said Ribburta’s mum.
And then, one day, they did. Every last one of them had new, green legs.
“Ribburta, it’s time for you to babysit!” exclaimed Ribburta’s mum.
“Sure,” said Ribburta. “How hard can it be?”
And then she found out.
“RIBBURTA!” squawked the mother ducks. “Tell your brothers and sisters to stop nibbling on our ducklings’ feet!”
“RIBBURTA!” groaned Old Man Turtle. “Tell your brothers and sisters to stop spoiling my nap in the sun with their squeaky songs!”
“RIBBURTA!” splashed the beavers. “Tell your brothers and sisters to stay away from the dam or they’ll be swept downstream!”
One hundred brothers and sisters, in trouble all over the pond — that was how hard it could be! But finally Ribburta managed to get them all together in a shallow bit of the water.
“What is the matter with you?” she scolded them. “Why are you causing all this trouble?”
A chorus of tiny squeaky voices cried:
“BECAUSE WE’RE BORED!”
“BORED!”
“PLAY WITH US, RIBBURTA! PLAY WITH US!”
Ribburta sighed. “All right. What do you want to play?”
“HIDE-AND-SEEK!”
“NO!” cried Ribburta, but she was too late. Every last one of the little frogs had scattered !

There were little frogs hiding in the pond-weed. There were little frogs hiding under the lily pads. There were little frogs hiding amongst the pebbles. There were little frogs hiding in every nook and cranny of the entire pond.
It took Ribburta FOREVER to find them all, and by the time she’d done that, she was completely, utterly, totally run ragged.

“I’m going to have a rest now,” she said sternly as she lay down in the grass beside the pond. “And I want you all to be good. I don’t want to hear one peep out of any of you — do you understand? NOT ONE PEEP!”
“YES, RIBBURTA,” said the little frogs in the water, but she didn’t hear. She was already fast asleep.
And were they good? Were they quiet? They were, until... they spotted something dangerous slithering toward their babysitter sister, something with supper on his mind.
It was a frog-eating snake!
“RIBBURTA! RIBBURTA! WAKE UP, RIBBURTA!” all the little frogs cried out in their squeaky voices. But Ribburta kept sleeping soundly.
The snake slithered closer.
“RIBBURTA — WATCH OUT! WAKE UP, RIBBURTA — UP!”
Ribburta didn’t stir.
And the snake slithered even closer.
There was only one thing left to do. Ribburta’s one hundred brothers and sisters took one deep breath and all yelled together, “CHARGE!”

The snake had never seen anything like it! Ribburta’s one hundred brothers and sisters used their new green legs to jump out of the pond and all over him! They came at that snake from right and left. Everywhere he looked, there were tiny green frogs popping up and hopping away, under his nose and over his tail and here and there and up and down.

Not having any ears, the snake wasn’t troubled by all the squeaking. But he did have eyes, and they kept crossing wildly — he couldn’t focus on any one frog at a time, and he was starting to get a headache.
“SSSSSTOP HHHHHHOPPING!” he hissed, but one hundred excited little frogs just hopped faster. The snake was feeling dizzy — instead of spots in front of his eyes, he had frogs!
I’m not hungry anymore, thought the snake. I’m getting out of here!
Just then Ribburta woke up. She yawned a big wide yawn and opened her eyes — just in time to see the snake slithering away as fast as his belly would take him.
She leapt to her feet in a panic. But a chorus of tiny, squeaky voices called out:
“DON’T WORRY, RIBBURTA!”
“WE SCARED THE SNAKE AWAY!”
“WE SAVED YOU!”
Ribburta looked down at her brothers and sisters in amazement.
“You saved me? But...I was supposed to be looking after you!”
“YOU ALREADY LOOKED AFTER US, RIBBURTA. THIS TIME IT WAS OUR TURN!”
“IT WAS FUN!”
“YEAH, WE BABYSAT THE BABYSITTER!”
“YOU KNOW WHAT ELSE WOULD BE FUN? TO SIT ON THE BABYSITTER!”
And so they did.
And Ribburta’s run-ragged day ended with one hundred — and one! — happy green frogs in a great big, giggly, wiggly pile.

articles.title: Domino Sundays
articles.author: Vivian Fernandez
articles.description: Yolanda joins her abuelo's domino game for the first time.
articles.intro: Dominoes is a game played with tiles that have a certain number of dots on each end. Tiles are flat, hard pieces that are often rectangular. In this story, Yolanda and her abuelo , the Spanish word for grandfather, are going to the park where he plays dominoes each Sunday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Yolanda’s feelings about dominoes.
articles.permissions: "Domino Sundays" by Vivian Fernandez, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Sunday is domino day for my abuelo at the corner park. One day, maybe I’ll join the game.
Abuelo knocks on my bedroom door. "Ready, Yolanda?” he asks.
“Si!” I say.
Abuelo hands me the long, wooden box that holds the fichas , the domino tiles. "Today is a special day. I have a new partner.”
“Who?” I ask.
“You’ll see.”
I wonder who Abuelo’s new partner is as we walk along the shady sidewalk. Abuelo has been teaching me to play. Could it be me?

Our neighbors are starting their days, too. Salsa music flows from open windows. I smell bread and imagine it pressed flat in a plancha , like Mami makes for me. From someone’s yard, a rooster steps out in front of us. Papi complains about the roosters. He says Miami is a big city. Roosters belong on farms. Abuelo and I don’t mind the roosters. We like the jerky way they move.

Abuelo imitates the rooster, moving his head from side to side. I think about being Abuelo’s partner, helping him to win, and I let out a loud cock-a-doodle-do ! The rooster turns a beady eye at us and crosses the street with an angry squawk.

By the time we reach the park, my bangs are wet from sweat. Abuelo peels the pale blue guayabera from his skin. He pulls a handkerchief from his pants pocket and wipes his forehead. "It was never this hot in Cuba. The weather was — ”
“ — always perfect in Cuba,” I finish for him.
Abuelo taps my nose. “Well, not perfect. But close.”
The square tables are filled with old men playing dominoes. “Where’s your partner?” I ask, looking around.
He puts his hands on my shoulders. “She’s right here.”
I jump up and down. “We’re going to make a great team, Abuelo!”
He takes my hand and leads me to the table with his friends.
“You’re late!” Mr. Perera greets us. He always says that, even when he’s the last one to get there.
“Buenos días , Yolanda,” Mr. Castillo says.
“Buenos días , Señor Castillo.”
Mr. Castillo pours Cuban coffee into a small plastic cup. I breathe in the warm, sweet smell as it passes by me on its way to Abuelo. In one gulp, the coffee is gone.
Mr. Perera checks his watch. “Where’s Juan Carlos?” he asks.
Abuelo takes the box from me and sits down. “Yolanda is my partner today!”
Mr. Perera slaps his hand on the table. “What does this señorita know about dominoes?”
“I know how to play,” I say. "I have a good teacher.” I sit across from Abuelo, in his partner’s chair. My chair.
“I’m sorry, Yolanda, but this is a game just for us old men. I will only play with Juan Carlos.” Mr. Perera stands up and crosses his arms.
“Then you forfeit,” Abuelo says, “and Yolanda and I win.” I try not to smile. We all know Mr. Perera will never forfeit.
Mr. Perera sits back down, grumbling like a summer thunderstorm. “ Está bien . What are you waiting for? Let’s play!”

Abuelo slides off the top of the domino box, then pours the fichas onto the table. We flip all of the fichas facedown. No matter how hot the day is, the tiles always feel cool. They click-clack against one another when we mix them.

Each of us turns over one of the remaining tiles. Mr. Perera’s tile has the most dots. “Ah! I go first,” Mr. Perera says. He slams down a tile — eight dots on one half, six on the other — and the game begins.

Abuelo puts down the second tile, and then Mr. Castillo slides one into place. Now it’s my turn. I need a tile with either nine or four dots. I don’t have either. I tap my knuckles twice on the table — l pass.
Oh no! I do have a tile with four dots. How did I miss it? Abuelo smiles at me from across the table. He doesn’t know I messed up.
The others place their tiles, and then it’s my turn again. Mr. Perera drums his fingers on the table and shakes his head.
“Take your time,” Mr. Castillo says with a smile.
I study my tiles, and then relax, sliding a tile into place.
We keep taking turns, adding tiles or skipping when we have none to play. Soon we’ve covered the table with a maze of fichas .
When we’re down to a few tiles each, Mr. Perera places one with three dots on the open end. I squirm in my seat. If no one adds a tile to it, I can get rid of my last tile!
The three-dotted tile remains open. I connect my last ficha to it and yell out, “Domino!”
None of the other players has tiles they can play. The game is over — Abuelo and I win the round.
Mr. Castillo pats my shoulder and smiles, his tanned face crinkling even more.
Mr. Perera frowns as he counts the dots on his tiles, but says, “Good game, Yolanda.”
Abuelo doesn’t say anything. He just winks at me.
Abuelo jots down Mr. Perrera and Mr. Castillo’s points. We will play until one team reaches 150 points. We flip over all the tiles and shuffle them again.
Sunday is domino day for Abuelo and his friends… and me!

options.label: Be kind to your neighbors.

articles.title: The Golden Sequin
articles.author: Janice Milusich
articles.description: A young boy helps his uncle create elephant costumes for Sri Lanka's Esala Perahera festival.
articles.intro: Sri Lanka is an island country in South Asia that is home to over 22 million people. Buddhism is the major religion, or set of beliefs, followed in Sri Lanka. For hundreds of years Buddhist people there have celebrated Esala Perahera, or The Festival of the Tooth. This festival, or yearly celebration, has a parade to honor the tooth of Buddha, the person who started Buddhism. During the parade, an exact copy of the tooth is carried through the streets by a special elephant. In this story, a young boy named Asiri is excited to help his Uncle Thad prepare for the Esala Perahera by sewing costumes for the elephants. When someone sews, they use a needle and thread to put cloth together.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Asiri’s feelings about helping his uncle.
articles.permissions: "The Golden Sequin" by Janice Milusich, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Asiri woke up early. School was out. Just like on every break, Asiri would go with his uncle to his workshop. His Uncle Thad made costumes for — believe it or not — elephants!

For as long as he could remember, Asiri had been asking to help make the costumes. But each time, he’d been told he was too little and that his fingers weren’t steady or quick enough to sew the fine stitches needed for the elaborate costumes.

As he sipped his tea, Asiri smoothed the square of fabric beside him — his practice piece. Across it he had sewn, pulled out, and resewn stitches just as Uncle Thad had taught him. He couldn’t wait to show it to his uncle.
“Eat,” his mother scolded.
Asiri nibbled at his egg hopper. What’s taking Uncle Thad so long? he wondered.
When his uncle finally came over the hill, Asiri leapt from his seat. “Bye, Mom!” he called as he rushed out the door.
“Look, Uncle,” said Asiri, holding up the stitched cloth.
“These are fine stitches, Asiri,” said Uncle Thad, patting his shoulder. “Straight and small, just right to fix sequins onto velvet.”

Together they walked to the workshop. Along the way, Uncle Thad stopped to greet neighbors and share news with friends. It seemed he knew everyone, and they knew him. By the time he and Asiri arrived at the workshop, the seamstresses were already waiting.

Uncle Thad and his team had stitched for months to make the costumes. They had to be ready for Esala Perahera, the biggest festival held in the city of Kandy, Sri Lanka. It lasted for ten days, with a parade every day. Since the elephants were the stars of the parade, their costumes had to be dazzling. They were made of velvet and embroidered with Kandyan designs. Asiri’s favorite designs were of twin swans and lotus flowers. He especially loved the sequins sewn on them that made them sparkle.

The festival was only two weeks away. Today, Uncle Thad, his team, and now Asiri, would put the finishing touches on the costumes. Asiri hoped this might mean he’d get to sew a sequin onto the Maligawa Tusker’s costume. The Maligawa Tusker was the biggest and most important elephant in the parade.
All morning, Asiri helped the seamstresses. He brought them thread, cloth, and padding and watched as they stitched the designs.
After lunch, Asiri was putting away a bolt of fabric when his uncle called. “Asiri?”
“Yes, Uncle?”
“Please bring me the bin of golden sequins.”

Here was his chance, thought Asiri. Those sequins were for the Maligawa Tusker’s costume. He’d bring the bin to Uncle Thad and then ask to sew one on. Uncle Thad was sure to say yes. Asiri lifted the bin from the shelf and turned a little too quickly. A few sequins flew into the air. Uncle Thad had his back to Asiri, so he hadn’t seen. Thank goodness, thought Asiri. Uncle Thad always said that only careful fingers should move quickly in the workshop. Making the costumes for Esala Perahera took time and patience. Their beauty was not to be rushed.
Asiri eyed the smattering of shiny sequins scattered on the floor. He had to clean them up before Uncle Thad saw them. Asiri hurriedly knelt to set the bin down.

“Ow!” Stubbing his toe on a table leg, Asiri stumbled. The bin tilted. The seamstresses and Uncle Thad turned to stare — first at Asiri and then at the stream of shimmering golden sequins that spilled across the workshop floor.

Uncle Thad’s brow furrowed, and a frown pulled at his face. Asiri felt his cheeks turning red. How could he have been so clumsy? Now, Uncle Thad would never let him sew a single stitch — and certainly not a sequin — on the costumes this year. He might never let him help again.
Asiri dashed out the door, down the street, and all the way back home.
Hearing the rush of his footsteps, Asiri’s mother came to the door. “What’s wrong, Asiri?” she asked.
“I spilled the sequins!” he blurted, almost in tears.
“Did you clean them up?” she asked.
“No.”
“Go back and clean them up,” she said with a wave of her hand. “Uncle Thad will expect you to.”
Asiri shook his head. But he knew it was only right to clean up a mess he’d made. It was one of Uncle Thad’s workshop rules.
“You wanted to help,” said his mother. “Go help.”

As he trudged back toward the workshop, Asiri avoided his neighbors’ glances. He was sure that everyone knew of his carelessness, his clumsiness. When he got to the workshop, Asiri peered in its doorway. Uncle Thad wasn’t there, but the sequins were. They lay scattered across the floor, waiting for him. He’d clean them up and return home. Asiri didn’t want to see the disappointment in his uncle’s face.

The seamstresses busied themselves with their work, while Asiri gathered the sequins into a pile and swept them into a dustpan. Embarrassed, Asiri kept his head down as he poured the sequins into a sieve. Carefully, he shook it, separating the dust from the sequins.

Only when the last sequin slipped into the bin and settled among the others did Asiri glance up. Uncle Thad stood in the doorway with his arms crossed. But instead of a frown, a smile brightened his face.

“I’ve been searching everywhere for you, Asiri. Yet, I find you here.” He chuckled. “I should have known.” Crossing the room, he squeezed Asiri’s shoulder. “Good workers always clean up after themselves.” He clapped his hands. “Now, come. There are costumes to finish and...” He cocked his head toward Asiri and winked. “Sequins to sew.”
Asiri’s smile was wider than his face as he stitched the first golden sequin onto the design decorating the maroon velvet of the Maligawa Tusker’s headpiece.

articles.title: Our Family Treasure Chest
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: A speaker describes their family's tradition of unwrapping ornaments for their Christmas tree.
articles.intro: Jenny Whitehead is a poet and an illustrator of children's book stories and poems. In this poem, a speaker shares a special Christmas tradition.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens in each stanza. A stanza is a group of lines in a poem.
articles.permissions: "Our Family Treasure Chest" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday-Stew: A Kid's Portion of Holiday and Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Around the world, how can it be
That every single Christmas tree
Is decorated differently?
Up the attic stairs we creep,
Down go boxes where we keep
Our ornaments all tucked asleep.
As tissue crinkles, tales unfold
About the treasures that we hold —
The handmade new, the hand-down old.
And though we’ve heard these tales before,
We keep unwrapping to hear more…
We keep unwrapping to hear more.

If students do not feel comfortable sharing, they can research and present on the holiday customs of others.

What memories do they remind you of? Do you think it is important for people to hold on to objects that remind them of special times? Why or why not?

articles.title: All Saints' Day at Night
articles.author: Linda Rae Apolzon
articles.description: A boy visits a cemetery at night to celebrate All Saints Day.
articles.intro: Linda Rae Apolzon is a children's author who has written for Highlights . In this short story, a boy celebrates All Saints' Day in Poland. All Saints' Day is a Roman Catholic holiday celebrated on November 1st. In Poland, it is traditional for people to leave candles at the graves of family members who have passed away.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Tomek feels about All Saints Day.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: The last place Tomek wanted to visit was a dark cemetery. Yet here he was, zipping up his coat, getting ready to go.

“But we already went today,” Tomek said. Earlier, Tomek and his family had taken flowers and candles to his grandfather’s grave for All Saints’ Day. The cemetery had been filled with others doing the same thing. A priest had said that all over Poland families were remembering those who had died.
“We go again after dinner,” Mama said now.
“Is there a problem?” Tata asked. How could Tomek tell his father that he was afraid to go into the cemetery at night?
“I’m coming!” sang Babcia, his grandmother.
Mama whispered to Tomek, “You know this means a lot to Babcia.”

Babcia entered the room and smiled at Tomek. “I want you to be warm,” she said wrapping a red scarf around his neck. It smelled like her perfume. Babcia was always nice to him. Mama was right — Babcia would want him to be there.
“How about you walk with me, Tomek,” said Aunt Dorota. She took his hand, and they went outside.
All the way to the cemetery, Tomek kept his eyes down. The light from houses helped them see their way. They’d be at the dark cemetery soon enough.
“Can you remember your grandfather?” asked Aunt Dorota.
“No,” said Tomek without looking up. Aunt Dorota sighed. “He was so much fun!”
Tomek wanted to hear more. After all, he was named after his grandfather. “He was fun?”
“He did magic tricks,” said Aunt Dorota. “He would make a coin disappear and then find it in your pocket.”
Tomek smiled. He watched his feet: left, right, left, right. Aunt Dorota chatted on about his grandfather. Then their path turned. They were at the cemetery. He just knew it.
But it did not get dark! In fact, it seemed to be getting lighter.
Slowly, Tomek raised his eyes. Candles flickered everywhere! The gravestones were covered with burning candles — candles in little jars or tall glasses, big pots of candles with several flames.
Together they filled the cemetery with brilliant light.
Tomek blinked. As if in a dream, he followed his parents. Families nodded and greeted one another in hushed voices.

Tomek looked at this grandfather’s grave, where Babcia was bowing her head in prayer. When she opened her eyes and saw Tomek, she smiled and beckoned to him. “He was very proud that you had his name,” she said.
Tomek stood next to Babcia, and she hugged him. Then his smaller hand took her larger one, and they left the cemetery together.
When they arrived at the house, Tomek turned to look back at the cemetery. It glowed under the night sky.
“Isn’t it beautiful?” said Babcia.
Tomek nodded. “I like All Saints’ Day. Especially at night.”

options.label: You never truly recover after losing someone you love.
It's a family member's job to comfort you when you're scared.
Sometimes you have to do things you don't like for your family.
It brings families together to remember people they have lost.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the short story?

"Tomek stood next to Babcia, and she hugged him. Then his smaller hand took her larger one, and they left the cemetery together." (Paragraph 20)
