articles.title: Kevin's Big Adventure
articles.author: Kris Bitar
articles.description: A dog realizes the importance of rules after getting lost.
articles.intro: Kris Bitar is a writer and teacher in Aberdeen, Washington. She has taught every level in K-12 for 30 years and her joy is working with children. In this short story, a dog named Kevin visits his grandma's house and goes on a surprise adventure when he decides to follow his nose.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Kevin’s thoughts about Ashley’s rules.
articles.permissions: "Kevin's Big Adventure" by Kris Bitar. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: “Oh, boy!” thought Kevin. The small black and white Pomeranian dog with a missing front tooth was loaded inside the car. “I get to go on a car ride with Ashley. I love car rides! I hope wherever we are going has treats.” Kevin bounced around the car like a wrecking ball.
“Be careful, Kevin!” said Ashley. “You’re jumping on my homework!”

Kevin didn’t see any reason to stop jumping around in the car. He was too excited. Ashley even had the windows open — he loved that! Kevin accidentally put a wet, dirty paw print on Ashley’s homework.
“Kevin!” said Ashley. “Calm down or you won’t be able to go next time.”

Kevin paid no mind and continued jumping from window to window to see outside. He could smell all the wonderful smells in the air. Like the neighbor’s barbecue, fresh-cut grass, and other dogs in their neighborhood. Kevin knew to sit down in the car, but he was just too excited.

Ashley pulled into her parents’ driveway. “Jackpot!” thought Kevin. He could smell fresh baked sugar cookies coming from the house. Kevin jumped from the front seat to the back seat and back to the front seat. He wouldn’t calm down enough so Ashley could pick him up.
“Stay in the car, Kevin, while I bring in the laundry,” commanded Ashley. She opened the car door and stepped out. “Then I’ll come back for you.”
But Kevin was too fast. He bolted out of the car and ran to the front door. Ashley followed behind him with a large, white laundry basket.
After unlocking the door, Ashley went inside and put down the basket. Kevin ran around her in a circle, then he ran around the living room. He barked happily the whole time.
“Please stop barking, Kevin!” said Ashley.
Kevin barked again. He wasn’t watching where he was going, and he ran into the laundry basket. It tipped over, and Ashley’s clothes spilled onto the floor.
Ashley sighed. “Kevin, you are like an unruly child,” she said. “You need to learn to do what I ask.”
But Kevin wasn’t listening. His nose was picking up the strong scent of butter, vanilla, and warm cookies. It was going to be a marvelous day, Kevin decided.

Kevin loved Ashley’s mom. Everyone said Kevin was like her grandson, even though he was a dog. He felt like a king at Grandma’s house. He ate part of a wonderful, soft sugar cookie. He was supposed to get only a bite, but half the cookie broke off and fell to the floor. Kevin moved quicker than Grandma to get it.
“Kevin is being quite a rascal today,” Ashley told her mom. “He just isn’t listening as he should.”
Kevin heard Ashley but didn’t care. His tummy was so full and warm. All he wanted now was a nap.

Grandma brought Kevin a fuzzy, baby blue dog bed. Kevin spotted it immediately, and off he went for a snooze. Kevin climbed in and made three trips around in a circle before settling down. He stretched out his full belly, rubbed his face into the soft bed, and closed his eyes.

Some time later, Kevin woke up to the smell of a steak cooking on the grill. He decided to explore. Kevin’s nose led him to the front door. He realized Ashley had forgotten to close the door. She had probably been too distracted by Kevin running around and knocking over the laundry basket to remember.

Kevin stuck his head out of the door and breathed in a big whiff of steak. Kevin knew he shouldn’t leave the house. Ashley had a rule that he couldn’t leave the house alone. But it smelled so yummy! He stepped outside. “I’ll just go to the edge of the yard,” thought Kevin.
Here, the smell was even stronger. “I’ll just go a little farther,” decided Kevin.

The smell was so delicious. Kevin decided to walk to the neighbor’s house and see if they would give him a little bite. “Hmm,” thought Kevin, “I don’t see anyone cooking.” He thought it must be farther down the street.

Kevin moved as quickly as his furry, little legs would carry him, but he still didn’t see anyone cooking delicious food. “Maybe it’s around this corner,” thought Kevin. He continued his adventure down the street.
Kevin walked and smelled some more. He couldn’t find anything! He kept walking and smelling.

Eventually, Kevin stopped and looked around. He looked to the right, to the left, and behind him. He didn’t know where he was! He spun around, but that just confused him even more. Kevin trembled. He didn’t know what to do. He needed Ashley and Grandma, but they were nowhere to be seen. He remembered Ashley’s rule. This was why he was not to leave the house without her. Kevin decided he needed to start listening to Ashley, whom he missed very much.
Kevin scampered over to a nearby tree to sit and figure out what to do. The tree’s shade was nice and cool, and he was starting to get thirsty.

It was then that he heard voices coming toward him. He stood up on all four legs, but it wasn’t Ashley or Grandma. Frightened to meet strangers, Kevin left the cool shade of the tree and walked a little more. Kevin knew he had been gone a while because his tummy was grumbling. It must be close to dinner time.

All of a sudden, a large tabby cat jumped out in front of Kevin, It hissed and ran past him, scaring him. Kevin wished he never left Grandma’s house that smelled like warm sugar cookies! He wished for his soft, baby blue bed. He wished he could tell Ashley he was sorry for not following the rules. He wanted her to hold him and call him a good boy.
Near the sidewalk where Kevin had been walking, a car pulled up to a stop sign. The door opened, and a lady said, “What are you doing out here? You must be lost!”

Kevin was scooped up and placed in a cool, air-conditioned car. He was still scared, but he was happy to be away from the heat. The lady talked to him like Ashley did. She also gave him a snuggle, which Kevin enjoyed.

They pulled up to a house Kevin didn’t recognize. The lady brought him inside and gave him a blanket. She took his picture, and Kevin — forgetting he was lost — smiled. He remembered all of the pictures Ashley took of him. He loved the attention!

Thinking about Ashley and how much she must be missing him made Kevin sad again. “Who will Ashley give treats to?” wondered Kevin. “Who will she call a good boy now?” Kevin couldn’t eat the food or drink the water the lady gave him. He wished he had never wandered away. He was scared he would never get to go to Grandma’s house again. He fell asleep, exhausted and afraid.

The next morning, the lady put Kevin in the car. They drove for a bit, and he wondered if he was heading home. Instead, he found himself at the vet. “Oh, no,” thought Kevin. “Now I have to get another shot.”
Instead, the vet brought out a little scanner and held it over Kevin. “Yep,” said the vet, “there’s an id chip.” Kevin didn’t know if that was good or bad.

After the vet, the lady drove him to somewhere that smelled like the coffee shop Ashley liked to visit. He smelled chocolate, vanilla, and other yummy scents. The lady gave him a little cup with whipped cream in it. Kevin gobbled it up, and his tummy felt good. They continued driving, and Kevin continued to worry that he would never return home.
Soon, he was being scooped out of the car again. To Kevin’s surprise, his family ran out to greet him. Ashley hugged him tight, and Grandma gave him a bite of a cookie. He had made it home!

As the lady was leaving, Kevin licked her hand. He was thankful she took care of him. Kevin wanted another cookie and a big nap. It was good to be home, but now Kevin understood why following Ashley’s rules was a good thing. Kevin’s big adventure had come to an end.

articles.title: Hearing Hoofbeats
articles.author: Pamela Love
articles.description: A young girl learns an important lesson about taking care of the horses on her family's farm.
articles.intro: In this short story, a young girl learns an important lesson about taking care of horses.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Grace’s actions in the story.
articles.permissions: "Hearing Hoofbeats" by Pamela Love. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Grace was going to stay with her Aunt Lucy and Uncle Frank while her parents were away on a business trip. She had jumped up and down when she first heard the news. Aunt Lucy and Uncle Frank owned a farm and Grace loved horses more than anything else. The farm now had three, since the horse Rose had given birth to a new foal, Buddy. “Dad, please take me to the library,” Grace said. “I want to read about horses since I’m going to be around them.”
There was so much to learn. Different breeds of horses, different colors of horses, how to ride one — oh, how she hoped her aunt and uncle would let her!
Grace tried to memorize the parts of a horse’s body. She giggled when she found out part of the horse’s hoof was called a frog.

When she got to the farm, after saying a quick goodbye to her folks, Grace pulled her suitcase (which held two horse books besides her clothes) to the guest room. Then Aunt Lucy told her, “Go call your uncle in for dinner. He’s in the paddock, putting a halter on Buddy.”

Thrilled to see the gray foal, Grace scrambled over the fence and dashed up behind Buddy and his mother. Startled, Rose kicked. Only Uncle Frank’s yank on Grace’s arm saved her from the mare’s hooves.
“You’re old enough to know better than that. It’s not safe. Keep away from the horses until I tell you otherwise,” Uncle Frank said sternly.

The mare flattened her ears against her head and let out a high whinny. Much more gently, Uncle Frank went on, “Easy there, Rose. It’s okay. Nobody’s going to hurt you or Buddy.” He stroked her brown neck.

“I’m sorry.” Grace looked down. She did know better. Hadn’t she read that horses got scared when anything ran up behind them? How could she have forgotten that? It was a lot more important than what a horse’s frog was. Forgetting all about dinner, she trudged back to the house.
Aunt Lucy took one look at Grace and put down a bowl of spaghetti. “What happened?”
Grace explained. “I know I made a mistake, but I’ll never do it again, I promise. And I’m not hurt.”
“You might have been,” said Uncle Frank, walking in. “And what if Rose had accidentally kicked Buddy instead?”
“I’m really sorry,” Grace repeated.
Uncle Frank folded his arms. “I’d never forgive myself if you got hurt. Horses are wonderful animals, but they can be dangerous if you don’t use your head.”
So for the next few days, Grace did other things around the farm, like picking strawberries and feeding the chickens. She had a good time, but it wasn’t the same as helping with the horses.

Her guest room was right outside the paddock. Whenever she had the chance, Grace would slide up the window and lean on the sill, watching and listening. Rose is rolling. That’s how horses scratch their backs. The flies must be bothering Mac — he’s switching his tail to shoo them away. Buddy is galloping like he wants to be a racehorse. Oh, I’m so sorry I messed up.
One morning, Grace woke up and found a note on the kitchen table from Aunt Lucy. “Went to the supermarket. Back soon. Don’t wake your uncle. He was up late working last night.”

After eating a bowl of cereal, Grace headed back to her room. She slid open the window as usual. But this time, she shut her eyes. She wondered if she could tell which horse was which without looking, just by the sound of their hoofbeats.
Clip, clop, clip, clop. That had to be the big black and white pinto, Mac, walking slowly by.
Tap-tap-tap, tap-tap-tap. Those light hoofbeats belonged to Buddy.
Rose would come next. She was never far from her foal. But a few minutes passed before Grace heard thump…th-thump…thump. It wasn’t the “trit trot, trit trot” she’d expected.

Grace opened her eyes and peeked around the blue curtain. It was Rose whose hoofbeats sounded strange. Is she all right? I think she’s limping. Grace couldn’t decide whether to get her uncle. What if Rose needs help now? she asked herself. But she also thought Uncle Frank might be angry if she woke him up. Maybe it would be better to wait for Aunt Lucy to come back.

Worried, she watched Rose for a few seconds more. She’s walking like her hoof is sore. But why? Hooves are very tough. Then Grace gasped. The bottom of a hoof has a soft spot — the frog! She remembered something from one of her books — sometimes things get caught in the frog, and it can cause a serious injury if it’s not taken out right away.
Grace hurried down the hall. “Uncle Frank!” She knocked hard on his bedroom door.
“What’s wrong?” he grumbled.
“Rose is limping. I think she’s hurt!”

Five minutes later her uncle was holding Rose by the halter. “You’re right, Grace. Looks like there’s a rock in her frog. To take it out, I’m going to need my hoof pick. Can you bring it to me? It’s on the bottom shelf by the stable door. I’d get it myself, but I’ve got to keep Rose still.”

Grace nodded and soon returned. Uncle Frank crooned to Rose while he removed a jagged-edged pebble. “Good work, Grace. Thanks for getting me right away. It could have been much worse if you’d waited. I think Rose will be okay now, but I’ll call the vet to make sure.”
Smiling with relief, Grace said, “I’m glad.”

“You know, someone who uses her head can be a real help on this farm. Tonight, we’ll feed the horses together.” Uncle Frank grinned. “After the vet stops by, you can help me saddle Mac and take him for a ride.”
Grace smiled again. “I’d like that,” she said.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In "Hearing Hoofbeats," Grace is excited to visit her family's farm because she loves animals, especially horses. Think about a time you were excited to visit a place. Where was it, and why were you looking forward to going there? How did you prepare for your visit? Did you learn all about the place before visiting, like Grace learns about horses?

In "Hearing Hoofbeats," Grace has to make a quick decision to help Rose when she notices she is hurt. Have you ever had to make a split-second decision about something? What was it and what did you do? How did your decision affect the situation? You can use your own experience or the experience of a character from a book, history, or movie.

Some people would say Grace's actions in "Hearing Hoofbeats" are an example of "going with your gut." What do you think this expression means? Have you ever been in a situation where you have had to "go with your gut," like Grace does in the story? What was it and what did you do? How did you know you made the right decision?

In "Hearing Hoofbeats," Grace has to take care of an animal. Have you ever had to take care of an animal? If so, what was the animal and how did you prepare to take care of it? If not, how would you prepare to take care of an animal for a friend or family member?

articles.title: Coquí
articles.author: Carmen T. Bernier-Grand
articles.description: Coquí the tree frog includes his brother in his namesake song.
articles.intro: Carmen T. Bernier-Grand is a Puerto Rican author and poet. This poem is about a coquí, a very small tree frog found in Puerto Rico. Coquí make the sound they are named after: "KO" and "KEE."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Coquí does.
articles.permissions: "Coquí" by Carmen T. Bernier Grand, from Book Links. Copyright © 2013 by Carmen T. Bernier Grand. Reprinted by permission of Carmen T. Bernier Grand.

blocks.text: One tiny tree frog
with big eyes
sings happily,
“Kokee! Kokee!”
His brother comes to bother.
Coquí doesn’t push him.
Coquí doesn’t bite him.
Coquí tells him,
“Kokee-Kee! Kokee-Kee!”
Two tiny tree frogs
with big eyes
sing happily,
“Kokee! Kokee!”

The coquí is the unofficial national symbol of Puerto Rico. Based on the poem, why do you think the people of Puerto Rico may consider this animal as their national symbol?
Coquí and his brother end up singing happily together. What is something that you like to do with your siblings or friends? How does doing this activity together make you feel? Why?

articles.title: The Tummy Beast
articles.author: Roald Dahl
articles.description: There is a person in the speaker's tummy that demands to be fed.
articles.intro: Roald Dahl (1916-1990) was a British writer best known for his children's books Charlie and the Chocolate Factory , Matilda , and James and the Giant Peach . In this poem, a young child tells their mother about their tummy beast.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker and mummy feel about the tummy beast.
articles.permissions: 'Tummy Beast' from Dirty Beasts by Roald Dahl (Penguin Young Readers Group), © The Roald Dahl Story Company Limited", reproduced by permission of David Higham Associates.

blocks.text: One afternoon I said to mummy,
“Who is this person in my tummy?
“Who must be small and very thin
“Or how could he have gotten in?”
My mother said from where she sat,
“It isn’t nice to talk like that.”
“It’s true!” I cried. “I swear it, mummy!
“There is a person in my tummy!
“He talks to me at night in bed,
“He’s always asking to be fed,
“Throughout the day, he screams at me,
“Demanding sugar buns for tea.
“He tells me it is not a sin
“To go and raid the biscuit tin.
“I know quite well it’s awfully wrong
“To guzzle food the whole day long,
“But really I can’t help it, mummy,
“Not with this person in my tummy.”
“You horrid child!” my mother cried.
“Admit it right away, you’ve lied!”
“You’re simply trying to produce
“A silly asinine excuse!
“ You are the greedy guzzling brat!
“And that is why you’re always fat!”
I tried once more, “ Believe me , mummy,
“There is a person in my tummy.”
“I’ve had enough!” my mother said,
“You’d better go at once to bed!”
Just then, a nicely timed event
Delivered me from punishment.
Deep in my tummy something stirred,
And then an awful noise was heard,
A snorting grumbling grunting sound
That made my tummy jump around.
My darling mother nearly died,
“My goodness, what was that?” she cried.
At once the tummy voice came through,
It shouted, “Hey there! Listen you!
“I’m getting hungry! I want eats!
“I want lots of chocs and sweets!
“Get me half a pound of nuts!
“Look snappy or I’ll twist your guts!”
“ That’s him! ” I cried. “ He’s in my tummy!
“So now do you believe me, mummy?”
But mummy answered nothing more,
For she had fainted on the floor.

articles.title: Petal Power
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Different plants use the features of their flowers to attract pollinators.
articles.intro: Petals are one of the leaves that form on the outside of a flower's head. They are usually a different color from the plant's other leaves. In this informational text, Tracy Vonder Brink explains how flowers help plants.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why some flowers are colorful.
articles.permissions: "Petal Power" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: A monarch butterfly flutters above a bright red flower. It lands and uncurls its long tongue to sip the flower’s sweet nectar. As the butterfly sits among the petals, an orange-yellow powder called pollen sticks to its body. The butterfly flies away and lands on another flower. This time, some of the pollen on its body brushes off.

The pollen left behind by the butterfly travels down into the flower. It combines with tiny eggs that grow into seeds. Moving pollen from one plant to another is called pollination. It’s the reason many plants have flowers.
Pollen Partners

Most plants can’t pollinate by themselves — they need helpers, called pollinators. Some plants use the wind. They make small, light pollen that’s easy to blow along. Plants pollinated by the wind may still have flowers, but their flowers don’t need to be big and colorful.

Other plants rely on living creatures such as insects, birds, and bats to spread their pollen. They make nectar these creatures use as food. Plants need their pollinators to come close and brush up against the flower’s sticky pollen. A flower’s color, shape, size, and smell all work to attract its pollen partners.
Color Me Yummy

A brightly colored flower brings pollinators, but they all have their favorite colors. Hummingbirds like red. Bumblebees prefer blue and yellow. (In fact, bees can’t see red at all.) Some insects and birds can see colors that people can’t. These colors are called ultraviolet, or UV. Some flowers have UV markings to guide pollinators to the nectar that waits inside. These markings mean “Land here!” A petal’s striped pattern works the same way.

The petals themselves are like little landing pads. Most butterflies, beetles, and bees can’t fly and eat at the same time. They need somewhere to sit and sip. The flower gives them a place to do that and coats them in pollen at the same time.
What’s That Smell?

A flower’s scent also attracts pollinators. They pick up the smell in the air and follow it to the flower. Some bats sniff out flowers that smell musty. Bees like sweet scents. Beetles may prefer a spicy smell. Some flowers even stink like rotting meat to attract flies!
Not all flowers are scented. Birds don’t have much of a sense of smell, so flowers pollinated by birds may not have a scent. They don’t need one. Birds find them by their color instead.
One Size Fits Most

A flower’s shape and size may fit its pollen partner. Beetles aren’t skillful fliers. So, they look for wide-open flowers with large petals where it’s easier to land. Flowers that partner with hummingbirds grow in a tube shape that only a hummingbird’s skinny beak and long tongue can reach.
A few flowers fool their pollinators. A bee orchid has brown and yellow markings in the shape of a female bee. Male bees land, thinking they’ve found a mate! They get a dusting of pollen instead.
Win-Win

Plants provide insects, birds, and bats with food. Pollinators spread the pollen that helps make new plants. Flowers may be pretty, but they do much more. They help make plants and their pollinators perfect partners.

"The Dream Team: Flowers and Pollinators."

articles.title: Inventing Bubble Gum
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Walter Diemer accidentally invented bubble gum for the Fleer company in 1928.
articles.intro: When someone invents something it means they think of or make something new. This informational text tells the story of how bubble gum was invented.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how bubble gum was first made.
articles.permissions: "Inventing Bubble Gum" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Bubble gum is chewy. It’s pink. It’s fun! But did you know bubble gum was invented long after chewing gum?

People have loved to chew gum for 11,000 years. Prehistoric peoples in Europe made it from the bark of birch trees. The Indigenous peoples of North America made theirs from spruce tree sap. The ancient Maya and Aztecs added the sap of the sapodilla tree to their gum. In 1859, sapodilla sap also became the base of chewing gum made in the United States. By the late 1800s, the largest chewing gum factory in the world was in New York City.

In 1906, the Fleer Chewing Gum Company tried to make bubble gum. It was called Blibber Blubber. It was not very good. It was too sticky to blow a big bubble. When Walter Diemer went to work at Fleer in the 1920s, the company was still trying to make bubble gum. He was an accountant, the person who looked after the company’s money. Walter worked with numbers, not candy, but sometimes he was asked to watch batches of gum as they cooked. Soon Walter wanted to make gum himself.

Walter didn’t mean to invent bubble gum. He was experimenting with a gum base, the part that makes the gum chewy. Walter tried different ingredients and boiled mix after mix. One day he noticed one batch was surprisingly bubbly. Walter discovered he could blow bubbles with it. Walter had created bubble gum!

When Walter tried to make it again the next morning, it didn’t work. It took him four more months to create another bubbly batch. He asked the people in his company to test it. They loved blowing bubbles. Would everybody else? It was time to try it in the real world.
Walter made 300 pounds (136 kg) of his gum around Christmas in 1928. He almost forgot to add color to it. Walter grabbed the only food coloring he had. It happened to be bright pink.

The Fleer Chewing Gum Company named Walter’s gum Dubble Bubble. They sent five pounds of Dubble Bubble to a small candy store in Philadelphia. It cost one penny per piece. Dubble Bubble sold out right away.

Everyone wanted bubble gum. Dubble Bubble was a hit. Soon other companies made their own. Today you can find Dubble Bubble, Bazooka, Bubble Yum, and many more in stores. They’re all pink, just like Walter’s first bubble gum.

Walter worked for Fleer for the rest of his life, and he never told anybody but his company the secret to his bubble gum recipe. He also never made any money from his invention. He didn’t seem to mind. Creating something kids loved was more important to him. In 1998, his wife told The Capital Times newspaper that Walter often said, “I’ve done something with my life. I’ve made kids happy around the world.”

options.label: The first form of bubble gum was invented thousands of years ago.
People invented chewing gum when they got tired of bubble gum.
Bubble gum was not well-liked when it was first invented.
It took many years of tests to finally invent bubble gum.

They show that people liked Walter's bubble gum.
They show that people at Walter's company were smart.
They show how Fleer Company failed to sell Dubble Bubble.

questions.prompt: What do paragraphs 7 and 8 help readers understand?
How did Walter Diemer invent bubble gum?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Many kids love bubble gum, but it is not allowed in many classrooms. Do you think bubble gum should be allowed in class? Why or why not? What do you think the author of the text might say?
What helped Walter succeed as an inventor? If you were going to invent a candy or snack, what would you invent and why?
If you were Walter, would you be okay not earning money or fame from your invention? Why or why not?

articles.title: Lulu Poole's School for Ghouls
articles.author: Joanne Lamond
articles.description: A little ghost goes to school to learn how to be scarier.
articles.intro: In this short story, a little ghoul, or ghost, worries that he is not scary enough for Halloween.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Culpepper learns at school.
articles.permissions: "Lulu Poole's School for Ghouls" by Joanne Lamond, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Culpepper was a little ghost. He lived in a large, old barn. “I’m too little to scare anyone,” he complained sadly to his friends.
“Let me hear you moan,” said Archibald.
Culpepper took a deep breath. He let it out slowly. “Moo,” he said. “Moo, moo.”
“You sound like a cow,” Archibald said, shaking his head.
“Let me hear you scream,” said Selena.
Culpepper took a big, deep breath. He let it out quickly. “Eek, eek,” he squeaked.
“That sounds like a mouse,” Selena said, trying hard not to laugh.
“Let me hear you wail,” said Deacon.
Culpepper threw his head back. He sucked in a great gob of air. He let out his best wail. “Whooo.” His wail was no louder than a whisper.
Deacon rolled his eyes. “You sound like a very quiet owl.”
“I’m in trouble,” Culpepper said. “How will I scare anyone on Halloween?”
“You need help,” Archibald said.
“You need lessons,” Selena said. “You need to get out of the barn,” Deacon said.
“Go to school!” said Culpepper’s three friends.

That night Culpepper left his friends in the old barn. He drifted up Cemetery Lane. He turned left on Dead End Drive. At the top of the hill he saw a rickety building. Words were painted crookedly on the front: LULU POOLE’S SCHOOL FOR GHOULS. Culpepper floated up the front steps.
He saw a sign in the window: OPEN ALL NIGHT.
He saw a sign over the door: COME IN AND WE’LL BEGIN. ALL YOUR WORRIES ARE BEHIND YOU!
Culpepper passed through the door. Two candles glowed in the dim hall.
“Hello,” Culpepper said. “Is anyone here?”
No one answered.
A door at the end of the hall opened. Culpepper floated toward it.
“Lulu Poole,” Culpepper said. “Are you here?”
No one answered.
He peeked through the open door. The door led to the kitchen. Culpepper went in. The door slammed shut.
A log fire burned in the stone fireplace. A black pot hung on a hook in the fire. Something strange bubbled in the pot and spattered into the ashes.
“Lulu Poole, it’s me, Culpepper. I’ve come for lessons. Can you teach me to be scary?”
No one answered.
A gray-green cloud rose from the pot. Culpepper pinched his nose. “Ooooh, this stinks!”
Culpepper’s eyes watered. His head hurt. He wanted to leave the kitchen, but an orange cat with blue eyes blocked the door. It hissed and spat at the little ghost.
The stinky cloud grew larger until it filled the kitchen. “OOOOOH!” Culpepper moaned.
The cat arched its back and yowled. It scampered behind a broom to hide.
The kitchen door opened. From behind Culpepper, a blast of air pushed him into the hallway. Up, up he rose, up to the top of the house.
Culpepper was tossed through the attic door. He grabbed a rafter and hung on tight. Soon the wind stopped blowing. The little ghost peered around the room.
“Lulu Poole, are you here?” Culpepper asked.
No one answered.
Moonlight streamed through a hole in the roof. On the attic floor Culpepper could see a basket with worn clothes. He could smell a box of old shoes.
“Lulu Poole, it’s me, Culpepper. I’ve come for lessons. Can you teach me to be scary?”
No one answered.
High in a dark corner something moved. Culpepper could hear the beating of great wings. Bats!
“Eek, eek!” Culpepper twisted to the right and swerved to the left as the bats swarmed around him.
“Stop,” said Culpepper as the bats circled his head. “EEEEAAAK!” He screamed. The bats fled through the hole in the roof.

Culpepper was ready to go home. Lulu Poole was not here to give him lessons. He would come back some other night. But the strange wind returned. He felt a huge blast of air from above him. He tumbled out of the attic door and down the staircase. Down, down the little ghost dropped, down to the cellar.
Culpepper landed in a tumble. Floop! The cellar was dim. He picked himself up off the floor. Cobwebs stuck to him. He saw wet, drippy walls. The air was heavy with the smell of rot.
“Lulu Poole,” Culpepper demanded. “Are you here?”
No one answered.
It looked as if dead flowers were hanging from the ceiling. Chains hung on nails against the walls.
“Lulu Poole, it’s me, Culpepper. I’ve come for lessons. Can you teach me to be scary?”
No one answered.
Culpepper sighed. He looked at the dead flowers. They seemed to be crawling on the ceiling. Then each bloom dropped from its place and swayed gently on a thin silver thread.
“Spiders,” Culpepper said, smiling. “Let’s play!” He grabbed a chain from the wall and shook it at the spiders.
“WHOOOO, AAAHHHH!” Culpepper wailed and rattled the chain. The spiders scurried into every crack in the cellar ceiling.
“Well done, little ghost,” whispered a voice.

Culpepper turned. A haggish figure, covered in glowing green mist, floated toward him. Yellow bulging eyes glared from an ashen face. The little ghost shuddered at the odor that seeped from the creature’s mouth as it spoke.
“Tonight you scared the cat, banished the bats, and startled the spiders,” it wheezed. “Now, show Lulu Poole the best you can do.”
Culpepper moaned his deepest moan. “OOOOOOH!”
He screamed his highest scream. “EEEEAAAAK!”
He wailed his loudest wail. “WHOOOOO, AAAAHHHHH!”
Lulu Poole disappeared in a stinky, gray-green puff of smoke.
A whisper reached Culpepper’s ears. “You have learned your lessons well. Refresher courses are offered at a discount. Don’t forget to study. School is dismissed.”
The wind swept Culpepper out the cellar door, into the hallway, and through the front door of Lulu Poole’s School for Ghouls. He hurried back to the barn. His friends were waiting.
“Did you go to Lulu Poole’s school?” Archibald asked.
Culpepper nodded.
“Did you learn your lessons?” Selena asked.
Culpepper nodded.
“Can you be scary on Halloween?” Deacon asked.
“What do you think?” Culpepper answered. And he practiced his homework.
“OOOOOOH!”
“EEEEAAAAK!”
“WHOOOOO,
AAAAHHHHH!”

options.label: It is important to face your fears.
It is important to listen to your teachers.
It is important to always do the right thing.
It is important to help your friends when they need you.

"Culpepper was ready to go home." (Paragraph 42)

to show that no one cares about Culpepper

to laugh at someone

How does Culpepper learn to be scary?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Culpepper learns that he has what it takes to be a scary ghost. Have you ever felt that you could not do something, but then you did it? What helped you realize that you could actually do it?

As a ghost, Culpepper is expected to be scary but worries that he will not be scary enough. Think of a time you felt pressured to be a certain way. You can use your own experience or the experience of a character from a book, history, or movie. How did you handle the pressure? What are some good strategies for handling pressure?

As a ghost, Culpepper wants to be and feel scary. What words would you use to describe yourself? How might others describe you? Which parts of yourself do you feel are most important and why? As an extension activity, have students create identity maps for themselves using character trait words. Then have them share their maps with a partner to see how they are alike and different.

articles.title: Truman's Last Chance
articles.author: Andrew W. Hamilton
articles.description: A boy gets some unexpected help as he tries to come up with the best science project idea.
articles.intro: Science is the study of the world around us. In this story, a boy named Truman has to come up with an idea for a science project for school. In this project, Truman must write a report, or paper, and make a model.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Truman’s feelings about Bryan.
articles.permissions: "Truman's Last Chance" by Andrew W. Hamilton, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Rachel Pettinghill.

As Truman trudged home from school, he repeated the name in his head. Throughout third grade and the year before, Rachel Pettingill had earned the highest science grade in the class. The truth was she scored highest in every subject. Truman knew this because they compared scores on every test, every report, and every quiz.
Truman was used to being second best to Rachel. “But science is my thing,” he declared to a stop sign.

Truman’s parents were scientists, and he would be a scientist. That was just a fact. Somehow, with only one week and one assignment to go before break, he and Rachel were tied. Dead even. To finish on top, his last report had to be great. It had to be better than great.
The subject of the project was beehives. Truman’s class had studied bees for three days, so he was ready. But, as his teacher Mrs. Lawrence had explained, to earn an A+, he needed a “new angle.”
Truman pushed open the front door to find his four-year-old brother, Bryan, sprawled on the living room rug, hard at work. Paper towel tubes were all over the floor.
Bryan leaped to his feet. “Truman, help me build a city!”
“I’d like to,” Truman shrugged, “but I have to do a report on beehives and — ”
“Can I help you?” Bryan begged.
“I don’t think so, Bry. Sorry.” Truman stepped carefully between the scattered tubes. He went to his room, dropped onto the bed, and studied the ceiling.
Rachel Pettingill .
Her report would be pretty ordinary but perfectly executed and accompanied by one of her incredible drawings. Truman had to come up with something great, but what?
“Truman?” Bryan poked his head in.
“Bry, I said I can’t play with you now.” Truman tried not to sound mean. His brother could burst into tears at the hint of an unkind word.
“I know where there’s an old beehive.” Bryan smiled.
“Where?” Truman sat up.
“In the wood pile by the garage.”

Bryan copied Truman’s stride as the boys marched to the stack of firewood. Bending down, Bryan pointed out the hive deep inside the pile. Truman took off several top layers of the stacked wood and carefully removed the hive from its hole.
“You got it!” Bryan shouted.

Back in his room, Truman sat at his desk, turning the fragile hive under a lamp. It smelled like old leaves. Each cell was a perfect hexagon. How did the bees fit the cells together so neatly? And how did they make each cell six-sided? Could they count?
“Boys, dinner’s ready!” his mother called. Truman fought the rising panic as he glanced at his desk clock.

All through dinner, Truman struggled to keep his cool. If his parents found out he was working on a science project, they would run around pulling out books and volunteering ideas. He wanted to beat Rachel without their help.
As he carried his half-empty plate of spaghetti to the kitchen, his father called, “Don’t forget, Truman — it’s your night to do the dishes.” Truman glanced at the clock again. 7:05.
Rachel Pettingill .
Truman’s heart fluttered as he scraped a plate. If he didn’t think of something soon, his chance to beat Rachel would be lost…maybe forever. The thought of it made his stomach hurt.
As he turned around to put the butter into the refrigerator, his foot came down on something with a crunch .
“Truman! You’re wrecking my city!” Lost in thought, Truman hadn’t seen Bryan settle in behind him with a mess of paper tubes.
“Get your tubes out of here, Bryan! I’m trying to finish the dishes!” Truman blurted.
Bryan began to cry. Loudly. He gathered an armful of dented tubes, threw them under the running faucet, and ran sobbing into the living room.
Truman sighed and gathered the dripping tubes in both hands. As he held them over the wastebasket, they suddenly reminded him of something. The beehive!

Looking closer, he noticed the tubes were arranged with one in the middle, surrounded by six others. Truman tore open the drawer near the sink, grabbed a handful of paper clips, and clipped the tubes together. As he suspected, the soggy center tube formed a hexagon, just like the cells of the hive.
Just to be sure, he tried five and then seven surrounding tubes, but neither way fit. Six was the only number that worked.
He said aloud, “Bees just build round tubes and stick the sides together. The cells have to be six-sided.”

Truman ran to the living room and threw his arms around his brother. “Bryan, you did it! I’ve got my angle!” he gushed. “And a real beehive. And now I can build a model beehive with your tubes! I mean — if it’s OK with you.”
“Well…” Bryan pulled away, still sniffling.
“You can help me glue them,” Truman offered. Bryan beamed and began picking out his finest tubes.
Rachel Pettingill didn’t stand a chance.

options.label: It is hard to have a little brother.
Learning about bees can be scary.
Winning is the most important thing.
Sometimes the answer is right in front of you.

"If he didn't think of something soon, his chance to beat Rachel would be lost…maybe forever. The thought of it made his stomach hurt." (Paragraph 25)
"'Get your tubes out of here, Bryan! I'm trying to finish the dishes!' Truman blurted." (Paragraph 28)
"'You can help me glue them,' Truman offered." (Paragraph 36)

options.label: to flap one's wings

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Truman feels annoyed by his little brother Bryan. Have you ever felt annoyed by a sibling or a friend? How do you handle conflict with your siblings or friends? How do you show them that you care about them?

Truman wants to come up with the best science project for school. Think of a time you had to come up with a school project on your own. What was the hardest part of the project? What advice would you give to someone who has to come up with a project for school?

In the story, Truman wants to come up with a better project than Rachel Pettingill. Do you think that it is good to compete with others in school? How can competition be positive? How can competition be negative? In your opinion, what are some of the best ways to stay motivated to do well in school?

articles.title: My Great-Grandma
articles.author: Nancy Machlis
articles.description: A brother and a sister visit their great-grandmother in a nursing home.
articles.intro: Nancy Machlis has written for Highlights. In this short story, a brother and a sister visit their great-grandmother in a nursing home.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the children feel when they visit their great-grandmother.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: My great-grandma lives in a nursing home. She didn’t always live there.
My great-grandma sits in a wheelchair now. She can’t walk anymore. But she used to.
My great-grandma doesn’t always know me these days. But when I hold her hand, she smiles at me. She knows me even if she doesn’t.
We sit in the big cafeteria. Great-Grandma has trouble chewing now, so she eats soft foods. Tonight they give her a bowl of matzo-ball soup. I bring the spoon to her lips.
“Is it good?” I ask.
She nods to let me know I can give her more.
Great-Grandma used to make the greatest matzo-ball soup in the world.
I look at her and see the gold locket she always wears around her neck. Inside, there’s a tiny picture of Great-Grandpa.
Mom and my little brother, Zach, have been in the gift shop. Now they come into the cafeteria to meet us. Zach is carrying a small bunch of flowers.
“Hi, Great-Grandma!” says Zach. He hands the flowers to her.
Great-Grandma looks at the flowers. She looks at Zach. She doesn’t remember him.
“That’s not Great-Grandma!” Zach yells and runs down the hall, crying.
I want to cry, too. I follow Zach and ask him if he remembers the time Great-Grandma told us about her locket.
“Yes,” he says, sniffling.
Before Great-Grandma first went into the nursing home, she showed us her locket. “Your great-grandpa gave it to me on our 50th wedding anniversary. He told me he’d always be close to my heart.”
“Aren’t you afraid that you’ll forget him?” I asked.
She shook her head. “Love is a light in your heart that lives forever.”
I look at Zach now. His eyes are still wet. “Don’t worry,” I say. “Great-Grandma’s love for us will never fade away.”
I take his hand, and we go back into the cafeteria.

There is music playing, and Great-Grandma taps the arm of the wheelchair. She holds the flowers in her other hand. I know she’s still there inside. Some people just see her as an old lady with white hair. They don’t know her the way I do.

When it’s time to go, I kiss Great-Grandma’s cheek and she smiles. I wish I could help her be the way she was before, even though I can’t. But I believe she knows deep down that the love in our hearts will live forever.

options.label: While memories can be forgotten, love will always remain.
Memory is a necessary part of a person's identity.
If someone loves you, they can never truly forget who you are.
Memory loss can damage the relationship between family members.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence best describes the theme of the short story?

"His eyes are still wet. 'Don't worry,' I say. 'Great-Grandma's love for us will never fade away.'" (Paragraph 18)
"Some people just see her as an old lady with white hair. They don't know her the way I do." (Paragraph 20)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from that text best supports the answer to Part A?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Before the narrator's great-grandmother entered the nursing home, she said, "'Love is a light in your heart that lives forever'" (Paragraph 17). How do you think this description of love affected the narrator? Do you agree with the great-grandmother's description of love? Why or why not?

In your opinion, what makes a family, the memories you have or the love you share with them? Have you ever had a family member who struggled with memory loss? If so, how did that affect your relationship with them?

articles.title: Seeking a Hidden Hive
articles.author: Jocelyn Rish
articles.description: A small boy and his grandfather search for a beehive to collect the honey from inside.
articles.intro: Jocelyn Rish is a writer and filmmaker. In this short story, a boy and his grandfather search for a beehive to collect the honey from inside.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Guyo feels about his size.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: Guyo kicked the ground, stirring up a cloud of red dust.
“What’s wrong, Guyo?”
Guyo looked up to see his grandfather.
“Father won’t let me herd the cattle. He says I’m too little,” said Guyo.
“Your father has a lot on his mind. Times are tough,” said Grandfather.
“That’s why I want to help! But every time I try, people say I’m too small.”
“You can help your mother by getting honey.”
Guyo jangled the few coins in his pocket. “But I don’t have enough money.”
His grandfather smiled. “We’ll gather honey the way Borana tribe has gathered honey for years.” He opened his hand. It held a snail shell with a hole cut in it.
“What’s that, Grandfather?”
“It’s a fuulido. When we use it, a honey guide will come and lead us to a hidden beehive.” He handed Guyo the shell. “Call one.”

Guyo blew through the hole, creating a high-pitched whistle. When nothing happened, he whistled again. Before long, a gray-brown bird fluttered to a nearby branch. She called tirr tirr many times, then turned and flashed the white patches on her tail feathers. Once she had the attention of Grandfather and Guyo, she flew off toward the west.
Guyo was eager to follow. His grandfather picked up a tin pail and said, “Let’s go.”

They hurried through the dry brush, always keeping the honey guide in view. But when they came to a high ledge, they paused so Grandfather could help Guyo scramble over it. They lost sight of the bird. “I’m sorry, Grandfather,” Guyo said.
“Don’t worry. She’ll be back.”
Soon Guyo heard the trilling call again. The honey guide landed on a limb, waving her tail feathers.
Grandfather said, “She will make sure we go the right way. This has been the custom between our tribe and the honey guides for hundreds of years.”
The bird flew into the air.
“Why do they help us?” Guyo asked.
“The honey guides are experts at finding hives. But because they’re small, bee stings would kill them. They need our help to get what’s inside the hive.”
Guyo and his grandfather followed the bird for half an hour. Each time the bird returned, she perched closer to the ground. Grandfather said, “That means we’re getting closer.”
Finally the honey guide’s call changed. She swooped toward a crack in a cliff wall. “We’re here,” said Grandfather.
Guyo’s heart beat faster as they approached the crack. An army of angry bees guarded the entrance. The buzzing tickled his ears.
Grandfather gathered small sticks and started rubbing them together. “Help me build a fire in front of the crack. The smoke will calm the bees.”

Guyo was afraid he might get stung. But he had to prove he was big enough to help. He kept an eye on the bees as Grandfather blew on the smoky bundle of sticks. When the smoke drifted over the bees, the buzzing got quiet.
Grandfather tried to push his hand into the crack, but it wouldn’t fit. He said to Guyo, “You should be able to reach the honeycomb.”
Guyo trembled. He couldn’t reach into that dark hole! What if the bees stung him? He looked at his grandfather. He couldn’t disappoint him.

Guyo took a deep breath and stretched out his arm. Then he jerked back in fear. I can do this, I can do this , he repeated to himself. He closed his eyes and thrust his fingers into the crack. No stings! The smoke worked.
Reaching as far as he could, he tugged out chunk after chunk of honeycomb dripping with sticky amber honey.
Grandfather said, “That’s plenty. We need to leave enough for the bees.”
They backed away from the hive just as the bees started to buzz again.
Grandfather put most of the honeycomb inside his tin pail. He handed Guyo a piece. “Give this to the honey guide, with the thanks of the Borana tribe.”
Guyo placed the honeycomb at the base of the tree where the honey guide perched. “Thank you for sharing this treasure with us.”

The honey guide trilled and dropped to the ground to eat her reward. As they started walking home, Grandfather placed a hand on Guyo’s shoulder. “You were able to reach so much honey that we’ll be able to sell the extra in the village. That will be a big help to our family.”
Guyo smiled. “I guess being little is sometimes a good thing.”

options.label: It explains why collecting the honey at the end of the story made Guyo happy.
It helps the reader understand why Guyo's family was going through a tough time.
It shows why Guyo is so frustrated and upset with his father.
It tells why Guyo doesn't want to help his father or the rest of his family.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to the story that Guyo's father won't let him herd cattle to help his family?

options.label: Strength is more important than bravery.
Honey is one of nature's greatest gifts to mankind.
All animals are naturally helpful to humans.
The things that we think are weaknesses can be strengths.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the main theme of the short story?

options.label: "'She will make sure we go the right way. This has been the custom between our tribe and the honey guides for hundreds of years.'" (Paragraph 17)
"Guyo was afraid he might get stung. But he had to prove he was big enough to help. He kept an eye on the bees as Grandfather blew on the smoky bundle of sticks." (Paragraph 25)
"Grandfather tried to push his hand into the crack, but it wouldn't fit. He said to Guyo, 'You should be able to reach the honeycomb.'" (Paragraph 26)
"He handed Guyo a piece. 'Give this to the honey guide, with the thanks of the Borana tribe.'" (Paragraph 32)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: Guyo's grandfather promises that he will not be hurt.
Guyo wants to prove to his grandfather that he is helpful.
Guyo knows that the smoke has frightened the bees buzzing and he won't be stung.
Guyo fears his grandfather will be upset with him if he doesn't.

questions.prompt: What motivates Guyo to stick his hand in the crack for the honeycomb?
What happens to make Guyo change how he feels about his small size?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Guyo and his grandfather need the honey guide to find honey. How do they show their respect and appreciation for the honey guide? What are other examples of ways in which humans rely on animals? Do you think that humans thank animals enough for what they do for humans?

In the short story, Guyo uses his small size to reach honeycomb. How are Guyo's actions brave when he retrieves the honeycomb from the crack? Describe a time that you were brave. What motivated you to be brave?

articles.title: Mussels Attack! (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: Trix, a miniature horse, discovers an invasive species on the farm where she lives.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. This short story is about a miniature, or very small, horse named Trix who finds a new animal on her farm.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Trix feels.
articles.permissions: "Mussels Attack! (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, they’re exploring an unwanted visitor on the farm.
Trix felt warm sweat dripping down her neck. It was too hot today on the farm. There didn’t seem to be any shade.
Where was that farm assistant with the hose? she wondered.
Pendragon, the lizard, was a cold-blooded reptile. So he enjoyed lying in the sun on a rock with the turtles by the pond.
Cold-blooded animals have bodies that can’t stay warm on their own. They have to take heat in from their surroundings. Trix sure wished she were cold blooded right now.
Her tail swished away an annoying fly. Beyond the fence, the pond’s cool water looked so refreshing. Even Garbanzo, her raccoon friend, sat at the edge cooling his feet and tail.
Trix backed up, took a running start, and jumped over a crooked board in the fence to the other side. When she landed, she kicked up her feet and snorted.
“Woo! Go Trix,” Garbanzo yelled. “Ten points!”
“I’m the champion,” she called, trotting proudly to the pond’s edge.
The fountain’s gentle mist fell on her fur, cooling her instantly.
“Trix!” Garbanzo called. “Watch out for the… ”
But it was too late. She went to stick one foot in the water. Crunch.
“... zebra mussels,” the raccoon finished.
There were hundreds of them. Their shells were covered in black and white stripes. They didn’t move much. Thankfully, the one Trix stepped on was not hurt.
Trix looked closely into the water. “Why are there so many of them? What are they?”
“Zebra mussels,” Garbanzo explained. “They’re a real nuisance! I cut my toe on one just last week. The farm assistants take them out of the drain every few months.”
“That’s no good! We should check the list,” Trix said. “See if they’re supposed to be here or not.”
Garbanzo groaned. He was sure this was going to involve work and note taking, and he didn’t like either.
The two headed toward the educational area of the barn. Usually, they avoided this area because of the miniature humans and the regular-sized humans wandering about.

They approached a big board of species that had always been in the Midwest area. There was another board that showed species that weren’t from here, but also weren’t causing harm. A third board showed invasive species, these were animals that had been accidentally introduced to the area because of humans and caused harm to the natural environment.
“Invasive species are bad ,” Trix said. “They crowd out the animals and plants that live here.”
“Yeah, and they eat all the food!” Garbanzo grumbled. “Speaking of, where’s my snack?”
“You always have food on the brain,” Trix laughed. “So let’s see… hmm, zebra mussels, you said?”
Trix searched the board until she found the right species. She flipped up the information card to read:

The zebra mussel is one of the most harmful invasive species in North America. They belong in Eurasia (Ukraine and Russia) freshwater. They arrived in the Great Lakes through visiting ships and spread across the country. They eat up all the food that local mussels eat – algae.

You can spot zebra mussels by their unique zig-zag dark pattern on their white shells. They clog waterways, boat engines, and pipes. Watch out for sharp shells on beaches and in lakes, ponds, and rivers.
Trix nodded. “That makes sense now. How do we get rid of them?”
“I know how! I saw the humans do it,” Garbanzo said. “But you might want to wear boots.”
Despite the heat, Trix put on all of her protective gear for good measures. Thick purple rubber boots? Check. Blue snorkel mask? Check. Straw sunhat? Check.
Garbanzo waddled across the lawn wearing huge black galoshes and a bright orange snorkel mask. He dragged several large buckets and a butterfly net behind him.
“You brought the ducky buckets?” Trix asked, giggling at the cute pattern on the buckets. “Must be serious.”
“It was all I could find,” Garbanzo replied.

Together, the two of them waded into the water. They were careful not to step on any sharp mussels as they went back and forth. Trix grabbed at the sharp mussels with her teeth and loosened them by scraping gently with her hooves. Garbanzo scooped them up with his net. The cool water felt good on their skin. It gave them the energy to fill the buckets in no time.
“What do we do with them now?” Trix asked.
Garbanzo and Trix gazed at the long line of buckets. The mussels smelled fishy in the hot summer afternoon.
A gaggle of ducks quacked loudly behind them. The startled miniature horse and raccoon turned around.
Abby, the large white duck, wiggled her tail feathers and quacked again. “Those would make a tasty meal… ”
“Your duck and goose friends would like to make a deal,” added Nancy, a black, white, and brown Canada goose.
“Zebra mussels are a steal,” Quincy, a Mallard duck with bright green head feathers finished.
“Why do all the water fowl around here speak in rhymes?” Garbanzo muttered to Trix. He rolled his eyes. “They’re terrible poets.”
“I don’t know, but I think they just solved our problem,” Trix whispered. She cleared her throat and said: “We’d like to make a trade.”
The ducks and geese chattered amongst themselves in their own language for a few minutes. It sounded like a thousand kazoos going off at once.

“I’ll show you a trick,” said Drake, a handsome wood duck. Drake had black feathers on its head that were slicked back like hair, and it was the most colorful duck of the bunch. “You’ll learn it quick .”

Trix and Garbanzo looked at each other puzzled and shrugged. Drake waddled away toward the garden, so they waddled after it in their rubber boots. They could hear the sound of the ducks and geese slurping behind them.
Drake pulled a knotted rope out of the grass. Thankfully, it didn’t need to explain in a sing-songy rhyme what it was doing as it tugged and tugged until the garden gate opened with a loud click .
“ Food ,” Garbanzo breathed, sniffing the air.
Trix and Garbanzo didn’t need to be told twice. They scuttled inside and headed straight to the apple orchard. They deserved a little treat after all their hard work, didn’t they?

When Trix looked back with a mouth full of apple, Drake was gone. They had solved a problem, fed some of the park’s animals, and got a tasty reward out of the deal. Not a bad day in the life of a miniature horse that dreams of one day being a scientist!

options.label: Try to make a difference when you see a problem.

It is important to live in the same place you were born.
Try to do something by yourself before you ask for help.

articles.title: Garden Invasion (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: Trix, a miniature horse, learns about invasive and native species of plants.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic, has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast, and worked with many other publications. In this story, a miniature, or very small, horse named Trix and her friends learn about plants in the garden on their farm. Some of the plants they learn about are invasive species, which are plants or animals brought to an area they normally do not live and cause harm to their new home.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Trix learns in the garden.
articles.permissions: "Garden Invasion (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, Trix is calling on her friends to help fix the mess she caused in the garden.
Trix woke up early with a rumbling tummy. She looked outside. The sky was a cotton candy pink. The sun peeked out over the top of the trees.
Mmm , thought Trix. Cotton candy would be a delicious breakfast .

She nudged the door to the pasture open with her nose and followed the smell of apples all the way to the orchard. She lifted the garden gate rope and tugged until the gate clicked open. She nudged it gently behind her, but it didn’t close all the way.

Trix picked her way down the rows carefully. She didn’t want the farm assistants to know she was visiting the garden to sneak extra treats. She munched happily on the sweet grass at the edges of the orchard between bites of apple.
Trix only ate the fallen apples that weren’t too wormy. Then it would look like the wild critters had snuck under the fence for a nibble during the night.
When her belly was full, she decided to take a quick nap.

When she woke up again, her mouth fell open. The garden was a mess! Some plants had been pushed over and some were chewed down to nubs. Fruits and veggies everywhere had bites taken out of them or were squished with hoofprints.

It looked like the handiwork of Godfrey the Goat. He lived in the stall three doors down from Trix. He was a nuisance. He would always nibble at the edges of her notebooks, swallow her favorite snacks, and slurp liquid out of her science test tubes with his long tongue.
“What happened?!” Trix shrieked. She had only fallen asleep for a second .
Trix jumped as Rory the Rooster crowed across the farm. It announced the start of the farm day and breakfast time. If Trix wasn’t in her stall, she would be in big trouble.
She cleaned up as best she could and rushed back to her stall. She pretended to be asleep when the farm assistant brought the grain to the barn.
Later that morning, the farm assistants found the destroyed garden. They looked sad and angry as they picked through trampled plants and ruined vegetables.

Trix felt bad. It was her fault that the garden had been destroyed. It wouldn’t have happened if she hadn’t left the gate open. She had to fix it. And she had to find the animal who destroyed the garden, so that they would help her make it right.

Trix went around to all her animal friends, but none of them seemed to know who had done it — not the cats, ducks, geese, chickens, turtles, sheep, wild birds, or frogs. Most of the animals on the farm were diurnal, which meant they slept at night and were awake during the day.
That evening after the farm assistants left, Trix woke Garbanzo, her raccoon note-taking research assistant. Then she woke Godfrey, the trouble-making goat. They tramped out to the ruined garden.
“Okay, team! We have a lot of work to do,” Trix said, pacing back and forth in front of her gathered helpers. “Godfrey, do you know who did this?”
The goat shook his stubby tail and shook his head. He didn’t know.
Trix sighed. Another dead end.
“What is this?” Garbanzo asked, holding up a smooth dark purple vegetable. He took a bite and spit it out immediately. “Blech! What is this? A purple sponge?”

“Eggplant,” Trix corrected. “It’s actually a fruit. So are peppers, tomatoes, squashes, cucumbers, gourds, green beans, peas, avocados, olives, corn, and okra. Plant scientists describe fruit as anything that can spread seeds into the world.”
Garbanzo bit into a green pepper and spit that out, too. “Gross! It tastes like a ball of grass juice. Not sweet at all.”
Godfrey lapped up Garbanzo’s leftovers cheerfully. He then chomped down on a mouthful of shrubs sticking through the garden fence.
Trix watched Godfrey thoughtfully. “Wow, you’re actually useful. Godfrey, can you help us clean up this mess?”
The goat bleated and took another mouthful of shrubbery.

Trix nudged scraps out of the garden as Godfrey ate up weeds, sticks, and plants whose names she didn’t know. Weeds were any plants that the farm assistants didn’t want in the garden. They ate up all the nutrients and water that should go to the vegetables and fruit that people grew for food. Trix had seen the weeds break up sidewalks and driveways. Weeds even killed other plants.
“What are these?” Trix asked, pawing at the ground. The weed wound its way up the fence and covered it in pretty pink and white flowers.
“That’s a noxious weed or invasive species called bind weed,” Godfrey said, sounding a bit like an encyclopedia. “These ones are like super villains. They really hurt the environment around them.”
“Oh dear! We should pull these out,” Trix said, motioning Garbanzo over. “Thanks, Godfrey.”
The goat pointed out all the noxious weeds: quack grass, Canada thistle, nutsedge, buckthorn, honeysuckle and more. The list went on and on.
“Gosh,” Trix said. “There are so many of them.”

“So many were brought here by accident,” Godfrey replied. “Because they were pretty or because people from far away places missed home. But they’re hurting the plants that grew here in the first place. They taste bad, so I don’t eat ‘em.”
“So we should plant more homegrown plants,” Trix concluded. “What are the best ones?”

Godfrey rattled off some names: “Butterfly weed, cone flower, milkweed, columbine, compass plant, wild geranium, cardinal flower, black-eyed susan, blazing star, false indigo, spiderwort, blue sage, goldenrod, spice bush, rattlesnake master, wild ginger… ”
“UGH!” Garbanzo yelled, interrupting the list.
Trix sighed, trotting down a row of tomatoes to the raccoon. “What did you eat? Spit that out!”
“Foxglove and snakeweed are highly poisonous,” Godfrey pointed out.
Garbanzo made a choking sound and rubbed his tongue.
“... but what you ate isn’t either of those,” the goat added.
The racoon gave a big glare, and Trix giggled. “Let’s get back to work, you two.”

They worked long into the night and took a quick nap. When they woke up, a group of what looked like large, skinny horses peered in at them. It was so foggy and dewy out, it seemed like a dream at first.
“Deer!” Trix exclaimed. “Did you do this to the garden?”
“We were hungry,” a doe sheepishly admitted. “We saw you working and came to apologize. We brought you some seeds to grow new plants.”

One by one, each deer dropped a packet of seeds at Trix’s feet. The crepuscular animals were only awake at dawn and dusk, so it made sense why Trix hadn’t noticed them before. Both times she’d nodded off around dawn.
Trix very politely accepted their gift. “Thank you!” she called as they waved their white tails and galloped away across Central Acre Park in the misty morning.
Trix gave a sleepy smile. She had learned a lot today. Maybe her true calling was to be a scientist who studies plants.

options.label: When you make a mistake, do your best to fix it.

discussionQuestions.prompt: After working in the garden, Trix thinks she might want to be a scientist who studies plants. Would you want to be a scientist who studies plants? Why or why not?

Trix learns a lot by working in the garden with her friends. What is something you learned about the world by spending time outside or hanging out with friends? What were you doing and what did you learn?

When Trix wakes up, she tries to clean up the garden but she does not tell anyone that she is responsible for leaving the gate open. She rushes back to pretend to be asleep so she does not get in trouble. What would you do if you were Trix? Do you think Trix does the right thing? Why or why not?

articles.title: I'm Thankful for My Puppy
articles.author: Margarita Engle
articles.description: In this mirror décima poem, a speaker goes on a run with their dog.
articles.intro: Décima mirror poetry began in Spain and is written in Latin America. It has 10 lines with 8 syllables per line. In the mirror décima, the rhyming pattern of the poem's second half repeats back (or "mirrors") the first half of the poem. In this décima poem by Margarita Engle, a Cuban-American author, the speaker shares what they are thankful for, or what they want to say thank you to.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the speaker does with their puppy.
articles.permissions: "I'm Thankful for My Puppy" Copyright (c) 2019 Margarita Engle. Originally published in Thanku: Poems of Gratitude edited by Miranda Paul and published by Lerner Publishing Group, Millbrook Press. Reprinted by permission of the author.

blocks.text: I’m grateful for the bouncy way
your four legs lead me on a race
at this wild, impossible pace
that invites me to run, leap, play!
I can’t imagine a whole day
of dull commands like down, sit, stay.
I love the colorful array
of trees and birds in each new place,
as your swift legs lead the footrace
back home to our restful doorway.

options.label: spending time with animals can be joyful.
you must learn the rules first to have fun playing.
to be healthy and happy, you should exercise every day.
learning about others can help you learn about yourself.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In a mirror décima poem, the rhyme scheme of the poem's second half reflects back (or "mirrors") that of the first half of the poem (abbaaccddc). What action is "mirrored" in the poem? Why do you think the author chose to structure this poem as a mirror décima?
The speaker shares that she is thankful for her puppy. What are you thankful for? As a creative extension, write a mirror décima poem about something you are thankful for.
The speaker is grateful for time spent with her pet. Do you have any pets? If so, how do you feel about spending time with them? If not, who do you like to spend time with?
In the poem, the speaker treats her puppy like a good friend. In your opinion, can humans truly be friends with animals? Why or why not?

articles.title: Dream Home for Earthworms
articles.author: Susan Yoder Ackerman
articles.description: A family learns the sometimes icky yet interesting truth about earthworms as they garden together.
articles.intro: Earthworms may seem icky — but they are actually very important! In this text, Oscar and Rosie help their mom plant flowers and learn how worms help their garden.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how earthworms help the garden.
articles.permissions: "Dream Home for Earthworms" by Susan Yoder Ackerman, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “What’s in your pocket, Oscar?” asked Rosie. “Did you catch another toad?”
Mom came out of the garage, a pot of zinnias in each hand. “Oh, Oscar. Please be careful! I don’t want any of your little friends ending up in the washing machine!”
Oscar reached into his pocket and gently pulled on something. Something soft and thin and pink.
“I rescued it,” he explained. “It’s an earthworm. But it wasn’t in the earth. It was out on the sidewalk on the cement.”
It had rained hard the night before. Now the pavement was covered with worms.
“Earthworms always feel kind of wet and watery,” Rosie said. “How come they aren’t fine in the rain?”
“They are,” Mom said. “Their little bodies are mostly water, and they need to stay moist. It’s usually too dry for them above ground.”
“Oh,” interrupted Rosie. “I get it. When it rains, it’s wet enough for them to come up and explore!”
“I don’t know if they’re exploring,” said Mom, laughing. “But they don’t always make it back down before the rain stops.”
The worm wriggled and curled in the palm of Oscar’s hand. “This guy didn’t seem very happy on the sidewalk,” he said.
“Of course not,” said Rosie. “He wants to go home to the dirt. Worms can’t dig through cement.”
“They need something soft,” muttered Oscar, looking around.

Mom carried the flowerpots over to the garden. She knelt down and started pulling raggedy old plants out of the ground. “It’s getting too hot for lettuce,” she said. “We’ll put summer flowers in this row instead. Rosie, can you take the lettuce to the compost pile? And Oscar, this might be just the place for your worm.”

“What about my sandbox?” Oscar said. “Sand is nice and soft. I’ll take my worm there.” He ran to the sandbox, where trucks and shovels were half buried in a white sparkling pile of sand. He started digging a deep hole where he could bury the worm.
“Wait!” said Mom. “This guy is an earthworm, and sand isn’t really earth.”
“What do you mean?” Rosie asked. “Isn’t sand just white dirt?”

“Well, sand is a type of soil,” said Mom. “But it’s made entirely of tiny bits of rock and minerals. It pours and slides around, so worms can’t build tunnels in it. It dries out fast too — too fast for worms. Remember, they need to stay moist.”

She dug a hole in the soft dark dirt where a lettuce plant had been growing. “Good garden soil contains sand, but it also has broken-down rotted leaves and other stuff called organic matter. It’s nice and crumbly and holds water.”
Rosie peered into the hole. “Eek!” she shrieked. “There’s a worm here already!”
“It might be looking for something to eat. Worms find dead leaves and grass near the soil’s surface. They grab a mouthful, then carry it down through tunnels into the soil.”
“Is that why you spread chopped leaves all around the garden plants?” Rosie wanted to know. “So the worms will have something to eat?”

“Leaf mulch helps the flowers and the worms both,” Mom said. “Decaying plants have healthy bits that living plants and worms both need. That old lettuce you threw on the compost pile? When it breaks down, I’ll put it back here for the worms and next year’s lettuce.”

Oscar knelt down and dangled the worm over soft brown earth. “He’s wiggling! I think he likes it,” he said. He carefully laid the worm in the hole, where it quickly disappeared into the crumbly soil. “He must have found a tunnel.”

Mom placed a zinnia into the hole and began filling the hole with soil. “These little zinnias like worm tunnels too. The tunnels are like tiny water pipes where water can drain away and air can move. Plants can put their roots into those tunnels, finding food and water.”
Rosie said, “Maybe the zinnias would like it better if the worms weren’t eating the leaves and bits of food the flowers need.”

“But here’s the cool part,” Mom said. “It’s all about worm poop. Or, as the scientists call it, ‘castings.’ A worm can eat its own weight in soil each day, and then it also poops that much. But the worm’s tummy is like a magic machine. When the poop comes out, it still has lots of healthy bits that plants need to grow well. But now they’re broken down and easier for a plant to soak up.”
“I think my worm is already working for our zinnias,” said Oscar. “Probably going to get a mouthful of leaves now. That’s so awesome.”
Rosie and Oscar helped their mother plant the rest of the zinnias and spread leaf mulch over any bare spots in the garden.
At bedtime that night, lightning flashed and rain dashed against the windowpane. Oscar looked up at Mom. “More worms!” he said, grinning.
“Not in your pocket, I hope!” she said.
“Nope, more worms for me to carry from the sidewalk to the garden tomorrow,” Oscar said. “We’ll have the best zinnias in town!”
