discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, Oscar and Rosie learn about earthworms from their mother. Many people think of earthworms as slimy or gross. Thinking about this text, why is it important not to judge a book by its cover? How can Mom's lesson help people think about other creatures people may not like based on their looks?

In the text, Mom helps Oscar and Rosie learn more about earthworms by showing them their home and telling them how they help in the garden. Mom helps her kids learn "by doing," or by experience. Why do you think learning "by doing" is helpful? Do you learn by experience, or would you rather learn about something by reading about it? Why?

In the text, Mom explains that the plants use the tunnels that worms build when they want to grow their roots. What are some other animals and/or plants that have a symbiotic relationship, or one where living things help each other? How do they work together to stay alive?

articles.title: Building Liberty
articles.author: Susan Yoder Ackerman
articles.description: A girl in Paris writes letters with exciting updates about The Statue of Liberty's construction.
articles.intro: A statue is a piece of art in the shape of a human or animal that is often made out of stone or metal. The Statue of Liberty was created by French artist Frederic Auguste Bartholdi at the workshop of Gaget, Gauthier & Company. In this story Colette, a daughter of Gaget, writes exciting letters updating her American cousin, Philip, on the statue.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why the sculpture is called the Statue of Liberty.
articles.permissions: "Building Liberty" by Susan Yoder Ackerman , Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: October 28, 1876
Paris
Dear Cousin Philip,
Happy 100th Birthday! Of course, I know you’re only eight like me. But Papa says that now your country, the United States, has had its freedom for a whole century.

Did you know that French children like me — and grown-ups too — are giving money to make a birthday gift for your country! It’s being made right here in Paris by Papa’s friend Mr. Bartholdi. He is making a gigantic sculpture to remind people of liberty.
Papa says it will be the tallest statue in the world. But it will take a long time to finish.
Your cousin,
Colette Gaget
Paris
Dear Cousin Philip,
You asked so many questions in your last letter! I guess now that Mr. Bartholdi is building his statue in Papa’s workshop, you think I know everything. Well, I’ll tell you what I can.

First, Mr. Bartholdi took clay and made a four-foot-high model of a lady. He made a torch of freedom for her to carry. He made her walking away from broken chains. She carries a tablet with the date of American independence on it: July 4, 1776. And her crown will have seven rays to represent the seven seas and the seven continents of the world.

When Mr. Bartholdi was happy with his small statue, he made bigger and bigger copies out of plaster until he had one 38 feet tall. It was so big that Papa built a special shed in the workyard to hold it. But it was only one-quarter of the size Mr. Bartholdi wants the statue to be when it is finally done.

You will not believe what they did next. They cut the plaster model into big slices! That’s because they can’t build a full-size model of the statue in one piece — it would be too big. So they have to make one section at a time, instead. They take each slice and measure very carefully, over and over, in order to make a full-size copy.
There’s so much to do. Papa has 50 men working on it every day!
Your cousin,
Colette Gaget
January 5, 1880
Paris
Dear Cousin Philip,

These days there’s hardly room for the cat to walk through Papa’s shop! There are layers of the Statue of Liberty everywhere! Each layer is about 30 feet wide and 12 feet high — as big as a house! You asked me if the finished statue will be made of plaster. Oh, no! It’s going to be made of thin copper. To get the copper the right shape, wooden molds made of lots and lots of boards are built using the plaster model. Then, when sheets of copper are hammered into the wooden molds, they will be exactly the same shape as the model! It’s so clever. Papa’s workers will then bolt and rivet the sheets of copper together to make the statue.

But there was a big problem. Mr. Bartholdi didn’t know how he was going to make the thin copper strong enough so that the statue wouldn’t bend or blow over in the wind. He thought of filling the statue with sand or stones, but it is much too big.

Luckily he talked to Mr. Eiffel, the man who builds bridges. Some people call him the Magician of Iron. Mr. Eiffel had the great idea of making a wrought-iron skeleton inside the statue to hold it up.
Your cousin,
Colette Gaget
August 20, 1884
Paris
Dear Cousin Philip,
The statue is finished! She towers over the buildings of Paris.
On July 4th Mr. Bartholdi proudly led American officials up the spiral staircase inside the statue to her crown. The view of the city from there is wonderful!

I often stand by the workyard gate to take money from people who want to see her before she goes to America. Her reddish copper color will become a dull green in time, but I think she will always be beautiful.

I’m glad the Americans don’t have the money yet to finish building the pedestal where she will stand. I will miss her when she goes to her island in New York. I can’t remember Papa’s shop without the statue.
Your cousin,
Colette Gaget
March 30, 1885
Paris
Dear Cousin Philip,
Mr. Bartholdi has lost his patience. He is taking the statue apart and packing it up. He wants to see it standing in New York Harbor. Coming, ready or not!
Each piece is carefully marked before it is packed. I hope it won't be too hard for the Americans to figure out how to put the puzzle together! There are more than 200 huge crates.

I'm glad that the American schoolchildren are sending their pennies to help pay for the huge pedestal the Statue of Liberty needs to stand on. It's so nice that they like the gift the French are giving them.
And here's another gift just for you. When the ship arrives in New York in June, with all the pieces of the Statue of Liberty, I will be waving from the deck. Look for me; I'm all grown up!
Till then,
Colette Gaget

articles.title: No Road Will Take You There
articles.author: Susan Yoder Ackerman
articles.description: A young girl learns all about Ocracoke Island during a visit with her cousin.
articles.intro: Ocracoke Island is a very small island off the coast of North Carolina. In order to get to Ocracoke Island, you must take a plane or a ferry, which is a boat that carries people back and forth from one place to another. In this text, a young girl named Hannah learns all about Ocracoke Island during a visit with her cousin, Skip.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Hannah learns about how people keep Ocracoke Island safe.
articles.permissions: "No Road Will Take You There" by Susan Yoder Ackerman, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: I watched for the island, but all I could see was bright blue water, flat and big as the sky. I stood on tiptoe to see over the railing of the ferry. I’d never been to my cousin’s island home and didn’t know what to look for.

“Hannah, look! There’s Ocracoke Island!” Mom called out. At first all I could see was a dark bumpy line where the sky and the water touched. As the ferry got closer, I saw sand and low brushy trees, and then the dock.
The boat horn blared. I covered my ears.

I covered my eyes too, the minute we got off the boat, when the first thing I saw was my cousin Skip holding a squirming crab between his thumb and finger, and dancing around on his wet, sandy bare feet.
“Skip! Don’t be a pest!” said Aunt Mim, as she gave us big hugs. Then we piled into the car —without the crab.
“How was the ferry, Hannah?” Uncle Jack asked. “Only way to get here is by ferry or plane!”
“Fun!” I said. As far as I could see were sand dunes and grass and scrubby pines.
Uncle Jack saw me looking. “Ocracoke is really just a big sand bar,” he said. “It’s part of a group of islands called the Outer Banks. They’re barrier islands, between the ocean and the mainland.”
“This side’s calm,” said Skip, “but wait till you see the big waves on the Atlantic side!”

“Maybe you’d like to see who was here on the island before people ever settled it,” said Uncle Jack. He turned the car off the highway. We stopped where a dozen shaggy ponies were eating grass near the beach. “Wild Bankers, we call them.”
I gasped with delight. “Oh, can I pet them? I love the brown one with the white star!”
“Wild ponies,” said Skip. “Get it? It’s not a petting zoo!”
“OK,” I said. I took a deep breath. I hoped I could have fun with Skip this week, but I wasn’t sure.
“But where did they come from?” I knew ponies weren’t like squirrels and deer and raccoons, the kind of animals you see everywhere in North Carolina.

“Maybe they swam ashore from shipwrecks hundreds of years ago,” Uncle Jack said. “They might even be from the lost colony of Roanoke. Nobody really knows. The ponies are protected now, but we used to have beach races with them when I was a kid.”

I wished I could pet the brown pony’s woolly mane, even if I couldn’t ride her along the beach, but soon we were heading back down the long, straight highway. There wasn’t a single building, restaurant, or shopping mall to be seen. “Most of Ocracoke is protected as a national seashore,” Uncle Jack told me and Mom. “The village of Ocracoke is the only place people can live.”

And the village was tiny! A few narrow streets with stores and restaurants, some neat old houses, and lots of fishing boats in the harbor. “You’ve come at a good time,” Aunt Mim said. “Not too many tourists yet. It gets so crowded here in summer!”
Uncle Jack slowed down to pull into a driveway. “We’re home!”

My sandals crunched on sand and broken white oyster shells when I got out of the car. I thought I smelled fish. A great big tree spread over the small white house. “A live oak,” Mom said, admiring it. Through the branches I could see the flat blue sea stretching to the sky.
I picked up my backpack and followed Skip inside. Something smelled warm and good, like peach cobbler baking in the oven.
“Bet your house doesn’t have a hole in the floor!” Skip whispered to me.
“Of course it doesn’t!” I said.
“Ours does,” Skip said proudly.
“No way,” I protested. “Why?”

“All these old houses were built with them,” he said. “So they wouldn’t wash away in storms. People cut a hole in the floor with a plug in it. Then if the water rose, they could knock out the plug and let the waves fill the house. When the water drained back out, they just cleaned up, put in another plug, and went on living there.”
Suddenly Skip flashed a grin at me. “Come on,” he said. He bounded back outside. “I’ll show you around town.”
“Just us?” I hurried to catch up.
“Sure,” he answered. “We can walk everywhere. It’s safe. That is, if you’re not afraid of pirates—”
“Pirates aren’t real!” I said.

“They are! Or at least, they were! When ships came across the ocean to America, Ocracoke was the first land they saw. But the captains didn’t always know where the water was deep enough for ships. Even now the sand bars are always shifting around. So there were lots of shipwrecks — and lots of pirates to rob the ships.”
We had reached the docks, but the boats looked like fishing boats, not pirate ships. We watched for a minute, then Skip pulled me forward. He sure had a lot of energy!

“Do you like fudge?” he asked. “We have the best fudge store just up this street. I’ll treat. And then I’ll show you the lighthouse, and my school, and where Dad gets our fish, and the old cemetery, and—”
Now it was my turn to grin. My island visit had begun, and Skip wasn’t so bad after all!

articles.title: The Day the Dogs Disappeared
articles.author: Shelley Walden
articles.description: Three coyotes switch places with dogs in an attempt to win over humans' love.
articles.intro: A coyote is a North American animal that looks close to a fox or a dog. In many folktales from Native American tribes, the coyote is often a clever and tricky character. In this folktale, three coyotes come up with a plan to make humans, or people, love them.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the coyotes’ feelings about living with humans.
articles.permissions: "The Day the Dogs Disappeared" by Shelley Walden, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: There was a time, many moons ago, when humans and coyotes lived in harmony. People told stories of the great trickster Coyote and how he brought fire to humans, winter to the world, and stars to the Milky Way. They honored coyotes with dances and songs. They even saved the best scraps of meat for them.
But as the years passed, people’s relationship with the land changed. They plowed the prairies, chopped the forests, and paved the deserts. They even found new animals to adore...
Dogs! The people oohed and aahed over pugs and Chihuahuas, Shih Tzus and terriers, retrievers and cockapoos. Their hearts became so filled with dogs that there was no room left for coyotes.
“The people have forgotten us,” Chief Coyote said one day. “They no longer tell stories of our adventures.”
“It is true,” Brother Coyote said. “They gave bones to our ancestors. Now those mongrels get them all.”
“We must find our way back into their hearts,” Sister Coyote said.
“Then we should go into their homes, for that is how dogs won their love,” Chief said.
“Good idea,” Sister said. “But first we need to get those pesky dogs out of the way somehow.”
“Simple,” Brother said. “We’ll convince them to switch places with us.”

It was a very coyote thing to say. For they had a long history of impersonating other animals — like woodpecker, snowbird, and even buffalo. Although it never ended well, coyotes kept trying to be something they weren’t.
The very next day, the coyotes sneaked into the nearby city. Whenever they found a dog, they chewed through its rope or opened its pen. Then they tried to convince the dog to swap places.
“But I don’t want to leave my human,” Collie said. “I love him.”
“Me too,” Hound said.
“Ruff!” Mastiff said. He wasn’t the smartest dog in the litter, but he sure was cute.
“Imagine how great life would be without a leash,” Chief said. “You could sniff every tree you wanted and chase squirrels all day long.”
“SQUIRRELS?!” the dogs said.
“Yes,” Sister said. “There are more squirrels in the wild than you can even imagine.”
So the dogs agreed. But first, Chief Coyote had a question.
“Tell us, dogs, how did you weasel your way into humans’ hearts?”
“We let them pet us,” Collie said.
“We played fetch,” Hound said.
“And we chased the mailman!” Mastiff said, as a glob of drool fell from his mouth.
Armed with this knowledge, the coyotes went to the humans’ houses, while the dogs ventured out into the wilderness.

Although the humans missed their dogs, they were so glad to see furry animals again that they welcomed the coyotes into their homes. The coyotes tried to follow the dogs’ advice. They let the people pet them, but their fur was crawling with fleas. They played fetch, but they weren’t very good at retrieving balls. They even tried to chase the mailman. They tore his mailbag and ripped the envelopes to shreds.
But the harder the coyotes tried, the sadder the humans became. Coyotes just weren’t the same as dogs.

When darkness fell, the coyotes howled. They had found their way into the homes of men but weren’t happy there. They missed seeing the sun set over the mountains, casting pink light on the juniper trees. They longed to breathe fresh air, chase rabbits, and feel dirt on their paws.

That night, the coyotes said goodbye to the humans and returned to their den, where the dogs were waiting. They, too, had had a rough time. When they tried to befriend a skunk, he sprayed them. When they sniffed bushes, they got covered in thorns. And they couldn’t catch even one squirrel. Their coats were matted, their stomachs growled, and their paws were sore.
“We missed you!” the dogs said, licking the coyotes’ muzzles.
“They really are delightful creatures,” Brother Coyote whispered to Sister.
“We tried to take your place,” Chief Coyote said. “But we are not meant for human homes, just as you are not meant for the wilderness. Come, my friends. It’s time to go home.”
When the dogs returned to the city, they were so excited to see their humans that they slobbered all over them. The humans didn’t care. They were just happy to have their friends back.

Although the coyotes’ plan had failed, it reminded humans of how lucky they were to have dogs in their lives. For that, the humans would always be grateful. For their part, the coyotes learned that they were happiest when they were true to their nature.
From that day forward, humans, dogs, and coyotes lived in harmony. And at night, humans told stories about the clever coyotes and the day the dogs disappeared.

articles.title: Mary and the Peregrines
articles.author: Maria Hlohowskyj
articles.description: Mary Hennen helps protect the peregrine falcons of Chicago.
articles.intro: Peregrine falcons are a type of bird that were once endangered, or in danger of dying out. In 1988, peregrine falcons started to nest in Chicago, a large city in the state of Illinois, where animals do not usually make their homes. This article describes how a woman named Mary Hennen spends her time with the peregrine falcons of Chicago.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Mary’s actions towards the peregrine falcons.
articles.permissions: "Mary and the Peregrines" by Maria Hlohowskyj, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Mary Hennen loves teaching people about animals, especially birds. She helps manage the Bird Collection at the Field Museum in Chicago, Illinois. The museum collection does not include live birds, but it does have more than 500,000 bird feathers, bones, eggs, and more. Some of the items are very old. Scientists study them to learn more about — and help — living birds. One bird that the collection helped is the peregrine falcon.

When Mary was a kid, there were no peregrine falcons in Illinois and hardly any in the rest of the country. At that time, many farmers used a bug killer called DDT to help crops grow. They didn’t know that DDT hurt birds. But people noticed that there were fewer peregrine falcons each year.

Scientists compared eggshells from new peregrine nests with old eggshells in the Field Museum collection. They learned that DDT caused the new eggshells to be very thin. When a mother falcon sat on her eggs — crack! — the shells broke. No baby birds were hatching.

The United States soon passed a law to protect the peregrine falcons and other endangered animals. Farmers stopped using DDT. Scientists learned how to hatch the thin peregrine eggs safely in nurseries and release the grown birds back into the wild.

But the scientists got a big surprise. Two of the peregrines they released chose to nest in downtown Chicago! Usually, peregrine falcons nest on the sides of cliffs, where great horned owls sometimes attack the nests. The falcons must have thought the city skyscrapers were like cliffs—but better, because there were no owls.
Mary got a job watching the peregrines. She wrote down what they did each day, so the scientists could see how well the peregrines did living wild in the city.

Mary loved watching the birds so much, she decided to spend her life helping peregrines! Now, when she’s not working at the museum, she directs the Chicago Peregrine Program, a volunteer group that helps these special birds.
Mary and other volunteers keep track of where each peregrine in Illinois lives, hunts, and builds a nest. They do this by putting a small metal bracelet, or band, around the bird’s leg.
Each band is stamped with a different number. When someone spots a bird and reads the band, researchers know exactly which falcon it is.

Thanks to Mary and other falcon watchers, we know that the peregrines still love living in downtown Chicago. Today, dozens of pairs of falcons nest in and near Chicago. That means part of Mary’s job is talking to people who wake up to discover that peregrine falcons have laid eggs on their balcony!

Having peregrine falcons for neighbors can be alarming. They are messy eaters and poop everywhere. They chase anyone who gets too close to their chicks, and they make a lot of noise. But Mary teaches people how to respect and enjoy their peregrine neighbors.

You don’t have to have a peregrine nest on your porch to watch these amazing birds grow up. Mary and her team installed tiny cameras in some of the nests so people everywhere can watch peregrine chicks grow up each summer. Have fun watching!

discussionQuestions.prompt: The article explains how Mary Hennen spends her time helping the peregrine falcons. Think of a time when you helped an animal. How did it feel? If you have not helped an animal, think of a time you saw someone else help an animal. It could be from a TV show, a movie, or a book. How do you think that person felt when they helped the animal?

The article explains how DDT caused the peregrine falcons harm. What other human actions can you think of that might harm animals? Is there anything being done to stop these harmful actions? What could you do to help stop these harmful actions?

Mary Hennen spends a lot of her time tracking and researching the peregrine falcons. Why do you think it is so important for scientists to spend time researching? What do scientists learn by researching? Think of a time when you researched something. Maybe you researched a topic for a school assignment, or maybe you researched something outside of school that was interesting for you. What did you learn from your research? Why is it important to research a topic if you want to know more about it?

The article explains how some people in Chicago do not like having peregrine falcons nest on their balconies because they can make a mess or be noisy. Are there any noisy or messy animals in your neighborhood? Do you think it's still important to protect these animals? Why or why not?

articles.title: A Nest on My Porch
articles.author: Buffy Silverman
articles.description: A young bird notes in his journal how a pair of robins nest, lay eggs, and care for their chicks.
articles.intro: A young boy writes in his journal, or a notebook where someone shares what they are thinking, about what he sees through his window.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the young boy’s point of view.
articles.permissions: "A Nest on My Porch" by Buffy Silverman, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Journal date April 15
Grandma gave me this cool nature notebook for my birthday. I'm going to write about all the animals I see.

This morning a robin with grass in its beak flew to a post on our front porch. Another robin sang in the maple tree. I watched through binoculars from the living room window. Last year a pair of robins raised a family on our porch. Maybe these robins will too!
Journal date April 20

Yay! The robins built a nest! They worked all week, carrying twigs, grass, and mud. Mom says the robin with a black head and a dark, rusty red breast is the papa. Mama Robin's head is gray, and her feathers are not as brightly colored. She's harder to spot. I hope that will keep squirrels and other egg eaters from following her to the nest.
Mama Robin did most of the building. She shaped the nest into a little cup by pressing her chest inside it. Her feathers got so muddy! Then she added a soft blanket of grass inside.
Journal date April 25

I can peek right into the robin nest from the upstairs window. Mama Robin laid her first egg four days ago. Now that four blue eggs fill the nest, she sits there most of the day to keep them warm. I saw Papa Robin bring her a worm to eat! If anyone comes too close to the nest, the robins swoop overhead and yell tuk, tuk, tuk .
Journal date May 10

Mama and Papa Robin are busy, busy, busy! Two babies hatched five days ago, and two more the next day. The four hungry nestlings open their beaks wide all day, begging for food. At first Mama Robin stayed and kept the babies warm while Papa looked for food. But now that the nestlings have some feathers, both parents fly back and forth, bringing worms and grubs. Two of the nestlings opened their eyes today!
Journal date May 15
The nest is getting crowded. The baby robins squirm and stretch and flap their little wings. Their bodies are covered with feathers. Their beaks are usually open, peeping and begging for food.

But yesterday a cat snuck into the bushes and stared up at the nest. Mama and Papa Robin chattered teek, teek, teek and flew at the cat until it slunk away. While the parents squawked, the babies stayed still and silent in the nest.
Journal date May 17

Two of the babies left the nest today. Mom says that's called fledging. I spotted the fledglings hopping under the bushes. Papa Robin followed, feeding them when they opened their beaks. Two babies are still peeping in the nest. Mama Robin brings their meals.
Journal date May 19

The last robin fledged! It stood on the edge of the nest, flapped its wings, then tumbled down to land in a potted plant. It peeped for Mama Robin, and after a snack, it hopped and fluttered and even flew a little, but it's still kind of wobbly. I'll miss my robin family. I hope Mama Robin lays more eggs soon!

options.label: Keeping a journal helps kids remember things better.
A boy watches robins build a nest and raise a family.
Birds are different from other types of animals.
Robins are birds that like to be around people.

by explaining why the robins built their nest at the boy's house

In the text the young boy keeps a journal of what he notices. Have you ever kept a journal? Would you like to? Why do you think that scientists, writers, and other people keep journals?
How are the ways that the robins take care of their chicks similar to and different from the ways that humans take care of their babies?

articles.title: Kwanzaa Days
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: A young speaker describes some of the traditions of Kwanzaa.
articles.intro: Kwanzaa is an important celebration in African American culture that takes place each year from December 26th to January 1st. Culture is the language, customs, and values of a group of people. In this poem, the speaker explains how to celebrate, or honor, the holiday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens in each stanza. A stanza is a group of lines in a poem.
articles.permissions: "Kwanzaa Days" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday Stew: A Kid's Portion of Holiday and Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: In the seven mornings,
Greet me with Habari gani ?
And I will try to answer
Umoja or Imani .
During the seven days,
Lay out the mkeka and cup.
And I will count the muhindi
And gifts that you wrap up.
In the seven evenings,
Light the candles: red, black, green.
And I will grow up knowing
What my African roots mean.

options.label: Children enjoy holidays more than adults.
Some holidays take a week or longer to celebrate.
You should learn about a new thing before trying it.
Holiday practices help people learn about themselves.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the central message of the poem?

options.label: The speaker is proud to learn about Kwanzaa.
The speaker is struggling to understand Kwanzaa.
The speaker wishes they received more presents on Kwanzaa.
The speaker hopes that more people will learn about Kwanzaa.

questions.prompt: How does the speaker feel about Kwanzaa?

options.label: It describes how the speaker's feelings change.
It describes the gifts that the speaker is wrapping.
It shows that the speaker is talking to their family.
It shows which day the speaker's family spends wrapping gifts.

questions.prompt: How does line 8 add to the reader's understanding of the poem?

options.label: beginnings.
flags.
plants.
words.

questions.prompt: In line 12, the word "roots" most closely means —
How do line 1, line 5, and line 9 add to the reader's understanding of the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you celebrate Kwanzaa? If so, how is the speaker's celebration the same or different from how you celebrate the holiday? If you do not celebrate Kwanzaa, how does the celebration described in the poem seem the same or different from holidays you celebrate or know about?

The speaker says that celebrating Kwanzaa will help them "grow up knowing / What my African roots mean" (Lines 11-12). How do holidays help us stay connected to our past and our history? What are some ways you stay connected to your family or your community's history? What does understanding where you come tell you about yourself?

articles.title: Why Leaves Change Color
articles.author: An Ojibwe Story - Retold by Margi Preus
articles.description: According to Ojibwe legend, leaves change color each fall to remember Nanabozho's happy accident.
articles.intro: The Ojibwe people are among the largest groups of native, or first, people in North America. They live in the cold climates of Canada and the northern part of the United States. In this story, author Margi Preus shares an Ojibwe story about why the leaves change color in the fall.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Nanabozho feels throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Why Leaves Change Color" An Ojibwe Story - Retold by Margi Preus, Ladybug . © Cricket Media, Inc.. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Nanabozho is considered the great-uncle of the Ojibwe people. He is part man, part manito (spirit) and so is capable of silly mischief as well as great deeds.

When Nanabozho was a boy and the earth was new, his grandmother, Nokomis, made him some paints. She mixed dyes out of moonlight and shadows, snowflakes and sand. She made silky blues from night and soft greens from reindeer moss. She squeezed the sparkle out of shooting stars. Then she put all the colors into pots and put the pots into a pouch for her grandson.
“Make beauty, Nanabozho,” she said. “Paint the flowers today.”

Nanabozho skipped off to a field full of pale white coneflowers. He dipped his brush into the purple paint pot. Then he painted the petals gently, one by one, being careful not to slop over the edges.

He saw a ladybug crawling among the blossoms. In those days, ladybugs were plain red. Nanabozho painted a black dot on the ladybug. That was interesting, he thought, and he painted another spot. This was great fun, and he ran about the field, dotting every ladybug he could find. He only missed one. If you ever see a ladybug without spots, that’s a great-great-granddaughter of the one he missed.
Nanabozho caught a bee in his hand. This was before bees had stingers. They didn’t have stripes, either. Nanabozho painted stripes around the bee’s body.
What else can I decorate? he wondered.
Raccoon’s plain, gray tail hung down from a tree. Nanabozho quickly painted rings around it. That was pretty!
Nanabozho caught Heron and painted him blue. He grabbed Fox and painted her red. Nanabozho put spots on Salamander. He painted a red cap on Woodpecker and a black cap on Chickadee.
Then he noticed Sparrow, perched on a low tree branch.
“Poor Sparrow,” Nanabozho cried, “not a shimmery feather nor a speck of bright color… ”
Sparrow hopped onto a higher branch.
“Come here!” Nanabozho called.
Sparrow hopped one branch higher.
“I want to make you beautiful!”
Sparrow fluttered yet higher, perched, and peeked down at Nanabozho.

Nanabozho slung the pouch with his paint pots and brushes over his shoulder and climbed onto the lower branches. “Don’t you hear me, little brother?” said Nanabozho. “You’d look handsome with a red coat.”
Sparrow hopped up two more branches. Nanabozho struggled after him. “Orange?” called Nanabozho.
Sparrow flitted to the uppermost branch and watched as Nanabozho clambered up the tree.

“Pink and purple stripes? Yellow-green feathers?” He reached out to grab Sparrow, but the little bird darted away. Nanabozho lost his balance and tumbled backward. His shirt snagged on a branch, and there Nanabozho swung.

But the paints and dyes! They spilled! Every pot of twilight, dawn, and sunset. Every pot of flame and fire, sunlight and starlight, gold and silver. And worse, Wind chose that very moment to rush over to see what was happening. Wind’s sudden whoosh spattered the falling colors all over the trees.
The trees shrieked, “Look what you’ve done! Our lovely green leaves!”
“I’m sorry!” said Nanabozho. “It was an accident.”
“Oh!” the trees groaned and shook their branches. “Nanabozho, you must scrub us clean!”
Nanabozho looked around at the trees. The colors were everywhere! How could he ever clean them up?
All the birds and animals were gathering to see what had happened. They stared up at the trees.
“Aaaah… ,” said Raven.
“Oooooh… ,” said Owl.
“Beautiful,” croaked Frog.
Chickadee twittered, “So pretty! So pretty!”
The trees hushed their shaking branches and listened. Were they really prettier now? Oak blushed deep red. Aspen’s golden leaves glowed.
Then Maple whispered, “Don’t feel bad, Nanabozho. I think I will like my new colors.”
“Thank you, Maple,” said Nanabozho.
Soon the other trees decided their bright new colors were not so bad after all. They forgave Nanabozho, too.
Now, every year we remember when Nanabozho spilled his paints and made the trees so beautiful. And every year, Maple, the one who forgave Nanabozho first, is the prettiest of all.

options.label: Adults often know what is best for us.
If you help others, one day they will help you.
When we work together good things can happen.
Sometimes mistakes can lead to something beautiful.

calm because he waits for the animals to be painted.
thoughtful because he wants to make the animals look beautiful.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In some parts of the world, the leaves change color in the fall. Do the leaves change color where you live? If so, do you enjoy this season? Why or why not? If not, what happens in the fall where you live? Which of the four seasons is your favorite? Why do you like it best?
Noticing the changing colors of the leaves in fall is one way to observe and appreciate nature. What are some other ways we can enjoy nature? How does it make you feel to spend time in nature?

Some people would say that Nanabozho's actions in "Why Leaves Change Color" are an example of a "happy accident." What do you think this expression means? Have you ever caused a "happy accident" like Nanabozho does in the story? If so, what was it and what did you do after it happened? After reading about Nanabozho, what might you do next time?

The folktale shares a story of why the leaves change color in the fall. Have you heard this story before or another story like it? Why do you think people from all over the world have stories to explain parts of nature? Think of something else that is hard to explain, (e.g. why zebras have stripes, why spiders have eight legs) and create a story to explain it.

articles.title: Cat
articles.author: Marilyn Singer
articles.description: A cat describes its favorite way to spend a chilly day.
articles.intro: Marilyn Singer is the author of more than 100 books for children and young adults. In this poem, Singer shares about a cat.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s point of view on their owner.
articles.permissions: "Cat" by Marilyn Singer from TURTLE IN JULY. © 1989 Macmillan. Used with permission by the author.

blocks.text: I prefer
warm fur,
a perfect fire
to lie beside,
a cozy lap
where I can nap,
an empty chair
when she’s not there.
I want heat
on my feet
on my nose
on my hide.
No cat I remember
dislikes December
inside.

options.label: People should care for their pets.
Cats like to be cozy during cold weather.
It is hard to get warm when you are a cat.
We can learn a lot from watching our pets.

questions.prompt: Which describes the main idea of the poem?

options.label: it helps the reader understand who the speaker is.
it explains how the owner feels about their cat.
it helps the reader picture the house.
it explains the problem in the poem.

options.label: the speaker has just come in from the cold.
the speaker wants to feel warm all over.
the speaker cannot get comfortable.
the speaker needs a winter jacket.

options.label: to put away from view.
to try not to be found.
the secrets one keeps.
the skin of an animal.

questions.prompt: As it is used in line 12, "hide" most closely means —
What is the cat's point of view about their owner?

discussionQuestions.prompt: This poem is told from the point of view of a pet cat. Do you have any pets? If you do, what would they share about what they prefer, like the cat in the poem? If you do not have any pets, do you think this poem would be different if it was told from a different pet's point of view, such as a dog? Why?

The cat shares that "No cat I remember / dislikes December / inside." What do you like to do in December, or when it is cold out? Are you more similar to the cat, or do you like to be outside during the winter? Why?

Why do you think the poet chose to tell this poem from the point of view of a cat? How would this poem be different if it was written by the cat's owner? As a creative extension, rewrite this poem from the point of view of the cat's owner, using the same rhyme scheme that the poet uses in "Cat."

articles.title: Aliens from the Big Water (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: A horse named Trix and her friends leave their farm to explore an aquarium.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. The word "alien" is sometimes used when something seems strange or odd. This short story is about a miniature horse, or a very small horse, named Trix who goes on an adventure to an aquarium. An aquarium is a building where water animals and plants are kept and shown to visitors.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Trix’s feelings about the aquarium.
articles.permissions: "Aliens from the Big Water (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers. It’s full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, the animals are heading outside the farm and into the city for the first time.
Trix stood in front of the Central Acre Park sign with a bus pass clenched in her teeth. She waited patiently by the black bench beside the bus stop.

Trix had seen a big picture on a passing bus. It was a picture of a weird animal she’d never seen before. She wanted to learn more about it. It had a large head with eyes that stuck out on either side of its body. It also had sharp teeth and a long, smooth gray body. It lived at a place called an “Aquarium” downtown.
She had watched the humans pull out small plastic rectangles and tap them on a machine inside the bus before they took a seat. The miniature horse had observed this strange behavior for weeks.
Then one day she’d tried the bus herself. It took her all the way around the city and back again! Today, she was determined to find out about the weird animal in the bus picture.
Ten minutes went by. No bus. She sighed and stamped her foot impatiently.
Garbanzo, the raccoon, peeked out of Trix’s right saddle bag pocket and yawned. “Are we there yet?”
“No,” she mumbled around the plastic rectangle in her mouth. “We haven’t even left yet.”
“What?” Garbanzo climbed up her shiny black mane and grabbed the bus pass from Trix’s mouth. He turned it over in his furry hands. “What’s this for?”
“Perhaps money,” Pendragon said, peering out of the left saddle bag pocket.

Today, the bearded dragon lizard wore a scarf, wool hat, and puffy coat on his spiky body instead of his usual hat and bow tie. The weather in the city was much colder than he was used to. It made him want to take a nap.
“Here it comes!” shouted a small voice from the top of the Central Acre Park sign. It was Beatrice, their brown feathered friend. She wished them luck and flitted off back to the warm, safe barn.
A huge rectangular vehicle rolled into view. It came to stop in front of them with a sharp metal squeal. Trix climbed inside and tapped her pass on the card reader like she’d seen the humans do.
The other passengers didn’t seem to mind the animals on board. Their eyes were glued to their phones or books. Some of them were even asleep, mouths wide and snoring.
Trix found a seat at the back and couldn’t help but grin. She felt like an explorer on an adventure to another planet.

The bus passed all sorts of weird things. Giant bronze statues of animals with claws and large, pointed teeth. Tall buildings that towered over the bus like giants. A mirrored fountain with smiling faces projected on it. Dozens of people in fluffy marshmallow coats gliding across ice on what looked like shiny metal blades. A man speeding down the street on a strip of light-up plastic. A woman with silver skin and hair dancing on the street. A group of humans with hair as pink as Trix’s tongue.
“Aliens,” she whispered, shivering with delight. She wished she’d brought her space suit that she hadn’t gotten to test outside yet. Maybe next time.

The bus stopped among a few giant, old-looking buildings covered in a dusting of snow. They looked like they’d been made hundreds of years ago by ancient humans from another world. Trix craned her neck to look at her research assistants peeking out from the warmth of their saddle bags.

Pendragon was Trix’s sketch artist, and Garbanzo was her chief note taker. The two jumped out of the saddle bag when they entered the warm building, which was called an “Aquarium.” They had no idea what this word meant, but they wrote it down anyway.

Strange creatures peered out at them from large glass tanks and up at them from small pools of water. It was nothing like their pond at home! They’d only seen small fish, tadpoles, and a few turtles. These things were brightly colored and weirdly shaped. Some of them had huge eyes and some had no eyes at all. Some had many legs, and some had none. Some had long whiskers or wavy feelers.

Trix was like a foal in a grainery! She trotted to each tank and her warm breath fogged the cool glass in wonder. Her eyes lapped up every delicious detail, just like she had crunched up grain when she was a young horse. Everything was so weird.

There was a huge thing called a “whale” with smooth white skin and kind eyes. One creature had a long snout like a bottle. Another was squiggly like a huge worm with big, gnashing teeth. There were pink animals shaped like stars with suction cup arms, deep purple animals with built-in flashlights on their heads, and what looked like rust-colored giant bugs with razor sharp claws.
“What’s a… lobster?” Trix asked, squinting at the claw-bugs.

The only animal that did seem familiar to Trix was a thing called a “sea turtle.” It had a shell just like grumpy old Mr. McGee, the snapping turtle at home, but its legs didn’t look like they’d be good for walking. The legs looked more like fish fins.
“Trix, come over here! This is strange,” Garbanzo muttered. He shivered and pointed at the tank of see-through ooze creatures. They had no eyes, no mouths, a dozen dangling arms, and no organs.
“It’s sort of cute in a horrifying sort of way,” Pendragon whispered. He flicked his scaled tail nervously as he sketched. “It’s called a jellyfish.”
“I wonder what it tastes like.” Garbanzo grinned.
Trix gave him a fierce look and he pretended to scribble on his pad.

Trix tapped on the glass of one of the tanks. “Excuse me, what planet are you from?” she asked a large, sharp-toothed beast. Its head was shaped like the hammer the humans used to fix the fences. “Um, Shark?”
“What’s a planet?” the shark asked. “I’m from the ocean. My name is Gerald. What’s yours?”
“Trix,” the miniature horse replied. “What’s an ocean? We have a pond, but it doesn’t have anything like you in it.”
“Big water,” the shark replied with a toothy smile. “I’m a saltwater fish.”
“Big water like the lake outside?” Trix wondered.
“ Much bigger,” Gerald laughed. “And much saltier. I can’t live in freshwater like the fish you know.”
“Well, thank you, Gerald,” Trix said. “We’ll come back for another visit soon.”
“I’ll be here,” he replied cheerfully. “Next time, bring snacks.”
“What do you eat?” Pendragon gulped as the big dark eyes turned to him.
“I’d take a smallish-fish, octopus, or squid.” Gerald motioned at Garbanzo and Pendragon. “Or something like you two. I like to try new foods.”
The two research assistants couldn’t get out of there fast enough. They scuttled toward the exit.
Trix thanked the hammerhead shark again. She caught up with her friends on the stone steps.
Garbanzo listed off his code names he’d thought of for each fish. Pendragon labeled each of his drawings with the names.
“Blobby Legs, Sucker Fingers, Flashy Forehead, Pinchy,” the raccoon said. “Oh, and good old Gerald Long Face.”
“Good work, team,” Trix whinnied. “Let’s get back to the park.”
Garbanzo pulled out the plastic rectangle and waved the bus down. He couldn’t wait to get back to the safety of his dumpster.

Learning how to do new things takes practice.

You can learn a lot by visiting new places.

questions.prompt: How do paragraphs 21-23 help readers understand the story?

options.label: Trix has never seen a shark before. Garbanzo and Pendragon have seen a shark before.
Trix does not want to talk to the shark. Garbanzo and Pendragon want to talk to the shark.
Trix is excited to learn about the shark. Garbanzo and Pendragon are afraid the shark will eat them.

questions.prompt: How is Trix's point of view of the shark different from Garbanzo and Pendragon's point of view of the shark?
How does Trix feel about visiting the aquarium?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Trix is excited to see and explore a new part of the city she has never seen before. Think of a time you explored a new place. How did you feel? What did you discover?

Since Trix does not have hands, her "research assistants" Garbanzo and Pendragon help her take notes. Think of a time your friends helped you do something or a time you helped a friend. How did it feel to have friends helping you? How did it feel when you helped your friends?

Trix learns about sharks by asking Gerald questions and listening to his answers. Sometimes people interview someone, or ask questions, to learn more about something. If you could interview anyone or any animal in the world, who would you choose, and why? What questions would you ask?

Trix learns a lot about other animals by visiting the aquarium. Some people think that aquariums are not good homes for sea creatures. Other people think that aquariums do make good homes for sea creatures. Why might people think these ways? Do you think sea creatures should be placed in aquariums? Why or why not?

articles.title: Game On!
articles.author: Kris Bitar
articles.description: A girl uses video games to help a new student feel welcome at the start of the school year.
articles.intro: Kris Bitar is a writer and teacher in Aberdeen, Washington. She has taught every level in K-12 for 30 years and her joy is working with children. In this story, a girl named Jo-Jo looks for a way to help a new classmate named Dee.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Jo-Jo’s actions in the story.
articles.permissions: "Game On!" by Kris Bitar. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: “My name is Jolene Johnson, but you can call me Jo-Jo. I am excited to be back in school. I have two new puppies, and you are all invited to see them after school!” Jolene announced to the 3rd grade class when it was her turn to introduce herself. Jolene knew she was outgoing and sometimes loud. With her bright red, curly hair and mismatched socks, Jo-Jo appeared a little intimidating to the shy kids. She couldn’t help it! She loved school, her friends, and being the center of attention. Jo-Jo’s mom said she attracted friends like fruit flies to apple juice.

Mrs. Anderson, the teacher, smiled and thanked Jo-Jo for sharing. Mrs. Anderson then introduced a new kid named Dee Rogen. When he spoke, he was quiet and didn’t say much. Jo-Jo thought she had seen him at breakfast. He had been sitting alone and not eating. Mrs. Anderson then partnered him with Ben as his “buddy” for the day.

By recess, Jo-Jo had forgotten all about Dee. She played four square, swung across the monkey bars twice, and shot a hoop that didn’t make the basket. “I love being back at school,” later thought Jo-Jo as she took her seat and got ready to work on math.

At lunchtime, Jo-Jo noticed that Dee was sitting with Ben and his friends but not talking to anyone. He looks lonely, thought Jo-Jo, as she gobbled up her pizza, drank her milk, and went outside to play tag with a group that was already playing.
When the bell rang to go home for the day, Jo-Jo walked up to Mrs. Anderson and gave her a big hug. “Thank you for a great first day!” Jo-Jo said.
“I’ll see you tomorrow!” Mrs. Anderson told her. She smiled to herself about her outgoing student.

The next morning, Jo-Jo saw that Dee was by himself at breakfast again. She also saw Ben was sitting with his own group of friends. Jo-Jo didn’t like anyone to feel alone. She noticed that Dee had a shirt on that said “I Paused My Game to be Here.” Jo-Jo had an idea.
She took her lunch tray near Dee and said, “I like your shirt. What games do you play?”

Dee smiled and said his favorite was Minecraft. Jo-Jo loved that game! They spent the lunch break talking about the best ways to survive in the game by finding materials to build with and ways to find food. Ducking creatures was Dee’s favorite part, especially the dragon.

On Friday, Jo-Jo announced that she was having Minecraft Monday at her house after school and everyone in her 3rd grade class was invited. It would be from three-thirty to five o’clock. Her mom had agreed to make popcorn and give everyone juice. Jo-Jo pulled out a sign-up sheet from her backpack. Several students ran up to her, but Mrs. Anderson said sign-ups would be at recess only. The students groaned and sat back down in their seats.
After recess, Jo-Jo noticed Dee hadn’t signed up. She waited until lunchtime and asked him about it.
“I don’t really know anyone, Jo-Jo,” Dee answered. “I wouldn’t feel comfortable being there and playing by myself.”
Jo-Jo was confused because she thought he would love the idea. It was on her mind all day until she went home from school — where she had another idea!

The next morning at breakfast, she sat by Dee and told him her plan. “Hey,” she said, “I was thinking that putting on Minecraft Monday would be a big job. How about you and I be partners? Your gaming name could be Drogen.”
Dee started to smile. “Do you really want me to be part of Minecraft Monday?”
“Of course!” said Jo-Jo. “You know more than anyone about the game! We would be a great team!”
“I think so too,” said Dee, grinning wide. “I like how you put my name together to be Drogen — that’s a good gamer name. I like that a lot!”

Jo-Jo smiled. She felt good helping Dee make friends and knowing that she had made a new friend herself. Jo-Jo decided right then and there that she would invite all new students to the 3rd grade to her house for Minecraft Monday. The more friends, the better, she thought as she walked home after school. She would become the “Welcoming Committee Ambassador for 3rd Grade.” Well, if Mrs. Anderson said it was okay.

options.label: Always follow the teacher's directions.
Too many video games are bad for your brain.
Finding a common interest is a great way to make friends.
You should wait for someone to invite you to be their friend.

questions.prompt: What is the lesson of "Game On!"?

options.label: "When he spoke, he was quiet and didn't say much." (Paragraph 2)
"By recess, Jo-Jo had forgotten all about Dee." (Paragraph 3)
"She noticed that Dee had a shirt on that said 'I Paused My Game to be Here.'" (Paragraph 7)
"Dee smiled and said his favorite was Minecraft." (Paragraph 9)

questions.prompt: Which detail from the story shows that Dee is shy?

options.label: to laugh
to say yes
to work on
to think about

questions.prompt: In paragraph 10, "agreed" most closely means —

options.label: She knows how to play a lot of different sports.
She is friendly and likes talking to people.
She likes playing video games.
She has two puppies.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 18, why does Jo-Jo want to become the "Welcoming Committee Ambassador for 3rd Grade"?
How does Jo-Jo help Dee in the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Have you ever been a new kid, like Dee? How did it feel? Did anyone help you make friends like Jo-Jo helped Dee?
How do you think Jo-Jo felt after she helped Dee? Why is it important to help other people in your community? What are some other ways people can welcome newcomers to a community?
Jo-Jo was able to connect with Dee because they both enjoy video games. How do shared interests bring people together? What hobbies or activities do you like to do with others?

articles.title: A Dragon in Central Acre Park (A Central Acre Park Adventure)
articles.author: Jestine Ware
articles.description: A miniature horse and her friends help a bearded dragon that is lost on their farm.
articles.intro: Jestine Ware is a freelance editor, writer, and writing coach. She's the co-author of Good Night Stories for Rebel Girls: 100 Real Life Tales of Black Girl Magic , has written several episodes of Rebel Girls: The Podcast , and worked with many other publications. A dragon is a made-up monster that looks like a large lizard that breathes fire. In this story, a miniature horse, or a very small horse, named Trix and her friends find something surprising on their farm.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Trix acts towards Pendragon.
articles.permissions: "A Dragon in Central Acre Park (A Central Acre Park Adventure)" by Jestine Ware. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Trix, the miniature horse, has big dreams. She wants to be the best scientist in the world. She wants to observe everything and know everything. There is just one problem. She has hooves and no hands. How will she record her amazing discoveries?! With the help of all of her animal friends, of course!

In the middle of a big city surrounded by honking horns, concrete sidewalks, and skyscrapers is a huge patch of green. Welcome to Central Acre Park! It’s a farm in the city full of curious animals who have a lot to learn about the scientific world. Each day is a new adventure. Today, they will meet a fierce reptile believed to be a dragon.

Trix the miniature horse trotted out of the barn to find her Chief Research Assistant and Note Taker: Garbanzo the raccoon. Unfortunately, he wasn’t a very good assistant, but at least he could hold a pencil.
A huge clang came from the dumpster.
“Garbanzo, what are you doing?”
“I’m looking for… samples,” the raccoon replied through a mouthful of food.
Trix could see only his striped tail poking out above the smelly garbage bags. The stench was awful . She wrinkled her nose.
“You mean you found snacks,” she snorted, tossing her black mane against her brown and white fur.

A tiny, brown sparrow landed on Trix’s back with a chirp. It was her friend Beatrice. “Trix, news from the pond! We just spotted… ooh bug!” Beatrice gulped the bug down. “What a dee-EE-licious ant! Did you know that ants can carry up to 5,000 times their body weight?”
Garbanzo emerged with a day-old hamburger bun in one hand and a bruised apple in the other. “Bea, focus. What’s the news?”
“Oh, yeah! There’s a spooky voice coming from the cave. A dragon . Aren’t they fire-breathers?” The tiny, brown bird shuddered.
“Let’s go check it out,” Trix said. She stamped her foot on the concrete path. Her heart beat fast with the excitement of a new discovery. “Tell me everything you saw.”
While Bea and Trix talked, the food in Garbanzo’s hands mysteriously disappeared.
The raccoon finally spoke up. “Dragons aren’t real ,” he said.
“Are too!” Bea exclaimed.

Trix trotted across the farm. She ignored her two friends bickering back and forth about mythological creatures. She had no proof whether dragons were or were not real. Still, she was sure there had to be another explanation, and she was going to find out.

The cave that Bea mentioned was actually an old sewage drain. They would have to travel through a lot of the park to get there. On her way to the “cave,” Trix passed the paddock and an old chicken coop full of cackling hens. She rounded the magnificent fountain that shot water straight up in the sky like glittering gems in the sunlight. Then she came to the marsh where the turtles basked on rotting logs among green lily pads and duckweed. Finally, Trix trotted up to the perfectly straight row of trees inside the park fence.
Nestled inside a small hill was a wide, round metal pipe. Trix squinted, but she couldn’t see into the darkness.
The miniature horse tapped the pipe. It gave a dull clang.
“Um, hello? Excuse me,” Trix said in her sweetest, most polite voice.
Most humans and animals trusted Trix because of her warm, brown eyes and quiet, gentle manner. She learned a lot of important facts just by listening. Her ears pricked forward.
A faint but fearsome voice roared: “Pendragon the Mighty is most displeased! Leave now if you value your life!”
The miniature horse snorted. Though the voice certainly sounded mighty, the echoing metal made the noise much louder than it actually was.
“You can come out,” Trix said. “It’s safe.”
“I’d rather not,” said Pendragon the Mighty.
Garbanzo and Bea arrived later, still arguing.
“Garbanzo, Bea… I need a little help,” Trix said to her friends. “Our new… uh… dragon friend won’t come out.”
“I’m not going near that thing,” the sparrow declared.
“I’ve got this.” The raccoon pretended to roll up his sleeves.
With his excellent night vision, Garbanzo looked inside the tunnel. He reached in and tugged on a scaly tail.

Out came a squirming, unhappy reptile about the size of a small rabbit. It hissed like a cat. Its chin was covered in a beard of spiky scales, and there were more all over its body. It wore a smart, checkered bowtie collar and a tiny hat.
“Pendragon the Mighty is most displeased!” it hissed at Garbanzo. “Put me down!”
“You got it, dude.” The raccoon dropped the squirming newcomer in the grass.
Bea peeked out from behind a leaf. “I told you it was a dragon.”
“I’m a bearded dragon.” Pendragon straightened his hat and bowtie. “I’m a lizard, you silly bird.”
“Nice to meet you, Pendragon,” Trix said kindly. “You look lost. Can we help you?”
“My human misplaced me, and I got hungry,” Pendragon said huffily. “I was hunting crickets.”
Trix shook out her mane eagerly, and a pencil and pad of paper fell out. “Write this down, Garbanzo.”

The raccoon pretended to take notes. His scribbles didn’t mean anything. They were just squiggles and lines. When Trix asked him to read back what he’d written, he repeated word for word using his memory. He never forgot a thing.

“Bearded dragons are cold-blooded reptiles that come from Australia,” Garbanzo said. “Pendragon climbed a tree while basking in the sun and fell asleep. His human couldn’t find him because he blended in. When Bea interrupted his meal, Pendragon lashed out to defend his territory from what he thought was another reptile.”
Pendragon’s spiky neck puffed up indignantly. It looked like he’d swallowed a balloon.
“Birds are very similar to reptiles,” the lizard said. “Well, except for their feathers and flight. We are both distant relatives of ancient beasts.
“Dinosaurs,” Trix added. “It means ‘terrible lizards.’ And Pendragon is right. You do share ancestors, Bea.”
Bea puffed up until she was perfectly round and balloon-like too. “I’m not related to this thing! It looks crusty!”
“You both eat bugs,” Garbanzo pointed out.
“And you both have scaly, clawed feet,” Trix added.
“Well, he can’t fly,” Beatrice said. The sparrow turned and flitted off to find more ants to eat.
Pendragon hissed after her. Trix and Garbanzo just laughed. Not everybody on the farm got along with Bea.
“Let’s help Pendragon,” Trix said to Garbanzo. “Check your notes. Where should we look first?”

Garbanzo picked an area from his memory, and off they went. The miniature horse, raccoon, and bearded dragon hid behind parked cars and newspaper stands. They peered down shadowy alleys. They circled the neighborhood around the farm half a dozen times. They walked miles and miles. They didn’t see Pendragon’s picture on any of the missing pet signs near the farm.
Pendragon’s human wasn’t anywhere to be found.
After a long afternoon of searching, Trix offered Pendragon a hidey hole in the barn behind a loose board in her horse stall.
“Pendragon the Mighty is pleased,” the bearded dragon said. “Thank you, Trix.”
“You’re welcome,” Trix replied.
Garbanzo relaxed in a ratty, cloth hammock that dangled near the ceiling. He nibbled on a fresh watermelon rind.
“Don’t forget to add bearded dragons to the farm species list,” Trix said.
Garbanzo pretended to scratch the words into the wall with his other scribbles.

Trix felt one step closer to becoming the scientist she’d always wanted to be. Maybe she was meant to be a herpetologist who studies reptiles and amphibians? Or an ornithologist who studies birds? She fell asleep dreaming about feathers and scales.

options.label: Things may be different from what they first seem to be.
Taking notes can help you remember things better.
The bigger an animal is, the scarier it seems.
It is unpleasant to talk to bearded dragons.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of the story?

options.label: "The cave that Bea mentioned was actually an old sewage drain." (Paragraph 15)
"Garbanzo and Bea arrived later, still arguing." (Paragraph 24)
"'Birds are very similar to reptiles,' the lizard said. 'Well, except for their feathers and flight. We are both distant relatives of ancient beasts.'" (Paragraph 40)
"Bea puffed up until she was perfectly round and balloon-like too. 'I'm not related to this thing! It looks crusty!'" (Paragraph 42)

questions.prompt: Which sentence best shows how Beatrice feels about Pendragon?

options.label: to sing with joy
to shake with fear
to shout with surprise
to move with excitement

questions.prompt: What does the word "shudder" mean as it is used in paragraph 9?

options.label: They show how Garbanzo helps Pendragon.
They explain that the animals are in trouble.
They show what Central Acre Park looks like.
They explain why Trix likes to go on adventures.
