discussionQuestions.prompt: When Trix hears about the "dragon in the cave," she is excited to make a new discovery. Think about a time you discovered something new. What did you discover? How did it feel to learn something new?
Pendragon says he was misplaced by his human. What would you do if you found a lost pet or object? Do you think it is okay to keep something that you find? Why or why not?
Garbanzo and Trix try to help Pendragon find his human and then give him a place to stay. Think of a time you helped someone. What did that person need, and how did you help? How did it feel to help?

At the end of the story, Trix dreams about becoming a scientist who studies animals. The text says, "Maybe she was meant to be a herpetologist who studies reptiles and amphibians? Or an ornithologist who studies birds?" (Paragraph 56). If you had to choose a species or group of animals to study, which group would you choose? Why?

articles.title: Celebrating Chinese New Year
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: The poet describes the festivities and traditions of Chinese New Year.
articles.intro: Chinese New Year is the holiday that celebrates, or joyously marks, the beginning of a new year on the Chinese lunar calendar, which uses the patterns of the moon to mark the passing of time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the poem’s use of color.
articles.permissions: "Celebrating Chinese New Year" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday Stew: A-Kid's Portion of Holiday and Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Red lai se e for the children,
Red banners for the walls,
Happy red is everywhere
When Chinese New Year calls.
Gold oranges for giving,
Peach blossoms to bring luck,
White shark fins for our special soup,
Pink sweet sauce for the duck.
Hot yellow lions dancing,
Warm yellow lantern light,
Pink-yellow-red-green dragons
Who snarl but never bite!
Brown sticky cake and dumplings,
One great big black bass fish,
A Gung Hay Fat Choy ! greeting;
To all our New Year wish!

options.label: Chinese New Year is a happy and lucky time.
Chinese New Year is more for children than adults.
Chinese New Year is about playing tricks and giving treats.
Chinese New Year is a holiday that teaches children about the past.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of the poem?

options.label: surprised by how many things there are to do
excited to take part in the celebration
curious about why it is celebrated
amazed to learn something new

questions.prompt: How does the speaker seem to feel about Chinese New Year?

options.label: They talk about special foods that are eaten in celebrating Chinese New Year.
They tell about animals that are in Chinese New Year parades.
They explain what children like most about Chinese New Year.
They teach lessons about the meaning of Chinese New Year.

questions.prompt: How are lines 7-8 and lines 13-14 alike?

options.label: kind and caring.
playful and lazy.
funny but unsafe.
fierce but harmless.

questions.prompt: In line 12, the phrase "snarl but never bite" shows that the dragon is —
How do the colors used in the poem show the reader how Chinese New Year is celebrated?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you celebrate Chinese New Year? If so, how is the speaker's celebration the same or different from how you celebrate the holiday? If you do not celebrate Chinese New Year, how does it seem the same or different from holidays you celebrate or know about?
What is a holiday that is special in your family, community, or culture? What are some special foods that you eat for that holiday? How does food help us celebrate important events?

The poet uses colors to describe Chinese New Year. What colors represent your favorite holiday? As a creative extension, write a poem inspired by "Celebrating Chinese New Year" that uses colors to tell about how to celebrate your favorite holiday.

articles.title: The Valentine Box
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: A speaker helps a child keep their Valentine's Day memories safe.
articles.intro: Valentine's Day happens on February 14th each year. In this poem, a speaker talks about how they spend a special Valentine's Day.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens in each stanza, or group of lines.
articles.permissions: "The Valentine Box" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday Stew: A Kid's Portion of Holiday and-Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: I grew up a shoe box
With only one job —
To carry your new pair of shoes.
But look at me now —
A fancy red box
Covered in glitter and glue.
It’s Valentine’s Day —
I’m going to school!
I sit on your lap on the bus.
Soon I’m a mailbox
With a new job —
Hold valentines given to us.
And on the way home
You read every note,
Returning your favorites to me.
Then back in the closet
I start the best job —
Preserving this memory.

options.label: It is important to hold on to special memories.
You should always say thank you for the gifts you receive.
You should tell the people you love how you feel about them.
It can be hard for young children to remember special holidays.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of the poem?

options.label: confused
curious
disappointed
proud

questions.prompt: How does the speaker seem to feel about their job?

options.label: a bit strange.
liked the most.
scary to look at.
the most colorful.

questions.prompt: As it is used in line 15, the word "favorites" describes notes that are —
How does the speaker's job change in each stanza, or group of lines?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why do you think the poet chose to write this poem from the point of view of the Valentine box? How does hearing about the experience of the Valentine box help you understand Valentine's Day better? How would this poem be different if it was told from the point of view of the child? As a creative extension, rewrite the poem from the point of view of the child.

In lines 1-6 of the poem, a child turns a regular shoe box into a glittery, red box for their valentines. Why do you think the child decorated the box rather than keeping it plain? How would you decorate a Valentine box? Draw a picture or describe how you would make your Valentine box special.

In the poem, the child stores their valentines in a special box in their closet. Why do you think they want to keep their valentines safe? Do you like to save things that people give you, like cards and notes? Why or why not? How can looking back on good memories make us feel?

articles.title: Lit Up by Lanterns
articles.author: Kerilyn Acer
articles.description: Kerilyn Acer describes how people make and decorate their own lanterns to celebrate the end of Chinese New Year.
articles.intro: Chinese New Year is the holiday that marks the beginning of a new year on the Chinese lunar calendar. A lunar calendar uses the patterns of the moon to mark the passing of time. One important part of Chinese New Year is the Lantern Festival. A lantern is a hand-held light. This text talks about how the Lantern Festival is celebrated.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people get ready for the Lantern Festival.
articles.permissions: From Scholastic News Edition 2, February 2016. Copyright © 2016 by Scholastic Inc. Reprinted by permission of Scholastic Inc.

blocks.text: Golden, orange, and red lanterns hang from above. Lights sparkle overhead. It is Chinese New Year, and the Lantern Festival has begun!
The Journey of a Lantern

This year, Chinese New Year starts on February 8. It lasts for 15 days. There is a Lantern Festival on the last day and night. Let’s follow a lantern from the time it’s made to when it lights up the festival!

All across China and in places where Chinese people live around the world, people will celebrate the Lantern Festival. They will hang thousands of lanterns above streets, parks, and homes. People will walk under the lanterns and look up at the beautiful lights. First, they must make their lanterns!
Making a Lantern

You can buy lanterns in a store, but many people make their own. They use wood or metal to make the lanterns’ frames. Lantern makers shape their lanterns into globes, cubes, animals, or creatures like dragons.
Then they cover the frame in paper, silk, or glass. People often put lights inside the frame. When the lantern is lit up, it will be luminous. That means the lantern will give off bright light.
Decorating a Lantern
After the lantern is made, it is time to decorate it. Some people draw flowers or designs on their lantern. Others write their wishes and hopes for the new year.
One popular tradition is to decorate a lantern with a riddle. A riddle is a puzzling question that is hard to answer.

People think of a riddle. Then they write it down and hang it on a piece of paper from their lantern. On the night of the festival, others try to solve the riddle. If you get the right answer, the lanterns owner might give you a prize!
Here is a riddle that might be on a lantern. “Which building has the most stories?” Can you guess the answer? It’s “a library”!
Enjoying the Lanterns
People hang up their lanterns in the days before the festival. Workers on tall ladders string lanterns over the streets. Soon, the last day of Chinese New Year arrives. The Lantern Festival begins!

Families and friends stroll through neighborhoods and gaze at the colorful, luminous lanterns. They read the wishes for the new year and try to solve the riddles. Performers dance and sing in the streets as the lanterns glow high above them.
When the next morning comes, the festival is over. Workers take the lanterns down. But some people are already planning what kind of lanterns they will make next year!

options.label: The Lantern Festival is a time for families to mark the beginning of Chinese New Year by hanging lights in the street.
The Lantern Festival is a celebration during Chinese New Year when families give money to children in red envelopes.
The Lantern Festival is a celebration during Chinese New Year when children receive lanterns with riddles on them as presents.
The Lantern Festival is a time for people to mark the end of Chinese New Year by buying or making lanterns to be seen by others.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?

options.label: beautiful.
serious.
strong.
useful.

questions.prompt: As it is used in paragraph 6, "decorate" most closely means to make the lanterns more —

options.label: It is important to use a cover that is dark so that the lantern's riddle can be read.
It is important to use a cover that is see-through so the lantern can give off light.
It is important to use a cover that is dry so that the lantern will catch on fire when it is lit.
It is important to use a cover with a dull color so that the lantern will blend in with other lanterns.

questions.prompt: Why do people use paper, silk, or glass to cover the frame of their lantern?

options.label: What is Chinese New Year?
Who celebrates the Lantern Festival?
What happens after the lanterns are made?
How do people write riddles on their lanterns?

questions.prompt: Which question does the section "Enjoying the Lanterns" help to answer? (Paragraphs 10-12)
What important things do people do to get ready for the Lantern Festival?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Have you ever taken part in a Lantern Festival? If so, how is the celebration in the text similar or different from how you celebrated the holiday? If you have not taken part in a Lantern Festival, how does it seem the same or different from holidays you celebrate or know about?

The Lantern Festival is a special tradition that is part of Chinese New Year. What is a holiday that is special in your family, community, or culture? What are some special traditions that you do to get ready for this holiday?

The author says that lanterns are usually decorated with wishes and hopes for the New Year or with riddles. What is a wish, hope, or riddle you would want to include on your lantern? Why? As a creative extension, have students draw or construct their own lantern using the steps from the text.

articles.title: The Sparrow's Quest
articles.author: Elizabeth Laird
articles.description: A sparrow goes on a journey to find the most powerful thing on earth.
articles.intro: "The Sparrow's Quest" comes from a collection of Iranian short stories, retold by Elizabeth Laird. Laird is a British writer known for her children's fiction and her collections of folktales from other countries.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the sparrow learns from each thing it encounters.
articles.permissions: "The Sparrow's Quest" from Pea Boy and Other Stories from Iran retold by Elizabeth Laird. Reproduced by permission of The Agency (London) Ltd, © Elizabeth Laird 2009. All rights reserved and enquiries to The Agency (London) Ltd. 24 Pottery Lane, London W11 4LZ, info@theagency.co.uk

blocks.text: A hungry sparrow, one cold winter’s day, fluttered about hunting for something to eat. At last she saw a movement on an icy pond.
Aha! she thought. A grub! and down she swooped, hoping for a meal.
But there was no grub, only a twig blowing in the breeze.
The ice was so cold that the sparrow’s feet began to sting.

“Oh, cruel ice!” she exclaimed. “Nothing has ever hurt me as much as this. There’s no one else like you, who can give a poor little bird such pain. How does it feel to be the greatest power there is?”
The ice creaked and cracked as a laugh shivered along his bright surface.

“Powerful? Me?” he growled. “How wrong you are. The sun is much stronger than I am. When the spring comes, he’ll shine his hot rays down on me and I’ll splinter and melt away. You must ask the sun how it feels to be powerful, little bird.”
The sparrow, glad to lift her feet from the ice’s freezing surface, flew up and up into the sky, as close to the sun as she dared.

“Tell me, great sun,” she chirped, shielding her face with a wing from the sun’s blinding rays, “are you the greatest power there is? I thought it was the ice, but he tells me you’ll melt him away when the spring comes.”
Flames shot out from the sun’s fiery heart, threatening to singe the sparrow’s feathers.
“The greatest power on earth?” he roared. “It’s not me, no no, not me. How could it be, since the clouds can cover me and hide me from below? Ask the clouds, sparrow. Ask the clouds.”
The sparrow flew on, seeking refuge from the sun’s tremendous heat in the wet mistiness of the clouds.
“Cool wet clouds, mighty storm clouds, are you greater than the sun?” she asked. “For that’s what he tells me.”
Lightning flickered and thunder groaned all around, making the sparrow’s feathers shiver.
“Powerful? Not us,” rumbled the clouds. “The wind can blow us anywhere it chooses. Go and ask the wind.”
The wind picked up the sparrow and tumbled her about in its gusts and eddies until she hardly knew where she was.
“Stop, wind! Is it true what the clouds have told me, that you are the greatest power there is?” shouted the sparrow.

The wind snatched up flurries of leaves from the ground below and played with them as it answered, “Little sparrow, how can that be? There is something I can never move, however hard I blow. The mountain is more powerful than me. Go to the mountain and ask him.”
The sparrow flew to the mountain, and perched on the very top.
“Mountain, can you hear me?” she sang out. “The wind tells me that you are more powerful even than him. Are you the greatest thing on earth?”
Deep inside the mountain, rocks clashed and ground together, before at last the mountain spoke.
“Look around you, little bird. Grass covers me from my feet to the crown of my head, and there’s nothing I can do to shake it off. Ask the grass who is most powerful here.”
So the sparrow, looking down at the grass, called out, “Grass, grass, did you hear the mountain? Are you the most powerful thing there is?”

The blades of grass all around her shook and rattled as a thousand voices called out in reedy tones, “Oh sparrow, how can you think we are powerful, when worms can burrow through us and disturb our roots?”

The sparrow, who was very tired by now, and faint with hunger, looked around until she saw a worm hole. She stood above it and called down, “Worm, are you there? The grass tells me how powerful you are — the most powerful thing on earth. Tell me, is it true?”

The humble worm had never been asked such a question before. Unwisely, he poked his head out of his hole, and sighed, “Oh, how I wish I was! Then I would never be afraid of being eaten by a hungry sparrow.”
Too late, he saw who it was who had called down to him, for at that moment the sparrow opened her beak and began to gobble him up.
Just before he disappeared altogether, the worm managed to gasp, “How does it feel then, cruel sparrow, to be the greatest thing on earth?”
The sparrow swallowed, and wiped her beak on the grass.
“A little less hungry,” she said.

options.label: The largest creatures tend to be the most powerful.
The environment is more powerful than the animals that live in it.
It is always more important to be clever than it is to be strong.
Even if we're small, we sometimes have more power than we think we do.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the story?

options.label: "Flames shot out from the sun's fiery heart, threatening to singe the sparrow's feathers." (Paragraph 10)
"'Little sparrow, how can that be? There is something I can never move, however hard I blow. The mountain is more powerful than me.'" (Paragraph 18)
"The sparrow, who was very tired by now, and faint with hunger, looked around until she saw a worm hole." (Paragraph 25)
"the worm managed to gasp, 'How does it feel then, cruel sparrow, to be the greatest thing on earth?'" (Paragraph 28)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: She believes that she is the most powerful creature on earth and wishes to prove it to the rest of the world.
She is hungry and hopes that the most powerful thing on earth will be able to provide her with food.
She learns that there are things in nature that are more powerful than what originally harmed her.
She is searching for food and assumes that the most powerful thing on earth will be a worm she can eat.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes why the sparrow begins her search for the most powerful thing on earth?

options.label: to show that there is always something more powerful when you compare yourself to others
to emphasize that the sparrow is the weakest creature, compared to everyone she meets
to show that it's unlikely the sparrow will ever meet the most powerful thing on earth
to reveal how little knowledge the sparrow has about other creatures

questions.prompt: Why does the author have the sparrow repeat the lines "are you the greatest power there is" or "are you the most powerful thing" to every other character she meets in the story?
How does the sparrow's conversation with the worm affect how she feels at the end of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this story, a small sparrow learns about her own power in relation to things around her in nature. In your opinion, how is nature powerful? Are human beings or nature more powerful? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

In this story, each creature or force of nature that the sparrow talks to thinks of themselves as less powerful than something else. When have you seen people compare themselves to others and feel "less than?" When can it be helpful to compare yourself with others? When can it be harmful?
How does knowing the relationships between different plants and animals help us understand nature? How does nature work together?

articles.title: A Space is More Than Just a Space
articles.author: Isaac Akinkunmi
articles.description: Personal and community spaces should be treated with respect.
articles.intro: This text explains different kinds of spaces and why they should all be treated with respect.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, find examples of the three kinds of spaces.
articles.permissions: "A Space is More Than Just a Space" by Isaac Akinkunmi. Copyright © 2019 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 2.0.

blocks.text: Think of space! What comes to mind? No, I’m not talking about planets and aliens. I’m talking about the space we live in!
The world is full of different spaces. There are large spaces like parks and schools! And small spaces like our bedrooms!
We can think about space in three different ways: public space, private space, and personal space.
Public space
Public spaces are often called “Commons”. Commons are the places and things that we share. Roads, parks, and libraries are examples of Commons. Can you think of any others?
Commons are important. They are social spaces for everyone in the community to come together. We are all responsible for their care.
Public spaces belong to everyone. But wait! Before you camp in your local park or throw a party at the library, remember that there are rules. The rules are set to keep these spaces clean and safe.
Private space
A private space is a space that has an owner. Privates spaces are also important. We all need a place where we can go to relax and be alone.
Homes and offices can be examples of private spaces. The rules of these spaces are made by their owners. We must ask for permission before entering a private space.
Personal space
We all have personal space. This is the space that is our own. It is where we sit and stand.
Everyone feels different about their personal space. People can feel upset or angry when someone they don’t know gets too close. That is why we should respect everyone’s personal space.
Respecting our Spaces
It is important to respect all spaces in our community and in our school.
We can respect public spaces by “leaving no trace.” This means we don’t leave a mess. It means we put our trash in the trash bin. It also means we don’t take anything that doesn’t belong to us.
We can respect private spaces by asking for permission before we enter them.
We can respect personal spaces by finding out how much space a friend or classmate needs to feel comfortable.
Respecting public, private, and personal spaces makes us better people. It helps keep our community safe and fun!

options.label: Commons are places for everyone to use.
Private spaces have rules created by their owners.
People become angry when strangers enter their personal spaces.
Public, private, and personal spaces are important and should be respected.

questions.prompt: PART A: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: "No, I'm not talking about planets and aliens." (Paragraph 1)
"There are large spaces like parks and schools!" (Paragraph 2)
"It is important to respect all spaces in our community" (Paragraph 11)
"we don't take anything that doesn't belong to us." (Paragraph 12)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: having parties
respecting space
being responsible
made for all people

questions.prompt: What does the word "social" mean as it is used in paragraph 5 of the article?

options.label: Public space
Private space
Personal space
Respecting our Spaces

questions.prompt: In which section can you find examples of spaces that belong to everyone?

options.label: to make sure other people can find the place
to show other people that the place has an owner
to help people feel comfortable around each other
to keep the place nice for all of the people who visit it

questions.prompt: Why is "leaving no trace" important when visiting a public place?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Think about the spaces that are important to you. How do those spaces make you feel? Do you share these spaces with other people? Why or why not?

In the text, the author says that it is important to respect all spaces in our community and school. What happens when spaces in our communities and school are not respected? How does this make people feel?

Why is it important for people to feel comfortable and safe in public, private, and personal spaces? What makes your personal and private spaces comfortable and safe? What can communities do to keep public spaces safe?

articles.title: Humpty Dumpty
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker describes Humpty Dumpty's attempts to find safety under a chicken.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson is a writer of books for both children and adults. Humpty Dumpty is a character in an English nursery rhyme and usually appears as a talking egg. In the original rhyme, Humpty Dumpty falls off a wall and shatters into pieces. In this poem, a speaker describes Humpty Dumpty's attempts to find safety under a chicken.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what is happening to Humpty Dumpty under the chicken.
articles.permissions: From Black Stars in a White Night Sky by JonArno Lawson. Copyright © 2008 by JonArno Lawson. Published by WordSong, an imprint of Boyds Mills Press. Used by permission.

blocks.text: Humpty Dumpty
hid himself
underneath a chicken:
“I’m far from a wall,
so how can I fall?
I’ll never again be stricken
by sad misfortune
or bad luck;
if I fall from this nest
I’ll get right back up…”
But then something inside of him started to stir —
he had no idea
what was bound to occur
with that chicken on top of him,
though he knew something odd
was tapping about
right under his fragile skin.
He had no idea,
not the faintest idea,
of the danger
he was now in.
And before the poor fellow
could figure it out,
before he could reason
or voice a doubt,
a crack appeared in his neck —
and he broke apart from within.
Peck peck.

options.label: It can be difficult to prepare for every possible disaster.
Even things that look safe can be deadly.
Sometimes bad things happen without warning.
It is important to be aware of your surroundings.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the poem?

options.label: "Humpty Dumpty / hid himself / underneath a chicken" (Lines 1-3)
"'if I fall from this nest / I'll get right back up… '" (Lines 9-10)
"though he knew something odd / was tapping about / right under his fragile skin." (Lines 15-17
"before he could reason / or voice a doubt, / a crack appeared in his neck — " (Lines 24-26)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It suggests that Humpty Dumpty is the egg that the chicken has laid.
It shows that Humpty Dumpty could easily be crushed by the chicken.
It prompts readers to consider that Humpty Dumpty could be eaten.
It helps readers understand the Humpty Dumpty is nothing more than a fragile egg.

questions.prompt: How does the speaker's discussion of the chicken in stanza 1 help readers understand what happens at the conclusion of the poem?
How is the speaker's point of view different from Humpty Dumpty's point of view?

discussionQuestions.prompt: How does Humpty Dumpty try to change his future in the poem? Do you think we can control our future and our fate? Why or why not?
How do Humpty Dumpty's fears drive his actions in the poem? When have you been driven to do something out of fear?

articles.title: The Lights of Saint Lucia
articles.author: Sara Matson
articles.description: Two sisters have important morning traditions for Saint Lucia Day.
articles.intro: Sara Matson has written for Highlights. In this short story, two sisters have important morning traditions for Saint Lucia Day. Saint Lucia Day is a festival of light to celebrate a Saint. It is celebrated on December 13, most commonly in Sweden.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Eva and Britta’s traditions for Saint Lucia Day.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “Don’t go!” Britta’s younger sister, Eva, cried.
Britta stuffed her pajamas into a backpack. “I can’t miss Kalie’s birthday party.”
“You’ll miss our Saint Lucia Day tradition!” Eva said. “Can’t you come back in the morning? Kalie’s house is right next door, so you could be here in a minute.”
“Silly! No one leaves a slumber party at 6:00 A.M.,” Britta said. “Anyway, this is your chance to be Saint Lucia. With me gone, you’ll be the oldest girl.”
“The only girl,” Eva muttered.

Britta felt a twinge of regret. Even though Britta was glad Kalie had invited her to the party, part of her didn’t want to go. Saint Lucia Day was one of her favorite holidays. Seeing the happy yellow candlelight on a cold December morning always made her feel cozy. Plus, it was fun to wake up Mom and Dad for once, instead of the other way around.
But friends were important, too — especially when you were the new girl in school. Sometimes, Britta decided, you had to choose between friends and family.
“Time for me to go,” she said, zipping her backpack.
Tears ﬁlled Eva’s eyes. “I don’t know how to make coffee!” she said. “And who will tie my sash?”
“You know how to tie a bow,” Britta said patiently. “And you can skip the coffee this year.”
“But Saint Lucia is supposed to serve coffee!”
Britta sighed. “Eva, you can do this.” She opened a box on her desk and lifted out a crown of plastic candles.
After setting it on Eva’s head, she turned the candles on and ﬂipped on the overhead light. Her sister’s face glowed in the dark room.
“Perfect,” Britta said. “Once you have the tray ready, carry it upstairs into Mom and Dad’s room and sing the song. Easy as breathing.”
“Do you really think I can do it?” Eva asked, taking off the crown.
“For sure.”
But as she watched Eva trudge out of the room, Britta couldn’t help worrying a little.

The next morning, Britta woke up while it was still dark. She glanced at the clock on Kalie’s dresser. 5:45. She snuggled deeper into her sleeping bag, trying to fall back to sleep. But she kept thinking about Eva. Am I a terrible sister for leaving Eva to do everything herself? she wondered.

She stepped over the sleeping girls to Kalie’s bedroom window, where she had a perfect view down into her family’s kitchen. Pretty soon, the light clicked on and Eva appeared, wearing a white dress with a crookedly tied red sash. Britta watched Eva arrange lussekatter and pepparkakor — Lucia buns and gingersnaps — on a plate. Last year, Britta remembered, the two of them had giggled and shushed each other while munching about ﬁve cookies each. The year before, they’d had to replace the crown’s batteries at the last minute, and Eva had rushed to ﬁnd some in one of her toys.
Suddenly, Britta knew she’d made the wrong decision. She needed to be with her family. But it was too late to do anything about it.
Or was it? Like Eva had said, it would take only a minute to get home.
Britta tiptoed over to Kalie and shook her awake. “I need to go home,” she whispered.
Kalie sat up. “Are you sick?”
“No. There’s something I have to do,” Britta said. “But I promise I’ll come back as soon as I can. And I’ll bring lussekatter.”
“Looseh-what?”
“Swedish bread. You’ll like it.”
Kalie nodded. “I’ll watch from the window to make sure you get home OK.”
Moments later, Britta ﬂew through the darkness to her own front door. After turning to wave up at Kalie, she unlocked it and slipped inside.
A wide-eyed Eva stood in the hallway, holding a tray in her hands. She had already switched on her crown’s lights. “What are you doing?”
Britta grinned. “I changed my mind.”
To Britta’s surprise, Eva’s face fell. “But I was going to wear the crown for the ﬁrst time. Now that you’re here, you get to wear it.”
“No, I don’t,” Britta assured her. “This year, I’ll be your helper. But ﬁrst, let’s start the coffeemaker.”
“OK,” said Eva. “I’ll get the cream and sugar.”
As they bustled about in the kitchen, Eva smiled. “I’m glad you came back, Britta.”
“Me, too! I can’t believe I almost missed it,” Britta said.
When everything was ready, Eva picked up the tray. Britta followed her sister up the steps, carrying the electric candle Eva had held in past years.
At the top of the stairs, they began to sing: “Outside it’s dark and cold... ”

options.label: Introducing friends to your family's traditions can be fun.
Sometimes it's important to put your family and their traditions above others.
Taking on new responsibilities that you're unfamiliar with can be scary.
No matter the situation, think about yourself, and then those around you.

questions.prompt: PART A: What is the main theme of the short story?

options.label: "Tears filled Eva's eyes. 'I don't know how to make coffee!' she said. 'And who will tie my sash?'" (Paragraph 9)
"Suddenly, Britta knew she'd made the wrong decision. She needed to be with her family." (Paragraph 20)
"'But I was going to wear the crown for the first time. Now that you're here, you get to wear it.'" (Paragraph 31)
"Britta followed her sister up the steps, carrying the electric candle Eva had held in past years." (Paragraph 36)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: She tries to get Britta to stay to help her with the traditions.
She accuses Britta of not wanting to spend time with her.
She is understanding about Britta wanting to make new friends.
She is excited to take on some of the traditions by herself.

questions.prompt: How does Eva react when Britta shares that she won't be home for the morning of Saint Lucia?

options.label: She feels happy that she was able to make a new friend.
She feels confident that Eva will be able to handle the traditions.
She feels jealous that her family will be celebrating without her.
She feels unsure about her decision to miss the Saint Lucia Day traditions.

questions.prompt: How does Britta feel when she wakes up at her friend's house?
Why is it important to the story that Britta decides to go home on the morning of Saint Lucia Day?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Eva and Britta usually take part in the Saint Lucia Day traditions together. Why is spending time with family on holidays important? Describe a holiday that is special to you and your family and how you celebrate together.

In the story, Britta allows Eva to wear the crown, even though Britta is the oldest. What do you think this means to Eva? Describe a time when someone let you take on a more adult responsibility or task. How did it make you feel?

articles.title: Kissy Face
articles.author: Nancy Jean Northcutt
articles.description: A boy tries to avoid receiving kisses from his aunts and grandmas.
articles.intro: Nancy Jean Northcutt has written for Highlights. In this short story, a boy doesn't want to receive kisses from his grandmas and aunts.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how James feels about his new sibling.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: James did not like to be kissed. But people loved to kiss James!

Both of his grandmothers and all of his aunts would tweak his cheek or forehead and say “You are just the cutest thing.” Then they would kiss him on his cheek or forehead. James could not get away fast enough.
James’s best friend, Ben, lived next door. Ben would always tease James. “Kissy Face! Kissy Face!” he’d say.
James didn’t know what to do. He tried hiding when family came to visit. But his father always called him in to say hello.
He tried wearing an old Halloween mask, but his mother made him take it off so his grandmothers and aunts could see his “cute little face.”
James even tried eating a peanut butter-and-jelly sandwich when he knew they were coming. He thought that if his face and hands were sticky, no one would want to kiss him.
But the grandmothers and aunts just found napkins and wiped off the stickiness. They kissed his forehead and tweaked his cheek even harder.

One day, James’s mother told him she had a wonderful surprise. There was going to be a new baby in the family. James was going to be a big brother. James listened but didn’t say anything. He wasn’t sure if a baby was a wonderful surprise or not.
James told Ben about the new baby.
“Will it be fun?” asked Ben, who was not a big brother.
“I don’t know,” said James. He thought he would wait and see the baby before deciding whether it was fun.

On the day James’s parents brought the new baby home, there were balloons and streamers everywhere. But the party wasn’t for James. There were lots of wrapped presents. But they were all for the new baby, not for James.

His mother put the baby in a rocking basket. James looked in. The baby was asleep. Its face was red and wrinkly. James thought it smelled funny. So far, the baby did not seem to be a wonderful surprise.
Soon the whole family arrived. James cringed.
Now, on top of everything else, he was going to get kissed and tweaked.

But the grandmothers and aunts barely looked at James. They all went straight to the new baby and said, “Oh, you are just the cutest thing!” They leaned over the baby’s basket and kissed its little face.
Then one of the aunts came over to James. “Looks like you’re a big brother now,” she said. Instead of kissing his cheek, she just patted his shoulder and smiled.
James smiled, too. Maybe his days of being Kissy Face were over. It turned out that having a new baby in the family was a wonderful surprise after all.

options.label: Having a new baby can make older siblings feel ignored.
It's difficult to allow someone else to be babied by the rest of the family.
Having a younger sibling can help adults realize that you're growing up.
No matter how old you get, your family will always treat you like a baby.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which sentence describes the theme of the short story?

options.label: "James's best friend, Ben, lived next door. Ben would always tease James. 'Kissy Face! Kissy Face!' he'd say." (Paragraph 2)
"He tried wearing an old Halloween mask, but his mother made him take it off so his grandmothers and aunts could see his 'cute little face.'" (Paragraph 5)
"On the day James's parents brought the new baby home, there were balloons and streamers everywhere. But the party wasn't for James." (Paragraph 12)
"'Looks like you're a big brother now,' she said. Instead of kissing his cheek, she just patted his shoulder and smiled." (Paragraph 17)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: He is a cute kid and the youngest in the family.
He pretends to like being kissed and pinched.
They know it bothers him to be treated like a baby.
They think it is funny to kiss and pinch him.

questions.prompt: Which of the following describes why the aunts and grandmas kiss James and tweak his cheeks?

options.label: James doesn't like his grandmas and aunts very much.
James is good at avoiding kisses and cheek tweaks.
James can't escape kisses and cheek tweaks from his family.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the context of the text, how does James' family show their love for him? Why doesn't he like it? Does this mean that he doesn't love his family? How do your family members show you that they care?

In the story, James' grandmas and aunts stop kissing him once he has a new baby sibling because he seems more grown-up. How have your family members started treating you differently now that you're growing up?
In the story, James is bothered by his family. What are some things that your family does that bother you?

articles.title: The King's Breakfast
articles.author: A. A. Milne
articles.description: Chaos ensues when a king asks for butter with his bread.
articles.intro: A.A. Milne (1882-1956) was a British children's author and poet. He is most famous for coming up with the character Winnie-the-Pooh. In this poem published in 1925, a king asks for something extra to go with his slice, or flat piece, of bread.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the problem in the poem.
articles.permissions: "The King's Breakfast" from When We Were Very Young by A.A. Milne (1925) is in the public domain.

blocks.text: The King asked
The Queen, and
The Queen asked
The Dairymaid:
“Could we have some butter for
The Royal slice of bread?”
The Queen asked the Dairymaid,
The Dairymaid
Said, “Certainly,
I’ll go and tell the cow
Now
Before she goes to bed.”
The Dairymaid
She curtsied,
And went and told
The Alderney:
“Don’t forget the butter for
The Royal slice of bread.”
The Alderney
Said sleepily:
“You’d better tell
His Majesty
That many people nowadays
Like marmalade
Instead.”
The Dairymaid
Said, “Fancy!”
And went to
Her Majesty.
She curtsied to the Queen, and
She turned a little red:
“Excuse me,
Your Majesty,
For taking of
The liberty,
But marmalade is tasty, if
It’s very
Thickly
Spread.”
The Queen said
“Oh!:
And went to
His Majesty:
“Talking of the butter for
The royal slice of bread,
Many people
Think that
Marmalade
Is nicer.
Would you like to try a little
Marmalade
Instead?”
The King said,
“Bother!”
And then he said,
“Oh, deary me!”
The King sobbed, “Oh, deary me!”
And went back to bed.
“Nobody,”
He whimpered,
“Could call me
A fussy man;
I only want
A little bit
Of butter for
My bread!”
The Queen said,
“There, there!”
And went to
The Dairymaid.
The Dairymaid
Said, “There, there!”
And went to the shed.
The cow said,
“There, there!
I didn’t really
Mean it;
Here’s milk for his porringer,
And butter for his bread.”
The Queen took
The butter
And brought it to
His Majesty;
The King said,
“Butter, eh?”
And bounced out of bed.
“Nobody,” he said,
As he kissed her
Tenderly,
“Nobody,” he said,
As he slid down the banisters,
“Nobody,
My darling,
Could call me
A fussy man -
BUT
I do like a little bit of butter to my bread!”

options.label: Kings are fussy before they eat breakfast.
It is important to be kind to dairymaids and cows.
Important jobs should be given to dairymaids, not queens.
Do important jobs right away so you will not have to do them later.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of this poem?

options.label: She is afraid and cannot make milk.
She is tired and does not want to work.
She is excited and wants to try marmalade.
She is angry with the King and wants him to go hungry.

questions.prompt: What do the following lines show about the Alderney? "That many people nowadays / Like marmalade / Instead." (Lines 23-25)

options.label: It bothers me when she yells.
Please don't bother me at work!
Don't bother! I'll clean it up later.
Oh, bother, the cat spilled the milk!

questions.prompt: Which of these sentences uses "bother" in the same way as the King in line 54?

options.label: They show that the cow does not want to help the King.
They show that the Queen helps the King all on her own.
They show how everyone works together to help the King.
They show how the King enjoys the marmalade on his bread.

questions.prompt: Why are lines 67-79 important to the poem?
How does the Alderney make the problem worse in the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this poem, a King insists on having butter with his bread. What certain topping, spread, or condiment do you love having on a certain food? How would you feel if you could not have that topping with the food? Would you react like the King? Why or why not?
Twice in the poem, the King says that he is not a "fussy man." Do you agree with this statement? Why or why not?

When the Dairymaid first asks the Alderney to make butter, she does not do it. Think of a time you did not want to do a chore or something you were asked to do. Did you refuse to do it like the Alderney? If yes, were there consequences for your actions? What consequences would there be if you did not do your chores?

articles.title: A Little Tiny Thing
articles.author: Anonymous
articles.description: A young girl learns a big lesson from a small insect about how to treat others.
articles.intro: This short story was first published in a book of tales that teach children lessons. In this story, a young girl learns a big lesson with the help of a small insect.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the girl’s feelings and actions toward the ant change throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "A Little Tiny Thing" from A Christmas Hamper: A Volume of Pictures and Stories for Little Folks by Anonymous (1889) is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Out in the garden Mary sat hemming a pocket-handkerchief, and there came a little insect running — oh, in such a hurry! — across the small stone table by her side.

The sewing was not done, for Mary liked doing nothing best, and she thought it would be fun to drop her thimble over the little ant. “Now he is in the dark,” said she. “Can he mind? He is only such a little tiny thing.”
Mary ran away, for her mother called her, and she forgot all about the ant under the thimble.

There he was, running round and round and round the dark prison, with little horns on his head quivering, little perfect legs bending as beautifully as those of a race-horse, and he was in quite as big a fright as if he were an elephant.

“Oh,” you would have heard him say, if you had been clever enough, “I can’t get out, I can’t get out! I shall lie down and die.” Mary went to bed, and in the night the rain poured. The handkerchief was soaked as if somebody had been crying very much, when she went out to fetch it as soon as the sun shone. She remembered who was under the thimble. “I wonder what he is doing,” said Mary. But when she lifted up the thimble the little tiny thing lay stiff and still.
“Oh, did he die of being under the thimble?” she said aloud. “I am afraid he did mind.”

“Why did you do that, Mary?” said her father, who was close by, and who had guessed the truth. “See! He moves one of his legs. Run to the house and fetch a wee taste of honey from the breakfast-table for the little thing you starved.”
“I didn’t mean to,” said Mary.

She touched the honey in the spoon with a blade of grass, and tenderly put a drop of it before the little ant. He put out a fairy tongue to lick up the sweet stuff. He grew well, and stood upon his pretty little jointed feet. He tried to run.
“Where is he in such a hurry to go, do you think?” said father.
“I don’t know,” said Mary softly. She felt ashamed.
“He wants to run home,” said father. “I know where he lives. In a little round world of ants, under the apple tree.”
“Oh! Has such a little tiny thing a real home of his own? I should have thought he lived just anywhere about.”

“Why, he would not like that at all. At home he has a fine palace, with passages and rooms more than you could count; he and the others dug them out, that they might all live together like little people in a little town.”
“And has he got a wife and children — a lot of little ants at home?”

“The baby ants are born as eggs; they are little helpless things, and must be carried about by their big relations. There are father ants and mother ants, and lots of other ants who are nurses to the little ones. Nobody knows his own children, but all the grown-up ones are kind to all the babies. This is a little nurse ant. See how she hurries off! Her babies at home must have their faces washed.”
“O father!” cried Mary; “now that is a fairy story.”

“Not a bit of it,” said father. “Ants really do clean their young ones by licking them. On sunny days they carry their babies out, and let them lie in the sun. On cold days they take them downstairs, away from the cold wind and the rain. The worker ants are the nurses. Though the little ones are not theirs, they love them and care for them as dearly as if they were.”
“Why, that’s just like Aunt Jenny who lives with us, and mends our things, and puts baby to bed, and goes out for walks with us.”
“Just the same,” said father, laughing.
“Is that the reason we say Ant Jenny?”

“You little dunce! Who taught you to spell? But it is not a bad idea, all the same. It would be a good thing if there were as many ‘ant’ Jennys in this big round world of ours as there are in the ants’ little round world — folk who care for all, no matter whose children they are.”
While they were talking, the little ant crept to the edge of the table, and down the side, and was soon lost among the blades of grass.
“He will never find his way,” said Mary with alarm.

“Let him alone for that,” said father. “The ants have paths leading from their hill. They never lose their way. But they meet with sad accidents sometimes. What do you think I saw the other day? One of these small chaps — it may have been this very one — was carrying home a scrap of something in his jaws for the youngsters at home. As he ran along, a bird dropped an ivy berry on him. Poor mite of a thing! This was worse than if a cannon ball were to fall from the sky on one of us. He lay under it, not able to move. By-and-by one of his brother ants, who was taking a stroll, caught sight of him under the berry.”
“What did he do?” said Mary.

“First he tried to push the berry off his friend’s body, but it was too heavy. Next he caught hold of one of his friend’s legs with his jaws, and tugged till I thought it would come off. Then he rushed about in a frantic state, as if he were saying to himself, ‘What shall I do? what shall I do?’ And then he ran off up the path. In another minute he came hurrying back with three other ants.”
“Is it quite true, father?”

“Quite. The four ants talked together by gentle touches of their horns. They looked as if they were telling one another what a dreadful accident it was, and how nobody knew whose turn would come next. After this they set to work with a will. Two of them pushed the berry as hard as they could, while the other two pulled their friend out by the hind legs. When at last he was free, they crowded round as if petting and kissing him. You see these little ant folk have found out that ’Tis love, love, love, that makes the world go round . I shouldn’t wonder if that ant you teased so thoughtlessly is gone off to tell the news at home that there is a drop of honey to be had here.”
“Oh, he couldn’t, father!”
“Wait and see,” said father.
In a little while back came the ant with a troop of friends.
“He has been home and told them the good news about the honey,” said father. “Do you think that all children are as kind as that?”
Mary said, “No, they’re not. I don’t run to call all the others when I find a good place for blackberries.”
“Then,” said father, “don’t be unkind to the ant, who is kinder than you, though he is only a little tiny thing.”

options.label: You should treat others nicely.

Being a good friend is helpful for success.
Sometimes animals need help finding their way home.

articles.author: Deborah Underwood

blocks.text: A lake that’s full of orange juice,
A boulder made of bread,
A mashed potato mountaintop
That towers overhead.
A carrot stick enormous
As a mighty redwood tree
Perches on the shoreline
Of a choppy spinach sea.
A sprig of parsley turns into
A huge primeval plant.
That’s how my dinner looks
When I pretend to be an ant.

Playing make believe can make life seem more exciting.

articles.title: Lucy
articles.author: Susan Marie Swanson
articles.description: A young girl shares memories of her name.
articles.intro: Susan Marie Swanson is an American author of children's books and poetry. In this short story, the narrator shares what makes her special.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the narrator feels.
articles.permissions: "Lucy" by Susan Marie Swanson, Ladybug. © 1996 by Cricket Media, Inc.. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: When I was born, my mom wrote my name everywhere. She wrote my name in my Mother Goose book. She wrote my name on the backs of pictures. She wrote it on the picture of me sleeping crooked in my car seat. She wrote it on the picture of me pulling off my blue cap. She wrote my name on my T-shirt so that everyone would know I was LUCY.
I was brand-new, and so was my name.

When I was one, my grandpa spread chocolate frosting on my birthday cake. He wrote my name on top of the cake with orange frosting. He squeezed the frosting out of a tube. First he squeezed out an L. He kept squeezing until my whole name was squeezed out on the cake — Lucy.
My grandma took a picture of me smooshing my orange name into the brown cake. I made that cake look like a volcano. My name was the hot lava gushing out of the volcano.
When I was two, Uncle David made a stepstool for me. He cut the wood with his electric saw. He nailed the pieces of wood together. He sanded the edges smooth.
He painted the stepstool blue, and then he painted my name on the top — LUCY.
He painted chipmunks running around my name. He painted trees so the chipmunks and my name would have some shade. When it was time to wash up, I stepped up on my stepstool and stood on my name.

When I was three, I scribbled my name everywhere. I scribbled my name on my hand with a green marker. I scribbled my name on the couch with red paint. I scribbled my name on the floor with a blue pen. I scribbled it with a brown crayon on my door. I wanted everything to belong to me. I thought that my scribbles said LUCY.

When I was four, my dad made surprise pancakes. They were alphabet pancakes. Dad flipped them onto my plate one at a time. I ate the L with maple syrup. I ate the U with jam. I ate the C with apple butter. I ate the Y all by itself. My name tasted wonderful.
When I was four, I got a baby sister named Madeline. She could not eat her big long name, so I helped.

When I was five, I read my name on my coat hook at kindergarten. I hung my jacket up right under my name. One day, I lost my cap on the playground. I looked under the slide. I looked by the swings. I could not find my cap anywhere, so I went to look for it at the lost and found. There were two red caps, just the same, but my cap had Lucy written in tiny letters on the tag. I hung my cap back up under my name.
When I was six, I stomped my name in the snow. The snow was my giant paper. The letters of my name were bigger than me. I would not let Madeline write over my name with her boots!

I wrote a letter to my Uncle David to tell him about the paper made of snow and my giant name. I told him how I would not let Madeline write over my name with her boots. I signed my name at the end of the letter — Lucy.
Madeline signed her name, too:

Now I am seven. I am writing a story about a girl who lives deep in the woods. She can talk to animals. One of her friends is a chipmunk. When it is cold outside, the chipmunk hides in the girl’s jacket. The girl’s name is written on the door of her cabin. Can you guess what her name is? LUCY.

options.label: Our names can change what others think of us.
Our names are an important part of who we are.
We should thank our families for the names we are given.
We should change our names to something that feels special.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Identity is what makes a person who they are. How are names an important part of people's identity? What might your name tell others about your identity? Is your name an important part of who you are? Why or why not?
Lucy loves her name. What is something you love about yourself? What could you say to yourself to express or show this love?

In the short story, Lucy shares memories about her younger sister, Madeline. Do you have any siblings? What is a special thing that you do with your sibling? If you do not have a sibling, who is a friend or family member that you like to spend time with? What is a special thing that you do with that person?

articles.title: Martin Luther King, Jr.
articles.author: Stephanie Kraus
articles.description: Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. fought for equal rights for Black people during the U.S. Civil Rights Movement.
articles.intro: Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. (1929-1968) was a leader of the African American Civil Rights Movement. During the Civil Rights Movement, people fought against segregation, the act of separating people by race and discrimination, the act of treating people unequally. This article shares about Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.'s important life.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on why Martin Luther King, Jr. day is a national holiday — a holiday that Americans honor.
articles.permissions: "Martin Luther King, Jr." by Stephanie Kraus, ©TIME For Kids. All rights reserved. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media,Inc.and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: When Martin Luther King Jr. was born, his parents, Michael and Alberta, gave him a different name from the one we know today. They called him Michael. But Michael Sr. later changed his name to Martin. He changed his son’s name too. They took the name Martin Luther, after the famous religious leader. Young Martin grew up in Atlanta, Georgia. His father and grandfather were pastors. The King children grew up in a loving home. But they experienced racism from an early age.
Separate but Never Equal

King attended public schools in Georgia. The schools were segregated at the time. King skipped grades 9 and 11 and graduated from high school at age 15. Around that time, he joined the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), a civil rights organization.

In 1948, King graduated from Morehouse College, in Atlanta. He went on to study religion at Crozer Theological Seminary. The school was in Chester, Pennsylvania. He graduated in 1951. Soon after, he enrolled in a Ph.D. program at Boston University, in Massachusetts. He met music student Coretta Scott in Boston. The couple married in 1953. They later had two sons and two daughters.
Peaceful Protests

In 1954, King moved to Montgomery, Alabama. He became a pastor at the Dexter Avenue Baptist Church. Soon after, on December 1, 1955, Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat to a white passenger on a Montgomery city bus. She was arrested. In response, the NAACP led a citywide boycott against segregated buses. King was chosen to lead the boycott.

For 381 days, Black people refused to ride the buses. The boycott ended on December 21, 1956. That was after a decision by the U.S. Supreme Court. The court ruled that all people had equal rights to seats on buses. It was a key victory for civil rights. Eight years later, the Civil Rights Act outlawed racial discrimination in all public places.
“I Have a Dream”

In the spring of 1963, King organized a protest in Birmingham, Alabama. The city police blasted protesters with fire hoses. They let attack dogs loose. King and others were jailed. People across the nation heard about the event.

After their release, King laid out plans for a march on the nation’s capital. The March on Washington attracted more than 250,000 people. On August 28, 1963, King gave his “I Have a Dream” speech. He did so at the Lincoln Memorial. In his address, he spoke of a day when all people would be treated equally. King went on to win the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize. He was 35 years old. He was also the youngest person to have received the honor.
The Promised Land

The fight for equal rights, however, was far from over. On March 7, 1965, King organized a march from Selma to Montgomery, in Alabama. Police used nightsticks and tear gas on protesters. Seventeen people were hospitalized. Soon after, President Lyndon Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act of 1965. The law stated that all citizens had a right to vote. It said they could not be discriminated against because of the color of their skin.

On April 3, 1968, King attended a labor strike in Memphis, Tennessee. He told supporters, “I want you to know tonight, that we, as a people, will get to the Promised Land.” The next day, he was struck by a bullet. The shot ended King’s life. He was 39 years old. The accused shooter, James Earl Ray, pleaded guilty. He was sentenced to 99 years in prison.
King’s birthday is observed as a national holiday on the third Monday in January. On that day, Americans honor the civil rights leader’s service and commitment.

articles.title: Be Nice to Spiders!
articles.author: Tracy Vonder Brink
articles.description: Spiders may look scary, but they benefit people, animals, and our planet.
articles.intro: There are thousands of different kinds of spiders that live all over the world. In this informational text, Tracey Vonder Brink shares interesting facts about these eight-legged animals.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author describes spiders.
articles.permissions: "Be Nice to Spiders!" by Tracy Vonder Brink. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Don’t jump now, but there’s a spider in your room! It’s lurking in the corner with its long, hairy legs stretched out all around it. Is it ready to pounce and bite you? Is it the kind with lots of eyes? You don’t want to get close enough to tell. I know what you’re thinking — you’ll grab your heaviest shoe to squash the creepy thing. But don’t do that. It’s better to let the spider live.
Why? Because the world needs spiders! (Really!)
Spiders Eat Pests…and Are Eaten

Firstly, spiders are champs at chowing down. They eat between 400 to 800 million tons of insects every year. Some of the bugs they eat are the kinds that destroy the crops we need for food. Hungry spiders keep those crop-eating pests from gobbling up everything in sight. Don’t take my word for it. Norman Platnick, a spider expert, once told The Washington Post , “If spiders disappeared, we would face famine.” Famine! Spiders also eat bugs that annoy us and spread disease, like mosquitos and flies. So, basically, spiders are cleaning up for us and preventing the world from going hungry. Who knew?

Secondly, you’re taking food from others any time you get rid of spiders. Lots of creatures need spiders as a food source. Between 8 to 10,000 predators such as lizards and frogs rely on eight-legged meals. Birds need spiders too — 3,000 to 5,000 species of birds snack on spiders as part of their diet. You wouldn’t want birds, frogs, and lots of other animals to go hungry, would you?
Spiders Could Help Us In Other Ways

There are other reasons why we should be nice to spiders. Most spiders kill their prey with venom. Scientists think that venom could be used to make medicine because it contains stuff that blocks the body from sending pain signals to the brain. It’s thought that there could be as many as 9 million of these blockers in spider venom. But so far only .01% have been studied. It’s possible that one or more of them could become new medicines to treat pain. So if there are no spiders, there’s no venom to study, and maybe we miss out the chance to help a lot of people.

Spider silk could also be useful. More than 40,000 species of spiders make silk in a special part of their body called a gland. Spider silk is stretchy and incredibly strong. A single strand is stronger for its size than a piece of steel! If spider silk could be produced in large amounts, it could be used to make fabrics, bulletproof armor and more. For now, it’s mostly an idea (it’s not easy to farm spiders because they tend to eat each other), but one day spider silk could become an important natural material.
I Promise, They’re Not Out to Get You!

Maybe one way to be nice to spiders is to ask ourselves: what has a spider ever done to me? It’s true that 99% of spiders have venom. But unless you’re a bug, most spider venom is harmless. In the United States, only black widows and brown recluses have bites that can harm people. These bites need a doctor’s care, but they are rarely bad enough to kill. Spiders don’t release much venom when they bite, so only the very young and very old are at risk of death. In Australia, redback spiders and Sydney funnel-web spiders do have venom that can kill a person, but there are treatments for the bites.

Besides, spiders do not go around looking for people to bite. Like any other wild creature, spiders bite when they feel trapped or threatened. They don’t have bones, so spiders are actually pretty fragile. Those big, hairy tarantulas might look scary, but a fall of just a few inches can hurt and even kill them. Spiders truly are more scared of you than you are of them. So leave them alone!

So, the next time you feel the urge to smash a spider, stop and think. Do you really want to kill a helpful creature that’s not causing you any harm? If you can’t stand to have it in your room, use a piece of paper or a magazine to nudge it into a glass and release it outside. (Of course, if you find a black widow, brown recluse, or are in Australia, go get an adult first.) You can even take part in Save a Spider Day, which happens every year on March 14. Be kind to our eight-legged friends!

articles.title: A Very Special Gift for Grandma
articles.author: Stephanie Yu Lim
articles.description: A little girl writes letters in her diary as she chooses the perfect gift for her Grandma.
articles.intro: Stephanie Yu Lim is a children's book author who grew up wanting to become a concert pianist. In the story "A Very Special Gift for Grandma," a little girl named Lucy writes in her diary as she chooses the perfect gift for her Grandma.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Lucy’s feelings as she creates Grandma’s gift.
articles.permissions: "A Very Special Gift for Grandma" by Stephanie Yu Lim, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: January 27
Dear Diary,
Mom says it’s Grandma’s birthday next week. Jack says he’s going to make her something out of LEGO® blocks. Does Grandma even like that stuff? I want to give her something she’ll like.

I know she really likes music because she always takes me to recitals at Carnegie Hall. The music’s nice, but I really like the bucket of peppermints they have in the front. You can just help yourself. My grandma always takes one just in case she coughs in the middle of the performance, but I grab a whole bunch.
I need to think of something fast. It has to be really special and better than Jack’s gift.
Lucy
January 29
Dear Diary,
Guess what? I know what to get my grandma. A birthday recital! Shhh. I don’t want Jack to know.

I told my piano teacher today. She thinks it’s a great idea. She said I should play the first movement of Beethoven’s Sonata No. 20 in G major because it’s an impressive piece for a nine-year-old. I agreed because Beethoven is Grandma’s favorite composer.
My teacher also said that I should come up with my own version of “Happy Birthday” because that will be extra special. Now how am I supposed to do that? I better start practicing.
Lucy
January 30
Dear Diary,

Bad day! I’m never going to figure out “Happy Birthday.” I sang it first, just like my teacher said to, but I can’t figure out the notes on the keyboard. I tried it like twenty times. I had to stop because Mom doesn’t like tears on the piano.

The Beethoven sounds pretty bad, too. I keep messing up the trill. I can’t move my second and third fingers fast enough. My teacher says it should sound like “deedle-deedle.” She says to relax the pinkie and to practice slowly. But when I play the trill, my pinkie curls up.
A birthday recital was a terrible idea. I should just draw my grandma a picture like I did last year. I wonder how Jack’s project is going.
Lucy
January 31
Dear Diary,
I saw my brother’s gift — a jewelry box. It even has a lock.

After I saw that, I decided to try “Happy Birthday” again. First, I sang it out loud. Then, I listened carefully to find the notes on the piano. I took a deep breath every time I had to start over. Guess what? After five times, I figured out the first line! Then, it was easy to figure out the rest because it’s pretty much the same thing over and over.
The trill is sounding more like “deedle-deedle.” I tell myself to keep the pinkie relaxed, and I can actually move my fingers faster. Now I have to put the hands together. More practice.
Lucy
February 1
Dear Diary,
A good day today! I can play “Happy Birthday,” and I added in some chords in the left hand. My own version of “Happy Birthday” — I’m a composer, just like Beethoven.

Jocelyn came over today, and I played the Beethoven for her. She asked me how my fingers could move so fast until they were blurry. I told her the trick is all in the pinkie. She wanted to play outside after, but I said I needed to practice more because I only have two days left! I can’t wait.
Lucy
February 2
Dear Diary,

Tomorrow’s the big day. “Happy Birthday” sounds good. Now, I can even sing along as I play. I finally got the trill, too. Practicing slowly is like magic. You know, I kinda see why Beethoven liked trills. They’re so fancy.
Jack found out about my gift because he heard me practicing. He said it’s really thoughtful. I told him his jewelry box is cool, too. You know what he said? He said he’s going to make one for me!
Lucy
February 3
Dear Diary,
You should have seen Grandma’s face today when I handed her a peppermint and then walked up to the piano. I thought she was going to fall out of her chair.

I started with the sonata. I remembered to keep my pinkie loose, and I just let my fingers fly — the trill worked! Then I played “Happy Birthday,” and everyone joined in to sing. I even added some big bass octaves and a trill at the end. Grandma jumped up after and gave me a big hug. I thought she didn’t like it at first because she had tears in her eyes. But she said it was the best gift ever and that it was even better than going to Carnegie Hall!
Lucy
P.S. Jack gave Grandma her gift. She said she’d been looking for a jewelry box with a lock on it. Can you believe it? I better remind him to make me one. After all, he promised.

options.label: If you work hard and put your mind to something, you can do great things.
Trust yourself to make good decisions about important things.
Never surprise someone because they may not like it.
Listen to your family, because they know you best.

articles.title: Hazel Grove
articles.author: Leslie Barnard Booth
articles.description: A little girl with cerebral palsy worries about being different.
articles.intro: Cerebral palsy is a condition that affects a person's movement, balance, and stance. Many people with cerebral palsy use leg braces, crutches, or wheelchairs to help them move more easily. In this story, a little girl worries about her differences while on a school trip to a senior living center. A senior living center is a place where older people live when they cannot, or choose not to, live by themselves.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Maya’s feelings change throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Hazel Grove " by Leslie Barnard Booth, Ladybug , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Today was Community Service Day at school, and Maya’s class was going on a field trip to a senior living center called Hazel Grove. It was also the first day Maya would be wearing her leg braceto school.

Walking to school with her mom, Maya zipped her coat to her chin to fend off the chilly autumn air. She looked down at her shoes. Her left sneaker was a size larger than her right, so the brace could fit inside. Maya felt unsure about wearing the brace to school, even though it was decorated with a pretty butterfly pattern she had chosen from a book at the doctor’s office. It was supposed to help her walk more smoothly, and it did make her feel solid and steady as she picked her way along a muddy patch slicked with soggy leaves. But Maya didn’t know how she would explain the brace to other people. She was a little scared when her mom kissed her goodbye at the door to her classroom. All morning at school, kids kept glancing at the brace, but they didn’t say anything. Then the teacher announced that they could sit wherever they wanted while they worked on cards for the people at Hazel Grove.

Maya thought about the hazel bush in her backyard. She started to draw the little green buds that would burst from the branches in spring. Then she drew the flowers that blossomed in her yard at that time of year: crocuses, daffodils, and quince. It felt good to draw. Maya’s friend Samir came over and sat by her. When she stood to get another marker from the bin, he noticed the brace. “What’s that for?” he asked.
Maya pretended she didn’t hear him because she didn’t know what to say. Glancing back at him later, she saw that he looked a little sad.

That afternoon, the class got on a bus and headed to Hazel Grove. They brought the cards they had made. When they arrived, Maya noticed that the brace made it easier to walk up the ramp to the doors. She didn’t stumble or trip. Still, she felt a little flip-flop in her stomach when a man leaving the building glanced down at her leg.

Maya and her friend Gabriela were paired up with a woman who was 103 years old. It was hard to believe anyone could be so old. The woman was in a wheelchair that had a pretty knitted covering over the back. She wore bright pink lipstick and a necklace of big pink plastic beads. She smelled like coffee.
“I’m Joan,” she said cheerfully. “Glad to meet you.”

Shyly, they handed her the cards they had made. Joan squinted at Gabriela’s card. “You’ve written a poem!” she said to Gabriela. Gabriela blushed. Then Joan turned to Maya’s picture. “The first flowers of spring!” she cried in delight. “You like drawing?” she asked Maya.
“I do,” Maya said.
“You better keep it up, then.” “I will,” said Maya. Then, in a sudden burst of bravery, Maya asked, “What’s it like being in a wheelchair?”

“It helps me get around,” Joan said simply. Then, grinning, she added, “You’d be surprised how fast I can go in this thing!” Right then, she whizzed across the room. Then she turned around and whizzed back.
“You see?”

It was true. Joan was fast in her chair. The girls and Joan talked and talked: about pets and cats and little brothers, about what it was like in the olden days and what it was like now. When it was time to go, Maya gave Joan a big hug.
On the bus ride back to school, Maya sat next to Samir. She told him, “This brace keeps my toe up so I can walk better.”

“Really?” he said. “That’s cool.” Then they talked about the people they had met at Hazel Grove. Maya told Samir about Joan, how nice and funny she was. Samir told Maya about Bob, who had played Samir and Everett a song on the ukulele.

When Maya’s mom met her at the door at the end of the school day, Maya skipped into her arms. “Today was the best!” Maya said. Though it was still cold and windy outside, to Maya it felt like spring. Her mom let her stay and play for a while, and Maya ran all around the playground feeling strong and fast in her new brace.

options.label: Our differences make us special.
Helping others is very hard work.
Our parents always know what is best for us.
Everyone needs time to play with their friends.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Maya worries about how to talk to her friends about her new leg brace. Have you ever worried about how to talk to your friends about something? How did it feel when you were able to talk about it?
In the story, Maya learns a lot from Joan in the senior center. What have you learned from people who are older than you? What else would you like to learn?
