articles.title: Take a Deep Breath
articles.author: Howard Schwartz
articles.description: A speaker describes what happens around you while you breathe.
articles.intro: In this poem by Howard Schwartz, a speaker describes what happens as you breathe.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what happens during a breath.
articles.permissions: "Take a Deep Breath" by Howard Schwartz, Ladybug , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Take a deep breath in —
while you are breathing in
lots of things are happening —
a butterfly is landing on the trunk of a tree,
a baby rabbit is looking for its mother,
a caterpillar crawls across a leaf,
and a turtle pulls his head into his shell
and decides to take a nap.
And out —
while you are breathing out
the world’s not standing still —
a seed starts to take root,
a groundhog wakes from a long, long sleep,
a rose opens its petals,
the sun comes out from behind a cloud,
and the wind circles around your house,
hoping to play.

options.label: Many things are happening around you in a single moment.
Taking a deep breath will help you enjoy the animals around you.
It is helpful to think about plants and animals when you need to calm down.
There are many animals in the world, and it is important to think about them often.

articles.title: Fabulous Frederic
articles.author: Peggy Thorne
articles.description: A young magician perfects his act.
articles.intro: In this story, a boy named Frederic practices to become a magician, a person who does magic for an audience, or group of people.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what makes Frederic fabulous.
articles.permissions: "Fabulous Frederic" by Peggy Thorne, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: My show was terrific — right up until the last trick.

I had a wooden stage, a top hat, and my uncle Herbert’s wand. I pulled quarters out of kids’ ears and a stuffed rabbit out of my hat. I read my brother Theodore’s mind. I made a stream of colored confetti shoot out of my wand, and I made a baseball float across the stage. Around twenty-five people were in the audience, most of them kids from the neighborhood. I had them on the edge of their seats.
But my last trick was my greatest, my most stupendous.
What I mean is, it was supposed to be great.
It’s a complicated card trick I call the Color Switch. It’s not the fanciest trick I know, but from a magician’s point of view, it’s the hardest.
When I announced the trick, I saw Uncle Herbert straighten up and fix his eyes on my hands. He used to be a professional magician, so he knew exactly where to look.
There was only one problem: the Color Switch didn’t work.

You see, this trick depends on sleight of hand. I have two decks of cards that I have to switch — without the audience seeing. I’d practiced the trick a lot, of course, but I couldn’t do it as well as my other tricks. In fact, I only got it right about three-quarters of the time. (The first rule of being a magician is never to perform a trick that’s not perfectly prepared, but I really wanted to impress my uncle.)

Anyway, the worst thing that could possibly happen did happen: the hidden deck fell out of my sleeve when I was making the switch. So there they were, fifty-two cards the audience wasn’t supposed to see, spilled all over the stage.
Everybody laughed.

I tried to put on a good face, which is what you have to do when something goes wrong. I made a joke and smiled confidently when I took my bows. Then I called Theodore out for a bow. He’s a really good sport, which is why he agreed to be my assistant for the mind-reading trick. Incredible as it might seem, he’d rather draw pictures than do magic. Theodore is my identical twin, but my dad always says you couldn’t find two people more different anywhere .

After the show, Theodore and Uncle Herbert helped me clean up. Uncle Herbert told me I’d done a terrific job, which I knew was basically true, and then asked me to do the sleight of hand for the card trick I’d goofed up.

I did it for him, and it worked pretty well, but Uncle Herbert just shook his head. “Not smooth enough,” he said. “You’re good with your hands, Fred, but you’re only ten years old. They’re just not big enough for palming cards. If you keep practicing, though, you’ll be able to do that trick expertly — in another four years or so.”
Four years. That was a discouraging thought.

I looked at Uncle Herbert’s hands. They’re big and long-fingered. As the Fabulous Franzetti, sleight of hand tricks had been his specialty. I can remember seeing him brush his hand across a table and make an entire deck of cards disappear — like magic. That was years ago, before his arthritis got bad enough that he’d had to retire.
The Fabulous Franzetti was looking at me kindly. “Maybe you should play to your strengths,” he said.
“My strengths?”
“You have something very special — something almost no other magician has.” Then he nodded his head at my brother, Theodore.
I looked at Theodore and knew exactly what Uncle Herbert was thinking. “It wouldn’t work,” I said. “Everybody in the neighborhood knows us.”
“Your show is terrific — good enough to take on the road. In fact, I have an excellent idea where you could start.”
So that’s how Fabulous Frederic’s Magnificent Magic Show wound up at the Glenville Retirement Home. It had a big auditorium with a real stage. About a hundred people showed up.

I did the same show I’d done in my backyard, with two differences. First, instead of reading my brother’s mind, I read my sister Amanda’s. She wore her silver gymnastics outfit, and the crowd loved her.

Second, I replaced the Color Switch with Instant Teleportation. My new trick worked like this: I wheeled a coat rack with a black velvet curtain out to the center of the stage. I pulled the curtain aside to show the audience there was nothing behind it. Then I stepped past it with a showy twirl of my cape and snapped the curtain shut behind me.

My father was waiting at the back of the auditorium. As soon as I snapped the curtain shut, he rang a bell and set off a camera flash. The noise and the light made everybody turn to the back of the auditorium. Then Theodore stepped into the aisle. He was dressed exactly like me, and of course everybody thought he was me.

He bowed to the crowd, then walked down to the stage. Everyone stared at him, while I stepped back from the coat rack and disappeared backstage. When Theodore reached the stage, he grandly pulled the curtain aside to show that no one was there.
The crowd went wild. From their point of view, it looked like I had stepped behind the curtain and then instantly appeared at the back of the auditorium.
Amanda ran up onstage with Theodore then, and the two of them took their bows. Everybody stood up and clapped, which didn’t surprise me. It was a great trick.

I was backstage, of course, while Theodore and Amanda were getting all the applause, but that didn’t matter. The Fabulous Franzetti patted me on the back and told me he was glad I was using his wand. That was enough for me.

blocks.text: “Why were you in Aunt Kim's room, Daniel?” asked Evelyn.
Daniel shut the door behind him. “I'm looking for her pig.”
“Aunt Kim has a pig?” asked Evelyn, wide-eyed. “She didn't show it to me.”
Daniel and Evelyn were cousins. They were at Grandma's house for a family reunion. Right now, people were listening to each other play music on the front porch.
Daniel said, “Aunt Kim asked me to bring her pig down to the porch. She said she always plays the guitar with it.”
“Oh, her pig must be like my reading teacher's dog at school. When I'm reading to Coco, it's easier.” Evelyn smiled. “What's her pig's name?”
“She called it Lucky. She said it was pink, and she'd left it on her bed. But it wasn't there.”
Evelyn said, “Pigs like to eat. Maybe somebody's giving Lucky a snack in the kitchen.”
But the kitchen was empty of pigs and people. Just to be sure, they opened the pantry door and looked under the table. “It rained this morning. Maybe Lucky's rolling in a mud puddle,” said Daniel.

The cousins went out the back door. Their sneakers squished in the wet grass. They looked all over the yard. “No mud puddles out here,” said Evelyn. “And there aren't any holes in the fence, so Lucky didn't run away.”
Back inside, they checked around sofas and chairs and bookcases. “Here, Lucky!” But they couldn't find the pig.
Evelyn folded her arms. “I don't think Lucky's in somebody else's bedroom. The doors were all closed. Wouldn't you see a pig coming in when you were going out?”
“But Lucky is little. Aunt Kim told me she looked in her purse, and the pig wasn't there,” said Daniel.
“She keeps a pig in her purse? Lucky must be really small,” Evelyn said. “Are you sure it wasn't in Aunt Kim's room?”
“My cat hides under beds sometimes. Maybe Lucky does, too,” Daniel said. He led the way back upstairs. On his hands and knees, he lifted the blue and white quilt's edge. “No pig here.”
Meanwhile, Evelyn spotted something pink and plastic on Aunt Kim's quilt. It was shaped almost like a triangle. “Daniel, did Aunt Kim ask you to get her guitar pig? Or did she say guitar pick ?”
Her cousin shrugged. “It was hard to hear her, because Dad and Uncle Mike were playing their fiddles. What's a guitar pick?”
Evelyn held up the pink triangle. “Yesterday, before your family got here, Aunt Kim played the guitar for me. She was using this. I think it's called a guitar pick.”
Daniel tilted his head. “Well, it is pink, and it was on her bed...”

The fiddle music was over when the cousins walked onto the porch. Aunt Kim was talking to Grandma. Before the cousins could say anything, Aunt Kim said, “Oh thanks, you two! I really don't like playing without my lucky guitar pick.” Evelyn handed it over, and Aunt Kim strummed a few times. “I hope it wasn't hard to find.”
“It was just where you said,” said Daniel, winking at Evelyn. She laughed so hard her braids bounced.

options.label: Always respect your older family members' wishes.

It is easier to solve a problem with help than alone.
If you want to have a pet you need to be trustworthy.

articles.title: Happiness on a Piece of Paper
articles.author: Valerie Hunter
articles.description: A boy wants to follow his own path as an artist.
articles.intro: In early America, many people were farmers who took care of the land and harvested, or gathered, what they grew. In this story, Garrison is a young boy who lives on his family's farm.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Pa feels about Garrison’s art.
articles.permissions: "Happiness on a Piece of Paper" by Valerie Hunter, Spider, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Garrison looked at the trees, then back at his paper. The branches weren’t quite right. He wet his finger and carefully rubbed the paper until the branches disappeared.
Before he could redraw them, a hand grabbed the paper. “You’re supposed to be helping with the potatoes, not playing with your pencil,” Thomas snapped.

Garrison glared at him, but said nothing. He could have said he’d been up since four, milking the cows and stacking kindling while Thomas was still in bed. He was perfectly willing to help Thomas dig the potatoes; his brother could have just asked instead of crumpling his last sheet of brown paper. The paper was what the dry goods clerk wrapped Ma’s fabric in. As she hadn’t bought any lately, Garrison had been treasuring his last piece. All he had left now was a single sheet of the beautiful creamy paper Pa had brought back from his last voyage.

Garrison attacked the dirt of the potato patch, wishing Pa was home. Thomas might act nicer then. Still, Garrison knew that Thomas’s behavior was mainly due to the season. He always got bossier at harvest time, as though he had an enormous farm to oversee instead of a few fields of corn and potatoes.
“Thomas takes after your Ma’s people,” Pa had once said. “Farm folk. They’ve got soil in their blood like my family has the sea in theirs.”
“Who do I take after?” Garrison had asked, but Pa said he didn’t know yet. Later Garrison figured it out for himself — he didn’t take after anyone. All he ever wanted to do was draw.

“Pick the potatoes, don’t manhandle them! Do I have to do everything around here?” Thomas’s voice cut into Garrison’s thoughts, and he turned his mind to the potatoes. He had to show Thomas he could do his share, and do it well.

Pa came home two weeks later. Most sea captains lived close to their harbours, but Pa liked to say that he wasn’t most sea captains. The Dardens lived on their farm in Pennsylvania, and when Pa went to sea he took the train to Boston. He’d been gone for months, and they all watched him unpack his trunks. Garrison acted pleased when Pa gave him a fine new shirt, though he’d hoped for some drawing paper. Likely Pa believed he should have outgrown drawing. Garrison tried not to think about that.

Pa told stories about his voyage during supper, but afterwards he wanted to hear their news. Ma described the church social and the Chapmans’ new baby, and showed him the quilt she’d finished. Thomas and the girls chimed in, but Garrison was silent. He had an urge to draw, so he fetched his last piece of paper.

Garrison had made dozens of drawings of his family, but each time it was as if he were seeing them for the first time. He tried to capture the shadows in the folds of Ma’s dress, Pa’s prickly beard, Margaret’s quick smile, the spark in Thomas’s eyes, and the light flickering against Hannah’s curls.
Garrison was so absorbed in his picture that he barely heard Thomas describe the harvest. He heard Pa’s pride, though, when he praised Thomas’s work.
“I did pretty well,” Thomas said. “But Pa. Garrison needs a lesson in picking potatoes. His batch is always a raggedy mess!”
Garrison frowned at his brother. But Hannah had already changed the subject, showing Pa her sampler.
They stayed up later than usual, until finally Ma shooed them to bed. Garrison was the last one to move, and found himself alone with Pa.
“You’ve been quiet,” Pa said. “Can I see your picture?”
Garrison handed over his sketch. Pa studied it, then looked at his son. “You’re ten now, eh?” Garrison nodded, hoping Pa wouldn’t say that he was too old to be fooling around with pencils and paper.
“When I was ten, I wanted to stow away on my father’s ship,” Pa said. “When Thomas was ten, he convinced me to let him plant the corn.”
Garrison’s throat tightened. “I reckon I’m not like either of you.” Pa looked at the drawing again.
“No, you’re like both of us. You work hard, like Thomas. And you’re like me, too. I have wandering feet, but you have a wandering mind. It needs to see things differently to be content.”
Garrison frowned. “I’d rather love the land, like Thomas, or the sea . . .”

Pa patted his hand. “Thomas cares about our family very much — that’s why he’s worked so hard to bring us food. And my love for the sea earns good money. But there’s more in life than food and money. There’s happiness, for one. Does it make you happy to draw?”
Garrison nodded.

“And it makes me happy to look at your drawings. Not many folks can capture happiness on a piece of paper. Some might say drawing’s a waste of time, but they don’t understand how things are. Why, asking you to stop drawing would be like asking Thomas to stop farming, or me to stop going to sea. It would be like asking us to stop breathing, wouldn’t it?”

Garrison considered this, then replied, “No, sir. Not quite like breathing. If I couldn’t draw anymore, it would be...like someone took away my voice.” He hung his head. In his mind he could hear Thomas retorting, It’s not like you ever speak up, anyway.

But Pa squeezed his shoulder and said, “That would be a pure shame. Your voice is important, Garrison, whether it’s out loud or on paper. And speaking of paper...” Pa grinned. “Take a look in my trunk.”

Garrison reached into the trunk and curled his fingers around a sketchbook. For a moment he was speechless; happiness seemed to fill his body and stick in his throat. Then he managed to find his voice. “Oh, Pa — thank you!”

articles.title: I Am the Pine
articles.author: Clare Mishica
articles.description: A girl gathers the courage to go to art camp without her friends.
articles.intro: In this story, a girl named Tessa has to decide whether or not to go to art camp.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Mr. Weston says.
articles.permissions: All Highlights material is copyrighted by Highlights for Children, Inc., and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit www.highlights.com for more information.

blocks.text: “Look at this,” I said to my friend Mandy, holding up the bright-red paper I had grabbed as we entered the library. “There’s a five-day art camp at White Cliff this summer.”
Mandy took the paper and glanced at it. She stopped when her eyes caught the date. “It’s during the last week of July,” she said. “That’s the same time as the Hill County Fair.”
I had a feeling she’d mention that. “Yeah, that’s true,” I said. “But we always go to the fair.”
Mandy handed the paper back to me as she stood up to look at the shelf of new books.
“Tessa, you’re the artist.” She smiled. “My little brother thought the tulips I painted were rocket ships.”
“You may not think you can draw, but you can learn,” I said in my most encouraging voice. I really didn’t want to go alone.
Mandy giggled. “You sound like a TV commercial.”
Later, I called my friends Emily and Yoshiko. Yoshiko was going to the fair, and Emily would be out of town in July. I sighed and dropped my head down on the red paper.
“What’s this?” Mom asked, tugging the sheet.
“A flier for an art camp,” I explained.
“Would you like to go?” Mom asked.
“Probably not,” I said. “None of my friends are going.”
Mom patted my back and put the paper down. She knew I had a hard time jumping into something with no familiar faces around. “Maybe next year.”
“Maybe.” I sighed. “I’m going outside to draw the Westons’ pine tree. I’ll be out front.”
“Dinner will be ready in an hour,” Mom reminded me.

I opened the door and blinked at the bright sunshine as I sat on the front stoop. I thought about art camp and wished I felt a little braver. Mandy could make friends with a whole busload of kids in 10 minutes, but my stomach twirls like a roller coaster when I don’t know anyone around me. Besides, I did have fun at the fair with my friends. By the time I started sketching, I had convinced myself I was happy to skip the camp.

I used my ebony pencil and drew the pine’s strong trunk, straight and sturdy. I added the graceful branches, making sure the upper ones lifted gently toward the sun. Once I started sketching, I didn’t notice the fly buzzing around me or the sound of the lawn mower a few houses down. That’s why I jumped when Mr. Weston said, “You’ve done a great job capturing the strength of that tree on paper.”
“Thanks.” I smiled, glancing up. Mr. Weston was out for a walk. He used a cane, and I cut the grass for him every week.

“That pine was there when we moved in,” Mr. Weston said, “but I have planted pine trees before, when I worked for the park. You have to spread out the roots near the surface so the tree can get enough water and grow tall. If you plant the root ball too deep, the tree withers. I learned that roots grow out, not down.”
“That makes sense,” I said. I drew some prickly needles and added shading to the branches to give them depth.
“You have a good eye,” Mr. Weston said, then nodded good-bye before continuing on his walk.

I added a few more details before I stood up to stretch. I thought about what Mr. Weston told me as I stared at the towering pine. Now I noticed the knobby bumps of the roots sticking out of the ground all around the tree. I’d never realized how far a tree’s roots stretch outward. It made me think about art camp and my own roots. I wasn’t stretching them out at all. Instead, I was planting myself into one deep spot because it felt safer. I suddenly realized that if I wanted to grow, I had to start reaching out too.

That night, I took a deep breath and filled in the application for art camp. My stomach felt a little jumbled, but I also felt a shiver of excitement. I am stretching my roots out into new earth so I can grow , I thought. I am the pine .

articles.title: Guion Bluford
articles.subtitle: Space Explorer
articles.author: Linda Barr
articles.description: Linda Barr discusses the important life of Guion Bluford, the first Black astronaut in space.
articles.intro: In this text, Linda Barr tells about the important life of Guion Bluford, the first Black astronaut in space.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Bluford acts when he faces problems.
articles.permissions: Copyright © Highlights for Children, Inc., Columbus, Ohio. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Guy’s Goal

Did you ever wonder how things fly? Guion Bluford did. As a boy, he collected pictures of airplanes. He also built model planes. Bluford even thought about flying while he delivered newspapers. He experimented with ways to fold the papers. He wanted them to “fly” farther when he threw them in people’s front yards.
Guion Bluford is called Guy for short. He was born on November 22, 1942, in Philadelphia. In school, he liked to tackle hard questions in science and math. He liked to figure out how things work.

In 1957, Bluford was 15. Suddenly, everyone started talking about outer space. That year, Russia sent its first satellite into space. Satellites help telephones, radios, and televisions work. Some satellites gather information about what is happening on Earth.
The Space Age Begins
The Russian satellite was circling Earth. It was called Sputnik I . People in the United States began to worry. We had no satellite to send into space. The Russians were getting ahead of us.
Within a year, the U.S. began its own space program. It is the National Aeronautics and Space Administration. We call it NASA.
Now Bluford knew what he wanted to do. He would become an aerospace engineer. He would design satellites! He might even design a spaceship!

To become an engineer, Bluford knew he had to go to college. His parents and grandparents had gone to college. They encouraged him to go. However, a counselor at his high school discouraged him. He told Bluford he would not do well in college. Still, this young man wanted to reach his goal.
Bluford went to Pennsylvania State University (Penn State). The courses were not easy, but he did well. He was the only African American student in the engineering program.
Back then, all male students at Penn State had to complete military training. As part of college, Bluford completed an Air Force training program.

Bluford had fun in college, too. He met and married another student. Her name was Linda Tull. Their first son was born in 1964. That was the same year that Bluford graduated. He and his wife had another boy the next year.
Learning to Fly
After college, Bluford decided to become a pilot. He thought that would help him be a better engineer. He joined the Air Force. Bluford earned his pilot’s wings in 1966. He became a fighter pilot.

At that time, the United States was fighting a war in Vietnam. Bluford was sent to Vietnam in 1966. His work was very dangerous. In 1967, Bluford came back from Vietnam. He was a very good pilot. He was asked to teach other pilots.
Bluford still had his dream. He wanted to be an engineer. In 1972, he entered the Air Force Institute of Technology. He graduated in 1974. Bluford received a master’s degree in aerospace engineering.
By 1978, Bluford had earned a doctorate. A doctorate is the highest college degree. He was now Dr. Guion Bluford. Do you think his high school counselor might have been surprised?
Becoming an Astronaut

NASA had begun building a new kind of spacecraft. This kind would be used again and again. It would fly into space and return to Earth many times. These trips were called missions. This new spacecraft was called a shuttle.
Now NASA needed astronauts to fly some shuttles. Some of the astronauts would do experiments in space. They would be called mission specialists.
In 1978, more than 8,000 people applied to be mission specialists. Bluford applied, too. He wanted to learn more about space.
NASA chose only 35 people to be mission specialists. Bluford was one of them. He would be the first African American astronaut in space.

The 35 new astronauts started training in Houston, Texas. For a year, they studied many subjects. For example, they learned about medicine in case an astronaut got sick in space. They learned how to do many kinds of experiments.
Since there is no gravity in space to hold them down, astronauts can float! They wore spacesuits underwater to get ready to float in space.
Bluford enjoyed the training. He said, “The job is so fantastic, I don’t need a hobby. My hobby is going to work.”
First Shuttle Flight

Four years after he finished his training, Bluford was launched into space. It was August 30, 1983. This was the eighth trip for NASA’s new shuttles. It was the first nighttime launch of a shuttle. The shuttle Challenger thundered into the dark sky, lifted by a fiery cloud. The flames turned night into day.

Bluford’s parents were not there to see the launch. They had both died. Still, his wife and sons watched proudly. So did many important scientists and educators. Also watching were famous African Americans.
Bluford was excited, but his training helped him stay calm. This Challenger flight carried one pilot and three mission specialists.
At Work in Space

During the mission, Bluford helped launch a satellite into orbit. Soon it was circling 22,000 miles above Earth. The satellite belonged to India. It would improve the telephone and television service in that country.
Bluford also helped to perform experiments. Some of the experiments created special medicines. Many of these medicines can be made on Earth. However, Earth’s gravity makes it much harder.
In the future, it is hoped that many medicines will be made easily in space. First, someone must figure out how to do it. Bluford’s experiments helped.

During this mission, Bluford also helped to test a robotic arm. He used it to move around a very heavy object. Later, the arm would be used to pick up satellites in space. It would bring them inside the shuttle for repair.

This mission lasted six days. The Challenger landed at night, another first. A huge crowd watched it return. Bluford said, “I’m really humbled tonight to see so many people out here to welcome us back! I feel very proud to be a member of this team, and I think we have a tremendous future with the space shuttle — I mean all of us!”
Back to Space
Bluford made three more trips into space. They were in 1985, 1991, 1992. He spent almost 700 hours in space!

In October 1985, he flew aboard the Challenger again. This time the crew included eight astronauts. Three were from other nations, including Germany. That was the first time a shuttle had such a large crew.
The mission specialists performed 75 experiments during that flight. They worked in a laboratory inside the shuttle. It was called Spacelab.
Bluford helped the German specialists experiment with gravity. For example, they explored how liquids move when there is no gravity. The crew also melted and grew crystals.

Bluford explained what it’s like without gravity. “When you’re floating around in space, you feel the same when you’re upside down as you do when you’re right side up,” he said. “You don’t feel any different when you’re standing on the ceiling than you do when you’re standing on the floor. Imagine being able to walk across the ceiling or along the walls as easily as you can walk on the floor!”
Tragedy

The next time the Challenger was launched, it exploded. All seven crew members died. It was January 1986. The nation was shocked. “We had gotten very comfortable with shuttle launches,” Bluford said. People had almost forgotten about the dangers of space travel.
For nearly three years, NASA scientists worked on the shuttles. They made them safer. Bluford helped with the new design. The shuttles began to fly again in 1988.
In April 1991, Bluford traveled into space again. He was aboard the shuttle Discovery . The seven astronauts carried out military experiments. They also launched a small satellite.

Bluford’s last shuttle flight was in December 1992. He was aboard Discovery again. The crew studied how a lack of gravity affects muscle, blood, and bone. They explored ways to track space “junk” orbiting Earth.
After NASA
In July 1993, Bluford retired from NASA. He had been in the military for nearly 30 years. For half of that time, he had worked for NASA.

But Bluford did not stop working. He joined companies that provided engineering and computer services. They worked with NASA and other government agencies. In 2002, he became president of the Aerospace Technology Group. He is an inductee to the International Space Hall of Fame (1997); the U.S. Astronaut Hall of Fame (2010); and the National Aviation Hall of Fame (2019).
Bluford often visits schools and colleges. He encourages young people to follow their dreams. He tells them to work hard and prepare for whatever career they have chosen.
He warns, “A lot of times, people give up when they taste a little bit of defeat.” Bluford urges young people to keep working toward their goals.
“I had to struggle through those courses at Penn State — but if you really want to do something and are willing to put in the hard work it takes, then someday — bingo, you’ve done it!”
Guion Bluford had “the right stuff” to see his dreams come true. He thinks you do, too!

options.label: Bluford's first trip into space was very rewarding.

Less gravity makes going into space fun and exciting.
Bluford went to space many times for different reasons.

articles.title: Amy's Origami
articles.author: Mimi Chapra
articles.description: A young girl creates her own origami designs.
articles.intro: Origami is the Japanese art of paper folding. In origami, colorful paper is folded many times to make shapes and objects, such as paper animals or boats. One of the most popular animals to fold is cranes, birds with long legs, a long neck, and a long beak. In this story, a young girl practices making origami.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Mr. Shiko feels about Amy’s origami.
articles.permissions: Copyright © Highlights for Children, Inc., Columbus, Ohio. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Amy spreads her origami paper out like a fan. Which color should she choose? Fiery red? Sunny yellow? Moonlight blue? She picks out emerald green.
Closing her eyes, Amy remembers how her teacher, Mr. Shiko, turned flat gold foil into a jumping frog.
Amy makes her first fold, carefully. “My creases are sharp, just like Mr. Shiko’s,” she thinks. “Maybe I can do this by heart.”

Patiently Amy sticks with it, fold after fold, until her frog magically appears. “Now I’ll push on my frog and watch him hop,” she thinks. Amy pushes, but her frog doesn’t hop. He doesn’t budge an inch.

“Maybe a crane will be simpler,” Amy thinks. She chooses pale blue paper. Patiently, Amy sticks with it, fold after fold, until her crane sprouts wings. Then Amy tugs the tail back and forth to make the wings flap. But her crane stands still, without a flap or a flutter.

Taking a break, Amy looks out the window. She sees her brother bouncing a ball in the driveway. Suddenly she’s back at her desk, scrunching up a piece of paper, wrinkling it and crinkling it into a tiny ball.
“My balled-up paper is just right for a creature’s head,” thinks Amy. She cuts a long orange strip. Back and forth she fan-folds it as Mr. Shiko taught her, making a long accordion-shaped body.
“Now my creature needs feelers,” thinks Amy, pleating two teeny green strips. Then she paints eyes and a mouth on the head, and pastes the parts together. Ta da! A painted lady caterpillar steps out.
“I can make zillions of animals,” thinks Amy. She fan-folds thin strips for whiskers and wings, creases tiny triangles for ears and beaks, scrunches balls for bodies, big and small.
Now Amy makes...a yellow-bellied bumblebee. A pink-eared bunny. An orange-billed duck. A red-headed hen. A purple-feathered parakeet.
At the East Wind Craft Shop, Amy shows Mr. Shiko her creatures. Admiringly, he says, “Amy, you have talent.”
“But I used scissors and glue. Isn’t that cheating?” asks Amy.
Mr. Shiko thinks for a moment. “Well, no. You made fan folds. That’s traditional origami. And the rest is original Origami Amy! May I display ‘Origami Amy’ in my window?”
Amy nods, excited. Together they arrange the creatures so they’re swimming across a river of glass. Then Mr. Shiko puts up a sign.
Amy stands on tiptoe to read it. “ORIGAMI AMY was created by the talented AMY FUJITA.”
Amy smiles. “That’s me!” she shouts.

options.label: Using your imagination can lead to exciting things.
It is best to follow your teacher's directions.
Breaking the rules is never rewarded.
Origami is a very hard art to learn.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the story?

options.label: Amy works very hard on her origami.

Amy is not able to fold origami paper well.

questions.prompt: The author repeats the word "patiently" in paragraph 4 and 5 to show that —

options.label: It tells how Amy decides to make a sports ball out of paper instead.

It tells the steps for making folds in paper to create an object.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Have you ever seen origami or folded it yourself? Describe what you made or saw.
Origami is an art that takes a lot of patience to get right. What are some other things that take a lot of patience? Is it easy or difficult for you to do things that require a lot of patience?

Origami is an art that comes from Japan, but is now done all over the world. What is your favorite type of art to make? Do you know where this art form comes from? Do some research to learn more about it and share with others.

articles.title: Eating Green
articles.author: Teresa Bateman
articles.description: A leprechaun finds a way to escape being eaten on St. Patrick's Day.
articles.intro: Leprechauns are tiny elves with magical powers. In this story, a weasel-like animal called a pine marten comes across a leprechaun.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Davin says.
articles.permissions: "Eating Green" by Teresa Bateman, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Morna the pine marten crept silently on four soft paws, following something moving through the tall green grass of the Irish countryside.
Was it a mouse? A bird?
POUNCE!
“Faith and begorrah!” the creature shouted.
Wait. This prey had neither fur nor feathers. It was a wee little man dressed all in green.
Morna gave a toothy grin. “Hello, lunch,” she said.
“My name is Davin, not lunch,” the leprechaun grumbled, brushing off his coat.
“You’re ‘lunch’ to me,” Morna replied, slinking her long furry body around him. “I’ll use your shoelaces to floss my teeth afterwards.”
The little man gazed up with a pleading look in his eyes.
“Please don’t eat me. Today is Saint Patrick’s Day, and my brothers and I have been preparing a feast. If you’re hungry, join us. It would be a great pity to miss it.”
“Brothers?” asked Morna, eyes gleaming. “There are more of you?”
“Indeed, I come from a large clan,” Davin replied, “and each of us has been preparing a special dish for our holiday meal.”
Ah, thought Morna. I can eat their feast, then have the leprechauns for dessert!
“What might you be preparing?” she asked.
“Something green, of course,” Davin replied.
“Salads and such?” Morna sometimes ate vegetables, although she preferred meat.

“Nothing so obvious,” Davin assured her. “Michael has been working on a green pork dish. We’ve been using the meat as a doormat for the past month. It’s now a fine furry green, with an aroma that cannot be described.”
“Urp,” said Morna, her lips curling at the thought.
“Sean has been dunking loaves of soda bread in water, then leaving them out on the windowsill, spritzing them with spit every hour or so. They’re as green as emeralds now. I think I saw one move!”
“Ewww,” said Morna, swallowing painfully.
“And the eggs! We buried them in the ground a year ago. It’s time to dig them up. They should be fine and moldy green by now.”
“Gaaaah,” Morna groaned, covering her muzzle with her paws.
“Dougal milked a cow four months ago, then put the milk out in the sun. First it curdled, then it stank, but now it’s a pure sweet green, with something wiggling around inside it.”
“AAAAGH.” Morna could feel a squirming in her own stomach to match the one Davin was describing.

“Everything is nearly ready,” Davin continued, “I even had a taste this morning while my brothers weren’t looking. Aye, it’s a grand feast indeed. I can feel the food moving around inside me like a giant worm settling in for a happy stay. Come with me and you can try it, too.”
At that Morna dashed off, her face as green as everything Davin had described.

The leprechaun grinned and settled his hat back on his head as he hurried home, thinking of the feast that was to come. There would be no green pork nor any of the horrible food of which he’d spoken. Still, cabbage was a lovely shade of green and they’d have plenty of that with their corned beef.
And the best part of the meal, of course, would be eating it, not being it.

options.label: It is always safer to travel with a group.

Always throw out your food before it goes bad.
It is respectful to welcome strangers into your home.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Davin is going to celebrate St. Patrick's Day with his family by eating a feast of green food. Does your family celebrate St. Patrick's Day? If so, what are some foods or other customs your family uses to celebrate this holiday? If not, what is a holiday your family celebrates by eating special foods? How is this food important to the celebration of the holiday?
In the story, Davin tricks Morna to avoid becoming her meal. Have you ever had to trick someone to get out of something or to get your way? What was it and how did you trick them?

Leprechauns like Davin are clever, magical elves. Many cultures have stories of trickster characters. What are some other books or movies that you know that have a trickster character? How are the trickster characters in these stories similar and different to Davin?

articles.title: Annie's Decision
articles.author: Kristina Jordan Cobarrubia
articles.description: A little girl who is blind tries to find the perfect pet at an animal shelter.
articles.intro: An animal shelter is a place where pets that do not have a home live. People sometimes visit animal shelters to find a new pet to bring home and care for, or adopt. In this story, a girl and her mother visit an animal shelter to make a decision, or choice, about which cat to adopt.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Annie makes her choice.
articles.permissions: "Annie's Decision" by Kristina Jordan Cobarrubia, Ladybug, © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: On Saturday, the animal shelter is filled with voices. Dogs scratch at their doors, baying and yelping, but in a large, wire enclosure, the kitties meow softly. The dog area smells musty, but the kitty pen smells sweet, like alfalfa.
I thread my fingers through the cool wire of the gate. I can't see the kitties because I'm blind. I listen hard. Do any of those meows sound like kitten meows?
“I don't think I hear any kittens, Mom.”
“You're right, Annie. But there are lots of cats…fat ones, skinny ones, some with long tails, and one with no tail!”
I hold Mom’s warm hand as the shelter director, Mrs. Pat, takes us inside the cat pen. I sit down and stretch out my hands to pet the kitties. A big, warm kitty sits down against my side.
“Sammy likes you, Annie. He’s the big one,” Mrs. Pat says.
“I like him, too.” I pet Sammy’s soft, furry coat, his loud purr rumbling under my fingers. “Will Sammy get adopted today?”
“Maybe,” says Mrs. Pat. “He’s an older kitty, and most people want kittens. But he’s so loving, maybe today will be his turn.”

My mom and I take a pregnant cat, Muffin, home with us. We’ll care for her until she has her kittens. Then we’ll care for the kittens until it’s time for them to come back to the shelter to find new homes.
But I can keep one kitten for myself!
I give Muffin her food and water every day and brush her coat. One morning, I get a surprise! I can feel soft, mewing little bundles inside the box with Muffin! “Mom! The kittens are here!”
I try not to bounce while Mom checks each of the kittens. “How many are there?” I ask.
“There are six. They’re all about the same size, except for this one. He’s big!”
“Like Sammy,” I say.
“Not quite that big,” Mom says with a laugh.
The kittens lie like furry beanbags on the soft flannel. Mom tells me that the kittens’ eyes are shut at first. They’re kind of like me, I think.
When I hold them to my cheek, they feel silky and smell sweet and oaty, like a field of summer grass. Their mews are high and tiny.
“So, do you know yet which kitten you’ll keep?” Mom asks me one day.
I bounce on my knees. “Oh! How do I choose?”
“Well, try to listen to your heart.”
Once the kittens’ eyes are open, they begin to play, climbing on my legs and up my arms, their little claws pricking lightly like needles. Sometimes they fall asleep in my lap.

The kittens will go back to the shelter next week, but I still can’t decide which one to keep. Should it be Jet-Jet, with the long tail? Or Mimi, with the tiny voice? Or should it be Mr. Motor, the one with the humming purr?
A purr like Sammy’s, I think.
All the kittens are fun. But which is the right one?
On Monday, the shelter is quiet.
“What have we here?” Mrs. Pat asks.
“Six kittens and Muffin!” I tell her.
“And did you keep one at home for yourself?”
“No,” I say. “I can’t decide.”
“Now is the time,” says Mom. “You stay here and pick your kitty while I take care of the paperwork with Mrs. Pat.”

I sit down in the cat room. When I open the carrier, the kittens jump past my fingers to explore their new place. In a moment, the carrier is empty, and all the kittens are gone! I stretch out my hands, but I can’t find them anywhere. Then I feel a warm body curl up against my side. I pet the kitty, and hug the kitty, and the kitty loudly purr, purr, purrs.
“It’s Sammy, isn’t it?” I whisper. “I know it’s you! Why don’t you have a home yet?” Sammy just rubs his head against my cheek and purrs. His purr is so loud, it rumbles against my heart.
When Mom and Mrs. Pat come back, Sammy is snoozing in my lap.
“I’ve picked my kitty!” I announce.
“You have? Which one?” asks Mrs. Pat.
“Sammy!”
“But I thought you wanted a kitten,” says Mom.
“Nope,” I say. “I want Sammy. I listened to my heart!”

options.label: Annie and her mother take home Muffin.
Mrs. Pat tells Annie about Sammy.
Sammy comes home with Annie.
Muffin has kittens.

questions.prompt: Which event happens first in the story?

options.label: it introduces a problem that will be solved

options.label: "'There are six. They're all about the same size, except for this one. He's big!'" (Paragraph 13)

"A purr like Sammy's, I think." (Paragraph 23)

discussionQuestions.prompt: In this story, Annie and her family first foster, or take care of for a short amount of time, Muffin and her kittens. Why do you think some people choose to foster animals living at an animal shelter? How can fostering help both the animals and the people who take care of them? Would you consider fostering an animal from a shelter? Why or why not?

Annie learns that she has to "listen to her heart" when making tough decisions. What is a tough decision that you have had to make? How did you make your decision? What advice do you have for others who have to make a tough decision?

Annie decides to adopt an older cat, Sammy, rather than a kitten. What do you think the benefits of adopting an older cat might be? Why do you think it might be hard for older cats to get adopted? If you had the choice, would you like to adopt a kitten or an older cat? Why?

articles.title: Tata's Coyotes
articles.author: Betsy James
articles.description: A young girl named Ana learns about the coyotes living on her family's apricot farm.
articles.intro: Coyotes are wild animals similar to wolves that are found in North America. In this story, a young girl named Ana is woken up by coyotes outside her window.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Ana says about the coyotes.
articles.permissions: "Tata's Coyotes" Betsy James, Click , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: I thought I heard ghosts crying outside in Tata’s orchard. Mama came in all sleepy and hugged me in my quilt. “It’s just coyotes, Ana.”
Yip-yip yaooo, yap-yap! sang the quavery howls.
“Are they dogs?” I asked. I felt safe, now that she was there.
“Wild dogs. They live in the desert and the woods, but sometimes they come close to the house and sing.”
Outside my window the apricot trees bloomed white in the dark. Two bushy-tailed shadows flicked out of sight.
“Maybe they like apricots,” I said. “Like me.”
“Then they’ll have to wait a month or two,” said Mama, kissing me back to bed. “Like you.”
“Coyotes like Tata’s apricots,” I told Papa at breakfast.
“I do too. And last year’s jam is almost gone.” He licked his fingers. “I heard those coyotes. How times change! Tata would have shot them.”
“Shot them!” Tata was my grandpa. He planted those trees. Why would he shoot singing dogs?
“Tata raised sheep. Coyotes sometimes killed his lambs to feed their pups. Everybody’s got to eat,” said Papa. “But mostly coyotes eat mice.”
“Yay, coyotes!” said Mama. She hates mice.
“And frogs and snakes,” Papa added.
“Yuck!” I cried. “Coyotes eat snakes?”
“They get desperate,” he said, “when they can’t get apricot jam.”
It bothered me that Tata would shoot coyotes. But it also bothered me that coyotes would kill lambs. Even to feed their pups. I was glad there weren’t any lambs around.
The next evening Papa came in smiling and said, “Can you be quiet, Ana? I’ve got a surprise.”
We walked far along the irrigation ditch in the cold spring dusk and peeked through the willows. Papa whispered, “See that hole under the cottonwood root?”
It was hard to see. Then, out of the hole crept a coyote, so nearly the color of the ground it was almost invisible. It looked around, shook itself, and ran into the woods.
“There’ll be pups in that den,” said Papa after we’d tiptoed back to the orchard.
“Are they warm enough?”
“Coyotes dig cozy dens,” answered Papa. He looked up at the apricot trees. “It’s these blossoms that need to stay warm, or no jam.”
“Then we’d have to eat snakes,” I said.
But the weather stayed warm, and the blossoms turned into a thousand baby apricots. Papa said the pups would be big enough to play outside the den.
“Let’s go see!” I cried.
But he said no — if the parents got even a sniff of us they’d move to a different place. “And it’s not just people they have to watch out for. A hawk or an owl can carry off a pup, easy.”
Then one day Papa brought home a brand-new pair of binoculars.

We didn’t go near the den. Instead we climbed a faraway tree. Through the binoculars I saw a furry little face poke out of the dark den. Then another, and another. Four fat pups spilled out, biting and wrestling. Their mama — or maybe it was their papa — watched over them.
“When the parents go hunting, they chew up whatever they catch,” Papa whispered. “Then they spit it up for the pups to eat.”
“Spit-up mice for breakfast? No way!” I whispered back. “Papa, have you been watching them all this time?”
“Not from close by. I didn’t want to scare them.” He laughed softly. “Now, if Tata could hear me say that!”
The days got hot, and the apricots got ripe. Mama made apricot jam, apricot pie, and apricot bread. She gave apricots to all the neighbors and there were still so many that they littered the ground.
“Somebody needs to eat these apricots!” she said.
“Somebody is.” Papa showed us coyote droppings in the orchard — full of apricot pits!
Mama said, “I hope they’re leaving room to eat mice.”
We watched the pups grow bigger. At first there were four of them. But one day there were three, and by the end of summer only two.
“Where are the others?” I asked.
But I knew.
Papa sighed. “No matter how hard the parents try to keep their babies safe, the hawks and owls grab some. Like I said . . .”
I nodded. “Everybody’s got to eat.”
But I felt terrible. That night it got cold. I could hear owls calling in the dark trees, Hu-huu!
I scootched down in my quilt, like a coyote pup diving into its den. What if an owl swooped through my window? What if a hawk dove down? Then I heard it. Yip-yaoo, yap-yap!
Something was different. Along with the grown-up voices in the orchard there were two new, high, squeaky ones.
Yip-yip yaooo, squeak-squeak!
I sat up in bed and laughed.
Squeak-squeak! It was as if each little coyote were singing, “So what if the world is full of owls? I can take care of myself now. Hear me sing!”
I went to the window and leaned out. Wind rustled the apricot leaves.
“I heard somebody’s bare feet,” Papa said behind me. “You OK, sweetheart?”
“The pups are singing!” I said.
Together we leaned and listened. Yip-yaooo-squeak!
“What would Tata do if he knew his orchard was a safe place for coyotes?” I asked.
Papa kissed my ear. “He’d shake his head and laugh.”

options.label: Everyone has to eat, including animals.
Humans should be afraid of wild animals.
Saying goodbye to people we love can be hard.
There is a lot people can learn from other living things.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of "Tata's Coyotes"?

options.label: will do anything to get apricots.

How do Ana's feelings about the coyotes change from the beginning of "Tata's Coyotes" to the end?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Ana becomes less afraid of the coyotes by learning more about them. Are there any animals you are afraid of? Why are you afraid of them? What would you do if one of these animals lived near you? Is there something that you could learn or do to feel less afraid of that animal?
According to the text, Tata would have shot the coyotes if he was alive. Why would he do this? Do you agree with his actions? Why or why not?

In the story, how does the coyote family take care of the young pups? How is this similar to and different from how Papa and Mama care for Ana? What similarities do you see in how the coyotes take care of their families and how people care for their families?

articles.title: Raji's Race
articles.author: Mélina Mangal
articles.description: A girl looks for a way to race against the stars by using her family background and science.
articles.intro: In this story, a young girl wants to race the stars in the night sky.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Raji feels.
articles.permissions: "Raji's Race" by Mélina Mangal, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Raji dreamed of stars. She loved how they sparkled and danced, how they shared their light in the dark night. “I will reach them, somehow,” she thought. Before going to sleep, Raji imagined speeding among the sparkling lights. But when she woke up, she was still in her bed, her pillow under her head.
“Mama,” she asked one morning, “how can I race with the stars?”
“Maybe you need to practice,” Mama said.
Raji pondered the possibilities, then put on her running shoes. She ran up the block, then down. She ran through the yard, then around.
That night, when the first star came into sight, Raji was ready. She laced her shoes tighter and took off running. But as fast as she ran, she only reached the end of the block.

Mama tucked Raji in that night with an old rag doll. “This was passed down several generations, a reminder of how, with fortitude, our ancestors escaped from slavery. The North Star guided them all the way to Freedom.”
Ancestors visited Raji in her dreams that night, like stars, swirling and twirling. When she woke, she was still in her bed, her pillow under her head.
“Papa,” she asked, “how can I race with the stars?”
“Maybe you need some wheels,” Papa said.
Raji visualized a vehicle. Her wagon? Too slow. Her bike? Too big. “My scooter,” she said.
She scooted up the block, then down. She scooted through the yard, then around.
That night, when the first star came into sight, Raji was ready. She checked her tires and took off scooting. But as fast as she moved, she only reached the edge of her neighborhood.

Papa tucked Raji into bed that night, jingling her dancing payals on her bedpost. “When I was a boy in India, we learned of Dhruva, the boy who became the North Star. By showing devotion, he made his parents proud. He was meant to sparkle in the night sky, just as you, Raji, shine here.
Raji visited India in her dreams that night. She saw Dhruva, like a star, pulsing and praying. When she woke up, she was still in her bed, her pillow under her head.
“Mama and Papa,” Raji asked, “why can’t I race with the stars?”
“Who said you can’t?” they asked back.
Raji looked up at the sky. “I will find a way.”
All day she thought and she thought and she thought. There must be a way.
She ran up the block, then down. She scooted through the yard, then around. Then she had another idea. “I will read my star book.” Raji ran to her bookshelf.

Raji read about stars near and far: Altair, Vega, and the North Star. She read about ancient astronomers and pioneering space explorers. She read her book from cover to cover, until the moon began to hover. She fell asleep.
When she woke, the moon glistened and the stars shimmered, waiting. “I’m ready to race,” Raji whispered.

Raji strapped on her helmet. She wheeled her scooter into the moonlight. She put her rag doll into the basket along with her star book, then fastened her payals. She pushed off, and within seconds Raji’s scooter blasted into space. Raji was in the star race.

As she scooted faster and faster, the stars moved farther and farther. When she slowed down, Raji’s ancestors smiled, their faces glowing in the starlight. Dhruva waved as she rode by. Ancient astronomers nodded to her as they studied.

Raji finally reached the stars. She rode North with Polaris, marveling at the lights of the aurora borealis. She coasted with the dog star Sirius, as it rose in the sky. She scooted higher with Vega, then back down where Altair waited below.
Raji and her scooter rode with blazing bursts of light for the rest of the night. Exhausted from riding so fast and so far, Raji fell asleep just as the sun began to rise.
“Hey, star girl,” Mama said when Raji woke up. “How did you sleep?”
“I reached the stars last night!” Raji said excitedly.
“How?” asked Mama and Papa.
“With these.” Raji held up her doll and her star book and jingled her payals.
“Who won the race?” asked Mama and Papa.
Raji thought of the fortitude of her ancestors, the devotion of Dhruva, the astronomers’ years of study.
“It’s not a race,” Raji smiled. “It’s another time and place, and I have a long way to go.” She opened her star book. “I’ve only just begun.”
Raji looked up into the sky. In the daylight, she couldn’t see the stars. But she smiled anyway, knowing they were always there watching her.

options.label: A girl visits India to learn more about the stars.
A girl studies very hard to understand the stars.
A girl tries many different ways to visit the stars.
A girl asks her parents to take her to see the stars.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best summarizes "Raji's Race"?

options.label: It takes a lot of practice to reach the stars.

questions.prompt: What is a lesson Raji learns in the story?

options.label: "'I will reach them, somehow,' she thought." (Paragraph 1)

questions.prompt: After Raji reads about the stars and astronomers in her star book she —

options.label: how hot something feels.
how large something looks.
how bright something shines.

articles.title: The Danderfield Twins: Too Much Snow
articles.author: Polly Horvath
articles.description: Friends who are bored with winter try to find a way to have fun.
articles.intro: The Danderfield Twins is a series of books about a set of twins that goes on adventures. In this story, the Danderfield twins and their friends play in the snow.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes about the important events in the story.
articles.permissions: "The Danderfield Twins" by Polly Horvath, Spider , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: “I hate January,” said Kavita as she, Marty, and the twins met on Saturday to play. “I’m tired of playing inside, and there’s nothing to do outside. All it ever does is snow. I’m tired of making snowmen and going sledding.”

“What we need are more snow games,” said Marty. “Let’s put our earmuffs — er — heads together and try to think up some new ones . . .” The fat, falling snowflakes gave him an idea. “I’ve got one: let’s see who can catch the most snowflakes on his tongue.”
They all stuck out their tongues, and Plato jumped on them as they tried to catch flakes.
“No, Plato! Get down!” said Maisie. “How are we supposed to count? I can’t tell if I’m catching one or three at a time.”

“Yeah, how about a different game? We could play Fox and Geese,” said Kavita. “First, we run around in a circle to make a track, then we make spokes to a center point in the circle, like a wheel. The fox is in the center and has to try to catch the geese, but we can run only on the paths we’ve made. Any goose that gets caught becomes a fox. I’ll be the first one.”
The children played until Plato, who didn’t seem to understand about keeping to the path, made so many new paths that he ruined the wheel and Marty’s wheelchair got stuck in the snow.

“O.K., my turn,” said Maisie. “Let’s ask Mrs. Banks to help us make maple candy. She told me that when she lived in Quebec, her family boiled down maple syrup and poured it on fresh snow until it hardened as candy. She always keeps a big jug of maple syrup in her pantry.”
“What a lovely idea!” said Mrs. Banks a few minutes later, and she got right to work boiling the syrup.
“Wow,” said Kavita as Mrs. Banks poured the syrup into shapes on new snow. “It takes a lot of syrup to make a little candy.”
“Yeah,” said Deidre, “but it is so worth it.”
“Thanks, Mrs. Banks!” said Maisie.
“If you need anything else, kids, just let me know!” hollered Mrs. Banks as she headed inside.
“Let’s make a snowball stand next,” said Deidre. “You know, like a lemonade stand.”
“Who would want to buy a snowball, Dodo Head, when you can make your own for free?” asked Maisie.

“Someone who wants to find a prize hidden inside, Dodo Head yourself,” said Deidre. “We can take little things from around the house we don’t want anymore and make a snowball around each of them. They’ll be like surprise balls, and we can sell them for a quarter apiece.”
“Hey, that’s a pretty good idea . . . for you,” said Marty, grinning.
“Why you . . .” said Deidre. “Here, have a priceless sample!” She threw a snowball at him.
When they had sold their last surprise snowball and made a total of three dollars and twenty-five cents, it was Marty’s turn again.
“For my game, we need to go to the store and spend our money,” said Marty.
The girls groaned but agreed to run to the corner store and buy a big bag of marshmallows.
“What are we doing, making hot chocolate?” asked Maisie.
“What kind of game would that be? Go into the garage and stay there until I say it’s O.K. to come out,” said Marty. “And no peeking!”
“What is this game called, Being Bored in the Garage?” called Deidre when fifteen minutes had passed.
“O.K., come out,” said Marty, opening the garage door. “Mrs. Banks helped me hide the marshmallows all over the snowy backyard. Whoever finds the most, wins.”
“It’s like an Easter egg hunt,” said Maisie, “only the marshmallows blend in. This is going to be hard!”
“There are twenty-five hidden,” said Marty.
“There were only twenty-five marshmallows in that big bag?” asked Deirdre.
“Well, I may have eaten a few,” admitted Marty. “On your mark, get set, go!”
The girls raced around the yard. Even Plato found a few marshmallows.
“I don’t think it counts if you eat them, Plato,” said Deidre. “I found the most, so what’s my prize?”
“You get to think of the last game of the day,” said Marty.

“Some prize,” said Deidre. “All right. How about Stick the Carrot on the Snowman? We make a snowman and then blindfold each other. The person who gets the carrot closest to where the snowman’s nose should be, wins.”
The girls were starting to roll the big lower ball of the snowman when suddenly they were pelted with snowballs.
“It’s Bob and Rob! Looks like they’ve chosen the last game of the day for us!” shouted Deidre, grabbing a snowball for a counterattack.

Snow storms are fun and exciting.
Friends can help you have fun.

articles.title: No Plans? No Problem!
articles.author: Stephen Currie
articles.description: The story of how Benjamin Banneker saved the design of Washington, D.C.
articles.intro: Washington is the capital of the United States. In this article, Stephen Currie tells the story of the man who planned the city — and the man who saved the plan!
articles.annotationTask: As you read, highlight the problems that happened during the city’s design.
articles.permissions: "No Plans? No Problem!" by Stephen Currie, AppleSeeds , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: When the U. S. government decided to build the city of Washington in 1789, it hired a Frenchman named Pierre L’Enfant to design it. L’Enfant had big ideas. He was a hard worker. And he wanted the new city to be grand and glorious. He planned wide streets that ran straight, and other streets that ran diagonally. He planned public parks and fine buildings. With two assistants, he drew up careful plans that showed exactly what he had in mind. Unfortunately, L’Enfant was very hard to get along with. He was always picking fights with "Aerial view from above the U.S. Capitol, people. He kept demanding more money for looking west along the National Mall, his project, too, and he got mad when the Washington, D.C." by Carol M. Highsmith is in government didn’t want to give him more. the public domain. But what really made L’Enfant mad was when people started asking to see his exact plans. Oh, L’Enfant told them about some of his ideas — the streets, the parks, and the buildings. But he refused to show his drawings to anyone but the two assistants. Everybody else, he snapped, would just have to wait until he was ready. For more than a year, people asked. For more than a year, L’Enfant refused. Every month they got more frustrated. Every month he got more furious! Finally, President Washington fired him. L’Enfant grabbed all the copies of his plans and stormed back to France.

When the Americans found out, they were very upset. No plans? They would have to begin all 1. Glorious(adjective)worthy of praise; beautiful and inspiring 2. said angrily over again! The thought made everybody tired. But they were in luck. One of L’Enfant’s assistants was a free black man named Benjamin Banneker. Banneker was a farmer and a scientist. He had studied the moon, the tides, and the weather. He had designed and built a clock. Now, he was ready to help out his country. Banneker announced that he had a copy of the plans — in his head! Months of working with L’Enfant and studying his designs had helped him remember every detail. Within a few days, with the help of co-worker Andrew Ellicott, Banneker had drawn L’Enfant’s plans. He saved the day and foiled L’Enfant’s trick. Washington was built as planned, and most visitors love its wide streets, its beautiful parks, and its grand buildings. In the history books, L’Enfant gets most of the credit for the design. But now you know the whole story: how Pierre L’Enfant ran off with the plans, and how Benjamin Banneker (with Ellicott’s help) turned no plans into no problem. "No Plans? No Problem!" by Stephen Currie, AppleSeeds, © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

articles.title: Kobe Bryant: A Basketball Legend
articles.author: India James
articles.description: Basketball star Kobe Bryant (1978-2020) worked hard to achieve success.
articles.intro: The NBA, or National Basketball Association, is a professional basketball league in North America. Many of the world's best basketball players play for teams in the NBA. This informational is about the life of Kobe Bryant (1978-2020), one of the greatest and most well-known NBA basketball players of all time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Kobe Bryant’s actions.
articles.permissions: Kobe Bryant: A Basketball Legend" by India James. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Whispers and stories began to travel around Lower Merion, Pennsylvania. There was a new eighth grader in town. He could speak Italian, and that was interesting. But there was something else that got everyone talking. This new student could really play basketball. Finally, the stories reached the high school basketball coach’s ears. He decided that he had to see the new student play for himself. The coach invited the student to come practice with his team.

The next Saturday morning, a tall eighth grader came to practice with the high schoolers. The coach waved him into the gym. The eighth grader was much shorter and smaller than the others. Would he be able to keep up?
It only took five minutes for the head coach to make up his mind. He turned to the assistant coach and said, “this kid is a pro.”
The kid’s name was Kobe Bryant, and everyone knew he was going to be a star.
Basketball Beginnings

Kobe Bean Bryant was born in the U.S., but he moved to Italy when he was only six years old. Kobe’s dad played in the NBA, then moved his family to Europe to play basketball there. Kobe grew up playing basketball in Italy during the school year and flying home for summer camps in the States. He also played soccer and learned to speak Italian and Spanish.

The Bryant family moved to Pennsylvania in 1991 when Kobe was 13 years old. He had already decided he wanted to become a basketball player like his dad. Anyone who watched him knew that Kobe was one of the best players. He led his team to a state championship during his final year of high school at Lower Merion. They hadn’t won in 53 years!
At the end of high school, Kobe had to decide what to do. Should he go to college, or should he play professional basketball right away?

On April 29, 1996, Kobe sat in front of reporters to announce his decision. He was relaxed and joking. He wore a suit with sunglasses pushed up on his forehead. Kobe announced that “I have decided to skip college and take my talent to the NBA.”
Kobe was drafted by the Charlotte Hornets two months later. He was quickly traded to the Los Angeles Lakers.
High-flying Career
Kobe wasn’t the hero of his new team right away. He was the youngest starter in the NBA, but he didn’t see a lot of playing time at first. Yet he was determined to get better.

“Those times when you stay up late and you work hard; those times when you don't feel like working — you’re too tired, you don’t want to push yourself — but you do it anyway. That is actually the dream. That’s the dream. It’s not the destination, it’s the journey,” said Kobe. Hard work and constantly pushing himself to be better were always part of Kobe’s thoughts.
His practice paid off. By the end of his first season, Kobe was on the court for much longer than he had been earlier in the season. And he kept getting better.

Kobe would go on to win 5 NBA championships with the Lakers. He was an 18-time member of the All-Star team, a 15-time member of the All-NBA team, the 2008 NBA Most Valuable Player, and 2-time NBA Finals Most Valuable Player. Kobe played for the Lakers for 20 years. He retired from professional basketball after the 2015-2016 season.

Family had always been important to Kobe. He and his wife had four daughters. Kobe loved coaching his daughters and helping them learn to play. On January 26, 2020, Kobe was flying in a helicopter with his 13-year-old daughter. They were headed to one of her basketball games. The day was cloudy, and the helicopter crashed. Kobe, his daughter, and the seven other people in the helicopter all died in the accident.
“Life is too short to get bogged down and be discouraged,” Kobe once said. “You have to keep moving. You have to keep going. Put one foot in front of the other, smile and just keep on rolling.”
Kobe’s death was a tragedy, but we remember him for the hard work, talent, and determination that made him a basketball legend.

articles.title: Tickle Tackle Botticelli
articles.author: JonArno Lawson
articles.description: A speaker describes a baby learning how to speak.
articles.intro: JonArno Lawson writes books for both children and adults. In this poem, a speaker describes a baby learning how to speak.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the words the speaker uses to demonstrate babies before they can talk.

blocks.text: Tickle tackle Botticelli
chirping cockatoo
chock-a-lick a chocolate drop
kockamamie moo.
Chomp alompa omphalos
charber choparoo.
Listen up and look around and
think a little, too.
A click and a clack,
a blip in the black —
a jittery dog
on a dock —
can you remember
how you thought
before you
learned to talk?
Consider that, consider this,
consider it on the dot,
that words, however used,
are just the playthings
of a thought.
