articles.title: The Corn Maze
articles.author: Kris Bitar
articles.description: A child's plan to scare their younger brother backfires.
articles.intro: Kris Bitar is a writer and teacher in Aberdeen, Washington. She has taught every level in K-12 for 30 years and her joy is working with children. In this story, the narrator visits a corn maze with their family. A corn maze is a set of puzzling paths that are carved in between tall, dry corn stalks in the fall.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the narrator feels about Max.
articles.permissions: "The Corn Maze" by Kris Bitar. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The Friday night before our visit to Prickly Pete’s Pumpkin Patch, my mom comes and checks on me and my younger brother, Max. She does this every night before we fall asleep. It’s like she still thinks I am a baby. This probably wouldn’t happen if I wasn’t sharing a room with Max. Yet another reason to hate him. I hate pretty much everything about him. He cries a lot and is always complaining. Earlier at dinner, he cried about having to come along with us tomorrow to the pumpkin patch. Then, when mom said, “Fine, you can stay home with dad,” he changed his mind and cried about this too! Mom goes over to his bed first, to make sure he is tucked in properly and gives him a kiss on the forehead. Then, she comes over and tries to pull this silliness on me. I pretend as if I am already sleeping. I hear her for a little while, rummaging around the room, searching for the iPad. It’s under my pillowcase. I know she won’t search there.

She switches off the light. Once I hear her leave the room, I message Brian and Brody from the iPad to make sure they are still coming with us tomorrow. I let them know we’ll be picking them up at 2:00 pm. I also remind them to bring a bag to put their pumpkin in and, according to mom, to bring boots. It’s supposed to rain tonight and the field may be muddy tomorrow. Brody asks if we can have a sleepover, so that we can carve our pumpkins when we get back. I tell him I’m going to have to get back to him on that one. I have to see what kind of mood my mom is in, and promise her that we won’t be loud, that we will go to bed at a reasonable time and clean up all the pumpkin guts when we finish carving. This could shape up to be the best weekend ever!
But first, I have to carry out my plan.
“Max,” I say, “don’t you want to stay up and watch a movie with me?”
“It’s bedtime,” he says.
“Yes, but there’s no school tomorrow,” I whisper.
“It’s bedtime,” he says.
“I know,” I say.

I climb off the bed, careful not to step on anything in the dark, and sit beside him on his bed. “Look at this,” I say. I already have some YouTube videos ready after typing “scary pumpkin patch stories” in the search bar. I’m hoping, you know, even on silent, the videos might convince Max to change his mind again.

The first couple of videos I play are lousy. Silly cartoons that Max just giggles at. But then there’s this one video that has pumpkins with fangs in it, and it manages to look really real. The pumpkins crawl on little legs and they are just about to sink their teeth into a child who has fallen while trying to run away from them. I gasp and switch it off just before the bad part. It’s quiet. I can hear our cat, Goose, at the window. Max starts giggling in the dark. He doesn’t say anything. My heart is pounding and the picture of those pumpkins is stuck in my head. “Goose,” he says.
“Go to bed,” I say. “And don’t tell anyone about this.”

I am up early the next morning. My mom is busy fussing over my little brother. I expected to find him crying about having to come along with us. But he is upset because he doesn’t want to wear boots. Finally, mom gives up but tells him he has to wear them in the pumpkin patch because it is muddy. She lets him know it will be fun to stomp in the mud. Interesting , I think to myself, she has never told me how fun it would be to stomp in the mud before. In fact, she never wanted me to get dirty at all when I was little. I think about how things change from one kid to another. Why does he get to play in the mud when I never got to do it? I tell her I cleaned my room, put away my laundry, and oh, by the way, can Brody and Brian spend the night after the pumpkin patch? The pumpkins with fangs and little feet are still crawling in my head. Mom stops dead in her tracks. She looks at me with that sideways glance and I can almost see the wheels spinning in her head. I quickly jump in with, “We will be very quiet, I PROMISE!”

Mom raises her head, smiles, and says, “Okay, but only if you truly promise.” Happiness spreads from my heart to my toes. I jump up and grab my iPad to message Brian and Brody. They are equally excited and promise to keep things clean, quiet, and low-key. They ask if they should bring flashlights so we can tell ghost stories when we are all in our sleeping bags. I’m not so sure after last night, but I tell them it is a great idea.

We pick up Brian and Brody, and then we are on the road. Thankfully, my little brother falls asleep in the car and does not wake up until we get there. It was almost like being sibling-free, like the good old days. It’s drizzling. Yesterday was a warm, fall day, and today, light rain. When we get there, we pull into a parking spot and the car fills with the sound of all of us unclicking our seatbelts at once. We are bursting at the seams to get out and make some noise!

Mom wakes up my little brother and now he wants to wear his boots. He smiles and says, “red boots,” and my mom thinks he is so smart and going to be a genius. I’m sure I said something like, “My new red boots,” when I was his age and no one got excited. I feel like mom doesn’t know I am possibly a genius as well. I guess she will find out later and then feel bad for not knowing it was me who was the brilliant one, and not my little brother.
I’ll deal with that later though because right now, caramel apple time!

Brian, Brody, and I leave my mom and Max to go and wait in line for our caramel apples. The plan is to head to the pumpkin patch after this, and begin our search for the perfect pumpkin. But then Brian points to a sign on the food wagon: The Corn Maze is open this year.
“We got to do it,” he says with glee. “This year, we are all old enough for the age eight and up, Haunted Corn Maze.”

We head towards the corn maze after eating our caramel apples. It seems like it goes on for miles and miles. I am still thinking about the little pumpkins with fangs. I have been thinking about them since we got here. There’s a man dressed in a zombie costume, handing out flyers for the maze. I am kind of glad now that Max came along, otherwise we would’ve come here in the evening when it gets dark earlier. Maybe having that little guy around isn’t too bad after all. My caramel apple is doing flip-flops in my stomach. The pumpkins with fangs have returned. Brian and Brody take off to get in line and then they look back at me, as if to say ‘hurry up,’ and I join them.

Once we’re inside the Haunted Corn Maze, Brian and Brody run quickly to avoid the vampires, mummies, and ghouls who try to grab at us. I try to keep up, but they are fast! A clown with an evil grin reaches for me and I scream, duck and run as fast as I can. A pumpkin, no , a field mouse scurries in front of me and I almost slip and fall in the mud to avoid stepping on him. An arm with long, black nails reaches under the corn stalks and tries to grab my leg. I don’t see Brian and Brody. I’m not enjoying myself. I want out of here! A fake crow dive-bombs my head and I holler for help! Loud, creepy music is playing and I think I am going to throw up the caramel apple I ate. Brian and Brody should have waited for me. I know it is only a matter of time before the pumpkins on little legs arrive. I wipe away a tear and look around to make sure no one saw me. But I am alone. I know I am. I hear a noise on my left and realize I need to keep running. Someone…something is coming. I’m about to scream when, out of the corner of my eye, I see…the exit!
I make for the exit and jump for freedom once I am under the sign. I feel instant relief.
And then I see them.

Brody and Brian are laughing! I can overhear them talking about zipping around the evil clown as he walked straight toward them and how funny they thought the costumes were, and they wondered why the people in them chose to dress as strange creatures and reach for their feet. I run over to them and they pat me on the back and say, “It looks like you’ve seen a ghost!” There’s another round of laughter. I act like it was the funniest thing I had heard all day. I try not to shake in front of them. I know I will never do the big corn maze again. I’ll just offer to take my little brother, Max, to the kiddy corn maze for the next eight years and I’ll be fine.
“Were you scared?” Brian asks. I don’t like his sarcastic tone.
“This corn maze was for babies,” I say. They laugh again and we walk toward the pumpkin patch. I’m feeling a little better. I’m also glad I was able to keep my caramel apple where it belongs.

At the end of the night we eat popcorn and carve pumpkins with my dad. I make a happy, smiling pumpkin instead of the scary face pumpkins that Brody and Brian are making. It feels safer knowing nothing scary is going to be watching me at night with a candle in its mouth. We finish up, clean up and get out our sleeping bags. Brian suggests we sleep outside. I shut that idea down right away. I am staying inside, warm and cozy in my sleeping bag. Brody suggests telling ghost stories. Instead, I start talking about swimming at the lake last summer. We all end up talking about the fun things we plan on doing this summer. We keep the noise down like we promised. We hold back our laughter until one of us snorts. This causes us to laugh until our stomachs hurt. Mom comes in and tells us to quiet down before we wake my little brother. I decide I like my brother after all and we quiet down.

Within a couple of minutes, I hear Brian snoring. Then I realize Brody is sleeping as well. I roll over onto my back and think how silly I was to be afraid of people dressed as mummies, vampires and ghouls. I realize I let my imagination get the best of me. I will not be searching for videos on my iPad before bed anymore. But also, I won’t try to stop Max from being who he is.
All in all, it was a pretty good day. But as I drift off to sleep, I hear something at the window.
I sit up. It has got to be just our cat, Goose…right?
Right?

options.label: Treat your parents with respect.
It is okay to take your time growing up.
Being brave is the easiest part of getting older.
True friends know how to make each other laugh.

questions.prompt: What is the lesson of "The Corn Maze"?

options.label: The narrator is bothered by Brian.
The narrator is tired and ready for bed.
The narrator is worried about the corn maze.
The narrator is happy to spend more time with Max.

questions.prompt: What does the following sentence show about how the narrator feels? "I am kind of glad now that Max came along, otherwise we would've come here in the evening when it gets dark earlier." (Paragraph 19)

options.label: "Once I hear her leave the room, I message Brian and Brody from the iPad to make sure they are still coming with us tomorrow." (Paragraph 2)
"My heart is pounding and the picture of those pumpkins is stuck in my head." (Paragraph 10)
"'This corn maze was for babies,' I say." (Paragraph 25)
"But as I drift off to sleep, I hear something at the window." (Paragraph 28)

questions.prompt: Which sentence shows that the narrator tries to hide their feelings?

options.label: strong fear.
very certain.
feeling very full.
great happiness.

questions.prompt: The word "glee" in paragraph 18 most closely means —
How do the narrator's feelings about Max change from the beginning of the story to the end?

discussionQuestions.prompt: The narrator feels like their sibling, Max, gets treated differently than they were treated at the same age. If you have siblings, do you think your family treats you and your siblings differently? If you do not have siblings, do you think that the narrator's feelings are fair? Why or why not?
Do you think it was a good idea for the narrator to try to scare Max? Why or why not? Have you ever tried to scare someone? Would you like to scare someone? Why or why not?

Thinking about this story, what does it mean to be "grown up"? Do you think the narrator acts "grown up"? Why or why not? How do the narrator's fears make them act throughout the story? If someone is afraid of something, what does that mean about them being "grown up"? Why do you think that?

articles.title: On St. Patrick's Day
articles.author: Jenny Whitehead
articles.description: A speaker takes part in St. Patrick's Day traditions.
articles.intro: St. Patrick's Day happens on March 17th each year. It is a holiday that celebrates, or honors, St. Patrick, the patron saint of the country Ireland. In this poem, a speaker talks about this holiday.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what the speaker shares about St. Patrick’s Day in the poem.
articles.permissions: "On St. Patrick's Day" by Jenny Whitehead from "Holiday Stew: A Kid's Portion of Holiday and Seasonal Poems." Used with permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: A “Top of the Morning!”
Child, where is your green?
From my head to my feet,
I am green in between!
Got my leprechaun hat
And my shamrock balloon,
‘Tis an Irish parade
Passing by here at noon.
Aye, soda bread’s baking
Corned beef’s in the pot,
I’m dancing a jig —
My feet, they won’t stop!
A toast to good health —
Lift your green milk — SLAINTE !
Not Irish? No matter...
Ye’re Irish today!

options.label: St. Patrick's Day is celebrated only in the morning.
St. Patrick's Day is an important day to think about the past.
St. Patrick's Day is an Irish holiday that anyone can celebrate.
St. Patrick's Day is a great time to play tricks on other people.

questions.prompt: What is the central message of the poem?

options.label: The speaker is a child who is explaining St. Patrick's Day to an adult.
The speaker is an adult who is telling a child who is not Irish about St. Patrick's Day.
The speaker is a child who is teaching a friend who is not Irish about St. Patrick's Day.
The speaker is an adult who is teaching children how to dance in a St. Patrick's Day parade.

questions.prompt: What is the most likely relationship between the speaker and the person they are talking to?

options.label: an important color on St. Patrick's Day.
a strange color for food and clothing.
the best color to wear in the spring.
the speaker's least favorite color.

questions.prompt: The repeated use of the word "green" in the poem shows that green is —

options.label: The speaker is tired from celebrating St. Patrick's Day.
The speaker is learning new things on St. Patrick's Day.
The speaker is excited about celebrating St. Patrick's Day.
The speaker is nervous to be a part of the St. Patrick's Day parade.

questions.prompt: What do lines 11-12 show about the speaker? "I'm dancing a jig — / My feet, they won't stop!"
How are lines 15-16 important to the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you celebrate St. Patrick's Day? If so, how is the speaker's celebration the same or different from how you celebrate the holiday? If you do not celebrate St. Patrick's Day, how does it seem the same or different from holidays you celebrate or know about?

Have you ever been to a parade? What are some fun events that happen at a parade? What is a holiday that is celebrated with a parade in your town, community, or culture? How do parades help us celebrate important events?

Many people think that wearing green on St. Patrick's Day brings good luck. What are some other things that people do to bring themselves good luck? Do you do anything to bring yourself good luck? Do you think that these things help bring luck? Why or why not?

articles.title: Recess! Oh, Recess!
articles.author: Darren Sardelli
articles.description: A speaker shares why they love recess.
articles.intro: In this poem, the speaker shares their feelings about recess.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s point of view about recess.
articles.permissions: "Recess! Oh, Recess!" from Galaxy Pizza and Meteor Pie by Darren Sardelli. Copyright ©2009 Laugh-A-Lot Books. Used with permission from Darren Sardelli.

blocks.text: Recess! Oh, Recess!
We love you! You rule!
You keep us away
from the teachers in school.
Your swings are refreshing.
Your slides are the best.
You give us a break
from a really hard test.
Recess! Oh, Recess!
We want you to know,
you’re sweeter than syrup,
you’re special like snow.
You don’t assign homework.
You make the day fun.
You let us play kickball
and run in the sun.
Recess! Oh, Recess!
You’re first on our list.
We’d be in despair
if you didn’t exist.
We’re happy we have you.
You’re awesome and cool.
Recess! Oh, Recess!
We love you! You rule!

options.label: Kids learn more at recess than in class.
Recess should be used for trying new things.
Kids love recess more than their teachers do.
Recess is an important part of the school day.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?

options.label: recess
a friend
a teacher
the audience

questions.prompt: Who is the speaker addressing in the poem?

options.label: Recess is best during the winter.
Recess only comes a few times a year.
Recess brings enjoyment like a snowfall does.
Recess should take place during all types of weather.

questions.prompt: What does the poet mean when he describes recess as "special like snow" in line 12?

options.label: "Your slides are the best." (Line 6)
"You give us a break/ from a really hard test." (Line 7-8)
"you're sweeter than syrup," (Line 11)
"We're happy we have you." (Line 21)

questions.prompt: Which line from the text is an example of how the poet uses a comparison (metaphor) to describe recess?
What does the speaker's point of view reveal about recess?

discussionQuestions.prompt: How do you feel about recess? Are your feelings similar to or different from the feelings of the speaker?
What does the speaker like about recess? In your opinion, what is the best part of recess?

How do you think this poem would be different if it was told from a different point of view, like a teacher, parent or kid who was new at school? As a creative extension, write a poem about recess from a different point of view that shows a different perspective about recess.

An ode is a poem that celebrates something or someone. This poem is an ode to recess. If you were going to write an ode to something, what would you write about? Why? As a creative extension, write a poem in the form of an ode about something or someone you want to celebrate.

articles.title: I Can Hear Spring
articles.author: Sally Selleck
articles.description: Noisy Canadian geese mark the beginning of a new season.
articles.intro: In this text, the narrator shares how they know spring has arrived.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the narrator feels about their visitors.
articles.permissions: "I Can Hear Spring" by Sally Selleck, Ladybug . © 2018 by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Just around the time when most of the snow has melted, spring comes honking back to my home in Spencerville, Ontario. It comes loudly. Canada geese don’t care if you’re sleeping or if you’re eating breakfast — they honk like crazy!
The sound is a whisper at first: honk, honk, honk , coming from the south. Then I see tiny dots, way up high. Their calls get louder: honk, honk, honk!

The dots form the letter V, moving quickly toward me in the blue sky. There are many geese flying in that V shape. The one at the front has the hardest job. When he gets tired, he moves back and another one takes a turn being the leader. They are even louder now: honk, honk, honk!

The geese fly right over me. I can see their black heads with white chinstraps. Their long black necks stretch out like darts. Their dark brown wings wave to me as they flap through the air. I can’t hear anything else, just honk, honk, honk!
I watch the geese touch down in the field behind my house. Two black legs with webbed feet unfold from under their creamy white bellies. They stop flapping their wings and glide to perfect landings.

The geese are hungry after their long journey. A farmer’s field is a great place to eat and rest. They feast on bits of leftover corn in the mud and swim in large puddles of melted snow, looking for more food. When they find something, they tip forward and put their head and neck right underwater to get it. The water sure looks cold to me!

More and more land until, in a few days, the field is jam-packed with noisy Canada geese. They honk to say hello to each other: honk, honk, honk, honk . They honk to warn of danger, like when my big dog Harley splashes through the puddles, ready for a game of chase! Honk. Honk. Honk . They honk because they are happy: honk, honk, honk, honk .
I watch, and I listen. The geese will be leaving in another couple of days to continue on their noisy journey north. Spring is finally here!

options.label: Living on a farm is hard work.
Spring begins in different ways every year.
Geese are strange animals that are hard to understand.
There are signs that show that the seasons are changing.

questions.prompt: What is the message of the text?

options.label: fearfully.
noisily.
smoothly.
strangely.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 5, the word "glide" shows that the geese move —

options.label: "The dots form the letter V, moving quickly toward me in the blue sky." (Paragraph 3)
"The geese fly right over me." (Paragraph 4)
"A farmer's field is a great place to eat and rest." (Paragraph 6)
"The geese will be leaving in another couple of days to continue on their noisy journey north." (Paragraph 8)

questions.prompt: Which line from the text helps the reader understand where the story takes place?

options.label: to help the reader understand how noisy the geese are
to show that there are only a couple of geese at the narrator's house
to bother the reader in the same way the geese are bothering the narrator
to explain that geese are quieter than other animals at the narrator's house

questions.prompt: Why does the author repeat the words "honk, honk, honk, honk" throughout the text?

options.label: Working on a Farm
Spring Never Comes
Noisy Springtime Visitors
Different Birds of Canada

questions.prompt: A different title for this story could be —
How does the narrator feel about the geese coming back to their home?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you look forward to spring arriving? What are ways that you know spring has arrived? Are your feelings about spring's arrival similar to or different from the author's feelings?

The narrator of the story loves to watch the geese in their backyard. Do you think you would feel the same way as the narrator if you had hundreds of Canada geese in your backyard? Why or why not? What is an animal that you love to watch? Why do you love to watch this animal?

In the story, the sounds of the geese remind the narrator that spring is getting close. What sounds make you think of spring? Why? What sounds remind you of other seasons, like winter, fall, and summer? As a creative task, write a story about a sound that reminds you of your favorite season.

articles.title: The Danderfield Twins: Goodbye, Goodbye
articles.author: Polly Horvath
articles.description: The Danderfield twins have a surprising adventure with their family and friends
articles.intro: The Danderfield Twins is a series of books about a set of twins that goes on adventures. In this story, the Danderfield twins take a surprising trip with their family and friends.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Maisie and Deirdre’s actions.
articles.permissions: "The Danderfield Twins: Goodbye, Goodbye" by Polly Horvath, Spider . © 2007 by Cricket Media, Inc.. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: “Winning this holiday cruise for all of us by guessing the number of jellybeans in a six-foot jar is the smartest thing you’ve ever done,” said Kavita to Deidre as she stretched out on her deck chair around the ship’s swimming pool.
“It’s such a great adventure, and we’re so lucky they let you bring your family and two friends,” said Marty.
“And don’t forget pets,” said Deidre, giving Plato a pat on the head.

“It’s so sad when a story comes to an end,” said Maisie, closing her magazine. “I’m done with this issue and now I have nothing to read. I think I’ll go to the ship’s library and see if I can find another one.”
But just as she stood up, a sudden jolt rocked the ship so violently that the pool water splashed up, soaking them.

“Wow,” said Deidre. “Let’s go investigate.” Kavita and Marty chose to stay by the pool, but Maisie went off with Deidre to see what caused the jolt. When they came back, Mr. and Mrs. Danderfield and Marcus were with them. All five Danderfields looked grim.
“You know that bossa nova dance contest we were all going to enter tonight?” said Maisie, shaking her head gravely.
“Yes,” said Kavita. “What about it?”
“We’ll have to postpone it,” said Deidre.
“Oh no, why?” wailed Kavita. She had been practicing her bossa all morning and had almost perfected her nova.
“Because,” said Deidre. “We have very much hit an iceberg.”
“Very much?” asked Marty. “You’re sure it’s not just slightly?”
“In the middle of the south seas?” said Kavita. “That seems odd to me.”
“To me, too,” said Maisie. “But the captain said he can almost always tell an iceberg from a collision with a small craft. He told us to abandon ship right away.”
All the Danderfields and Marty and Kavita managed to squeeze into one lifeboat.
“Now this is cozy,” said Deidre, removing Commander Lapin’s paw from her ear and Plato’s tail from the collar of her shirt.
Fortunately, land was in sight. Unfortunately, when their boat got near to shore, it became apparent to all that the beaches of this fair isle were badly infested with tigers.
“If it had only been lions,” said Kavita.
“Are they less dangerous?” asked Maisie.
“No, but I’ve always wanted to dance the bossa nova with one. I’m almost sure it can be done.”
“Now what do we do?” asked Marty.
“I believe it’s time for me to swim to shore and practice my tiger-mesmerizing skills,” said Maisie.
“I didn’t know you knew how to mesmerize tigers,” said Marty.

“There’s so much you don’t know,” said Maisie. She dove into the water and did a dashing butterfly stroke, skirting the sharks and sea snakes and bounding onto the sand, where in just a few quick moments she had put all the tigers down for naps.
“What talent,” gasped Marty.
“What silliness,” said Kavita. “This whole episode in our lives is getting sillier and sillier.”
“Maybe because it’s the end, and endings are hard,” said Deidre.
“The end of what?” asked Marty.
“The end of the cruise. Part of the prize was a week on an island. I wanted to keep that part of the prize a secret until the last minute. Look at those tigers up close,” said Deidre, giggling.
“Hey,” said Marty. “They’re just people in tiger suits.”
“Welcome to Adventure Island!” said one of the tigers. “You’ve won a week’s stay here, learning survival skills, living in bamboo huts, and eating three gourmet meals a day.”
“Wow,” said Kavita. “This prize gets better and better.”
“But I’m sorry the cruise is over,” said Marty. “I really liked it.”
“Never mind. The third and final part of the prize is taking a tall ship home and learning to sail!” said Maisie. “It’s sad to leave an adventure, but there’s always a new one around the corner.”
“So say good-bye to the cruise ship!” said Deidre as they watched it sail into the sunset. “And let’s begin the new adventure.”
“Good-bye!” called the children and Mr. and Mrs. Danderfield. Plato barked and Commander Lapin twitched his nose. “Good-bye! Good-bye! Good-bye!”

options.label: Life can be a series of fun adventures.
Making mistakes is part of growing up.
Safety is an important part of having fun.
Learning to do something new takes courage.

questions.prompt: What is the main message of the story?

options.label: Kavita feels excited because she does not want to dance on stage.
Kavita feels disappointed because she has been preparing for the dance.
Kavita feels joyful because she wants more time to learn the dance moves.
Kavite feels confused because she does not understand why the dance will be later.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 10, how does Kavita feel about the dance contest getting postponed?

options.label: She is nervous and does not know how to swim.
She is afraid to go to the island to meet the tigers.
She is moving quickly and bravely towards the island.
She is trying to get closer to the sharks and sea snakes.

questions.prompt: In paragraph 24 what do the words "dashing," "bounding," and "skirting" show the reader about Maisie?

options.label: It explains what will happen when they go home.
It explains how Deidre and Maisie won the prize.
It shows how the Danderfields feel about being on vacation.
It shows that Deidre was playing a joke on her family and friends.

questions.prompt: Why is paragraph 29 important to the rest of the story?
How are Maisie and Deirdre clever?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, the Danderfield twins and their family and friends are on a vacation that Deirdre won as a prize. Have you ever entered a contest to win a prize? Have you ever won a prize? What did you win? How did you feel after you won? If you have not won a prize, how do you think you might feel if you did win? Would you share your prize, like Deirdre has done? Why or why not?

In the story, the Danderfield twins play a trick on their family and friends. Do you like playing tricks? Do you like it when people play tricks on you? Why or why not? How would you feel if you were on vacation with the Danderfield twins and they played those tricks on you? Why?

In the story, the characters go on a cruise and then arrive at an island where they are going to be "learning survival skills, living in bamboo huts, and eating three gourmet meals a day." What part of their trip sounds the most fun to you? What kind of vacations or trips do you like taking with your family, friends, or loved ones? Do you prefer to relax on vacation or do lots of activities? What would your dream vacation be?
